key_numbers.sub: targeted questioning

key_numbers.label: Right with 12 random extra tests
key_numbers.sub: same budget, unfocused questions

key_numbers.label: Hardest batch, fully solved
key_numbers.sub: 12 targeted questions

charts.chart_plain_title: Smart extra questions close the hidden-failure gap
charts.chart_read: Left to right, more extra test cases are requested; higher up means more programs pass every hidden test (better). One line asks the most revealing question each time, one asks at random, and a top line shows the best any questioner could do.
charts.chart_takeaway: The smart-question line rises well above the random line at every point, meaning deliberately chosen tests weed out wrong programs far faster.

charts.chart_plain_title: Six smart questions beat random on all three batches
charts.chart_read: Each group of bars is one batch of problems; taller bars mean more programs pass every hidden test (better). Bars go from asking nothing, to six random questions, to six smart questions, to the best-possible questioner.
charts.chart_takeaway: The six-smart-question bar nearly matches the best-possible bar and towers over the random bar on all three batches.

charts.chart_plain_title: Smart questions fully solve the hardest batch
charts.chart_read: Left to right, more extra test cases are requested; higher up means more programs pass every hidden test (better). One line picks the most revealing question each time, one picks at random, and a top line marks the best a questioner could do.
charts.chart_takeaway: The smart-question line reaches the very top by twelve questions, matching the best-possible line, while random questions stall below it.

qwen35_4b_adaptive_evidence_budget_polic.verdict_tag: Model learned when to stop gathering evidence
qwen35_4b_adaptive_evidence_budget_polic.concept_primer: Picture a detective who does not choose which tests to run, but decides after each result whether they know enough to name the culprit or should order one more test. That single stop-or-continue call is what we trained.
qwen35_4b_adaptive_evidence_budget_polic.plain_question: Can the small model learn when it has seen enough evidence to commit to an answer, instead of always running every possible check?
qwen35_4b_adaptive_evidence_budget_polic.plain_answer: Yes. After training, the model solved 92% of tasks while running only about 5 checks on average, matching the accuracy of always running all 10 checks at roughly half the effort, and nearly as efficiently as the best-possible stopping strategy.
qwen35_4b_adaptive_evidence_budget_polic.why_it_matters: A model can be taught to spend effort only when a task needs it, cutting wasted work on easy problems while still nailing the hard ones, rather than paying a flat cost every time.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved, before vs after training
key_numbers.sub: the untrained model quit almost immediately; training taught it to keep going

key_numbers.label: Checks used on average
key_numbers.sub: same accuracy as running all ten, at roughly half the cost

key_numbers.label: Best-possible stopping uses
key_numbers.sub: the trained model's ~4.8 is close to this ideal

key_numbers.label: Simple fixed cutoff of 3 checks solves
key_numbers.sub: stopping too early on every task leaves most unsolved

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is average checks used, the vertical axis is share of tasks solved correctly; up and to the left is better. One line traces simple fixed cutoffs of 3, 6, and 10 checks; separate dots mark the trained model and the best-possible stopping strategy.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained model's dot sits above the fixed-cutoff line, hitting top accuracy at fewer checks, close to the best-possible dot.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of tasks solved by each stopping approach
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different stopping rule; bar height is the share of tasks solved correctly, so taller is better. The untrained model and quick-cutoff rules sit at the left; the trained model, best-possible stopping, and the full ten-check budget sit at the right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained model's tiny bar jumps to a tall one after training, matching both the ideal stopper and the full-budget bar.

charts.chart_read: The two groups are an easy task type and a hard task type; bar height is average checks used, and lower means less effort. Bars compare the trained model, the best-possible stopper, and a rule that always runs all ten checks.
charts.chart_takeaway: On easy tasks the trained model's bar is short and on hard tasks it is tall, closely tracking the best-possible stopper instead of the flat ten.

qwen35_4b_adaptive_tool_controller.verdict_tag: Knowing when tools help beats running them all
qwen35_4b_adaptive_tool_controller.concept_primer: Picture a data clerk reshaping spreadsheets, usually answering by eye. One tell flags the hard cases: when her quick answer has fewer columns than the raw data holds, real restructuring is needed, so she runs a scripted tool instead of guessing.
qwen35_4b_adaptive_tool_controller.plain_question: When a small model can answer a table task directly or run a slower program, can one simple rule decide when the program is worth the extra cost?
qwen35_4b_adaptive_tool_controller.plain_answer: Partly. One structural cue — does the direct answer have fewer columns than the raw data implies? — safely flags the reshaping tasks where a program helps, lifting accuracy from 42% to 50% with zero broken tasks. But it captures only half the gain; the full 58% ceiling needs a slightly broader set of tool calls, not one cheap program.
qwen35_4b_adaptive_tool_controller.why_it_matters: Before wiring an expensive tool loop into a small-model system, look for one cheap structural signal that predicts when the tool actually helps, and route on it. Firing every tool by default costs many times more and gains nothing extra.

key_numbers.label: Accuracy from direct answering to cued tool routing
key_numbers.sub: exact-match on 50 unseen table tasks

key_numbers.label: Ceiling any policy reached
key_numbers.sub: hit by a fixed shape rule and a perfect-hindsight a perfect chooser

key_numbers.label: Tool calls that helped versus hurt
key_numbers.sub: every tool the router fired paid off

key_numbers.label: Extra compute from running every tool
key_numbers.sub: 17,801 vs 1,478 tokens per task, same 58% as a cheap rule

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy of each tool-use policy on the test tasks
charts.chart_read: Bars give exact-match accuracy over 50 test tasks, one per policy: answer directly, the learned one-cue controller, the best fixed shape rule, and two perfect-hindsight oracles. Taller is better; the oracles mark the 58% ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned controller lifts 42% to 50% but stalls halfway; the fixed shape rule and both oracles reach 58%, so one cue is not enough.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy versus compute cost per task
charts.chart_read: Each point plots a policy's accuracy against its average tokens per task, the compute cost. One line links the three deployable policies, another the two hindsight oracles. Up and to the left — more accuracy, less compute — is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deployable policies climb cheaply to 58%; the full-budget a perfect chooser sits far to the right at the same 58%, so heavy extra spending buys no accuracy.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task families the controller actually improves
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per task family, five tasks each: darker is direct answering, lighter is the learned controller. Higher is better; equal-height pairs mean tools changed nothing. Families run from structural splits to already-easy folds.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both gains come from two split-and-reshape families rising from 0% to 40%; every other family is unchanged, so all gains sit in a narrow structural niche.

qwen35_4b_answer_potential_trace_sft.verdict_tag: The signal was real but graded an unreachable
qwen35_4b_answer_potential_trace_sft.concept_primer: To judge a chunk of a model's own reasoning, this method staples the known-correct answer onto its end and measures how naturally the model would voice it next — like rating a diving board by how a champion's dive fits it, while the real diver always belly-flops off.
qwen35_4b_answer_potential_trace_sft.plain_question: Can you pick the best homegrown reasoning to train a small model on by measuring how confident it becomes in the correct answer after each attempt?
qwen35_4b_answer_potential_trace_sft.plain_answer: No. The score carried genuine signal — it tracked what the reasoning actually said, not its length or wording — but its top pick raised fresh-answer success only from about 13% to 20%, short of the useful margin needed to bank it. The deeper flaw: the model almost never stopped thinking on its own, so the score rated an answering moment it could rarely reach.
qwen35_4b_answer_potential_trace_sft.why_it_matters: Before training a small model on its own reasoning, confirm it can actually stop and commit to an answer unprompted — and demand a useful margin over random picking, not just any edge. A score can be genuinely informative yet grade a moment your deployed model never enters.

key_numbers.label: Best-scored reasoning vs a random pick
key_numbers.sub: chance the chosen thought led to a correct fresh answer

key_numbers.label: Thoughts that finished thinking on their own
key_numbers.sub: 13 of 2,048 stopped naturally; the rest ran into the length limit

key_numbers.label: Forced answers that came out readable
key_numbers.sub: though 87% of those readable answers were correct

key_numbers.label: Ranking margin delivered vs required
key_numbers.sub: edge over a random pick fell short of the launch bar

charts.chart_plain_title: The score missed every launch test that mattered
charts.chart_read: Each group is one launch requirement; the darker bar is what the score achieved, the lighter bar the minimum needed to proceed. The four checks: ranking power, margin over a random pick, margin over the shortest pick, and share of thoughts scoring high before naming the answer. Higher achieved is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every achieved bar sits below its required minimum on all four checks, so no reasoning was ever selected or trained on.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where the answer gets lost: stopping, not correctness
charts.chart_read: Three bars trace the finish, left to right: how often a thought stopped on its own, how often a forced-stop answer came out readable, and how often those readable answers were correct. Higher is better at every stage.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first two bars are tiny while the third towers — the model rarely stops or emits a clean answer, yet when it does it is almost always right.

qwen35_4b_balanced_core_answer_potential.verdict_tag: Which self-generated reasoning is worth teaching back
qwen35_4b_balanced_core_answer_potential.concept_primer: A small model solves each problem many different ways in its head. For every attempt, a scorer asks: does this line of thinking make the model surer of the correct final answer? The plan banks the most answer-boosting thoughts and retrains the model on them.
qwen35_4b_balanced_core_answer_potential.plain_question: If a model reasons through a problem many different ways, can we spot which reasoning to train on by which one makes the right answer likelier?
qwen35_4b_balanced_core_answer_potential.plain_answer: Not run yet — the data is collected but no model has been trained. The test compares six ways to pick which self-generated reasoning to teach back, then measures fresh-task accuracy. It matters because early tuning checks hinted that simply picking the shortest complete reasoning ranked answers better than the clever answer-likelihood score, so the smart method has real competition to beat.
qwen35_4b_balanced_core_answer_potential.why_it_matters: When building training data from a small model's own reasoning, don't assume a sophisticated answer-probability score beats the boring shortest-complete trace. Test against that cheap control before spending compute scoring every attempt.

key_numbers.label: Reasoning tasks banked
key_numbers.sub: collections of self-generated thoughts finished before the design was frozen

key_numbers.label: Selection strategies compared
key_numbers.sub: methods for choosing which reasoning to teach, including a shortest-reasoning control

key_numbers.label: Bar to declare a winner
key_numbers.sub: minimum fresh-task accuracy gain the smart selector must beat its best control by

key_numbers.label: Reasoning generated so far
key_numbers.sub: thinking tokens the model produced across all attempts

qwen35_4b_balanced_discriminative_bridge.concept_primer: Think of teaching someone to fix broken code by handing them a few practice problems first. The question: does an even, balanced practice set teach better, or a set hand-picked to be the trickiest?
qwen35_4b_balanced_discriminative_bridge.plain_question: Does hand-picking the trickiest practice problems teach the model to fix new code better than a plain, evenly-spread practice set?
qwen35_4b_balanced_discriminative_bridge.plain_answer: No. An even, balanced practice set lifted success on new tasks from about 60% to 99%, while hand-picking the hardest, most-confusing practice problems did clearly worse (around 83-90%) on the same practice budget.
qwen35_4b_balanced_discriminative_bridge.why_it_matters: When adding a small set of practice examples, favor even coverage across task types over clever selection of the hardest cases. Chasing difficulty can quietly regress task types that a balanced set handles cleanly.

key_numbers.label: Even practice set, tough new tasks

key_numbers.label: Hardest-picked practice set
key_numbers.sub: same budget, worse than the even set

key_numbers.label: Worked examples scrambled
key_numbers.sub: model genuinely reads the examples

charts.chart_read: Four training setups along the bottom, from a plain starter to the even practice set to two hand-picked-hard sets; paired bars show easier versus tougher new tasks; height is share of new tasks solved completely, and taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The even-practice bars stand tallest near 99%, while both hand-picked-hard setups sit lower, so picking harder examples hurt rather than helped.
