charts.chart_takeaway: The even set stays near the top across every task type, while the hand-picked sets crater on some, dropping as low as 17%.

qwen35_4b_bank_the_thoughts.verdict_tag: Its own reasoning teaches nothing; real plans do
qwen35_4b_bank_the_thoughts.concept_primer: Two ways to write down how a dish was made: a forward recipe that chains raw ingredients through each step to the finished plate, or a chef who already plated it and back-fills a tidy story. Only the forward recipe teaches a newcomer to cook.
qwen35_4b_bank_the_thoughts.plain_question: If you take the successful reasoning a small model produces on problems it solves and train it back on that reasoning, does it get better at new ones?
qwen35_4b_bank_the_thoughts.plain_answer: No. Training the model on its own successful reasoning taught it nothing beyond showing it the bare answers — both solved about 9 in 100 fresh three-step problems. But a short hand-written plan that builds the solution forward, one step at a time, lifted that to about 22 in 100, roughly 2.6 times better. The model's own "reasoning" was after-the-fact excuse-making, not a real method.
qwen35_4b_bank_the_thoughts.why_it_matters: Don't collect a small model's own successful reasoning and retrain on it expecting more depth — those traces are inert rationalizations. Bank genuine forward, step-by-step plans instead, and verify them: a plausible-but-wrong plan actively hurts, scoring below plain answers.

key_numbers.label: Model's own reasoning vs. bare answers
key_numbers.sub: share of fresh three-step problems solved given 16 tries — self-training its own thoughts adds nothing over answers

key_numbers.label: A genuine forward plan vs. bare answers
key_numbers.sub: same tasks, same code, only the reasoning differs — about 2.6 times better

key_numbers.label: Its own thoughts were far longer yet worse
key_numbers.sub: the model's rambling reasoning against the compact hand-built plan

key_numbers.label: A wrong plan vs. bare answers
key_numbers.sub: a mismatched plan on correct code solved below answers alone — wrong reasoning is worse than none

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct plans beat answers; wrong plans do worse than either
charts.chart_read: Five training setups along the bottom: untrained base, answers only, correct step-by-step plans, wrong plans, and correct plans deployed without letting the model think. Two bars each — solve rate over 16 tries and the single best guess. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Correct-plan training tops every bar; wrong plans sink below answers-only; a plan-trained model blocked from thinking collapses near zero — the skill lives in the reasoning it generates.

charts.chart_plain_title: Picking the right next step: answers help a little, plans need thinking
charts.chart_read: Three setups — untrained base, answers only, plans — each with two bars: how often it picks the correct next operation without thinking versus after thinking. A reference line marks blind guessing, about 3 in 100. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Answer-training lifts next-step picks to about 1 in 10, several times chance; the plan-trained bars read zero only because that measurement never finished — so look-ahead stays untested, not disproven.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model's own thoughts don't help; a hand-built plan does
charts.chart_read: Three versions trained on the same solved problems with identical code, differing only in the reasoning attached: bare answers, the model's own thoughts, an explicit forward plan. Two bars each — solve rate over 16 tries and the single best guess. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The own-thoughts bar sits exactly level with the answers bar; only the explicit forward plan rises, about 2.6 times higher — plan quality, not merely having reasoning, is what transfers.

qwen35_4b_banking_installs_structure.verdict_tag: Training teaches the plan; brute-force wins anyway
qwen35_4b_banking_installs_structure.concept_primer: Each task needs a tiny three-step program: a sequence of list operations (the plan) plus exact settings (the amounts). Like a recipe, picking the right order of actions—chop, then boil, then season—is the hard part; once the plan is set, filling amounts is easy.
qwen35_4b_banking_installs_structure.plain_question: When a small model can't write a correct three-step program, can training on similar tasks teach it the right sequence of steps—and finally make it useful?
qwen35_4b_banking_installs_structure.plain_answer: Yes, then no. Training lifted a 4-billion-parameter model from never proposing the right step-sequence (0%) to getting it right about half the time (51%) on brand-new tasks—a real new skill, not memorized answers. But if you can run code, blindly trying all 4,096 possible step-sequences and keeping what works solves 97.5%, crushing the trained model's 46%. The model becomes unnecessary.
qwen35_4b_banking_installs_structure.why_it_matters: Before paying to train program structure into a small model, count your possible program shapes. If a code interpreter can execute candidates and you can list every shape, brute-force search plus output-voting beats the model outright.

key_numbers.label: Right step-sequence, before vs after training
key_numbers.sub: found the correct order of operations on brand-new three-step tasks

key_numbers.label: Full program correct after training
key_numbers.sub: right plan 51% of the time, but exact settings too only 36% — a 15-point gap

key_numbers.label: Solving with search vs the model's plan
key_numbers.sub: trying all 4,096 possible plans and voting beats deploying the trained model

key_numbers.label: Trained model in a single pass
key_numbers.sub: one forward-pass attempt, no code execution

charts.chart_plain_title: Training gives the model the right plan half the time; base never does
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare the untrained base model against the trained model on three success measures: single best guess, correct within eight tries, and right step-sequence with any settings within eight tries. Bar height is solve rate on new tasks; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The base is flat zero on all three—it never even orders the steps right. Training rises to 51% on step-sequence but only 36% fully correct, exposing a leftover settings gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: With a code interpreter, brute-force search beats the trained model
charts.chart_read: Three bars show deploy success on new three-step tasks: the trained model's single forward-pass guess, its proposed plan plus a settings-search, and brute-forcing every possible plan plus settings-search then voting on outputs. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The brute-force bar towers at 97.5% versus 46% for the model's own plan and 20% single-pass—searching every plan and checking outputs near-solves the task without the model.

qwen35_4b_banking_thinking_stack.verdict_tag: Extra thinking sharpens easy moves, not long-range planning
qwen35_4b_banking_thinking_stack.concept_primer: The model must pick the right next move toward a goal several moves off, like hunting a forced checkmate. Two separate upgrades: drilling the winning pattern into its training, and letting it reason longer before each move. Does stacking both beat either alone?
qwen35_4b_banking_thinking_stack.plain_question: If extra training and extra thinking each help a small model pick better moves toward a goal, does combining both give you the sum of their gains?
qwen35_4b_banking_thinking_stack.plain_answer: It depends on how far the goal is. One move away, the two boosts stack almost perfectly: a plain model picks the right move 27.5% of the time, extra training lifts that to 52.5%, and adding thinking reaches 85% — the exact sum, no wasted overlap. But three moves out, extra thinking adds nothing (about 17% right, with or without it); only training moved planning. Blind guessing scores about 3%.
qwen35_4b_banking_thinking_stack.why_it_matters: Need multi-step planning from a small model? Earn it in training. A bigger thinking budget at answer time won't manufacture foresight the weights never learned — it only sharpens moves already near the goal. And score accuracy by distance-to-goal, not one blended number.

key_numbers.label: Near the goal, plain model vs both boosts
key_numbers.sub: how often the correct next move is ranked first, goal one move away

key_numbers.label: The two boosts near the goal
key_numbers.sub: training and extra thinking each help independently, with no overlap

key_numbers.label: Three moves out, what extra thinking adds
key_numbers.sub: barely moves the hard planning step; training alone did the lifting (2.5% → 17.5%)

key_numbers.label: Blind guessing
key_numbers.sub: chance of picking the correct next move with no skill, roughly 1 in 32

charts.chart_plain_title: How training and extra thinking combine on easy versus hard moves
charts.chart_read: Four conditions across the bottom: plain model, plain plus thinking, trained, trained plus thinking. Two bars each — how often the correct next move is ranked first with the goal one move away (easy) versus three moves away (hard). Taller is better; chance sits near 3%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The easy-move bars climb with each boost, peaking at 85% when both combine; the hard-move bars stay low and barely rise once thinking is added — planning resists extra thinking.

qwen35_4b_bucket_belief_probe_ranker.verdict_tag: Trained question-picker barely beats the simple rule
qwen35_4b_bucket_belief_probe_ranker.concept_primer: Think of Twenty Questions: to find a hidden answer, you pick tests that split the remaining possibilities. A good test rules out many wrong answers at once, so fewer rounds are needed.
qwen35_4b_bucket_belief_probe_ranker.plain_question: Can we train the model to choose smarter narrowing tests than a simple fixed rule already picks?
qwen35_4b_bucket_belief_probe_ranker.plain_answer: No. After training, the model solved 50% of puzzles in three tests, essentially tying the 49% from a simple fixed rule. A strategy allowed to peek at the answer reaches 87%, so almost none of that headroom was captured.
qwen35_4b_bucket_belief_probe_ranker.why_it_matters: Picking better narrowing tests from a fixed shortlist is not something training unlocks here. Real gains need a richer view of the puzzle state or freedom to invent new tests, not just re-ranking the same options.

key_numbers.label: Puzzles solved by a simple fixed rule
key_numbers.sub: share cracked in three tests, the baseline to beat

key_numbers.label: Puzzles solved after training the picker
key_numbers.sub: barely moved, not a real improvement

key_numbers.label: Solved by a strategy that peeks at the answer
key_numbers.sub: the best-possible ceiling, mostly unreached

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of puzzles solved in three tests, by strategy
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy; height is the share of puzzles fully solved after three narrowing tests, so taller is better. The left bars are the simple rule and the untrained and trained model; the two right bars are best-possible strategies that peek at the hidden answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained bar sits almost level with the simple-rule bar and far below the answer-peeking bars, showing training added almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate split by puzzle type
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped into two puzzle families, one easier and one harder; within each group, colored bars compare the strategies, and height is the share solved, so taller is better. The answer-peeking strategies are the reference for how much room exists.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the easy family everyone nearly maxes out, but on the hard family the trained model stays near the floor while the peeking strategy climbs, showing the gap lives there and stayed unclaimed.

qwen35_4b_code_abi_compiler_heldout_prim.verdict_tag: Prebuilt toolbox failed on new tasks
qwen35_4b_code_abi_compiler_heldout_prim.concept_primer: Think of a frozen toolbox of reusable code building blocks: the test is whether parts assembled for practice problems can also build brand-new problems the toolbox never saw.
qwen35_4b_code_abi_compiler_heldout_prim.plain_question: Can a fixed set of reusable code building blocks still solve brand-new coding problems it was never tuned for?
qwen35_4b_code_abi_compiler_heldout_prim.plain_answer: No. The toolbox solved 84% of the familiar practice problems but only 14% of brand-new ones, so the planned model-training step was called off before it started because there was nothing meaningful left to measure.
qwen35_4b_code_abi_compiler_heldout_prim.why_it_matters: Before teaching a model to assemble reusable parts, first prove the parts actually cover unseen problems; otherwise you would just be measuring a tiny, familiar slice, not real reuse.

key_numbers.label: New problems the toolbox could solve
key_numbers.sub: familiar practice problems vs brand-new ones

key_numbers.label: New problems, retested on random fresh batches
key_numbers.sub: three random samples, all near the low result

key_numbers.label: Solutions that looked right but were actually wrong
key_numbers.sub: familiar problems vs new problems

charts.chart_plain_title: Reusable toolbox stops working on brand-new problems
charts.chart_read: Bars group three problem sets (familiar practice, brand-new, and a training pool). Taller blue bars mean more problems solved (better); taller second bars mean more answers that looked right but were wrong (worse).
charts.chart_takeaway: The solve rate cliff-drops from a tall calibration bar to a stub on new problems, while the wrong-but-convincing bar jumps up on those same new problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every kind of coding task collapses the same way
charts.chart_read: Bars group five task categories, each with a familiar-problem bar and a new-problem bar; taller means more problems solved (better). Compare the two bars within each category.
charts.chart_takeaway: In every category the new-problem bar shrinks to a fraction of the familiar one, even for the category that started at 100% solved, so no task type escapes the drop.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fresh random batches confirm the poor result is real
charts.chart_read: Three bars, one per randomly drawn batch of new problems; height is the share of problems the toolbox solved, where taller is better. A flat, low set of bars means the weak result was not just bad luck.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three random batches land within a narrow low band near 18%, confirming the original low score was typical rather than an unlucky draw.

qwen35_4b_code_abi_oracle_coverage_ladde.verdict_tag: Kit can express most tasks, not auto-solve them
qwen35_4b_code_abi_oracle_coverage_ladde.concept_primer: Imagine a box of pre-built, individually tested code pieces—like a kit of snap-together blocks. The question: can every coding task's solution be built by combining pieces from this fixed kit?
qwen35_4b_code_abi_oracle_coverage_ladde.plain_question: Can a fixed kit of pre-tested code building blocks express most everyday Python coding tasks?
qwen35_4b_code_abi_oracle_coverage_ladde.plain_answer: Mostly. The full kit can build 84% of 160 tasks, up from 13% with a tiny kit. But automatically picking the correct build works only 59% of the time, so the kit is a foundation, not a finished solver.
qwen35_4b_code_abi_oracle_coverage_ladde.why_it_matters: A shared kit of verified building blocks is worth investing in, but you must still check each candidate against real tests before trusting it. Never rely on a single passing example.

key_numbers.label: Tasks the kit can build
key_numbers.sub: share of 160 tasks, tiny kit vs full kit

key_numbers.label: First plausible build is right
key_numbers.sub: of tasks, using the full kit

key_numbers.label: Fooled by a single example test
key_numbers.sub: tasks that passed one test but were wrong, tiny vs full kit

key_numbers.label: Solved by a single block
key_numbers.sub: covered tasks needing no assembly

charts.chart_plain_title: Bigger kit builds more tasks, but auto-picks the right one less reliably
charts.chart_read: Left to right the building-block kit grows from tiny to full. For each kit, one bar shows how many of 160 tasks the kit can build at all, the other how often the first plausible build is actually right. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars rise as the kit grows, but the 'first plausible build is right' bar stays far below the 'can be built' bar—expressible does not mean auto-solved.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coverage stays high across task types except the catch-all bucket
charts.chart_read: Each group is a task category. One bar is how many tasks in that category the full kit can build, the other how often the first plausible build is right. Taller is better; bars are shares within each category.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every category clears roughly four-fifths coverage except the mixed 'other' bucket, where both building and auto-picking drop sharply.

charts.chart_plain_title: A single example test fools far fewer builds as the kit grows
charts.chart_read: Left to right the kit grows from tiny to full. Each bar is the share of tasks where a build passed the one shown example yet was still wrong. Here lower is better—fewer tasks fooled by a single test.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fooled-by-one-test bar falls from 58% to 9% as the kit grows, but real checking is still needed since most individual builds remain wrong.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most solved tasks need just one building block
charts.chart_read: Each bar is how many of the 134 solved tasks were built using a chain of that many blocks: a single block, two chained, or three. Taller means more tasks at that complexity.
charts.chart_takeaway: The single-block bar towers over the rest—94 of 134 tasks need just one piece, so genuine multi-block assembly covers only 40 tasks.

qwen35_4b_code_confidence.verdict_tag: A single self-check beats averaging whole-program confidence
qwen35_4b_code_confidence.concept_primer: A model that just wrote a program can gauge its own confidence two ways. Average its word-by-word certainty across hundreds of code tokens, and the signal washes out. Or make it write the code, then answer one yes/no question about it and read that single verdict token — concentrated and sharp.
qwen35_4b_code_confidence.plain_question: When a small model writes eight versions of a program, can it tell which one is actually correct just by reading its own confidence, without running any tests?
qwen35_4b_code_confidence.plain_answer: Yes, but not the obvious way. Averaging the model's certainty across every token of a program barely beats a plain majority vote among the tries. The real winner: make the model write the code, then answer one yes/no question about it and read that single verdict token. It picks the correct program 76% of the time versus 70% by chance, no tests run — though one real test still beats it, at 82%.
qwen35_4b_code_confidence.why_it_matters: To pick the best of several small-model code drafts with no test harness, have the model self-judge each draft in one extra forward pass and take the highest score — don't rank by average token confidence. If even one real test exists, run it first.

key_numbers.label: Picking the right program by self-check vs by chance
key_numbers.sub: best of eight chosen, no tests run

key_numbers.label: Averaging token confidence vs a plain majority vote
key_numbers.sub: token-averaged signal is a statistical tie — no real edge

key_numbers.label: Keep only the third it is most sure about
key_numbers.sub: accuracy on the single answers it chooses to attempt

key_numbers.label: Running just one real test instead
key_numbers.sub: executing the visible test beats every no-test confidence signal

charts.chart_plain_title: Which method best picks one correct program out of eight tries
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way to pick a single program from eight samples; height is how often that pick passes the hidden tests, higher is better. Left to right: random guess, majority vote, average writing confidence, one-token self-judge, running the visible test, and the perfect pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: The one-token self-judge bar clears majority vote and averaged confidence, which barely beat random; running an actual test towers over every no-test signal, nearly reaching the perfect ceiling.

charts.chart_plain_title: Real confidence, or just longer, wordier code?
charts.chart_read: Three bars show how reliably each signal ranks a correct program above a wrong one for the same problem — half is a coin flip, higher is better: average writing confidence, the one-token self-judge, and code length as the decoy to beat.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both confidence signals sit well above the code-length decoy, so it is genuine self-knowledge, not verbosity; the concentrated self-judge edges out the averaged writing confidence.

charts.chart_plain_title: Skip the answers it distrusts and accuracy climbs toward 95%
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the fraction of problems kept, most-confident first; the vertical axis is accuracy on those kept — up and to the left is better. One line ranks by the self-judge, one by average writing confidence, and a flat line marks keeping everything.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-judge line climbs to about 95% when only the most-trusted third is kept and stays above averaged confidence everywhere — the model reliably knows which answers to trust.

qwen35_4b_commit_slot_jacobian_value_tra.verdict_tag: Forcing the format made it answer, not reason
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_jacobian_value_tra.concept_primer: Picture a multiple-choice bubble sheet handed to the model right after it thinks: one fixed answer line, twelve permitted words. It now fills a bubble almost every time instead of leaving the line blank. But filling a bubble is not the same as filling the correct one.
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_jacobian_value_tra.plain_question: After a small model thinks, if you force it to pick one of twelve allowed answer words, does the thinking it just did actually help it choose correctly?
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_jacobian_value_tra.plain_answer: Barely. Forcing the format fixed one problem outright: an allowed word was the model's top choice 85% of the time, versus 4% when it answered freely. But real step-by-step thinking beat the very same thought words scrambled into random order by only about eight points, small enough to be noise, and the edge piled onto just one or two easy words.
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_jacobian_value_tra.why_it_matters: When you box a small model into a forced-choice answer, don't assume the reasoning earned the result. Scramble the model's own thought words and re-measure: if shuffled thinking ties real thinking, the format is doing the work, not the thinking.

key_numbers.label: Produced an allowed answer word up front
key_numbers.sub: answering freely vs through the forced answer slot

key_numbers.label: Correct with real vs scrambled thinking
key_numbers.sub: exact-answer accuracy at the longest thinking budget

key_numbers.label: Tasks with both right and wrong answers
key_numbers.sub: one short of the minimum to keep testing

key_numbers.label: One answer word dominated the picks
key_numbers.sub: the model chose 'tiger' on over half of all rows

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct answers: real thinking vs scrambled vs none, by budget
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by thinking budget (256, 512, 1,024 tokens). Each group has three bars: real ordered thinking, no thinking at all (a flat 25% across budgets), and the same thought words shuffled. Taller means more exactly-correct answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only at the largest budget does real thinking clearly top both controls; at shorter budgets scrambled thinking ties or beats it, revealing a fragile, budget-dependent edge, not a general one.

charts.chart_plain_title: Did the forced slot actually produce a usable answer?
charts.chart_read: Three rates at the longest budget: how often an allowed word was already the model's top choice (85%), the average probability weight sitting on allowed words (68%), and how often free-form answers without the slot produced a usable answer (4%). Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two forced-slot bars tower over the free-form bar: the syntax alone cured the answering problem, even though picking the correct word stayed unreliable.

qwen35_4b_commit_slot_semantic_power_rep.verdict_tag: Scramble the reasoning and accuracy halves—twice
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_semantic_power_rep.concept_primer: The model works through its reasoning, then must pick its answer from a fixed twelve-word menu. Like a recipe: keep every step but shuffle their order and the same ingredients lead nowhere. Only the right sequence of steps produces the finished dish.
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_semantic_power_rep.plain_question: When a small model thinks out loud before answering, is it the actual order of that reasoning that helps, or just having extra text on the page?
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_semantic_power_rep.plain_answer: It's the order, and the result repeated independently. On the untouched check, reasoning in sequence picked the right word 29% of the time; shuffling those exact same reasoning words dropped it to 14%, and skipping reasoning gave 7%. The first equally large stage had already found 27%, 14%, and 10%. Each 113-problem stage passed its own task-level uncertainty and breadth gates without pooling.
qwen35_4b_commit_slot_semantic_power_rep.why_it_matters: Before trusting that a scratchpad helps your small model, shuffle its words and re-score. Here the ordered-versus-shuffled gap survived a fresh confirmation, so it is not just prompt padding, copied answer words, or the answer format. That licenses the next internal-state experiment, but does not yet prove a J-space mechanism, natural free-form answering, or installed capability.

key_numbers.label: Confirmation: ordered vs shuffled
key_numbers.sub: identical words, only their order changed

key_numbers.label: Confirmation gain lower bound
key_numbers.sub: one-sided 95% task-bootstrap lower bound, without pooling

key_numbers.label: Independent stages passed
key_numbers.sub: 113 fresh task units in each stage

key_numbers.label: Reasoning that named the answer word
key_numbers.sub: mentioning it did not improve confirmation accuracy

charts.chart_plain_title: The untouched check repeats the ordered-reasoning advantage
charts.chart_read: Four confirmation bars compare ordered thought, the same words shuffled, no thought, and open-ended free-form. Taller means more exact answers; the first three share one identical fixed twelve-word answer slot.
charts.chart_takeaway: Ordered reasoning remains about twice as accurate as shuffling the identical tokens, and about four times as accurate as close-only free-form answering.

qwen35_4b_confidence_guided_compute.verdict_tag: Trust the surest answer, not the most common
qwen35_4b_confidence_guided_compute.concept_primer: Ask a small model the same puzzle twelve times; each answer comes stamped with the model's own certainty. Majority vote trusts whichever answer repeats most, but the model often confidently repeats one wrong rule. Its single surest answer instead catches the rare try that truly cracked it.
qwen35_4b_confidence_guided_compute.plain_question: When a small model answers the same problem twelve times, is picking the most popular answer really the best way to combine those tries?
qwen35_4b_confidence_guided_compute.plain_answer: No. Picking the most common answer wastes the extra tries: accuracy stays flat near 48% however many you draw, because the model keeps confidently repeating the same wrong rule. Picking the answer it was surest of instead climbs from 47% to 62%, beating the popular vote at every budget and approaching the 83% a perfect picker would reach.
qwen35_4b_confidence_guided_compute.why_it_matters: When you sample a small model many times, rank the candidates by how sure the model is of each answer, not by how often each appears. It costs one cheap extra read, needs no separate checker, and also tells you when to stop.

key_numbers.label: Most-common answer as you sample more
key_numbers.sub: majority vote never improves with extra tries

key_numbers.label: Most-confident answer as you sample more
key_numbers.sub: picking the try the model was surest of

key_numbers.label: A perfect picker's ceiling
key_numbers.sub: if you always knew which try was right

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on the confident third
key_numbers.sub: after skipping the low-confidence problems

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra tries only help if you pick by confidence, not by vote
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis: tries drawn per problem (1 to 12). Vertical axis: accuracy, higher is better. The flat lower line picks the most common answer, the rising line picks the most-confident answer, and the top reference line marks a perfect picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: The vote line stays flat near 48% while the confidence line climbs to 62%, closing most of the gap to the perfect picker: confidence beats popularity at every budget.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where more tries help, and where they are wasted
charts.chart_read: Three problem types along the bottom; accuracy up the side, higher is better. Each type has two bars: a single best guess versus whether any of twelve tries was correct. Easy problems are solved either way; the two harder types differ sharply.
charts.chart_takeaway: On coverage-limited problems twelve tries jump from 21% to 90%; on capability-limited ones they stall at 59%, below the 72% blind guessing would give, so more sampling is futile there.

qwen35_4b_constrained_coverage_dpo.verdict_tag: Cheaper nudge, but more tries still wins
qwen35_4b_constrained_coverage_dpo.concept_primer: Think of giving the model several tries at each coding problem and counting how many problems get at least one working answer. This experiment nudges the model to try more varied approaches instead of just taking more tries.
qwen35_4b_constrained_coverage_dpo.plain_question: Can a small local nudge get the model to solve more coding problems per batch of tries than just letting it take more tries?
qwen35_4b_constrained_coverage_dpo.plain_answer: It depends. The gentle nudge solved a few more problems than the plain model at the same number of tries, without hurting first-try quality, but it still solved fewer than simply letting the plain model take twice as many tries.
qwen35_4b_constrained_coverage_dpo.why_it_matters: The nudge is a cheap, safe gain that does not degrade quality, but it cannot replace simply taking more tries. The promising next step is mixing several generation styles rather than pushing this one nudge harder.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved, 4 tries (plain vs nudged)
key_numbers.sub: share of 24 problems with a working answer

key_numbers.label: Best coverage, plain model with 8 tries
key_numbers.sub: the bar the nudge did not clear

key_numbers.label: Compute used (nudged vs 8 tries)
key_numbers.sub: 22k vs 45k words processed

key_numbers.label: First-try success (plain vs nudged)
key_numbers.sub: share solved on the very first attempt

charts.chart_plain_title: Problems solved and first-try wins, by method
charts.chart_read: Bars show, out of 24 practice problems, the share solved by at least one of several tries (taller is better) and the share solved on the very first try. Four setups: plain model with 4 tries, plain with 8 tries, the nudged model with 4 tries, and a scrambled-nudge control.
charts.chart_takeaway: The nudged model's 4-try bar clears the plain 4-try bar and the scrambled control on both measures, but stays below the plain model given 8 tries.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much compute each method burns
charts.chart_read: Bars show total words the model had to process across all 24 problems for each setup; shorter is cheaper and better. The plain 8-try setup is the tallest, the nudged 4-try setup among the shortest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The nudged model uses about half the compute of the 8-try plain run, so its coverage comes far cheaper even though it solves slightly fewer problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training difficulty over ten short tuning steps
charts.chart_read: Two lines track how hard the tuning task stays over ten steps, one trained on genuine good-vs-bad examples and one on deliberately scrambled examples; flatter and lower means the model is learning steadily rather than breaking down.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-example line stays flat and steady near its floor across all ten steps, showing the gentle nudge trained cleanly instead of collapsing.

qwen35_4b_context_composition.verdict_tag: Fine-tuning installed the skill but hijacked its answers
qwen35_4b_context_composition.concept_primer: Picture an interpreter who studies a new dialect so intensely they start replying in it — even when you asked for plain English. Their understanding genuinely sharpened, but now half their answers come back in a form you cannot read or use.
qwen35_4b_context_composition.plain_question: If you fine-tune a small model to add a skill and then walk it through the exact steps in the prompt, can it reliably use that skill?
qwen35_4b_context_composition.plain_answer: Only when its answer survives. The fine-tuned skill is genuinely the sharpest — 95% correct when the model replies in the required form, beating the untrained model's 83% under the same step-by-step procedure. But the training hijacks the answer format half the time, so real usable accuracy collapses to 51% — below the plain untrained model's 74%.
qwen35_4b_context_composition.why_it_matters: When you fine-tune a small model to add a skill, measure how often it still answers in your required format, separately from accuracy. A captured output channel can bury a genuinely better skill and make it undeployable — so mix your target format into training.

key_numbers.label: Skill accuracy when the format is readable
key_numbers.sub: untrained model with the procedure vs the fine-tuned model under the same procedure, counting only readable answers

key_numbers.label: Usable accuracy after the format gets captured
key_numbers.sub: correct-when-readable vs actually deployable, once half the fine-tuned replies come back unusable

key_numbers.label: Fine-tuned replies that arrive in the wrong format
key_numbers.sub: just over half (53%) are readable, vs 94% to 100% for the untrained model

key_numbers.label: Writing the correct program from scratch
key_numbers.sub: 8% to 13% across every strategy — no procedure, worked demo, or working simulator moved it

charts.chart_plain_title: The fine-tuned skill scores highest yet lands lowest once you must use it
charts.chart_read: Five setups along the bottom: the untrained model with a plain prompt, a step-by-step procedure, or worked examples, then the fine-tuned model plain and with the procedure. Three bars each — usable accuracy, accuracy among readable answers, and share of replies in the required format. Higher is better everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fine-tuned model with the procedure has the tallest readable-answer bar (95%) but the shortest format-survival bar (about half), so its usable-accuracy bar sinks below the untrained model.

charts.chart_plain_title: Nothing helped the model write the program from scratch
charts.chart_read: Two bars — the untrained model and the fine-tuned model — showing how often each writes the correct program from behavior alone, using a propose-then-test procedure. Higher is better; both sit near the floor of the chart.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars barely clear zero (8% and 13%). No context trick — procedure, worked demos, or a working simulator — lifts writing-the-program-from-scratch; proposing a program from its behavior is the wall nothing crosses.

qwen35_4b_context_local_jacobian_clamp.verdict_tag: Perfect concept edit, undone by a rounding error
qwen35_4b_context_local_jacobian_clamp.concept_primer: A prompt writes a chosen word early ("Selected key: apple"), then a later line looks up that word's digit. Editing the early word is like changing a kitchen order slip so a different dish gets cooked; editing the final answer only rewrites the receipt.
qwen35_4b_context_local_jacobian_clamp.plain_question: If you reach into a small model's memory and swap the trace of the word it picked earlier, does the separate answer it computes later follow the swap?
qwen35_4b_context_local_jacobian_clamp.plain_answer: Yes, but only when the edit lands on the earlier token that first stores the word. There the model looked up the swapped word's digit on all 48 fresh puzzles, up from zero without the edit, and a wrong-word swap produced that wrong word's own digit, not noise. Yet one of 96 control rows slipped past a precision limit, voiding the formal claim.
qwen35_4b_context_local_jacobian_clamp.why_it_matters: When steering a small model by editing its internals, edit the token that holds the concept, not the output position: a late edit rewrites only the next word while downstream steps keep using the original. And verify controls survive low-precision rounding.

key_numbers.label: The separately computed digit
key_numbers.sub: redirected on fresh puzzles, without vs with the early edit

key_numbers.label: A wrong-word swap
key_numbers.sub: each produced the wrong word's own digit, showing real meaning not disruption

key_numbers.label: A surface readout edit
key_numbers.sub: the same-size edit on an output-only direction changed nothing

key_numbers.label: Control row that voided the claim
key_numbers.sub: its rounding error edged just over the frozen precision limit

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each edit produced the intended word and digit
charts.chart_read: Six edit types run along the bottom: full state copy, the concept edit, a two-direction concept edit, a wrong-word edit, an output-only edit, and random noise. Paired bars give the share of 48 puzzles hitting the target word and target digit; taller is stronger steering.
charts.chart_takeaway: The concept edit hits 100% on both bars, matching a full state copy, while the output-only and random edits stay flat at zero: real steering, not disruption.

charts.chart_plain_title: How strongly each edit tipped the answer from old toward new
charts.chart_read: For an untouched baseline and five edits, paired bars show the confidence gap favoring the new answer over the original, for the reported word and the computed digit. Above zero favors the new answer, below favors the original; higher is a stronger flip.
charts.chart_takeaway: The concept edit swings both gaps from about minus-twelve and minus-nine up to about plus-eleven and plus-nine, nearly matching a full copy, while output-only and random edits stay pinned negative.

charts.chart_plain_title: Editing early layers steers the answer; editing late layers is too late
charts.chart_read: The bottom axis is how deep the full copy is applied, early layers on the left to late on the right. Three lines track how often it redirected the reported word, the computed digit, and a wrong word's own digit. Higher means the edit still took hold.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines hold at 100% through the early and middle layers, then collapse to zero around layer twenty, where downstream computation has already read the concept.

qwen35_4b_counterexample_directed_dsl.verdict_tag: Targeted examples help some tasks, not all
qwen35_4b_counterexample_directed_dsl.concept_primer: Imagine fixing a buggy recipe. A coach hands you the exact taste-tests that expose your particular mistake, not random ones. Here a program-fixing model gets such targeted examples: worked cases chosen to reveal its likely wrong guesses.
qwen35_4b_counterexample_directed_dsl.plain_question: When a model must fix a broken program from a few worked examples, does hand-picking examples that expose likely mistakes help more than showing random examples?
qwen35_4b_counterexample_directed_dsl.plain_answer: It depends. Picking targeted examples over random ones lifted correct one-attempt fixes from 51% to 58%. But when the model made several tries and kept the best, targeted examples gave no gain, and one whole task type collapsed to always answering wrong.
qwen35_4b_counterexample_directed_dsl.why_it_matters: Curating worked examples to expose likely mistakes can sharpen a model's single-shot fixes, but it is no cure-all: on some tasks the model locks onto one wrong pattern. Combine curation with letting the model try several times, and watch for collapse.

key_numbers.label: Fixed on the first try
key_numbers.sub: share of broken programs repaired correctly, random vs targeted examples

key_numbers.label: Best of several tries
key_numbers.sub: keeping the best of a few attempts, no gain from targeted examples

key_numbers.label: Hardest task type
key_numbers.sub: one family collapsed to always giving the same wrong answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Targeted examples help single-shot fixes but not best-of-several picking
charts.chart_read: Two situations along the bottom: fixing a program in one attempt, and keeping the best of several attempts. Paired bars compare a model trained on random worked examples versus targeted ones. Taller means more programs fully fixed.
charts.chart_takeaway: The targeted-training bar stands taller for one-attempt fixing but shorter for best-of-several, so the extra care pays off only without retries.

charts.chart_plain_title: Targeted training rescues two task types but wrecks a third
charts.chart_read: Three kinds of program-fixing task along the bottom. Paired bars compare training on random worked examples versus targeted ones, showing the share of programs fully fixed on unseen cases; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On one task the targeted bar drops flat to nothing while hitting full marks on another, showing the method's help is wildly uneven.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scrambled examples hurt more than showing no examples
charts.chart_read: Bottom axis shows what worked examples the model saw while fixing: proper matched examples, none at all, or scrambled ones. Each bar is the share of programs fully fixed on unseen cases; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled-examples bar is by far the shortest, well below the no-examples bar, proving the model genuinely reads the examples rather than ignoring them.

qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.verdict_tag: A model self-taught past its own ceiling
qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.concept_primer: Think of the model as a dart thrower aiming at correct little programs. Sampling more just throws more darts with the same aim. Training it on its own past bullseyes re-aims the arm — sometimes tightening onto a spot it already hits, sometimes reaching a new region of the board.
qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.plain_question: If you fine-tune a small model only on the correct answers it produced itself, can it start solving problems it could never solve before?
qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.plain_answer: Yes, but it depends on difficulty. On easy one-step problems it just pulls answers it already knew into its top guess (60% to 80%), gaining no new ground. On harder two-step problems something new happens: on brand-new tasks it proposes correct programs it had never found, tripling its solve rate from 15% to 45%. On the hardest problems, with no wins to learn from, nothing moves.
qwen35_4b_coverage_banking.why_it_matters: Before reaching for a bigger model or more sampling, collect your small model's own verified wins and fine-tune on them — it can raise its own ceiling, but only at difficulty levels where sampling already found some correct examples to learn from.

key_numbers.label: Two-step problems solved within sixteen tries
key_numbers.sub: brand-new tasks — the solve ceiling tripled after self-training

key_numbers.label: Single best guess on easy one-step problems
key_numbers.sub: self-training pulled answers it already knew into its top guess

key_numbers.label: Variety of guesses on two-step problems
key_numbers.sub: fewer distinct programs, not more — it aimed its guesses at correct ones

key_numbers.label: Hardest three-plus-step problems
key_numbers.sub: no verified wins existed to train on, so no gain

charts.chart_plain_title: What self-training changes at each problem difficulty
charts.chart_read: Four difficulty levels along the bottom, from one to four chained steps. Bar height is the fraction of brand-new problems solved, higher is better. Four bars per level: base single guess, base within sixteen tries, self-trained single guess, self-trained within sixteen tries.
charts.chart_takeaway: At one step the single guess jumps but the ceiling stays flat (it just concentrates); at two steps the ceiling itself triples (genuine expansion); three-plus steps sit empty.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving two-step problems as you allow more tries
charts.chart_read: Number of tries from one to sixteen along the bottom; fraction of brand-new two-step problems solved up the side; higher is better. Two lines: the original model (lower) and the self-trained model (upper).
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-trained line sits above the original at every try count, ending near 45% versus 15% — it reaches correct programs the original never proposed.

qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.verdict_tag: Small models fail by never guessing, not misjudging
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.concept_primer: Picture cutting 32 keys for a lock, then testing each against eight sample locks you already own. A key that opens all eight is almost surely the real one. You fail two ways: never cutting a working key, or cutting one but grabbing a dud.
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.plain_question: When a small model flunks a multi-step coding puzzle, is it because it never generates a right program, or because it can't tell which guess is correct?
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.plain_answer: It never generates it. Whenever a correct program shows up among 32 tries, running each candidate against eight known examples finds it every single time — the model judging its own work, and even a random pick among survivors, do exactly as well. Nine in ten example-passers are truly correct. Smarter selection buys nothing; only whether the right program ever appears matters.
qwen35_4b_coverage_vs_selection.why_it_matters: Skip rerankers and self-verifiers: filtering candidates against known examples already picks perfectly. For shallow tasks, sample several times and keep the example-passers — a free 2-to-5-times gain, no labels needed. For hard tasks, change what gets proposed, via tools or baking solved patterns into the weights.

key_numbers.label: Extra accuracy from a smart picker over a plain example check
key_numbers.sub: the model judging its own work, and even a random survivor, match the best-of-32 ceiling in every cell

key_numbers.label: One try versus sample-then-filter, two-step register puzzles
key_numbers.sub: sampling 32 times then keeping example-passers quadruples the solve rate

key_numbers.label: Programs passing all eight visible examples that are truly correct
key_numbers.sub: passing the visible examples also means passing the hidden ones

key_numbers.label: Three-step list puzzles solved even across 32 tries
key_numbers.sub: the right program is essentially never generated — the coverage wall

charts.chart_plain_title: On list-machine puzzles, every answer-picker hits the same ceiling
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by puzzle depth, 1 to 4. Four bars each: a single first guess, an execute-against-examples filter, the model judging its own guesses, and the best-of-32 ceiling. Height is share solved on held-back examples; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At depths 1 and 2 the three multi-sample bars match the ceiling while a lone guess trails; by depth 3 all four collapse near zero together.

charts.chart_plain_title: The same pattern on the register-machine puzzles
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by puzzle depth, 1 to 4. Same four bars: single first guess, execute-against-examples filter, model judging its own guesses, and the best-of-32 ceiling. Height is share solved on held-back examples; taller is better. The three multi-sample bars overlap exactly.
charts.chart_takeaway: A single guess trails 2 to 5 times below three overlapping bars — sampling then filtering recovers the gap, yet clever picking adds nothing over the plain example check.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many tries it takes to ever hit the right program
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is number of guesses drawn, from 1 to about 29; vertical axis is the chance at least one guess is correct. Four rising curves, one per puzzle depth on the register machine. Higher and steeper is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Depth 1 climbs toward 90 percent; each deeper curve starts lower and flattens sooner, depth 4 barely reaching 10 percent — deeper puzzles are drawn right far less often.

qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.verdict_tag: It runs any plan but cannot invent one
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.concept_primer: Picture a cook. Hand them a written recipe and they nail the dish every time, however long. Ask them to predict the dish in their head and it depends on the ingredients. Ask them to reconstruct the recipe from taste alone, and they fail fast.
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.plain_question: If a small model can follow a step-by-step plan perfectly, can it also work out that plan on its own, or run it in its head?
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.plain_answer: No. Handed the exact steps, this fixed 4-billion-parameter model wrote correct code almost every time across three unrelated task types. But asked to infer the same procedure from example inputs and outputs alone, it fell from about 80% at one step toward zero by three stacked steps. Running a plan and discovering one are separate abilities.
qwen35_4b_crossfamily_laws.why_it_matters: Never spend tool calls or search budget making the model run a plan you already have — it never fails there. Spend that compute finding the plan. Offload state-tracking to code only when the state is bulky text, not compact numbers.

key_numbers.label: Following a plan you hand it
key_numbers.sub: correct code, every task type, every depth

key_numbers.label: Inventing the plan from examples alone
key_numbers.sub: one step down to three stacked steps

key_numbers.label: Tracking state in its head at four steps
key_numbers.sub: compact number machine versus a shifting text string

key_numbers.label: Gap between running and inventing a plan
key_numbers.sub: at three-plus steps, in every task type

charts.chart_plain_title: Given the exact steps, the model writes correct code at every length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many steps are chained into the plan, one to four; the vertical axis is the share of correct code written from a supplied plan. Three lines: integer lists, text strings, a three-number machine. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines sit pinned at the top across every depth — handed the exact steps, the model writes correct code regardless of task type or length.

charts.chart_plain_title: Running steps in its head depends on how bulky the state is
charts.chart_read: Same three task types and one-to-four step axis; the vertical axis is how often the model tracked the running state correctly in its head, with no code. The three-number machine stays high, integer lists sag, text strings sit at the floor. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The number-machine line holds near three-quarters while the string line is flat at zero — head simulation is set by how heavy the state is to hold, not by depth.

charts.chart_plain_title: Inventing the procedure from examples collapses in every task type
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis chains one to four steps; the vertical axis is how often the model reconstructed the hidden procedure from example inputs and outputs alone. Three lines for integer lists, text strings, the three-number machine. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line dives from high at one step toward zero by three — only the number machine keeps a small nonzero foothold, so inventing a novel procedure is a wall almost everywhere.

qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.verdict_tag: Small models get the plan wrong
qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.concept_primer: Each task needs a tiny three-step program, like a short recipe: the order of actions (chop, then fry, then salt) plus the exact amounts. Getting the amounts right turns out to be easy — the model's whole struggle is choosing the right order of actions.
qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.plain_question: When a small model fails to write a short multi-step program, is it choosing the wrong sequence of actions, or just getting the numbers inside them wrong?
qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.plain_answer: The wrong sequence. Across three completely different kinds of programming tasks, a 4-billion-parameter model almost never solved one on its own — 0 to 2 percent — and its misses were wrong-order, not right-order-wrong-number. Hand it the correct sequence of actions and it fills the numbers perfectly every time (100 percent). A blind tool that tries candidate sequences and checks each solved nearly everything, 98 to 100 percent — beating the model outright.
qwen35_4b_crosssubstrate_structure.why_it_matters: Don't burn compute sampling a small model more, or fine-tune it hoping it plans multi-step logic — its weights can't propose the right structure. If you can check answers with an interpreter, enumerate candidate sequences and let cheap value-search plus run-and-check win.

key_numbers.label: What the model solves on its own
key_numbers.sub: single best answer across all three task types

key_numbers.label: Solved once handed the right sequence
key_numbers.sub: value-search fills the correct numbers every time

key_numbers.label: What the blind search tool solves
key_numbers.sub: enumerate sequences, fill values, run each and keep what works

key_numbers.label: Random wrong sequences, even at 200 tries
key_numbers.sub: the exact order of actions genuinely matters

charts.chart_plain_title: Model near zero, a search tool near perfect on all three task types
charts.chart_read: Four measures left to right: the model's single best answer, whether its program's actions match the right sequence, solve rate given the correct sequence, and a blind search tool. Three colors mark the task types (character edits, register machine, list operations). Taller means higher solve rate, which is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first two bars sit near the floor for all three task types while the last two hit the ceiling — the model cannot plan, but supplying or searching the sequence solves it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Random action-sequences rarely solve, even after two hundred tries
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis counts how many random sequences are tried (8, 50, 200), each filled with searched values and run to check; vertical axis is solve rate, higher is better. One line per task type: character edits, register machine, list operations. Compare against the near-100% from the correct sequence.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines climb only modestly, topping out between 9 and 32 percent, far below the near-perfect result from the correct sequence — the right structure is genuinely required, not incidental.

qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.verdict_tag: Steps plus a tool crack the wall
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.concept_primer: Picture a locked maze where each door has 23 possible keys. Instead of guessing all three doors from the entrance in one leap, you try one key, walk through, and let the room reveal itself before choosing again. The tool is that room-revealing step.
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.plain_question: If a small model can't write a correct three-step program in one shot, can it get there by taking one step at a time with a tool running each?
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.plain_answer: Yes — but not because the model got smarter. Taking three steps one at a time, with a tool that runs each and shows the result, solves about 2 in 5 versus 1 in 8 in one shot. The catch: blindly trying all 23 operations does just as well. The step-by-step structure and the tool crack the wall — not the model's judgment about which move is best.
qwen35_4b_decompose_compose_frontier.why_it_matters: When a small model stalls on multi-step tasks, wrap it in a step-running tool and search the moves — but always benchmark against blind enumeration, which can match clever ranking. Then fine-tune on the solutions found to bank the gain into the weights.

key_numbers.label: Hardest tasks solved: one-shot vs step-by-step search
key_numbers.sub: solve rate on unseen examples, about 3.4 times higher

key_numbers.label: Model's guidance vs blindly trying every operation
key_numbers.sub: both about 40%, but guidance needs about 2.5 times fewer tool calls (350 vs 895)

key_numbers.label: One-shot rate after training on found solutions
key_numbers.sub: best-of-five on unseen tasks after fine-tuning on 327 found solutions

key_numbers.label: Genuinely three-step tasks the search cracked
key_numbers.sub: one-shot writing solved none of them

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate: one-shot writing vs step-by-step search, by task depth
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by task depth — two-step and three-step. Each group has three bars: one-shot sampling, model-guided step-by-step search, and blind brute-force search. Height is the share of unseen tasks solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At three steps both search bars stand about three times taller than one-shot, but guided and blind sit nearly level — the search structure, not the model's guidance, cracks the wall.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate as the search is allowed more tool calls
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is the tool-call budget (rightward means more); vertical is the share of unseen tasks solved (higher is better). Four lines: model-guided and blind brute-force, each at two-step and three-step depth. Rising higher and sooner is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Guided lines jump early then flatten; blind lines start lower but keep climbing to match or overtake — the model's guidance buys a cheaper search, not more solutions.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training on found solutions lifts the model's own one-shot answers
charts.chart_read: Four one-shot measures on unseen tasks along the bottom; taller is better. Each has three bars: the frozen model, the self-trained model, and a repeat training run. Height is solve rate.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every trained bar clears the frozen one — best-of-five roughly doubles and three-step solving quadruples — and the repeat run confirms it, so search-found solutions transfer into the weights.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much of the depth-3 crack was truly three steps?
charts.chart_read: Two bars split the nominal three-step tasks by real difficulty: 16 that secretly collapse to two steps or fewer, and 24 that are genuinely three-step. Bar height is the share the step-by-step search solved; taller means more solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: Search solved all 16 secretly-shallow tasks but only 4 of 24 truly three-step ones — a real frontier gain, but far smaller than the headline 40% suggests.

qwen35_4b_deep_advantage_mopd.verdict_tag: Can one trained model beat switching per problem
qwen35_4b_deep_advantage_mopd.concept_primer: Picture one cooking student who already blends a fast style and a slow, careful style. You coach them extra only on the exact dishes where slow clearly wins, while re-drilling old dishes so nothing slips. Can that beat hiring a different cook per dish?
qwen35_4b_deep_advantage_mopd.plain_question: When a slow, careful answering style beats a fast one on certain problems, can you bake that edge into a single model instead of switching styles at runtime?
qwen35_4b_deep_advantage_mopd.plain_answer: Not run yet. The test is built and its instrument checks pass, but the deciding stage has not executed. It will first confirm, on fresh problems, that a strict rule reliably flags cases where the careful style beats both the fast style and the current combined model. Only then does it coach the model on those cases and check whether the result beats picking a style per problem and beats sampling eight answers and keeping the best.
qwen35_4b_deep_advantage_mopd.why_it_matters: If targeted coaching works, you ship one model instead of running two models plus a runtime picker, cutting latency and cost. If it fails, switching styles or sampling many answers at deployment stays necessary.

key_numbers.label: The starting combined model's recipe
key_numbers.sub: weight blend of the two styles it starts from

key_numbers.label: How often the careful style must clearly win
key_numbers.sub: minimum flagged problems required before coaching is allowed (16 of every 192)

key_numbers.label: Old-skill drilling mixed into training
key_numbers.sub: share of examples frozen to preserve existing behavior

key_numbers.label: Bar it must clear at deployment
key_numbers.sub: must beat sampling eight answers and keeping the best-scored one

qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.verdict_tag: The bottleneck is missing clues, not strategy
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.concept_primer: Picture a game of 20 questions to identify a hidden rule: a fair player picks the smartest next question without knowing the answer, while a cheating player who already knows the answer sets the ceiling.
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.plain_question: When the model struggles to pin down a hidden rule, is it because it lacks a clever questioning strategy, or because it just needs more clues?
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.plain_answer: It needs more clues. In the hardest setup the fair player scores only 4% while the cheating player hits 74%, but simply allowing more questions lifts the fair player to 86%, nearly matching the cheat and closing most of the gap.
qwen35_4b_deployable_information_ceiling.why_it_matters: When a model fails at this kind of deduction, spend effort feeding it more evidence or more questioning steps rather than hunting for a cleverer questioning strategy; the fair strategy is already close to the best possible.

key_numbers.label: Fair player, few clues and questions
key_numbers.sub: hardest setup: 4 clues, only 3 questions allowed

key_numbers.label: Fair player, more questions allowed
key_numbers.sub: same 4 clues but 10 questions allowed

key_numbers.label: Fair player, more starting clues
key_numbers.sub: 16 clues up front, still only 3 questions

key_numbers.label: Cheating player's ceiling
key_numbers.sub: in the hardest setup, knowing the answer in advance

charts.chart_plain_title: Fair player nearly catches the cheat once given more clues or questions
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare the fair player against the cheating player who already knows the answer, across three setups from hardest to easier. Height is how often the hidden rule is correctly identified; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: In the leftmost hardest setup the fair player's bar is tiny next to the cheat's, but in the other two setups the two bars stand almost level, showing more clues or questions closes the gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: More questions steadily lift the fair player toward the ceiling
charts.chart_read: Two lines track identification accuracy as the number of allowed questions rises left to right, holding starting clues fixed. One line is the fair player, the other the cheating player; higher lines are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fair player's line climbs steadily from bottom to top while the cheat's line flattens early, meaning extra questions keep helping the fair player long after the cheat has maxed out.

charts.chart_plain_title: More starting clues sharply raise the fair player
charts.chart_read: Two lines show identification accuracy as the number of clues shown up front increases left to right, with the number of questions held low. One line is the fair player, the other the cheating player; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fair player's line rises steeply and converges toward the cheat's line, showing that each batch of extra clues shrinks the gap between them from wide to nearly nothing.

qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.verdict_tag: The reasoning wall was data, not a ceiling
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.concept_primer: Picture puzzles built as three stacked layers of nested steps — a recipe that calls sub-recipes that call basic moves. The small model cannot write them alone, but a tireless search tool grinds out correct worked examples. Train on those, and the skill sinks into the model's weights.
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.plain_question: When a small model keeps failing at deeply nested, multi-step puzzles, is it hitting a hard limit of its size, or just starved for training examples?
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.plain_answer: Just starved for examples. Feeding a fixed 4-billion-parameter model more search-found solutions lifted its solve rate on fresh three-layer puzzles from 0% to 38% when given sixteen tries — climbing steadily at every dose, with no sign of leveling off. It even began solving 1 in 10 fresh puzzles on a single blind attempt with no reasoning shown, versus essentially zero before.
qwen35_4b_depth3_dose_response.why_it_matters: Before declaring a small model too small for a hard compositional task, use a cheap search tool to auto-generate verified solutions and chart solve rate against how many you train on. A still-rising curve means you are data-limited, not capped.

key_numbers.label: Solve rate on fresh puzzles, sixteen tries allowed
key_numbers.sub: as training examples grew from none to 640

key_numbers.label: Steady climb across doses, no plateau
key_numbers.sub: solve rate after 40, 160, then 640 worked examples

key_numbers.label: Single blind attempt, no reasoning shown
key_numbers.sub: deployable one-shot accuracy at the top dose, previously essentially zero

key_numbers.label: Fresh test puzzles overlapping the training set
key_numbers.sub: no shared structure with 750 training solutions — genuine generalization

charts.chart_plain_title: More worked examples, steadily more three-layer puzzles solved
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by training amount banked (0, 40, 160, 640 worked examples); bar height is solve rate on fresh three-layer puzzles, higher is better. Four bars per group: sixteen-try and single-shot rates, each shown with and without a reasoning scratchpad.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar climbs left to right with no flattening; the two deployable no-scratchpad bars stay pinned at zero until the largest dose, then lift off — capability tracks the amount of data.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate rises with both attempts allowed and training amount
charts.chart_read: Each line is one training dose (0, 40, 160, 640 examples); horizontal axis is attempts allowed per puzzle (1 to 16); vertical axis is solve rate on fresh three-layer puzzles. Higher is better; the untrained base line stays flat on the floor at zero.
charts.chart_takeaway: The lines fan out cleanly in dose order, each larger dose sitting entirely above the smaller ones and none crossing — more training data lifts the whole curve, with no saturation.

qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.verdict_tag: Variety keeps teaching, repetition barely helps
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.concept_primer: A tireless robot bolts together the 16 tools in its kit until it finds combinations that solve a puzzle, then writes each solution into a cookbook the model studies. What teaches the model is how many DIFFERENT recipes fill the cookbook, not how often it rereads them.
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.plain_question: When you teach a small model from a solver's worked solutions, does piling on more eventually stop helping, and is it the variety or just the extra training that matters?
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.plain_answer: Variety, decisively, and the gains never plateaued. Rereading the same 40 solved examples sixteen times over barely moved the solve rate (9% to 16%, within noise), but replacing them with more distinct examples at the exact same training cost jumped it (16% to 38%). And coverage kept climbing to 54% as distinct examples grew past a thousand, with no ceiling in sight.
qwen35_4b_depth_scaling_controls.why_it_matters: When self-training a small model on a solver's verified solutions, spend your budget harvesting more DISTINCT solved problems, not more passes over the ones you have. Track distinct-solution count as the real lever, and keep collecting, because the curve never flattened.

key_numbers.label: More training, same 40 examples
key_numbers.sub: solve rate barely moved (within noise)

key_numbers.label: More variety, same training cost
key_numbers.sub: solve rate, a clean real jump

key_numbers.label: Kept climbing, no plateau
key_numbers.sub: solve rate through 1,156 distinct examples

key_numbers.label: One rung deeper worked weakly
key_numbers.sub: harder-puzzle coverage, but first-try stayed flat at 3%

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate keeps rising as more distinct solved problems are banked
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the number of distinct solved problems banked, from 39 up to 1,156. The upper line is the share solved within 16 tries; the lower line is the share solved on the very first try. Up and to the right is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines climb steadily to the last point with no bend toward flat, so the model keeps gaining and there is still headroom past 1,156 distinct examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: Variety beats extra training at the same cost
charts.chart_read: Three bars show the share of unseen puzzles solved within 16 tries: 40 examples trained briefly, the same 40 trained sixteen times harder, and a larger, more varied set at that same heavy training. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The middle bar barely clears the left one (more training, no real gain), but the right bar more than doubles it at equal cost, so variety is the lever.

charts.chart_plain_title: One rung deeper: extra examples lift coverage but not first-try success
charts.chart_read: For harder, deeper puzzles, three models each show two bars, solved-within-16-tries and solved-on-first-try. Left is the untrained model, middle is trained only on shallower puzzles, right adds 320 deeper examples. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Adding deeper examples nearly triples the 16-try coverage (7% to 18%), but the first-try bar stays flat at 3%, so the skill is present yet not reliable enough to deploy.

qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.verdict_tag: It runs any recipe but can't reverse-engineer one
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.concept_primer: Picture a cook who follows any written recipe flawlessly. But set a finished dish before that cook and they cannot deduce which steps produced it, nor mentally re-cook a guessed recipe to check it against the plate. This model executes; it cannot infer.
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.plain_question: When a small model fails at chaining several data transformations together, is the hard part running the long chain of steps, or figuring out which steps produced the result?
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.plain_answer: It's figuring out the steps. Handed the exact sequence of operations, this 4-billion-parameter model writes correct code almost every time, even four steps deep, with zero execution deficit. Left to infer that sequence from before-and-after examples, it solves almost nothing past two steps, spotting each added operation only about twice as well as guessing among 63 candidates.
qwen35_4b_depth_wall_anatomy.why_it_matters: Don't fine-tune a small model to discover multi-step transformations from examples, its weakest skill. Let cheap external tools enumerate and propose candidate pipelines, and use the model for what it does near-perfectly: turning a stated plan into working code.
