key_numbers.sub: four-step pipelines, guessed from examples vs stated outright

key_numbers.label: How fast the odds of solving collapse per added step
key_numbers.sub: each composed operation, regardless of operation type

key_numbers.label: Power to identify each added step beyond the first
key_numbers.sub: vs random guessing among 63 candidate operations

key_numbers.label: Recognizing a single operation on its own
key_numbers.sub: but it cannot chain these recognitions together

charts.chart_plain_title: Told the plan the model wins; asked to guess it, it fails
charts.chart_read: Six task types along the bottom (chain length two to four, each with clean or information-destroying steps); bar height is share of tasks solved. Three bars each: from bare examples, from the stated plan, and with intermediate states shown. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The stated-plan bars stay pinned near the top through chain length four, while both guessing bars sit on the floor. The wall is finding the plan, not running it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate crashes after two chained steps, whatever the step type
charts.chart_read: Bottom axis is chain length (one to five steps); height is share of tasks solved. Four lines mark how many information-destroying steps are present, from zero to three. Upper-left is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line falls off a cliff between one step and two and hits zero by three, overlapping regardless of step type, so depth alone kills it, walling at length two.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even pre-cut steps and yes-or-no checks fade with depth
charts.chart_read: Six task types along the bottom (chain length two to four, clean or information-destroying steps). One bar per type is solving when steps are pre-separated; the other is accuracy telling the true pipeline from a decoy. Higher is better; half is a coin-flip.
charts.chart_takeaway: Pre-separated solving slides from half toward zero as chains lengthen, and true-versus-decoy accuracy drops to a coin-flip at the deepest lossy cell. Even isolated pieces and simple checks break down.

charts.chart_plain_title: The old three-step wins were secretly shallower tasks
charts.chart_read: Two task groups along the bottom: tasks labeled three-steps-deep that secretly collapse to something shallower, versus genuinely three-step tasks. Bar height is how often an external split-and-search method solved them. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The search cleared every secretly-shallow task but only one in six genuinely deep ones, so nearly all the celebrated three-step success was a measurement artifact.

qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.concept_primer: Picture a capable student who fails a problem only because they tried one approach and stopped; ask them for several different attempts and one usually works.
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.plain_question: When our small model fails a coding task, is it because it truly can't do it, or just because it didn't try enough different approaches?
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.plain_answer: Mostly the latter. A handful of tries solved 70% of tasks; asking for many varied attempts lifted that to 89%, rescuing most earlier failures. The ability was there — it just needed more varied shots.
qwen35_4b_diversity_keyed_coverage_gate.why_it_matters: Before retraining a small model to fix failures, first spend effort on diverse retries — much of the apparent weakness is untapped and cheaply recoverable. Re-editing wrong answers, by contrast, added nothing here.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved with a few tries
key_numbers.sub: handful of attempts per task

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved with many varied tries
key_numbers.sub: after lots of varied attempts

key_numbers.label: Earlier failures later fixed
key_numbers.sub: originally-missed tasks recovered

key_numbers.label: Fixed by re-editing wrong answers
key_numbers.sub: the repair step added nothing

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of tasks solved as retries grow more varied
charts.chart_read: Bars left to right go from a few tries to many varied tries; height is the share of tasks solved, and taller is better. The rightmost bars include a step that re-edits wrong answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars climb steadily from 70% to 89%, but the last two are identical — more varied sampling keeps helping while re-editing wrong answers adds nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Originally-missed tasks rescued by each retry strategy
charts.chart_read: Each bar counts how many of the 24 originally-failed tasks a strategy rescued; taller is better. The middle bars are single decoding styles, the right bars combine several.
charts.chart_takeaway: Combining three decoding styles rescues 13 tasks versus 10 for the best single style, showing different styles rescue different tasks rather than the same ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks solved versus total computing effort spent
charts.chart_read: The line moves right as more computing effort is spent and up as more tasks get solved; up-and-to-the-right is better. It traces the no-training ceiling to beat.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line rises then flattens, so extra effort keeps solving more tasks but with clearly diminishing returns at the high end.

qwen35_4b_error_localization.verdict_tag: Its confidence dips exactly where it first slipped
qwen35_4b_error_localization.concept_primer: Picture reciting a memorized phone number: your voice naturally steadies as you get going, but wavers exactly on the digit you misremember. Correct for that natural steadying, and the leftover waver pinpoints the wrong digit. That waver is the model's confidence dip.
qwen35_4b_error_localization.plain_question: When a small model makes a mistake partway through a multi-step calculation, can its own confidence tell you exactly which step first went wrong?
qwen35_4b_error_localization.plain_answer: Yes, and the dip lands on the exact step, not just late in the chain. Confidence naturally climbs the deeper the model goes, so "least sure" could just mean "last step." Correcting for that, the least-confident step is the true first slip 56% of the time, versus 36% for guessing the usual trouble spot and 19% for blind chance. When it slips several times, though, only 27%.
qwen35_4b_error_localization.why_it_matters: Log how confidently your small model commits to each reasoning step. When an answer has one error, redo from its least-confident step: cheaper than restarting and just as effective. Don't trust the signal when several steps look shaky.

key_numbers.label: First wrong step pinpointed
key_numbers.sub: the least-confident step is the true first slip, on single-error chains (vs 19% for blind chance)

key_numbers.label: Beats guessing by position
key_numbers.sub: how often the first slip is found: position guess vs the model's confidence

key_numbers.label: When it slips several times
key_numbers.sub: confidence catches the FIRST error only about 1 in 4 times

key_numbers.label: Cheaper repair
key_numbers.sub: redo from the flagged step vs redo the whole chain

charts.chart_plain_title: Confidence bottoms out exactly at the first mistake
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is each step's distance from the first error: negative before it, zero the error itself, positive after. The vertical axis is confidence with the natural climb-with-position removed; lower means less sure.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line sits high two steps before the slip, plunges to its lowest point precisely at the first error, then recovers: the dip marks the origin, not just late steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Confidence pinpoints the slip better than guessing by position
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way to guess which step is the first slip on single-error chains; bar height is how often it's right, so taller is better. Confidence-based methods sit beside guessing-by-position, always-guess-last, and blind chance.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two confidence bars (56% and 64%) tower over guessing by position (36%) and chance (19%), while always-last is near zero: confidence carries real information about where the error is.

qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.verdict_tag: Runnable-fix training transfers, but needs worked steps
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.concept_primer: Instead of describing a fix in words, the model is trained to rewrite the broken formula as a runnable mini-program the computer then checks by actually running it on real cases.
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.plain_question: Can the model learn to fix broken mini-programs by rewriting them, and still succeed on kinds of problems it never trained on?
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.plain_answer: Mostly. After training it fully solved unseen problem types about three-quarters of the time, versus a third with no worked-through steps in the prompt, but only when those matching steps were present, and one problem type stayed stubbornly hard.
qwen35_4b_executable_program_posttrainin.why_it_matters: Training a small model to emit runnable fixes can carry over to new problem types, but only if the prompt shows matching worked-through steps. Missing or scrambled steps erase most of the gain, so keep them aligned.

key_numbers.label: Unseen-problem success with worked steps
key_numbers.sub: share of new problems fully solved when the prompt shows matching steps

key_numbers.label: Without vs with worked steps
key_numbers.sub: full success on new problems, no steps versus aligned steps

key_numbers.label: Hardest problem type, after extra practice
key_numbers.sub: the two-part-condition type, before and after adding practice examples

charts.chart_plain_title: Success on unseen problem types across four setups
charts.chart_read: Each group is one training-and-prompt setup; bar height is the share of new problems fully solved. The two bars per group are one best guess versus picking the best of a few tries; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two setups that include matching worked-through steps tower over the two without, solving about three-quarters of new problems versus roughly a third.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-problem-type success before and after extra practice
charts.chart_read: Each group is one unseen problem type; bar height is the share fully solved. The two bars compare the first-trained model with one given extra practice on combined-condition problems; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The stubborn two-part-condition type climbs from zero to about a third after extra practice, while the other two types stay near-perfect.

charts.chart_plain_title: What happens when the prompt's worked steps are removed or scrambled
charts.chart_read: Each group is one unseen problem type; bar height is the share fully solved. The three bars are a prompt with matching worked steps, no steps, or scrambled steps; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Removing or scrambling the worked steps collapses one problem type from near-perfect to at most a fifth, showing the steps do real work.

qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.verdict_tag: A cheap shape check beats running every program
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.concept_primer: Picture a returns desk that opens a package for full inspection only when the box comes back lighter than it shipped, a weight drop hinting something inside went missing. Here the quick answer having fewer columns than the input table is that lighter box, triggering the slow repair programs.
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.plain_question: Can a cheap rule decide when a small model should run its slow, expensive extra program attempts on a table task, instead of always running them?
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.plain_answer: Yes, and here is the twist: running all five programs on every task scored lower (56%) than the cheap rule (58%), because blanket spending overwrote one answer the quick pass already had right. The rule fires only when the quick answer has fewer columns than the input, flagging exactly the hard cases, beating the quick answer's 42% and tying a perfect-hindsight ceiling at half the compute.
qwen35_4b_foofah_adaptive_program_budget.why_it_matters: Before training a learned judge, check whether your expensive fallback's wins cluster behind one cheap, visible signal; a hand-written trigger may match the best achievable accuracy. And beware: running every tool everywhere can clobber correct answers, not just waste compute.

key_numbers.label: Quick answer alone vs. the routed rule
key_numbers.sub: exactly correct answers on 50 table-transformation tasks

key_numbers.label: Running all five programs on every task
key_numbers.sub: below the rule's 58%, at double the compute

key_numbers.label: Compute per task, run-everything vs. rule
key_numbers.sub: the rule spends about half while scoring higher

key_numbers.label: Wrong answers fixed vs. broken by the rule
key_numbers.sub: captured every available fix with no losses

charts.chart_plain_title: Cheap rule ties perfect hindsight and beats running everything
charts.chart_read: Bars show exactly-correct accuracy on 50 tasks for four policies: quick answer only (42%), running all five programs everywhere (56%), the cheap shape rule (58%), and a perfect-hindsight policy that runs programs only when they help (58%). Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The cheap rule reaches the perfect-hindsight ceiling and edges past run-everything, while the quick answer trails far below, so selective spending beats blanket spending.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute spent per task by each of the four policies
charts.chart_read: Bars show average tokens per task for the same four policies: quick answer cheapest at 1,478, run-everything priciest at 17,801, the shape rule in between at 9,017, perfect-hindsight lowest among spenders at 3,959. Shorter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rule costs about half of run-everything yet scores higher, and the perfect-hindsight bar shows even more compute could still be shaved.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixed budgets never catch the selective rule
charts.chart_read: A rising line tracks accuracy as 0 to 5 programs are forced on every task, climbing from 42% to 56%; a flat reference line marks the shape rule at 58%. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The always-run line climbs then stalls at 56%, always under the rule's 58%, so spending the whole budget blindly cannot match spending it selectively.

charts.chart_plain_title: All the gains come from just two task families
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per task family (five tasks each) compare quick answer against the shape rule across ten families. Higher is better. Two families jump from 0 to 80%; every other family is unchanged.
charts.chart_takeaway: All eight rescues cluster in the merge/split and one synthetic family, and the rule never dents a family the quick answer already solved.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trying one program first barely helps
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per program strategy compare running that strategy alone against a version that escalates only when its answer disagrees with the quick answer. Higher is better; all bars sit between 42% and 50%.
charts.chart_takeaway: Escalating after one program adds at most two points, and no single-strategy approach reaches the shape rule's 58%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Requiring programs to agree lowers accuracy at every budget
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars at three portfolio sizes compare committing to the first plausible program against requiring two or three programs to agree. Higher is better; the agreement bars are shortest.
charts.chart_takeaway: Demanding agreement drops accuracy 8 to 12 points versus trusting the first plausible program, so consensus voting throws away correct lone answers.

charts.chart_plain_title: Looser trigger rules pay far more compute for the same accuracy
charts.chart_read: Bars show total tokens over 50 tasks for six trigger rules, from the tightest fewer-columns rule (231,365) to always-run (890,030). Shorter is better; the five selective rules all score 29 of 50, or 58%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The five selective triggers tie at 58% accuracy while the loosest costs about three and a half times the tightest, so trigger precision is what saves compute.

qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.verdict_tag: Asking directly beats the specialized tool
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.concept_primer: Imagine reshaping messy spreadsheet tables into a clean format. You can use a pre-built converter that only knows a fixed set of moves, or just ask the AI to write out the finished table itself.
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.plain_question: Does the pre-built table converter beat simply asking the model to write out the reshaped table itself?
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.plain_answer: No. Asking the model directly got 55% of tables exactly right, far ahead of the pre-built converter's 18%. The converter added only a tiny extra slice of cases the model missed on its own.
qwen35_4b_foofah_direct_vs_abi.why_it_matters: For reshaping messy tables, reach for the model directly rather than building a specialized converter; keep the tool only as a small backup for the few cases the model misses.

key_numbers.label: Tables reshaped correctly, asking directly
key_numbers.sub: Model writes the finished table itself

key_numbers.label: Tables reshaped correctly, pre-built converter
key_numbers.sub: Specialized tool with fixed moves

key_numbers.label: Solved only by asking directly
key_numbers.sub: vs just 10 solved only by the tool

key_numbers.label: Best possible combining both
key_numbers.sub: Direct-ask plus tool as a backup

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of messy tables reshaped correctly, by method
charts.chart_read: Bars show the fraction of 250 table-reshaping tasks each method got exactly right. Left bar is asking the model directly; middle bars are the pre-built converter tool; right bar combines both. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The direct-ask bar towers over the converter-tool bars, so simply asking the model outperformed the specialized tool by a wide margin.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which tables each method uniquely got right
charts.chart_read: Bars split the 250 tasks into four groups: solved by both, by direct-ask only, by the converter tool only, or by neither. Taller bars for the useful groups are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 'direct-ask only' bar dwarfs the 'converter-only' bar, showing the model handles far more cases the tool cannot than the reverse.

qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.verdict_tag: Writing a throwaway program did not help
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.concept_primer: Instead of reshaping a messy spreadsheet by hand, the model can write a tiny throwaway script that does the reshaping, test it on one worked example, then run it on the real data.
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.plain_question: Does having the model write and test a small reshaping script beat just having it reshape the spreadsheet directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.plain_answer: No. Answering directly got about 55% of tables exactly right, while the script route managed only 15%. Even combining both perfectly would reach just 59%, barely above answering directly alone.
qwen35_4b_foofah_ephemeral_program_induc.why_it_matters: On this kind of table-reshaping task, let the model answer directly rather than routing through a self-written script. Passing one worked example is a weak safety check and lets wrong scripts through.

charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; bar height is the share of 250 tables reshaped exactly right, so taller is better. Direct answering and the two combined bars sit near 55-59%, while the script-only bar is far shorter.
charts.chart_takeaway: The script-only bar is roughly a third the height of direct answering, and combining both barely clears the direct bar, so the script route adds almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which method solved which tables
charts.chart_read: Bars count the 250 tables by who got them right: both, direct-answering only, script only, or neither. Taller 'direct only' versus 'script only' shows which approach uniquely rescues more.
charts.chart_takeaway: 'Direct only' towers over 'script only' (110 versus 10), meaning direct answering uniquely solves eleven times as many tables as the script route does.

charts.chart_read: Bars follow the script route left to right: code written, code that runs, code that passes the one worked example, and code that is actually right on the real data. Each stage drops, showing where scripts fail.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bar collapses from 250 written to 47 passing the worked example, and 9 of those 47 still fail on the real data, so passing one example proves little.

charts.chart_plain_title: Early prompt tests on easy versus harder samples
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per test show direct answering, the script route, and the two combined. The first two groups are easy hand-picked cases; the last two are a harder mixed sample, where higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On easy cases every bar hits 100%, but on the harder sample the script bar collapses to 12%, revealing the easy cases had flattered the script route.

qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.verdict_tag: Fixed toolkit too narrow for outside tasks
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.concept_primer: Think of a fixed toolkit of table-reshaping moves, like a small recipe book, that tries to rebuild each messy spreadsheet into the desired clean layout using only the recipes it already knows.
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.plain_question: Can a fixed toolkit of table-reshaping moves handle a broad outside collection of real spreadsheet cleanup tasks?
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.plain_answer: No. The fixed toolkit rebuilt only 18% of the outside transformation tasks, failing 82% because it simply lacks the needed moves, not because it chose the wrong recipe or saw too few examples.
qwen35_4b_foofah_external_transform_gate.why_it_matters: Before investing in smarter recipe-picking, first widen the toolkit itself. Coverage was all-or-nothing by task type, so the gap is missing building blocks, not better selection.

key_numbers.sub: looked right but often failed on new rows

charts.chart_read: Bars show three ways of scoring the same 250 tasks: matching just the shown example, actually working on fresh data, and picking the first matching recipe. Height is the share solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three bars sit near one-fifth, and picking the first recipe nearly matches the best possible, so the toolkit itself, not the choosing, is the limit.

charts.chart_read: Each line tracks the share of tasks solved as the number of shown example pairs rises from one to five. Higher is better. Two lines stay flat; the top line only falls to meet them.

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.verdict_tag: Trust the first working program, not a vote
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.concept_primer: Think of fixing a messy spreadsheet: the model writes a few small transform-the-table programs, then checks each against one worked example whose answer is known. The question is whether making several programs agree beats simply running the first one that passes the example.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.plain_question: When a small model writes several little programs to reshape a table, does making them agree beat just running the first one that passes a checkable example?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.plain_answer: No. The simplest rule won: run the first program that passes a single worked example. It solved 52% of tables versus 44% when the model just answered directly, rescuing 23 tables it had otherwise botched while breaking only 4. Requiring several programs to agree was more careful but far too timid, recovering fewer mistakes and topping out at 49%.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_ensemble_consen.why_it_matters: For structured tasks where you can write code and check it against a known example, have the small model generate programs and deploy the first one that passes. Don't gate on ensemble agreement — it trades away most of the wins.

key_numbers.label: Best any rule could reach

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.verdict_tag: Repair adds coverage but not a replacement
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.concept_primer: Instead of typing the answer by hand, the model writes a small runnable recipe that reshapes the table, tries it on a solved worked example, and fixes wherever the result differs.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.plain_question: Does letting the model write a small conversion recipe, test it on a worked example, and fix its mistakes beat just asking for the answer directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.plain_answer: It depends. On its own the recipe path solved far fewer tables than answering directly (25% vs 55%), but fixing the recipe against the worked example and then keeping whichever path won caught 18 extra tables, reaching 62%.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_repair_agent.why_it_matters: Treat the recipe path as a complementary second opinion, not a replacement for direct answering, and never auto-trust a recipe just because it matches the worked example.

charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe-only bars sit well below direct answering, yet the combined bar is the tallest, showing the two approaches solve different tables.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the fixing round; the lines count tables. One line is tables still being fixed, one is tables now matching the worked example, and one is tables that also match the real data. Bigger gains per round are better.

charts.chart_takeaway: The stricter instruction doubled recipe correctness and nearly halved the misleading passes, so telling the model not to memorize the example helped.

qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.verdict_tag: Trust the first passing program, not a vote
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.concept_primer: To reshape a messy table, the model writes several small programs and runs each against the one worked example it can check, like trying a ring of keys against a test lock. Programs that pass become candidates; a commit rule picks which to trust.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.plain_question: When a small model reshapes messy tables, does having it write and test several small programs beat just asking it for the finished table directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.plain_answer: Yes, but modestly, and which passing program you trust matters more than the programs. Asking directly for the finished table got 42% right. The rule the team locked in, commit only when two programs agree, reached just 48%. A simpler rule they also wrote down beforehand, trust the first program that passes the check, hit 56%, nearly the 58% best-possible ceiling. Consensus quietly discarded correct lone hits.
qwen35_4b_foofah_program_strategy_portfo.why_it_matters: When candidate programs self-check against a worked example, commit the first that passes rather than requiring two to agree, since one correct program is usually right. Gains cluster in a few table shapes, so route effort, do not run every strategy everywhere.

key_numbers.label: Asking directly vs trusting the first passing program
key_numbers.sub: exact table matches on 50 reserved test cases

key_numbers.label: The cautious rule they actually shipped
key_numbers.sub: committed only when two programs agreed

key_numbers.label: Best possible from these candidates

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy: asking directly versus each rule for picking a program
charts.chart_read: Height is exact-match accuracy on 50 reserved test tables; taller is better. Left bar asks the model directly; the two middle bars require two or three programs to agree; the next trusts the first program that passes; the rightmost is the best-possible ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first-passing bar (56%) nearly touches the 58% ceiling while the agree-rules stall near 48%, so the picking rule, not the programs, capped the gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: What each picking rule commits, rescues, and breaks
charts.chart_read: For three picking rules, grouped bars count cases out of 50: programs committed, wrong direct answers rescued (higher is better), and correct direct answers broken (lower is better).
charts.chart_takeaway: Trusting the first passing program commits 23 and rescues 8 wrong answers at just 1 break; the agree-rules commit fewer and rescue only 3 to 4, safer but too timid.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute cost: asking directly versus running five program strategies
charts.chart_read: Two bars show total forward tokens across the 50-case test run; lower is cheaper. Left is asking the model directly; right runs all five program strategies with example-checked repairs on every case.
charts.chart_takeaway: The portfolio burns about twelve times more tokens (890,030 versus 73,911) for a modest accuracy gain, real added coverage but not yet an efficient policy.

qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.verdict_tag: Simple check helped, stress-test did not
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.concept_primer: For each table task the model can either write the answer straight out, or write a tiny program to build it; you can then run that program on the shown examples to see if it reproduces them.
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.plain_question: Does falling back to a table-transforming program beat answering directly, and does stress-testing that program make the choice safer?
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.plain_answer: It depends. Trusting the program whenever it reproduced the shown examples raised accuracy from 55% to 62% with zero cases broken, but the extra stress-test on made-up inputs helped nothing and slightly hurt.
qwen35_4b_foofah_selective_program_fallb.why_it_matters: A cheap correctness gate, does the program match the examples you were shown, can be a reliable trust signal. Adding elaborate self-consistency stress-tests can cost effort and quietly reject good answers.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy of each fallback rule for choosing an answer
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one rule for deciding when to trust the program instead of the direct answer; height is the share of tables solved correctly. The leftmost is direct answers only; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rightmost bar, trusting any program that reproduces the shown examples, is tallest, beating direct answers and every stress-test-gated rule.

charts.chart_plain_title: When program and direct answer disagreed, which was right
charts.chart_read: The 26 tables where the program and the direct answer conflicted. Left bar counts times the direct answer was actually correct; right bar counts times the program was. Taller means more often right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The direct answer was never correct here while the program was correct 18 times, showing the program covers cases direct answering misses.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many programs each rule used, and how many were right
charts.chart_read: For each rule, the first bar counts programs it chose to trust and the second counts how many of those were actually correct. Taller correct bars close to the trusted bar are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Stress-test-gated rules trust very few programs and reject good ones, while trusting every example-matching program keeps roughly four in five correct at full volume.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stress-test agreement cannot tell right programs from wrong ones

charts.chart_takeaway: Wrong programs actually scored slightly higher agreement than correct ones, so the stress-test points the wrong way and cannot guide the choice.

qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.verdict_tag: Self-invented recipes beat prompting but unlock nothing new
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.concept_primer: A small model studies a few already-solved table-reshaping puzzles and writes itself two named recipes: one pivots columns into rows, the other merges repeated rows. It freezes them, then follows a recipe to write fresh code for brand-new tables it has never seen.
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.plain_question: If a small model invents its own reusable recipes for reshaping messy data tables, does it then solve more new tables than just asking it directly?
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.plain_answer: Barely, and nothing genuinely new. The self-written recipes lift exactly-solved tables from 42% to 46%, edging out plain prompting. But a ready-made library of solved programs already handled 56 to 58%, and every table the recipes cracked, that library already covered. Net new tables unlocked: exactly zero. Discovery just re-derived a slice of what the model could already reach.
qwen35_4b_foofah_strategy_discovery_live.why_it_matters: Before trusting a model to invent reusable strategies, check what a curated library of solved programs already covers — here self-discovery only re-derived existing wins. If you do commit a discovered recipe, gate it on a table-shape match, which made every commit correct.

charts.chart_takeaway: Each recipe solves only about one-in-eight to one-in-five tables alone — narrow, family-specific reach, not a general-purpose reshaping skill.

qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.verdict_tag: A stop signal is not an answer slot
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.concept_primer: A reasoning model works in two phases: a private scratchpad where it thinks, then a final answer, split by a hidden "done thinking" marker. This test jammed that marker in early to cut thinking short, like telling a colleague mid-sentence "time's up" without handing them an answer form.
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.plain_question: If a small model keeps thinking past its budget and never wraps up on its own, can you just insert a stop-thinking marker to make it commit to an answer?
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.plain_answer: No. Inserting the model's own "done thinking" marker cut the reasoning off but almost never flipped it into answer mode. Across three thinking budgets, only 13% to 19% of forced stops produced anything readable, and just 1 of 48 was correct, versus the 90% readability the test demanded. Usually the model simply restarted its analysis instead of committing.
qwen35_4b_forced_commit_jacobian_value_t.why_it_matters: If you cap a small model's thinking to save tokens, don't trust the stop signal alone to produce an answer. Give it an explicit answer slot to fill, and verify output is readable, not just that thinking stopped.

key_numbers.label: Forced stops that produced a readable answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Readable and correct answers versus rambling, at three thinking budgets
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by the thinking budget before the forced stop (256, 512, 1024 tokens); height is the share of 48 traces. Three bars per group: readable-answer rate, correct-answer rate, and still-rambling-at-the-cap rate. Higher readable and correct is better; lower rambling is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Readable bars sit near one-fifth, correct bars hug zero, while rambling bars tower above 85% at every budget, so the stop signal never produced a clean answer.

qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.verdict_tag: It knew the answers but never wrote them
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.concept_primer: Each answer gets a fixed thinking budget, like a timed exam. When the clock runs out mid-thought, the model re-explains from scratch instead of writing its answer on the answer line, so correct reasoning is never scored. Training taught it to commit an answer when cut off.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.plain_question: When a small model keeps failing a benchmark, is it because it cannot reason well enough, or because it never actually commits to a final answer?
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.plain_answer: Mostly the second. On a blind benchmark the model scored about 14 percent, with six task types near zero, but it had usually reasoned correctly. It simply hit its thinking limit, restarted explaining instead of writing the answer line, and scored blank. Teaching it to commit an answer from a cut-off chain lifted scores to 36 and 45 percent, and the fix carried to tasks it never practiced.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_breadth_round1.why_it_matters: Before assuming a small model lacks a skill, measure how often it emits no readable answer within its thinking budget. Training it to commit an answer from its own cut-off reasoning can unlock skill it already has, and where you place that training signal beat adding more data.

qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.concept_primer: A small model has one fixed pool of internal wiring, like a blanket sized for a single sleeper. Pull it to cover fast, shallow tasks and the slow, deep-reasoning tasks go cold; pull it the other way and the fast tasks do. The blanket never grows.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.plain_question: When a small model plateaus, can feeding it harder and more varied practice make it excel at both quick tasks and slow, step-by-step reasoning at once?
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.plain_answer: No, not both at once. Piling on more, harder, or more varied self-generated practice all stalled at the same ceiling, and even hand-written expert solutions the model could not discover on its own failed to move it. A new trick, self-training the model's own shortest correct reasoning into its weights, finally cracked the slow-reasoning tier, but only by ceding ground on the fast one.
qwen35_4b_gauntlet_frontier.why_it_matters: Do not chase one small-model fine-tune that aces everything; ship two specialists, one tuned for speed, one for deep reasoning. And skip broad data harvesting: a single rich task family taught nearly the whole gain that broad variety did.

charts.chart_takeaway: Abstention and triage jumped most (+77 and +63 points); induction and optimization rose solidly; deep repair sat just below zero, the one skill still unmoved.

charts.chart_takeaway: Nearly two-thirds are solvable-but-slow, a time-budget problem rather than a capability wall, exactly what compressing the model's own solutions targets; the rest is a genuine gap.

qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.verdict_tag: Small models grade their own code only after
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.concept_primer: Picture a coder who dashes off eight rough versions of a function. Asked to grade them on sight, they nod along and mark almost everything correct. Given a minute to trace each line, they turn sharp — reliably catching the broken ones.
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.plain_question: If a small model writes eight tries at a coding problem, can it reliably tell which ones actually work — without running the code?
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.plain_answer: Only after it thinks. Judging on sight, the model rubber-stamps 91% of its tries as correct while just 77% truly pass — barely a check, mostly agreeing with itself. Given room to reason first, it becomes a real critic: letting it pick its best of eight lifts success from 77% to 86%, closing three-quarters of the gap to a flawless picker's 89% ceiling.
qwen35_4b_generator_verifier_gap.why_it_matters: Before training a separate model to rank candidate answers, first have the model reason through and grade its own — zero extra training, it beats blind picking, and it works from the task text alone with no hidden tests.

charts.chart_takeaway: The thinking-judge bar nearly reaches the flawless ceiling and towers over random and snap-judge picks — reasoning before judging recovers most of the achievable gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Skill at telling its own right answers from wrong ones
charts.chart_read: Two skill scores — grading accuracy that corrects for guessing, and how reliably a correct answer outranks a wrong one — each shown for snap-judging (lighter) versus thinking (darker). Higher is better; 50% is pure guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both scores jump sharply with thinking — grading from 63% to 83% — turning a near-coin-flip judge into a dependable critic of its own code.

charts.chart_read: Three bars: the share of candidates the snap-judge calls correct, the share the thinking-judge calls correct, and the true pass rate as the honest target. Landing closer to the true rate is better; far above it means rubber-stamping.
charts.chart_takeaway: The snap-judge sits well above the truth at 91% against a real 77%; thinking pulls its approvals down toward the actual rate — a sign of genuine judgment.

qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.verdict_tag: A fix-it pass catches noise, not real mistakes
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.concept_primer: The model writes a program two ways: a dense one-line formula, or an editable step-by-step diagram. A separate fix-it pass proofreads that diagram, but it practiced only on randomly scrambled versions, so it catches obvious damage while missing a clean-looking step that quietly computes the wrong answer.
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.plain_question: If a small model writes code as an editable step-by-step diagram, then runs a learned fix-it pass over its failures, does that beat a plain one-liner?
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.plain_answer: No. The plain one-line formula fully solved 29% of brand-new tasks; the step-by-step diagram managed only 22%, and the fix-it pass recovered part of that gap to 24% — still behind. That pass genuinely works: on randomly scrambled diagrams it lifted success from 3% to 27%, an eightfold jump. But the model's own errors are confident, consistent wrong logic, not obvious damage, so repair rarely helps.
qwen35_4b_graphir_self_repair.why_it_matters: Do not assume a fix-it loop that aces synthetically broken inputs fixes a model's real errors — it patches random damage, not systematic wrong logic. Test repair on the model's own failures, and check whether a fancier program format earns its complexity.

charts.chart_takeaway: Success jumps from 3% to 27%, an eightfold rise, proving the repair skill is real — yet it barely helps the full pipeline because real mistakes differ from random damage.

qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.verdict_tag: Smart stopping did not improve accuracy
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.concept_primer: Picture a quiz-solver that drafts several candidate answers, then runs sample inputs through them and keeps the answer shared by the biggest group; the model's only job is to say "commit now" or "run one more sample input."
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.plain_question: Can the model boost accuracy by deciding when to stop running sample inputs versus running one more?
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.plain_answer: No. Every stopping strategy landed at the same 17% correct-answer rate, and letting the model spend more sample inputs never moved the pick onto a right answer, even though a correct answer was available for every single task.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_adaptive_budget.why_it_matters: Deciding when to stop testing is a dead end when the sample inputs cannot tell right answers from wrong ones. Effort belongs in a better way to pick the winning answer, not in tuning when to quit.

key_numbers.sub: a right answer always existed to be found

key_numbers.label: Sample runs the trained model used
key_numbers.sub: cheaper stopping, but zero accuracy gain

charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-answer bar sits at the same low height for every strategy while availability stays at full height, meaning the pick almost never lands on the right answer that was there all along.

qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.verdict_tag: One yes-or-no check beats averaging the whole answer
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.concept_primer: A model writes a function one token at a time. You could gauge its confidence from how smoothly it "typed" hundreds of characters, but doubt in one spot blurs into the average. Better to ask point-blank, "Is this right?", and read how firmly it answers that one word.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.plain_question: When a small model writes several candidate programs and no tests are available to run them, how can it tell which one is actually correct?
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.plain_answer: Ask it directly. Averaging the model's confidence across every code token barely helps, nudging correct picks from 77% (blind guessing) to just 79%. But handing the model its own code and reading its confidence in one yes-or-no verdict jumps that to 84% — near the 87% ceiling of a flawless picker. How you ask matters more than whether you ask.
qwen35_4b_humaneval_code_confidence.why_it_matters: When no tests exist to run generated code, don't rank candidates by average per-token confidence — long answers dilute the signal. Feed each solution back, ask the model to judge it yes-or-no, and pick by its confidence in that verdict word.

key_numbers.label: Picking the right program

key_numbers.label: Best possible picker
key_numbers.sub: ceiling if a correct candidate were always chosen when one existed

charts.chart_plain_title: How well each method picks correct code when no tests can run
charts.chart_read: Four bars, taller is better, showing how often each method's pick passes the hidden tests across all 164 coding tasks. Left to right: blind random pick (77%), averaging confidence over every code token (79%), one yes-or-no self-verdict (84%), and a flawless picker (87%).
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-verdict bar clears both averaging and guessing and nearly reaches the flawless-picker ceiling, showing one concentrated judgment carries more correctness signal than the diluted token-by-token average.

qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.verdict_tag: A taught routine sticks to exactly its practiced
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.concept_primer: Show the model only before-and-after examples and ask it to reverse-engineer the hidden chain of operations that produced them: a two-step chain, then a three-step one. The taught routine is a chef's tasting drill: shortlist likely ingredients, imagine the dish, taste-check against the real one, revise.
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.plain_question: If a small model can't reverse-engineer three-step transformations from examples, can you fix that by teaching it a step-by-step guess-and-check routine for finding them?
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.plain_answer: No. Fine-tuning the model on 1,476 worked guess-and-check traces doubled its success at the two-step depth it practiced on (lists jumped from 37% to 70%), yet did nothing one step deeper: three-step success stayed near 5%, statistically flat and no better than an untaught model. The skill installed cleanly, then refused to stretch a single operation further.
qwen35_4b_hypothesize_verify_wall.why_it_matters: Match training depth to deployment depth: a model drilled on N-step reasoning gets strong at exactly N steps and stays at the floor at N+1. Hand deeper cases to external search or more thinking budget, not a smarter prompt.

key_numbers.label: Two-step reverse-engineering success (list tasks)
key_numbers.sub: after teaching the routine, the success rate roughly doubles at the practiced depth

key_numbers.label: Same task one step deeper (three-step)
key_numbers.sub: not a real change; the wall holds one composition step further out

key_numbers.label: A borrowed prior skill aimed at a new two-step task
key_numbers.sub: the transferred skill interfered — worse than doing nothing

key_numbers.label: Three-step success when the thinking budget is doubled
key_numbers.sub: raw compute, not any taught routine, was the only lever that moved the deep case

charts.chart_plain_title: Teaching the routine doubles two-step success but adds nothing at three steps
charts.chart_read: Two bar groups: the practiced two-step depth and the deeper three-step depth. In each, an untaught model and the routine-trained model appear for list tasks and string tasks. Height is the share of tasks whose operation structure was proposed correctly — taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At two steps the trained bars roughly double the untaught ones; at three steps all four bars collapse to the floor together — the skill does not stretch across depth.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every way of teaching the routine fails the three-step test
charts.chart_read: One bar per approach to the three-step task: the untaught baseline, a prompt-only routine scaffold, a borrowed prior skill applied as-is, and full routine fine-tuning. Height is the share of three-step tasks solved with correct structure; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar sits within a few points of the low untaught baseline, and none clears the pre-set improvement bar — the three-step wall is untouched by how the routine is delivered.

charts.chart_plain_title: The borrowed skill masters its home task but breaks on new ones
charts.chart_read: Three task groups. First the borrowed skill on its own home task, paired against its known score. Then two new task types (list and string, two-step), each pairing that same skill against a plain untrained model. Height is accuracy; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On home turf the skill matches its known score (92% vs 91%); on the new task types it drops to near zero, even below the untrained model — it interferes rather than transfers.

qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.verdict_tag: Follows a new rule but cannot discover one
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.concept_primer: A cook follows any written recipe flawlessly, but can only reverse-engineer a dish back into a recipe when it is one they already know. Hand them a strange new dish to taste and rebuild, and they fail — the very recipe they would have cooked perfectly.
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.plain_question: Can a small model work out a brand-new rule just from examples, or can it only reuse patterns it already picked up during training?
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.plain_answer: Only patterns it already has. Told a scrambled counting order outright, the model applied it almost perfectly — 97 percent right. But shown examples of that same order and asked to work it out, it scored 12 percent, no better than the 10 percent you would get by guessing. Examples surface familiar structure; they do not discover genuinely new structure.
qwen35_4b_icl_retrieval_vs_induction.why_it_matters: Do not expect examples to teach a small model a genuinely new rule — adding more only hurt here. Examples re-surface structure it already carries. When a task needs a novel mapping, state the rule outright instead of demonstrating it.

key_numbers.label: Applies a new rule when told it outright
key_numbers.sub: near-perfect execution of a scrambled counting order

key_numbers.label: Works out that same rule from examples
key_numbers.sub: about chance — pure guessing scores 10%

key_numbers.label: Works out a familiar rule from examples
key_numbers.sub: even known structure is only half-recovered

key_numbers.label: Effect of adding more examples
key_numbers.sub: working out the new rule gets worse, not better

charts.chart_plain_title: Applying a rule versus figuring it out, for familiar and new orders
charts.chart_read: Two bar groups: left, the rule is stated outright; right, it must be figured out from examples. In each, one color is the familiar 0-to-9 order, the other a scrambled new order. Taller is more accurate; 10 percent marks pure guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: When the rule is stated both bars stand near the top; when it must be inferred, the new-order bar collapses to guessing level — obeying is easy, discovering is not.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does adding more examples help the model figure out the rule?
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is examples shown (five then eight); vertical axis is how often the model works out the rule, higher is better. One line is the familiar order, the other the scrambled new order; both would climb if examples helped.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines slope downward as examples grow — familiar from 45 to 25 percent, new from 15 to 5 — so the limit is familiarity, not too little data.

qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.verdict_tag: Fixed toolkit misses most new tasks
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.concept_primer: Think of a pre-stocked toolbox of generic coding building blocks, assembled and locked before any test. A task counts as covered if some single tool from the box already solves it.
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.plain_question: Can one fixed, general-purpose toolbox of coding building blocks solve a useful share of brand-new programming tasks?
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.plain_answer: No. The locked toolbox solved only about 14% of brand-new tasks, down from 37% on the familiar tasks it was shaped around, too few to justify building anything on top of it.
qwen35_4b_independent_code_abi_coverage_.why_it_matters: A generic prebuilt toolbox does not stretch to unseen problems. Reusable coverage has to be proven on fresh tasks before investing in tooling; a passing sample check is not proof.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved from the toolbox
key_numbers.sub: familiar tasks vs brand-new tasks

key_numbers.label: Solved across three fresh task samples
key_numbers.sub: steady low level, not a fluke

key_numbers.label: Sample-passing matches that still fail
key_numbers.sub: passing the quick check but wrong on hidden checks

charts.chart_plain_title: Toolbox coverage drops sharply on brand-new tasks
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks the fixed toolbox can solve, for the familiar tuning set, brand-new tasks, and a broader task pool. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The brand-new bar falls to less than half the familiar bar, showing the toolbox does not carry over to unseen problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coverage stays low across three fresh task samples
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one random sample of brand-new tasks; height is the share the fixed toolbox solves. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three bars sit near the same low level, so the weak coverage is consistent, not an unlucky draw.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every kind of task is poorly covered
charts.chart_read: Bars break brand-new tasks into categories like text, list, and number tasks; height is the share the toolbox solves. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: No category rises above about a fifth, and number tasks nearly bottom out, so the gap spans every domain, not one weak spot.

charts.chart_plain_title: Passing the quick check rarely means a real solution
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often a toolbox match that passes the visible quick check turns out wrong, per task group; two shades track task-level and match-level failure. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On brand-new tasks over nine in ten quick-check passes still fail hidden checks, so the quick check gives false confidence.

qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.verdict_tag: Independent agreement never produced usable picks
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.concept_primer: Imagine asking several people who worked out an answer separately, then trusting only answers they all independently agree on, so you can pick a correct solution without having the answer key.
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.plain_question: Can several independently derived solutions, by agreeing with each other, tell us which answer is correct when we have no answer key?
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.plain_answer: No. Requiring independent solutions to agree committed an answer on zero of 24 hard problems, because the independent versions rarely converged; a looser close-match pool committed sometimes but mostly on wrong answers.
qwen35_4b_independent_retrieval_consensu.why_it_matters: Forcing variety among your reference solutions can backfire, making them less relevant and less likely to agree. Genuine independent agreement needs a richer, more relevant source library, not just diversification.

key_numbers.label: Committed answers, requiring independent agreement
key_numbers.sub: Independent solvers never agreed enough to pick anything

key_numbers.label: Problems the diversified pool could solve
key_numbers.sub: Diversifying lowered it below the close-match pool

key_numbers.label: Answers that looked right but were wrong (diversified pool)
key_numbers.sub: Most passing-looking candidates were actually incorrect

key_numbers.label: Best a perfect picker could reach
key_numbers.sub: Even the ideal pick tops out here

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each picking method commits, and whether it was right
charts.chart_read: Bars count how many of 24 hard problems each picking method committed an answer on; green is answers that were actually right, red actually wrong. Taller green and shorter red is better. The far-right bar shows the best any picker could do.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two independent-agreement bars sit flat at zero, so requiring independent solvers to agree never produced a single committed answer, right or wrong.

charts.chart_plain_title: Diversifying the source pool lowered its quality
charts.chart_read: For two ways of pulling reference solutions, one bar shows the share of problems the pool could actually solve (higher is better) and the other the share of answers that looked right but were wrong (lower is better). Compare the diversified pool against the close-match pool.
charts.chart_takeaway: The diversified independent pool has the lower solve share and the taller wrong-answer bar, so forcing variety made the source material worse, not better.

qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.verdict_tag: Correcting its live mistakes made it stop verifying
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.concept_primer: An interactive repair agent runs a loop: look, edit, run the test, commit the fix. Its correction clips were nearly all edits and almost none of the run-the-test moments — like an apprentice who watches endless bolt-tightening but rarely sees the engine started and checked.
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.plain_question: If you correct a small agent at the exact moments it stumbles mid-task, one step at a time, does it become a better multi-step problem-solver?
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.plain_answer: No — it backfired badly. Success on the practice tasks fell from about 60% to 35%, and on tasks never touched in training from about 69% to 35%. It kept editing fluently but stopped running its checks: the verify-and-commit step was only about 1 in 40 correction examples, so it washed out entirely. On one repair family it never verified once, scoring zero.
qwen35_4b_interactive_policy_curriculum.why_it_matters: Before fine-tuning a looping agent, count how often each decision type appears in your training data. Rare but decisive steps — verify, commit, stop — get drowned out by common edits while parsing, formatting, and surface scores keep looking healthy.

key_numbers.label: Practice-task success
key_numbers.sub: score on task families used for the correction training

key_numbers.label: Untouched-task success
key_numbers.sub: families never used in training, tested for transfer

key_numbers.label: How rare the verify step was
key_numbers.sub: 55 of 2,270 correction examples were the verify-and-commit action

key_numbers.label: Checks run on one repair family
key_numbers.sub: the retrained model never ran its test there, scoring zero

charts.chart_plain_title: How much the retrained model gained or lost on each task group
charts.chart_read: Three bars show the retrained model's score minus the original model's, for trained task families, untouched task families, and basic single-step skills. The zero line means no change; bars above zero are better, below zero are worse.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both multi-turn task bars plunge far below zero, roughly a quarter to a third of the scale, while the basic-skill bar barely dips — the damage is to in-task decision-making, not underlying knowledge.

qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.verdict_tag: Training did not build a usable shortlist
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.concept_primer: Imagine building a small gadget that needs two parts chosen from a 512-item parts catalog: the model must shortlist a handful of likely parts for each slot so the correct pair is among them.
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.plain_question: Can extra training teach the model to shortlist the right parts from a 512-item catalog so the correct pair is included?
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.plain_answer: No. After training, the model's shortlist still contained the correct two-part combination 0% of the time, exactly matching the untrained and scrambled-catalog versions, so the extra teaching added nothing here.
qwen35_4b_inventory_shortlister_training.why_it_matters: When a model cannot surface the right choice from a large catalog, adding more same-format training will not rescue it; invest instead in smarter search or better-designed test questions, which measurably narrowed the field.

key_numbers.label: Right pair found, before vs after training
key_numbers.sub: the shortlist never included the correct two-part combination

key_numbers.label: Options left after six test questions
key_numbers.sub: designed questions narrow the field far more than random ones

key_numbers.label: Picked the correct program
key_numbers.sub: with designed questions versus random test cases
