key_numbers.sub: coaching lowered its own starting point

key_numbers.label: Training rounds that gave any signal
key_numbers.sub: most rounds were all-fail or all-pass

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of unseen problems solved by at least one draft
charts.chart_read: Bars compare four ways of drafting; height is the share of problems where at least one draft works, so taller is better. The coached model is the third bar; the other three are the untouched model at different creativity or draft counts.
charts.chart_takeaway: The coached bar is the shortest, below even the plain untouched model, while the most-creative untouched sampling stands tallest.

charts.chart_plain_title: First-draft accuracy and how different the drafts are
charts.chart_read: For each drafting method, one bar shows how often the very first draft is correct and another shows how different the drafts are from each other; higher is better on both. The coached model is the third group.
charts.chart_takeaway: The coached model has the lowest bars on both measures, so it also weakened first-draft quality and made drafts more alike.

charts.chart_plain_title: Useful learning signal across the training rounds
charts.chart_read: Each point is one training round; the line shows how many of eight drafts passed. Rounds sitting at zero (all failed) or at eight (all passed) teach the model nothing; only in-between rounds help.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most points sit flat at zero or eight, so only a handful of rounds gave any usable teaching signal, explaining why the coaching stalled.

qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.verdict_tag: Draft-first search does not beat plain sampling
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.concept_primer: Normally the model writes each solution from scratch, then checks which ones pass. This test instead hands it promising half-written code drafts to finish, asking whether starting from a good draft finds answers more cheaply.
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.plain_question: Does starting from promising half-written code drafts help the model solve more programming problems, for the same compute, than writing complete solutions from scratch?
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.plain_answer: No. Every draft-first approach solved the same share of problems as plain from-scratch writing, about 75%, and never more. Starting from good drafts changed how the model explored but produced no extra solved problems, so the idea was set aside.
qwen35_4b_prefix_value_guided_search.why_it_matters: Before building machinery to score and pick promising code drafts, confirm it actually solves more problems. Here it did not: the drafts added variety but no new answers, so the added complexity was not worth it.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved, plain writing
key_numbers.sub: share of the 12 problems solved

key_numbers.label: Problems solved, best draft-first
key_numbers.sub: same share, no improvement

key_numbers.label: Compute per method
key_numbers.sub: units of compute; drafts cheaper but solve no more

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of problems solved by each search method
charts.chart_read: Each group is one search method: plain from-scratch writing versus five draft-first variants. Darker bars show the share of the 12 problems solved at all; lighter bars the share solved on a first pick. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every draft-first bar tops out at the same solved share as plain writing and none rises above it, so the drafts add no new solutions.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute used by each search method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one search method; bar height is the total compute it burned, so shorter is cheaper. Plain from-scratch writing sits on the left; the draft-first variants follow.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best draft-first bar is about four times shorter than plain writing, so it is cheaper, but only because the answers were already known when picking that draft.

charts.chart_plain_title: How varied the generated programs are
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one search method; taller bars mean the finished programs differ more. Two shades: how differently the programs run, and how differently they are written. Neither direction is inherently better here.
charts.chart_takeaway: Draft-first bars are much taller for how-differently-they-run, meaning more varied behavior, yet that extra variety never converted into more problems solved.

qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.verdict_tag: The model computes the operation, not the number
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.concept_primer: A mechanic hears an engine and instantly diagnoses "needs an oil change" — real expertise, held in the head. But the exact oil grade? They read it off the cap. This model transforms lists of numbers, diagnosing each step's KIND internally yet reading its NUMBER off the examples.
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.plain_question: When a small model transforms a list of numbers step by step, does it hold each step's exact settings inside its weights, or just read them off the examples?
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.plain_answer: It splits. The model genuinely computes the KIND of operation inside itself — reading its internal activity names the kind far better than the examples alone do (41% versus 27%, against 6% for blind guessing). But the specific number is not stored anywhere special: a trivial calculator run over the examples recovers it just as well (49% versus 53%). The model reads it off, same as anyone could.
qwen35_4b_probe_the_parameter.why_it_matters: Before mining a model's internals for hidden knowledge, test whether the fact is simply readable off the input-output examples. Derive those parts cheaply outside the model — faster and more accurate — and probe internals only for what it truly computes.

key_numbers.label: Reading the operation's KIND — the model's mind vs the raw examples
key_numbers.sub: the model's internal activity clearly wins; blind guessing is about 6%

key_numbers.label: Reading the operation's NUMBER — the model's mind vs the raw examples
key_numbers.sub: the no-model calculator ties or wins; blind guessing is about 30%

key_numbers.label: Solve rate when handed the full correct operation and number
key_numbers.sub: vs under 1% when told only the kind — the number is the real bottleneck

key_numbers.label: Delivering the number: cheap no-model pipeline vs reading the model
key_numbers.sub: you do not need the 4-billion-parameter model for the number

charts.chart_plain_title: Reading the operation's kind needs the model; the number does not
charts.chart_read: Three decode targets run along the bottom: the operation's kind, the full operation, and just its number. Bar height is decode accuracy — taller is better. One color reads the model's internal activity, the other a no-model classifier on the raw examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: For the kind, the model's bar towers over the examples bar; for the number, the examples bar matches or beats it — the kind is computed inside, the number just read off.

charts.chart_plain_title: Handing over the full operation unlocks solves; the model's guess barely helps
charts.chart_read: Six hint conditions run along the bottom: no hint, told only the kind, told the full operation, the model-decoded operation, the examples-decoded operation, and told a wrong number. Bar height is solve rate — one color a single best attempt, the other solved within six tries. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the full operation spikes; kind-only and wrong-number sit near zero — and the cheap examples-based hint outdoes the model's own decoded guess.

qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.verdict_tag: Hint the exact step, not just its type
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.concept_primer: Picture reconstructing a lost recipe. The cook's instinct reveals the first step needs something sour — the category — but not the exact amount, two spoons of lime — the value. Given the category, they land it within a few tries; given the value, first try.
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.plain_question: A small model's hidden state already seems to know a task's right first step. Can you read it out and feed it into the prompt to solve harder tasks?
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.plain_answer: Partly. Writing the concrete first step — exact value and all — into the prompt lifted the single-best-guess solve rate on two-step tasks sixfold, from 3% to 19%, where editing the model's internal state did nothing. But its internals reliably reveal only the step's category, and category alone merely helps it stumble onto answers across several tries — not solve first-shot. The exact value is the real unlock.
qwen35_4b_probe_to_prompt.why_it_matters: When a small model seems to internally know something, don't extract it by editing its internal state — decode the fact into the prompt instead. And decode the concrete value, not just the category; category-only hints merely widen the search without fixing the first answer.

key_numbers.label: Single best guess, two-step tasks
key_numbers.sub: with the exact first step — type and value — written into the prompt

key_numbers.label: Solved within six tries, two-step tasks
key_numbers.sub: same exact-step hint, allowing several attempts

key_numbers.label: Category-only hint, single best guess
key_numbers.sub: naming the step's type but not its value doesn't help the first try

key_numbers.label: Three-step tasks, even with the exact hint
key_numbers.sub: no hint conjures a step the model never computed

charts.chart_plain_title: Which prompt hint actually lifts two-step solving
charts.chart_read: Six prompt conditions run along the bottom, from no hint through category-only, the exact step, the model's own decoded guess, to a deliberately wrong hint. One color is single-best-guess solves, the other solved-within-six-tries; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the exact-step hint (type plus value) raises single-best-guess solving; the category-only hint lifts just the six-try bar, and the wrong hint flattens both to zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: The exact-step hint helps two-step tasks but not three-step
charts.chart_read: Five prompt conditions along the bottom; height is the single-best-guess solve rate, taller is better. One color is two-step tasks, the other three-step, showing how far each hint carries by task length.
charts.chart_takeaway: The exact-step hint lifts two-step tasks to 19% but leaves three-step tasks flat near zero — it can cash in latent knowledge but cannot create a step the model never computed.

qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.verdict_tag: Choosing the answer is not the bottleneck
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.concept_primer: Imagine solving each problem by writing several rough drafts, then handing in the one you judge best. You can fail two ways: none of your drafts is actually correct, or a correct draft exists but you hand in the wrong one.
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.plain_question: When a coding model writes several draft solutions and then picks one to submit, do failures come from picking the wrong draft, or from no correct draft ever being written?
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.plain_answer: No, picking was not the problem: the trained picker submitted a correct draft whenever one existed in the pool. On easier problems nearly every draft was already right; on harder ones, failures came entirely from no correct draft ever being written, not from bad picking.
qwen35_4b_real_sample_verify_commit.why_it_matters: If your coding model already writes several drafts and picks one to submit, invest in getting it to produce a correct solution at all, not in smarter picking. The picker is likely not where you are losing problems.

key_numbers.label: Harder problems where a correct draft exists
key_numbers.sub: going from one draft per problem to many drafts each

key_numbers.label: Correct answers the picker actually submitted (harder set)
key_numbers.sub: matches every correct draft that existed — nothing lost to bad picking

key_numbers.label: Easier problems solved
key_numbers.sub: almost every single draft was already correct

charts.chart_plain_title: How often a correct answer gets submitted, easier versus harder problems
charts.chart_read: Two groups of bars, one per problem set. Within each: the ceiling of problems where a correct draft existed, the result of grabbing the first draft that passed the visible example tests, and the trained picker's choice. Taller means more problems solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained picker's bar reaches the same height as the ceiling on both sets — it loses nothing. The harder set's ceiling itself is simply lower.

charts.chart_plain_title: Chance a correct draft exists as you write more drafts per problem
charts.chart_read: Two lines, the harder set and the easier set. The horizontal axis is how many drafts you write per problem; the vertical axis is the share of problems where at least one draft is correct. Higher and rising is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The easier set's line starts near the top and barely moves; the harder set's line climbs then flattens well below it — more drafts stop helping.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many drafts you must write with smart early stopping
charts.chart_read: Two groups of bars, harder set and easier set. Bars show the average number of drafts written per problem: always writing the maximum, an ideal stop-as-soon-as-solved rule, and a learned stopping rule. Fewer drafts is cheaper and better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned stopping rule's bars are far shorter than always writing the maximum, especially on the easier set — the same results for a fraction of the drafts.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do smarter picking rules beat simple ones for choosing a submission
charts.chart_read: Each group of bars is one picking rule, from the best-possible pick down to simple habits like taking the first draft that passed the visible tests or the answer most drafts agree on. Bar height is share of problems solved; taller is better. Shown for both sets.
charts.chart_takeaway: Nearly every bar sits at the same height within each set — smarter picking rules do not beat simple ones; only a couple dip slightly on the harder set.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much accuracy each early-stopping rule keeps
charts.chart_read: Three stopping rules side by side per set: an ideal stop-as-soon-as-solved rule and two practical learned rules. Bar height is the share of problems still solved after stopping early; taller means less accuracy given up. Shown for the harder and easier sets.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the easier set all three bars match; on the harder set the practical rules sit slightly below the ideal one — a small accuracy cost for stopping early.

charts.chart_plain_title: Drafts actually written versus the maximum allowed
charts.chart_read: Two lines, harder and easier set. The horizontal axis is the number of drafts allowed per problem; the vertical axis is how many were actually written before a draft passed the visible tests and writing stopped. Lower means it stopped sooner.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines bend below the maximum at higher limits — writing stops early once a draft passes the visible tests, and the easier set stops sooner than the harder one.

qwen35_4b_recovery_payload_budget_harnes.verdict_tag: Recovery gain did not beat its incumbent
qwen35_4b_recovery_payload_budget_harnes.concept_primer: Two coding policies can resemble two mechanics who fix different failures. A fair test must show that one consistently outperforms the other, not merely that their strengths differ.
qwen35_4b_recovery_payload_budget_harnes.plain_question: Does giving tool commands enough room turn the reason-enhanced recovery model into a consistently stronger coding agent?
qwen35_4b_recovery_payload_budget_harnes.plain_answer: No. It led on the first unseen task block at 71%, but confirmed at 69%, exactly tied with the action-only model and short of the required lead. The external benchmark stayed sealed.
qwen35_4b_recovery_payload_budget_harnes.why_it_matters: The larger command slot fixed a real interface problem, but changing one global model cannot combine both policies' strengths. The next test should fork both and let visible test evidence choose.

key_numbers.label: Development recovery
key_numbers.sub: reason-enhanced model passed every early gate

key_numbers.label: Confirmation recovery
key_numbers.sub: reason-enhanced and action-only models tied

key_numbers.label: Combined ceiling
key_numbers.sub: either local policy succeeded on both fresh blocks

key_numbers.label: Benchmark exposure
key_numbers.sub: stopped before external tasks

charts.chart_plain_title: The early lead disappears on confirmation
charts.chart_read: Each model or control is grouped along the horizontal axis, with recovery success vertically; taller is better. The two colors compare development with independent confirmation.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reason-enhanced bar leads during development but exactly matches action-only on confirmation, so it fails the required incumbent win.

charts.chart_plain_title: The two policies solve different cases
charts.chart_read: The horizontal groups are the two unseen blocks, and bar height is recovery success. The third bar counts cases solved by either policy and is only a diagnostic ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The combined bar repeats at 79% on both blocks, showing stable complementary strengths that a visible test selector might exploit.

charts.chart_plain_title: More answer room sharply reduces cut-off commands
charts.chart_read: Each group is an unseen task block; bar height is the share of generated turns that hit the answer limit, where lower is better. Colors compare base with the reason-enhanced policy.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reason-enhanced bars stay near 8% while base stays near 30%, confirming that command truncation was a real interface bottleneck.

qwen35_4b_recovery_reason_locality_inter.verdict_tag: Safe blend beat both parents, gates stopped it
qwen35_4b_recovery_reason_locality_inter.concept_primer: Two fine-tuned versions share one starting checkpoint: one only learned fix-it actions, the other also learned to explain its plan first. Rather than retrain, you average their learned weight changes on a dial and search for the mix that keeps the skill and drops the mistakes.
qwen35_4b_recovery_reason_locality_inter.plain_question: If you average two versions of a model, one that just acts and one that also explains, can you dial in a mix that fixes its mistakes without breaking unrelated behavior?
qwen35_4b_recovery_reason_locality_inter.plain_answer: Yes on skill, no on shipping. One dial setting recovered from broken code 97% of the time, about 12 points above the act-only version and 15 above a matched-training baseline, while barely moving unrelated behavior. But it tripped two automatic release checks, so the test halted before the transfer stage. On inspection, both checks were mis-specified, not the model.
qwen35_4b_recovery_reason_locality_inter.why_it_matters: When two fine-tunes each get part of a job right, averaging their weights can capture both without retraining. But write release gates around the real goal, not a proxy like doing it in one step, or genuinely good models get blocked.

key_numbers.label: Recovering from broken code
key_numbers.sub: baseline vs the best weight blend

key_numbers.label: Best blend beats the act-only version
key_numbers.sub: on repairing broken-code cases

key_numbers.label: Change to unrelated behavior
key_numbers.sub: the safe blend used about four-fifths of the allowed drift; the explain-only extreme hit twice the cap

key_numbers.label: "Invalid" turns that were really just cut off
key_numbers.sub: hit the answer-length cap mid-patch, not broken output; 9 of 11 such runs still solved

qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.verdict_tag: Cheap tricks could not spot correct code
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.concept_primer: A small AI writes several versions of a program. Some pass the quick sample checks a user can see, yet are secretly wrong on hidden cases. The goal here is a cheap way to tell the genuinely-correct version from the secretly-wrong one.
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.plain_question: When a small coding AI writes several programs that all pass the quick sample checks, can cheap tricks reliably pick out the version that is actually correct?
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.plain_answer: No. Three low-cost tricks all failed. Ranking programs by the model's own confidence picked fewer genuinely-correct versions than simply taking the first that passed the sample checks; making the writing more predictable did not help; and giving a helper a worked failing example nudged the AI no better than scrambled nonsense did.
qwen35_4b_reliability_exec_opsd_audit.why_it_matters: Before paying to train a picker, cheaply check that your reliability signal beats both a scrambled-nonsense control and a plain first-pass rule. If a hint helps no more than nonsense, it carries no real information, so look for independent evidence instead.

key_numbers.label: Genuinely-correct programs picked when ranking by the model's own confidence
key_numbers.sub: fewer than simply taking the first program that passes the sample checks

key_numbers.label: Secretly-wrong programs picked by that same confidence ranking
key_numbers.sub: more wrong picks than the simple first-pass rule, which made selection worse

key_numbers.label: Tasks actually solved at the best writing setting
key_numbers.sub: making the AI's writing more predictable did not raise this

charts.chart_plain_title: Does a worked failing example point the AI at the correct code?
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a type of hint given to a helper model; taller means it nudged the AI more strongly toward the genuinely-correct code. Bars include a scrambled-nonsense hint and an answer-leaking hint for comparison. Taller is a stronger nudge.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real hint's bar sits below the scrambled-nonsense bar and far below the answer-leak bar, so the hint carries no genuine task-specific information.

charts.chart_plain_title: Ranking by the model's own confidence picks worse programs
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method count committed programs out of 24 tasks: one for genuinely-correct picks, one for secretly-wrong picks. More correct and fewer wrong is better. Methods are the first passing program, the confidence ranking, and a perfect picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: The confidence-ranking method has a shorter correct bar and a taller wrong bar than simply taking the first passing program, so it makes selection worse.

charts.chart_plain_title: Making the AI's writing more predictable does not solve more tasks
charts.chart_read: Across three writing settings, from most predictable to most varied, two rates over 24 tasks: how often a workable program appeared and how often the chosen program was actually correct. Higher is better for both.
charts.chart_takeaway: No setting stands out: the most predictable setting solves slightly more tasks but yields fewer workable candidates, a wash with no clear winner.

qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.verdict_tag: Training on clean wins erases recovery skill
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.concept_primer: Imagine training a mechanic only on repairs that went perfectly the first time: inspect, fix, test, done. They nail that tidy four-step routine but never watch a stuck bolt get worked loose — so when a fix is rejected, they just resubmit the same broken move.
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.plain_question: If you train a small coding agent only on its clean, fully successful bug-fix runs, does it get broadly better at fixing bugs across new codebases?
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.plain_answer: No. It became flawless on the six codebase types it trained on, solving every task in exactly four clean steps. But on four never-seen codebase types its success collapsed from 68% to 35% — worse than just running the untrained model twice, which scored 53%. Stripped of every failed attempt, it never learned what to do when a fix gets rejected.
qwen35_4b_repo_search_compress_bank.why_it_matters: When distilling agent trajectories, keep the failures and recoveries, not just the shortest winning path. Balance error-conditioned moves — failed-test-then-revise — not just how often each action appears, and test recovery on unseen tasks before trusting it.

key_numbers.label: Practiced codebase types, before vs after
key_numbers.sub: success on the six trained families it memorized

key_numbers.label: Unseen codebase types, before vs after
key_numbers.sub: success on four never-trained families (a 33-point drop)

key_numbers.label: Retries a fix after a failed test
key_numbers.sub: untrained model re-patched 24 of 26 times; trained model 0 of 48

key_numbers.label: Beaten by running the untrained model twice
key_numbers.sub: same-compute baseline outscored it by 18 points

charts.chart_plain_title: Training perfected practiced codebases but broke unseen ones
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show the share of coding tasks solved. The left pair is the six practiced codebase types, the right pair four unseen types. The lighter bar is before training, the darker bar after clean-wins training. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The practiced pair climbs to a perfect 100%, but the unseen pair drops by a third — the gain was memorized cloning, not transferable coding skill.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every training example kept all four coding steps equally
charts.chart_read: Bars count target training rows per step — inspect, patch, verify, commit — in a 20-repository setup check. All four sit at exactly 20 rows, showing compression kept each step in equal amounts. This is a data check, not a model result.
charts.chart_takeaway: Perfectly level bars confirm the compression balanced how often each step appears — yet equal counts still failed, because they never say when to recover after a failure.

qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.verdict_tag: Lookups help but trusting them fails
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.concept_primer: Think of handing the model a cookbook of already-solved problems: for a task it can't crack alone, it finds the closest recipe and tweaks it to fit the new problem.
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.plain_question: When the model gets stuck, does looking up a similar solved problem fix it, and can the system tell which fix to trust?
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.plain_answer: Partly. Looking up a similar solved problem rescued a third of the stuck tasks, double a random lookup. But no automatic check could reliably tell the good fixes from the wrong ones, so most couldn't be safely trusted.
qwen35_4b_retrieval_adapt_verify_scale.why_it_matters: Retrieving and adapting past solutions is a real way to unstick a small model, but the payoff only lands if you can verify which candidate is right. Invest next in stronger checks, not more candidates.

key_numbers.label: Tasks the model solved on its own
key_numbers.sub: 56 of 80 problems

key_numbers.label: Stuck tasks rescued by looking up a similar solved problem
key_numbers.sub: 8 of 24 — double the 17% from random lookup

key_numbers.label: Best possible coverage if every good rescue were picked
key_numbers.sub: only with flawless picking

key_numbers.label: Committed answers that were actually wrong
key_numbers.sub: the shipped example checks couldn't separate right from wrong

charts.chart_plain_title: Meaning-matched lookup rescues the most stuck tasks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a lookup strategy; bar height is the share of the 24 stuck tasks it rescued into the candidate pool, so taller is better. The meaning-matched lookup is tallest and plain copying is shortest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The meaning-matched bar reaches a third of stuck tasks, roughly double the random-lookup bar, showing that matching by meaning is what recovers stuck tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Every automatic picker commits as many wrong answers as right ones
charts.chart_read: For each picking method there are paired bars, one for correct answers committed and one for wrong ones, out of 24 stuck tasks; fewer wrong is better. Only the last method, a cheat that peeks at hidden answers, has no wrong bar.
charts.chart_takeaway: The wrong-commit bar matches the correct-commit bar for every real method, meaning the checks cannot separate right from wrong; only the peeking baseline avoids mistakes.

qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.verdict_tag: Being best overall isn't being best right here
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.concept_primer: Picture a lead actor who keeps fumbling one exact scene. Two understudies each do several takes of that same scene. You swap one in only if it clearly beats both the actor and the rival, and only after a fresh, separate batch of takes confirms it.
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.plain_question: When a small model stalls partway through a problem, can you tell which of two specialist helper models would actually finish it better from that exact spot?
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.plain_answer: Not yet. The system is built and armed, but only its engineering checks pass, so no capability results exist. The insight is the reframe: a leaderboard tells you which teacher wins on average, not which one helps at a specific stuck moment, and here the stronger teacher's overall edge barely clears the bar. So each teacher must prove itself where the student fails, on fresh samples, or it sits out.
qwen35_4b_same_prefix_advantage_routing.why_it_matters: When merging specialist models into one small model, don't pick your teacher by leaderboard rank. Score each candidate on the exact states your model fails, copy its behavior only where it verifiably wins on separate samples, and skip the rest.

key_numbers.label: The stronger teacher's edge on medium tasks
key_numbers.sub: its error bar straddles the +32-point line it is judged against, so even the overall ranking is shaky

key_numbers.label: Forward tries per teacher at each stuck point
key_numbers.sub: one set of tries selects the winner, a separate disjoint set grades it, so the pick never grades itself

key_numbers.label: How the student model is built
key_numbers.sub: a blend of 40% quick specialist and 60% deep specialist; the strongest single checkpoint that exists and the bar every teacher must beat

key_numbers.label: Installation sanity check on identical prompts
key_numbers.sub: on eight fixed prompts the blended student behaved differently from the quick teacher every time and the deep teacher nearly every time, ruling out a silent do-nothing merge

charts.chart_plain_title: How many trial continuations feed picking versus checking each teacher
charts.chart_read: Two equal-height bars. The left bar counts trial continuations used to pick a teacher; the right counts a separate untouched set used to verify its edge. Each holds 4,608 continuations across two problem blocks. Equal size is the point.
charts.chart_takeaway: The pool that picks a teacher and the pool that confirms it are exactly the same size and fully separate, so a teacher can never be chosen and graded by the same evidence.

charts.chart_plain_title: How each training round splits its lessons across sources
charts.chart_read: Three bars showing where each round's 80 lesson-units come from: 30 from quick-teacher states, 30 from deep-teacher states, and 20 from replaying the original blend. Taller bars carry more of the training; the two teachers are matched.
charts.chart_takeaway: Three-quarters of each round teaches new behavior split evenly between the two teachers, while a quarter re-anchors the original skills as a guard against forgetting.

qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.verdict_tag: Shippable schedulers didn't beat sampling more
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.concept_primer: Think of several dice, each weighted to favor different answers. A 'portfolio' rolls a few styles and a manager decides which style deserves more rolls on each problem, hoping to beat just rolling one die harder.
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.plain_question: Can mixing several answer-writing styles and picking the best one per problem solve more coding tasks than just generating more tries from a single style?
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.plain_answer: No. Every manager we could actually ship tied or fell behind simply rolling one style harder. Only a perfect-hindsight picker did better, solving 75% of problems versus 67% for sample-more, showing the payoff exists but isn't yet reachable.
qwen35_4b_sampler_portfolio_scheduler.why_it_matters: The room to improve is real but only a smarter style-picker can claim it. Invest in predicting which style will help each problem, not in more blind sampling or scaling one style.

key_numbers.label: Best a shippable manager reached
key_numbers.sub: problems solved — same as just sampling more, no gain

key_numbers.sub: about 22k vs 44k units, cheaper than sampling more

charts.chart_plain_title: Which answer-writing style wins, per problem, across six approaches
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one approach for choosing generation styles; height is the share of 24 coding problems solved, so taller is better. The far-right bar is a perfect-hindsight picker; the others are approaches you could actually ship.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the rightmost hindsight bar rises clearly above the rest, while every shippable approach sits level with or below simply sampling more from one style.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving power of five style schedules on a larger 80-problem pool
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a schedule for spending generation effort; height is the share of 80 problems solved, taller is better. The far-right bar throws every candidate answer at each problem regardless of cost.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-scheduler bar actually dips below the plain fixed schedules, and the towering far-right bar shows lots of solvable problems nobody's approach reached.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute spent by each approach on the 24-problem set
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one approach; height is the total compute it burned across 24 problems, so shorter is better. Read it alongside the first chart, which shows how many problems each solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The perfect-hindsight bar is one of the shortest, about half the compute of sampling-more, yet it solved the most problems, so its payoff is cheap not costly.

qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.verdict_tag: A perfectly repaired skill reached no dependent task
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.concept_primer: This model runs a recipe forward: given the steps and an input, it computes each result in turn. The reverse — naming the hidden steps from a finished dish — is a separate ability. Drilling forward recipes to perfection never touched it; the two sit in sealed compartments.
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.plain_question: If a small model keeps failing tasks because it cannot mentally trace a multi-step process, does training it to trace flawlessly finally fix those tasks?
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.plain_answer: No. Training made the model trace a multi-step process almost flawlessly — even on longer chains and steps it never studied, so it learned a genuine skill, not memorized answers. Yet every task that supposedly needs tracing stayed frozen: naming the hidden steps from examples crept from 8% to 9%. Oddly, unrelated training that merely matched a task's answer format helped three times more.
qwen35_4b_simulation_keystone_repair.why_it_matters: When a small model fails a task, don't train the underlying skill and hope the fix spreads. Fine-tuning buys only the input-to-output mapping you train, plus format look-alikes. To add a truly missing ability, give the model a tool instead.

key_numbers.label: Tracing a four-step process
key_numbers.sub: frozen model vs after skill training, at a chain length it never trained on

key_numbers.label: Tracing steps it never studied
key_numbers.sub: proof it learned a real skill, not memorized answers

key_numbers.label: A task that supposedly needs tracing
key_numbers.sub: naming the hidden steps from examples — essentially unchanged

key_numbers.label: A format-matched task, trained on that format
key_numbers.sub: a threefold jump from matching answer shape, with no skill training

charts.chart_plain_title: Step-by-step tracing collapses as the process gets longer
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many steps are chained (one to five); vertical axis is the share of traces the untrained model got exactly right. Two lines: answering after deliberate thinking, and answering immediately. Higher is better; far below the 80% pass mark counts as broken.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines plunge from near-perfect at one step to about a third correct by four steps — the untrained model's tracing genuinely breaks at length, qualifying it for repair.

charts.chart_plain_title: Thinking helps tracing at every length, with or without lossy steps
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is chain length (one to five); vertical axis is the share of exactly-correct traces. Four lines split deliberate thinking versus immediate answers, each with and without steps that throw away information. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every thinking line sits above its immediate-answer twin, and information-losing steps hurt most without thinking — so slow deliberation is not the problem, chain length is.

charts.chart_plain_title: Each added step keeps less accuracy than the last
charts.chart_read: Three bars, one per added step (one-to-two, two-to-three, three-to-four), from the deliberate-thinking runs. Each bar's height is the share of accuracy kept versus the shorter chain. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Retention falls from 92% to 66% to 52% — each step costs more than the last, so errors compound rather than leaking at one fixed rate.

qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.verdict_tag: Only hand-written templates survive a new task
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.concept_primer: Think of a fill-in-the-blank blueprint: the system fills the blanks to build many candidate programs, then keeps the ones that run correctly. The test is whether the right program still lands in that candidate pile.
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.plain_question: When the task changes to need a genuinely new operation, does the correct program still show up among the candidates the system builds?
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.plain_answer: It depends on the blueprint. When the blueprint explicitly names the new operation, the correct program appears every time; when the blueprint is auto-generated it appears only 17% of the time, and a bare blueprint never finds it.
qwen35_4b_sketch_coverage_shift_probe.why_it_matters: Before building a smarter picker to choose among candidates, invest in blueprints that name and shape the new operations. A perfect picker cannot rescue a correct program that was never built in the first place.

key_numbers.label: New-operation task, auto-generated blueprint
key_numbers.sub: how often the correct program was even among the candidates

key_numbers.label: New-operation task, hand-written blueprint
key_numbers.sub: naming the operation in the blueprint fixes it completely

key_numbers.label: New-operation task, bare blueprint
key_numbers.sub: the correct program never appeared at all

charts.chart_plain_title: Does the right program survive when the task shifts?
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars across three task types (familiar task, same task just renamed, task needing a new operation). Within each, three bars for auto-generated, hand-written, and bare blueprints. Taller means the correct program was in the candidate pile more often; full height is best.
charts.chart_takeaway: Hand-written blueprint bars stay at full height everywhere, while the auto-generated bar collapses to a sliver once the task needs a genuinely new operation.

charts.chart_plain_title: Allowing more fill-ins per blank does not rescue auto blueprints
charts.chart_read: Lines show how often the correct program appears on the new-operation task as you allow more options per blank (left to right). Higher is better. One line for hand-written, one for auto-generated, one for bare blueprints.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hand-written line climbs to full, but the auto and bare lines stay flat along the bottom, because the new operation is simply never proposed no matter how many options each blank gets.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra check-queries sharpen a choice but cannot create a missing program
charts.chart_read: Lines show how often the chosen program is actually correct as the system spends more clarifying queries (left to right). Higher is better. Two lines have the right program available; one line (new operation, auto blueprint) does not.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two lines with the right program available climb to 100% within two or three queries, while the line missing that program stays flat, showing queries help only after the answer already exists.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where the correct answer survives: by task family and sketch style
charts.chart_read: A grid: each row is a task family, each column a way of giving the model a template (auto-built, hand-written, or none). Colour is the share of tasks where the correct program stayed reachable — darker is higher.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hand-written column is dark all the way down, but the auto column turns pale on the primitive families and the no-template column is pale almost everywhere — the template, not the family, decides survival.

qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.verdict_tag: One nearly-perfect skill made the plan impossible
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.concept_primer: Picture four high-jumpers, each ordered to clear a bar ten centimeters above their own best. The gym ceiling caps everyone. Three have room, but the fourth already jumps just under the ceiling, so ten extra centimeters simply have nowhere to go.
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.plain_question: Can you train four separate skill specialists, then merge them into one small model that combines all their strengths on brand-new tasks?
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.plain_answer: No — we never found out. Each of four required skills had to gain ten points before merging, but scores top out at 100. One tool-use skill already sat at 99.4%, so its target of 109.4% was impossible. The experiment stopped before a single training step. The real lesson: broad room on the overall task hid one corner already maxed out.
qwen35_4b_specialist_policy_integration.why_it_matters: Before spending compute on a multi-specialist plan, check improvement room skill by skill, not just on the averaged task. A fixed points-gained target is unreachable once a skill nears its ceiling — size targets to the room actually left.

key_numbers.label: Starting score on the tool-use skill
key_numbers.sub: already pressed against the perfect ceiling of 100%

key_numbers.label: Score the rule demanded
key_numbers.sub: impossible, since the maximum is 100%

key_numbers.label: Room to improve versus room required
key_numbers.sub: the fixed gain bar dwarfed the headroom left

key_numbers.label: Specialists actually trained
key_numbers.sub: the plan halted before any training ran

charts.chart_plain_title: Required scores versus the 100 percent ceiling, per skill
charts.chart_read: Four skill domains sit along the bottom: discovery-and-repair, stateful control, tool use, and composition. Each shows three bars — current score, the score its rule required, and the 100% ceiling. A required bar staying under the ceiling means the target is reachable.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the tool-use skill's required bar pokes above the ceiling, so its target can never be met; the other three sit well below and stay feasible.

qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag.verdict_tag: Does carrying memory forward beat repeating in parallel
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag.concept_primer: Imagine tracking a piece moving across a board. One method keeps a single running note, updating and feeding it into each next turn. The other re-reads the opening position many times independently and pools its guesses. Same total effort — does chaining the note win?
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag.plain_question: When a small model repeats the same computation to solve a step-by-step puzzle, does chaining its memory forward beat pooling many fresh, independent passes?
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag.plain_answer: Not run yet — no live model call has happened; only the test rig is built and checked on a laptop. It will decide whether wiring repeated computation as one evolving memory beats pooling equal-compute independent passes. The bar is strict: the chained version must win by at least five accuracy points on longer, unseen puzzles, hold across three separate training runs, and survive causal memory-swap checks.
qwen35_4b_state_carry_vs_state_bag.why_it_matters: If chaining wins, small models can get deeper by looping one shared memory instead of sampling in parallel — cheaper reliability on multi-step tasks. If it does not, extra loops are mere repetition, and that compute is better spent on wider sampling.

key_numbers.label: Practice length versus test length
key_numbers.sub: trained on 4-step puzzles, tested on unseen puzzles up to 12 steps

key_numbers.label: Winning margin needed to count
key_numbers.sub: chained-memory accuracy minus pooled-passes accuracy on unseen puzzles

key_numbers.label: Independent training runs that must agree
key_numbers.sub: the advantage must appear across all three, not cherry-picked

key_numbers.label: Memory-swap causal test bar
key_numbers.sub: how much more the answer must follow a swapped-in memory than resist it

qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.verdict_tag: A few harder examples double transfer, then stall
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.concept_primer: Picture the model as a code mechanic handed a broken tiny program plus a printout that, for each failing test, shows the answer it should have produced beside the wrong one it actually did. Its job: rewrite the program so every hidden test passes.
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.plain_question: If you mix a small batch of slightly-harder practice problems into a small model's training, does it learn a repair skill that carries to entirely new, deeper problems?
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.plain_answer: Partly. Folding in just 60 slightly-harder "bridge" examples, a quarter of the training budget, more than doubled success on deeper, never-seen programs, from 20% to 44% fully repaired, with no loss on familiar skills. But it stalled fast: 80 examples did no better. And the skill is genuine reading, not memorization: feed a scrambled test printout and success sinks below giving no printout at all.
qwen35_4b_static_bridge_ceiling_breaker.why_it_matters: When teaching a small model to repair code, add a thin slice of slightly-harder examples and always show real execution results in the prompt. Expect a hard ceiling, and confirm it truly reads that feedback by scrambling the results and watching accuracy collapse.

key_numbers.label: Fixing deeper, never-seen programs
key_numbers.sub: share fully repaired after adding 60 bridge examples, up from none

key_numbers.label: Bridge examples added
key_numbers.sub: a quarter of a fixed training budget

key_numbers.label: More examples, no payoff
key_numbers.sub: 60 versus 80 bridge examples on deeper programs

key_numbers.label: Correct vs scrambled printout
key_numbers.sub: a shuffled test printout hurts more than none at all

charts.chart_plain_title: A few harder examples lift both trained and never-seen program types
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by training mix: no bridge examples, 60 added, 80 added. One color is the familiar trained program families, the other the deeper unseen families never seen in training. Height is the share of programs whose fix passes every hidden test; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Trained families jump to a perfect 100% and hold, while unseen families roughly double from 20% to 44% then flatten, so transfer is real but capped.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixing new programs collapses when the test printout is wrong or missing
charts.chart_read: Three bars, the same 60-bridge model on the same unseen programs, differing only in the test printout shown: correct, removed, or scrambled. Height is the share of programs whose fix passes every hidden test; taller means it leaned on that printout more.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-printout bar towers over the rest, and the scrambled bar sinks below the removed one, proving the model genuinely reads the feedback rather than guessing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Bridge training helps some new families fully, others not at all
charts.chart_read: Six unseen program families along the bottom. Paired bars compare the no-bridge model against the 60-bridge model. Height is the share of programs whose fix passes every hidden test; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Several families leap from zero to near-perfect, one stays frozen at zero, and one regresses, so the benefit is family-specific, never a uniform lift.

qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.verdict_tag: Style-tagging guesses did not recover more misses
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.concept_primer: Imagine a puzzle-solver that fails a few coding puzzles on a quick attempt. To crack them, you can let it guess many more times, or nudge it toward different solving styles — loops, recursion, math — hoping variety helps.
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.plain_question: When a cheap first pass fails on some coding puzzles, can teaching the model to tag its guesses with a solving style recover more of them than just taking more plain guesses?
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.plain_answer: No. Teaching the model to label its guesses by solving style recovered about 38% of the missed puzzles — no better than simply taking more plain guesses at the same cost, which recovered 42%. A version with the style labels scrambled did just as well, so the labels themselves added nothing.
qwen35_4b_strategy_token_diversity_lora.why_it_matters: When cheap sampling misses hard problems, spend the budget on more varied plain guessing rather than training a style-tagging add-on. Tagging did not improve recovery, and only far more expensive pooling of many guesses reached noticeably higher.

key_numbers.label: Missed puzzles recovered by style-tagging
key_numbers.sub: out of the 24 the quick pass missed

key_numbers.label: Missed puzzles recovered by plain extra guesses
key_numbers.sub: same cost, and it comes out ahead

key_numbers.label: Same setup with the style labels scrambled
key_numbers.sub: matched the real labels, so the labels did not matter

charts.chart_plain_title: Recovering missed puzzles: style add-on vs simply guessing more
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one sampling approach; its height is the share of the 24 initially-missed puzzles that approach eventually solves. Taller is better. The two rightmost bars pool the most guesses; the middle bars use the style add-on.
charts.chart_takeaway: The style add-on bar sits no higher than plain extra guessing, and the scrambled-label bar matches it — the style labels added nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of all 80 puzzles solved, by sampling approach
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one sampling approach; its height is the fraction of all 80 puzzles solved. Taller is better. The left bar is the cheap quick pass; the far-right bars pool the most guesses.
charts.chart_takeaway: The style add-on bars land in the same 81-to-83% band as plain heavier guessing; only the far more expensive pooled approaches climb higher.

qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.verdict_tag: It picks the wrong steps, not wrong numbers
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.concept_primer: Picture a three-step recipe: the ordered list of operations, chop then boil then season, is the structure; the exact amount inside each step is the value. This tested a small model on multi-step list-transforming programs, asking whether failure comes from the order of steps or the numbers inside them.
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.plain_question: When a small model fails a three-step programming task, is it choosing the wrong sequence of operations, or the right sequence with the wrong numbers plugged in?
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.plain_answer: The steps. Handed the correct sequence of operations, a cheap number-search finished every single task, so the numbers were never the bottleneck. Left alone, the model almost never even lands the right sequence, and crediting any-numbers-right adds nothing: its right-sequence rate exactly equals its solve rate. There is no hidden pool of right-recipe, wrong-number near-misses waiting to be unlocked.
qwen35_4b_structure_or_values.why_it_matters: Stop fixing deep multi-step failures with better number hints or value tuning; they will not move the wall. Instead supply the operation sequence from outside, via tools, enumeration, or a library of known structures, then let cheap number-fill finish.

key_numbers.label: Three-step tasks solved: model alone vs handed the correct steps
key_numbers.sub: the whole gap is picking operations, not numbers

key_numbers.label: Extra tasks unlocked by getting the numbers right
key_numbers.sub: right-sequence rate equals solve rate; no wrong-number near-misses

key_numbers.label: Three-step tasks cracked by 200 random sequences
key_numbers.sub: vs 100% with the true sequence, so the right structure genuinely matters

key_numbers.label: Best single first-try attempt, three-step tasks
key_numbers.sub: roughly 0.8% solved outright

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate stays near the floor until you hand over the correct steps
charts.chart_read: Four setups, each shown for two-step and three-step tasks; height is solve rate, taller is better. Left to right: single best attempt, best of eight attempts, best of eight credited if any numbers give the right sequence, and tasks solved when the true sequence is supplied.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first three bars sit near the floor and barely differ, so any-numbers credit adds nothing, then jump to full height once the correct steps are supplied: steps are the bottleneck.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even 200 random step-sequences barely crack the three-step tasks
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis: how many random operation sequences were tried (8, 50, 200), each filled by number-search. Vertical axis: solve rate, higher is better. Upper line is two-step tasks, lower line three-step.
charts.chart_takeaway: Two-step tasks climb to 60% with enough random tries, but three-step tasks reach only about 11%, versus 100% with the true sequence, so the correct structure is genuinely required.

qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.verdict_tag: Brute-force search wins wider as tasks deepen
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.concept_primer: Picture a lock whose combination is a chain of dials, each with sixteen positions. The model tries to recall the right sequence from memory. Brute force instead tries every combination on the real lock and keeps only the ones that actually open it.
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.plain_question: When checking every possible answer gets too expensive, does the model's built-in sense of program structure finally beat blindly trying everything?
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.plain_answer: No. Adding one extra dial — a sixteen-times-larger space of combinations — did not flip things. Exhaustive search stayed near-perfect at about 97 percent, while the model's knack for guessing the right combination from memory collapsed from 51 percent to 10 percent. The gap did not shrink toward a crossover; it widened, from 46 to 87 points. Structure fades faster than search slows.
qwen35_4b_structure_search_scaling.why_it_matters: If you can actually run candidate programs and check their output, spend compute on enumerate-and-test search plus a cheap output filter, not on training a small model to internalize program structure — that internal structure erodes as tasks get deeper.

key_numbers.label: Model guessed the program's shape right
key_numbers.sub: as tasks grow from three to four chained steps

key_numbers.label: Brute-force search solved rate
key_numbers.sub: 96.7% at four steps, 97.5% at three — near-perfect at both

key_numbers.label: How far the model trailed search
key_numbers.sub: the shortfall grows, not shrinks, with depth

key_numbers.label: Shapes left after a cheap output check
key_numbers.sub: an eight-value consistency filter prunes what search must actually run at four steps

charts.chart_plain_title: Model's shape-guessing collapses while brute-force search stays near-perfect
charts.chart_read: The bottom axis is composition depth, three then four chained steps. The vertical axis is percent correct; higher is better. The upper line is brute-force search's solved rate; the lower line is how often the model guessed the right program shape from memory.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two lines spread apart instead of meeting: the model drops from 51 to 10 percent while search holds near 97, so no crossover ever appears.

charts.chart_plain_title: Program shapes brute-force must check explode with each added step
charts.chart_read: The bottom axis is composition depth: three, four, five steps. The vertical axis counts program shapes to enumerate, sixteen raised to the depth — about four thousand, then sixty-five thousand, then a million. Fewer shapes favors search.
charts.chart_takeaway: The count multiplies sixteen-fold per step, only turning punishing near depth five — yet the model's structure has already collapsed at depth four, before search runs out of room.

qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.verdict_tag: Reusable code parts did not generalize
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.concept_primer: Think of a parts kit: instead of writing each hard coding problem from scratch, you try to build it by snapping together reusable code building blocks pulled from a library of past solutions.
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.plain_question: If you build hard coding problems from a library of reusable code parts, can you solve ones that a big batch of direct attempts couldn't?
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.plain_answer: No. Reusing and adapting past solutions solved none of the 9 hard tasks, beating nothing. Parts hand-built for each specific task did solve all 9, but those were custom-carved per task, not reusable, so the reuse idea failed.
qwen35_4b_substrate_coverage_ladder.why_it_matters: Do not expect a shared library of reused solutions to expand what a model can solve, and never trust the small visible sample of tests as a source of correct training targets; they routinely hide wrong answers.

key_numbers.sub: none — the reuse idea failed

key_numbers.label: Hard tasks solved by parts hand-built per task
key_numbers.sub: but custom-made each time, not reusable

key_numbers.label: Passed the visible sample checks yet secretly wrong
key_numbers.sub: of answers that looked right on shown tests

charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way of building solutions; bar height is the share of the 9 hard tasks fully solved, taller is better. Custom-built-per-task and the combined pool reach the top; reusing adapted past solutions sits flat at the bottom.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reuse bar is flat at zero while the custom-built bar reaches full height, so reusing past solutions solved nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often answers that looked right on sample tests were secretly wrong
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a building method; height is the share of answers that passed the small visible sample of tests but failed the full checks, so lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar is high, from 41% to 100%, meaning passing the visible sample tests routinely hides a wrong answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Answers that passed sample tests versus those that were truly correct
charts.chart_read: For each building method, the taller bar counts answers passing the visible sample tests and the shorter bar counts those that also passed the full checks; a big gap means many false wins.
charts.chart_takeaway: For reusing past solutions, 8 answers passed the sample tests but zero survived the full checks.

charts.chart_read: Each bar is a building method; height is the average number of candidate answers generated per task, so lower is cheaper.
charts.chart_takeaway: The expanded hand-built method reached full coverage at about 15 tries per task, far cheaper than the combined pool's roughly 57.

qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.verdict_tag: Nudges fix confident mistakes, not coin-flip forks
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.concept_primer: A model's thinking has forks where one next word led to success, another to failure. This method nudges it toward the winner — like a GPS blocking a wrong turn. But at forks where both roads look equally good, nagging just makes the driver hesitate and run low on fuel.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.plain_question: If you find the exact moments where a model's reasoning branches toward success versus failure, and nudge it toward the winning branch, does it get smarter?
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.plain_answer: No. Nudging at those forks made the model worse, not better — success on fresh tasks fell about 4 to 8 percent instead of clearing the 5-point gain hoped for. The tell: feeding it deliberately scrambled labels did nearly the same damage, so the harm came from the nudging procedure itself, not the success signal. The nudge only helps when the branch it discourages was already a runaway favorite.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round1.why_it_matters: Before training a model to prefer some thinking-tokens over others, confirm the token you push down is already its runaway favorite — a clear misstep, not a near-tie. At coin-flip forks the nudge just disrupts reasoning and burns the thinking budget.

key_numbers.label: Success after steering
key_numbers.sub: single best-answer success on fresh tasks, shorter thinking budget; a gain was the goal

key_numbers.label: Damage from scrambled labels
key_numbers.sub: shuffling which branch was a success lost almost as much, so the signal wasn't the cause

key_numbers.label: Thinking finishes on its own
key_numbers.sub: how often reasoning reaches a natural end within budget, longer budget — roughly halved

key_numbers.label: Headroom left unconverted
key_numbers.sub: single-shot success vs how often at least one of eight tries was right, same tasks

charts.chart_plain_title: Steering and scrambled-label training both lag the untouched model
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show single best-answer success for three models — untouched base, steering-trained, and a scrambled-label control — with two bars each for a shorter and a longer thinking budget. Taller is better; base is tallest in both.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both trained models sit below base by similar margins, and the scrambled control tracks the steering model — so the training procedure, not the success signal, caused the drop.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the model finishes its thinking on its own
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each model reaches a natural end of its reasoning within the longer thinking budget, for untouched base, steering-trained, and scrambled control. Higher is better; both trained bars are far shorter than base.
charts.chart_takeaway: Base finishes on its own more than twice as often as either trained model — identically whether labels were real or scrambled — pinpointing disrupted reasoning flow as the damage.

qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.verdict_tag: A one-word nudge shifts the whole model
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.concept_primer: The goal was to fix the model's word choice at one confident wrong turn in its reasoning. But it's like retuning a single guitar string through a peg geared to all six: you can't shift that one note without detuning the whole chord.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.plain_question: Can you make a small reasoning model smarter by gently editing the exact moments where it confidently takes a wrong turn in its thinking?
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.plain_answer: No. Gently pulling the better word up at 155 hand-picked wrong-turn moments was cleaner than shoving the bad word down, and it carried real signal, beating a scrambled-label control by about 14 points on fresh coding repairs. But it still lost to the untouched model, 54% versus 60% of repositories fixed, and the nudge leaked into unrelated words well past the safety limit.
qwen35_4b_think_ftpo_round2.why_it_matters: Before trusting a targeted fine-tune to fix specific reasoning mistakes, measure how far it moves unrelated word-scores, not just its target, and always run a scrambled-label control. If drift clears your locality budget, more examples won't rescue it; shrink the dose.

key_numbers.label: Best edit vs untouched model on coding repairs

charts.chart_plain_title: Pulling a word up spills less than pushing one down, still too much
charts.chart_read: Three training styles along the bottom: push the wrong word down, pull the right word up, and pull up with scrambled labels. Bar height is how far unrelated word-scores drifted; lower is better, and a safety line marks the allowed spillover.
charts.chart_takeaway: Pull-up's bar is about a third shorter than push-down's, yet all three, even scrambled labels, rise above the safety line, so no method stayed local.

charts.chart_plain_title: The untouched deep agent still fixes the most repositories
charts.chart_read: Five agent versions along the bottom; bar height is the share of repository repairs passing hidden tests, higher is better: untouched model over eight serial turns, push down, pull up, pull up with scrambled labels, and the untouched model split into two shorter tries.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untouched eight-turn agent leads near 60%; the best edit reaches 54%, and splitting the calls into two short tries collapses to 31%, so long feedback loops mattered most.

charts.chart_plain_title: Real labels help on some tests but flip on others
charts.chart_read: Four evaluations along the bottom; bar height is the real pull-up minus the scrambled pull-up, so above zero means real labels helped: shorter and longer fresh reasoning, the practice gym, and the coding agent.
charts.chart_takeaway: Real labels beat scrambled by up to 14 points on coding and the practice gym, but dip negative at the longer reasoning budget, evidence of signal rather than a reliable gain.

qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.verdict_tag: A cheap recheck cuts thinking cost, not accuracy
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.concept_primer: Like a mechanic who tries a quick fix, takes one test drive, and only opens up the engine if the car still won't start. Here the quick fix is a fast draft answer, the test drive is the single example check shown in the task, and opening the engine is extra thinking.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.plain_question: When a model can choose how long to think on each task, can a simple rule spend that effort more wisely than always thinking a fixed amount?
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.plain_answer: For cost, yes; for accuracy, no. Drafting cheaply and thinking harder only when the draft fails the one visible example matches a generous fixed budget's accuracy (88%) using about a fifth of the thinking (113 versus 507 tokens per task). But it never beats the best fixed budget (91%), because that quick check passes a wrong answer about 1 in 10 times.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_controller.why_it_matters: Treat the thinking budget as an efficiency dial, not an accuracy dial: draft cheaply and spend more thinking only when a quick check fails. To actually raise accuracy, you need a commit signal better than a single example test.

key_numbers.label: Thinking spent per task
key_numbers.sub: best fixed budget vs the cheap draft-then-recheck rule, at near-equal accuracy

key_numbers.label: Accuracy: cheap rule vs best fixed budget
key_numbers.sub: about 3 points given up for a large cost cut

key_numbers.label: Quick check passes a wrong answer
key_numbers.sub: the visible example passes but the hidden tests still fail

key_numbers.label: Best-possible per-task ceiling
key_numbers.sub: a non-deployable chooser that knows the ideal thinking budget for each task

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy versus thinking spent: recheck rule versus fixed budgets
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is average thinking tokens per task (further left is cheaper); the vertical axis is the share of tasks fully solved (higher is better). One line traces fixed budgets, another the recheck rule, and a lone dot marks the best-possible ceiling. Up-and-left wins.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recheck line sits far left of the fixed line at the same heights, so equal accuracy for a fraction of the thinking, but stops just below the fixed peak; the ceiling dot floats highest.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy by strategy, from no thinking to the ideal ceiling
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one strategy's share of tasks fully solved, taller is better, running left to right from no thinking through two fixed budgets and the two recheck rules to the non-deployable ideal ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recheck rules land at 88 to 89%, wedged between cheaper fixed budgets and the 93% ceiling; only the best fixed budget, at 91%, tops them.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often a passed quick check still hides a wrong answer
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows the share of tasks a strategy commits to because the visible example passed, yet which fail the hidden tests; lower is better. Bars run from the best fixed budget to leaner, cheaper recheck rules.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rate climbs from 6% for the fixed budget to 11% for the leanest rule, so trusting thinner drafts leaks more errors, and this gap caps accuracy below the ceiling.

qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.verdict_tag: Capped thinking beats both silence and endless rambling
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.concept_primer: Before answering a coding task, this small model can fill a private scratchpad, working the logic out. With none, it just guesses. Given a page or two, it reasons and solves more. Given an unlimited pad, it talks itself out of the right answer.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.plain_question: If you let a small coding model reason to itself before answering, does it actually solve more problems, and is more thinking always better?
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.plain_answer: Yes, but with a sweet spot. Turning on the model's built-in reasoning lifted the accuracy of the answer you would actually ship from 76% to 91% on basic Python tasks — a genuine capability gain, not just a wider pool of guesses to pick from. But more is not better: accuracy peaks around a few hundred to a thousand reasoning tokens, then falls. Unlimited thinking scored only 84%, worse than a firm cap.
qwen35_4b_thinking_budget_scaling.why_it_matters: Turn on native thinking mode for small coding models — it is real capability many setups leave switched off. But cap the thinking length: unlimited reasoning overthinks and loses accuracy. Sweep the budget once; the peak sat near a thousand tokens here.

key_numbers.label: Shipped-answer solve rate, thinking off vs capped on
key_numbers.sub: single best answer on 100 basic Python tasks

key_numbers.label: Unlimited thinking vs a firm cap
key_numbers.sub: free-running thinking scored below a mid-size budget

key_numbers.label: Best-of-eight ceiling barely moved
key_numbers.sub: only +5 points, versus +15 for the shipped answer

key_numbers.label: Gain on middling tasks
key_numbers.sub: 40 tasks the model half-solved before; +23 points, the robust slice

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rate by thinking budget: a mid-size cap wins
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped along the bottom by thinking budget — none, then 256, 512, 1024 and 2048-token caps, then unlimited. One color is the single shipped answer's solve rate, the other the best-of-eight ceiling. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Shipped-answer bars climb from 76% to a 91% peak at the 1024-token cap, then fall to 84% unlimited — a little thinking helps, too much hurts.

charts.chart_plain_title: The rise-then-fall shape holds across every accuracy measure
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is the thinking-token cap, from zero (off) to unlimited; vertical axis is solve rate, higher is better. Three lines: the single shipped answer, one random sampled attempt, and the answer picked by the one visible example test.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines rise into a 512-to-1024 cap then flatten or dip; the shared hump shows overthinking is a real cost, not one metric's noise.

charts.chart_plain_title: Thinking helps the hardest tasks the most
charts.chart_read: Tasks split into three difficulty groups along the bottom — already easy (51 tasks), middling (40), and never solved without thinking (9). Paired bars compare no thinking against a fixed 1024-token budget; taller shipped-answer solve rate is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The gap widens with difficulty: +6 points on easy, +23 on middling, and a third of never-solved tasks (3 of 9) become solvable.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does scrambling the reasoning erase the gain?
charts.chart_read: Two thinking budgets along the bottom (512 and 2048 tokens); three bars each — no thinking, the model's own thinking with word order scrambled, and its real thinking. Taller shipped-answer solve rate is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Scrambled thinking recovers much of the lift here, hinting scaffold and compute matter — but the authors flag this as their shakiest result, likely understating real reasoning.

qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.verdict_tag: Only real reasoning helps, wrong reasoning wrecks answers
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.concept_primer: Treat the model's written thinking as a recipe it cooks from, not a warm-up that merely revs its engine. A blank card, or the ingredients listed in scrambled order, changes the meal not at all; a clear recipe helps; another dish's recipe cooks the wrong meal.
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.plain_question: When a small model writes out its reasoning before answering, does that help because it truly reasons, or just because generating extra text warms up its computation?
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.plain_answer: It genuinely reasons; the boost is content, not compute. Blank filler of the same length, and the real thinking scrambled into nonsense, both scored like skipping thinking entirely, around 74 to 75 percent. Only coherent thinking lifted accuracy to 86 percent. And splicing in another task's reasoning crashed it to 4 percent, because the model solves whatever problem its notes describe.
qwen35_4b_thinking_content_vs_compute.why_it_matters: Don't add reasoning tokens just to buy compute; padding and scrambled notes gain nothing here. Guard the scratchpad: a routing or retrieval bug that injects stale or off-topic reasoning is catastrophic, not harmless filler, because the model faithfully follows it.
