key_numbers.label: Same length of blank or scrambled thinking
key_numbers.sub: both stay right at the 75% no-thinking baseline

key_numbers.label: Fed another task's reasoning
key_numbers.sub: a 71-point collapse; it solves the wrong problem

key_numbers.label: Share of the gain from coherent reasoning
key_numbers.sub: not compute, not scaffold, not token presence

charts.chart_plain_title: Coding pass rate for five kinds of thinking
charts.chart_read: Five bars, one per thinking condition along the bottom (no thinking, filler dots, shuffled thinking, real thinking, another task's thinking), all matched to the same thinking length. Height is the share of Python tasks fully passed across eight tries; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first three bars sit together near three-quarters; only real thinking rises to about 86 percent, while foreign thinking collapses to a 4 percent stub, so content drives the result, not length.

charts.chart_plain_title: What each part of thinking actually adds to the score
charts.chart_read: Four bars, each the change in pass rate from adding one ingredient in turn: extra compute, having relevant words present, putting them in coherent order, and swapping in wrong-task content. Bars above zero help; bars below zero hurt.
charts.chart_takeaway: Extra compute and scrambled words hover at zero; coherent order alone lifts about 12 points, the entire gain, while wrong content plunges 71 points below baseline, toxic rather than inert.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coherent thinking's edge grows with a bigger thinking budget
charts.chart_read: The line tracks how much coherent thinking beats the same words scrambled (vertical) as the thinking-token budget rises left to right across three settings, from about 500 to 2000 tokens. Higher means coherence matters more.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line climbs from roughly 10 to 15 points, so more room to think widens coherence's advantage rather than diluting real reasoning into mere compute.

qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.verdict_tag: Thinking sharpens recognition but cannot plan ahead
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.concept_primer: Picture a chess puzzle. One move from checkmate, extra thinking time reliably helps you spot the winning move. But asked to name the first move of a forced three-move mate, with no board to picture the middle positions, thinking out loud simply doesn't help.
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.plain_question: If you let a small model think out loud at length before answering, can it learn to plan several steps ahead toward a goal on its own?
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.plain_answer: No. Asked to name the first of three moves toward a goal, the model stayed stuck at pure guessing — right about 1 time in 32 — no matter how long it thought, even with 2,048 tokens to think first. Yet recognizing a goal just one move away leapt from roughly 28% to 60% as thinking grew. So extra thinking amplifies spotting a near answer, not planning a distant one.
qwen35_4b_thinking_lookahead.why_it_matters: If your system needs genuine multi-step planning, a longer chain of thought won't manufacture it — here only training installed that ability. Spend thinking budget on recognition-style subtasks where the intermediate state is visible, and measure planning and recognition separately.

key_numbers.label: Planning the first of three moves
key_numbers.sub: next-move accuracy, no thinking vs longest scratchpad — not a real change, both near chance

key_numbers.label: Chance level
key_numbers.sub: guessing among the 32 possible moves

key_numbers.label: Recognizing a goal one move away
key_numbers.sub: next-move accuracy as thinking budget grows

key_numbers.label: What training did on that same first move
key_numbers.sub: training the model, not thinking, is what lifts planning

charts.chart_plain_title: Thinking helps only when the goal is one move away
charts.chart_read: Three groups along the bottom mark how far the goal sits: three moves away (real planning), two away, and one away (recognition). Within each, bars are no thinking, medium, and large scratchpad. Height is next-move accuracy; taller is better, about 3% is chance.
charts.chart_takeaway: The one-away group climbs steeply toward 60% as thinking grows, while the three-away planning group stays flat near chance — thinking boosts recognition, not planning.

charts.chart_plain_title: First-move planning stays at chance as thinking grows
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is scratchpad size (0, 1,024, 2,048 tokens); the vertical axis is accuracy at picking the correct first move. One line tracks the model; a flat reference line marks the 1-in-32 chance rate. Rising well above it would be better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line barely lifts from about 3% to 8% and hugs the chance line at every budget — more thinking buys essentially no planning ability.

qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.verdict_tag: Scrambled thinking reads correctness as well as real
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.concept_primer: Picture clipping a meter onto the model's inner wiring: from a single snapshot taken the instant it finishes writing code, the meter guesses whether that code will actually run. The test is whether making the model think first makes that reading sharper.
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.plain_question: After a small model writes code, does its own internal state reveal whether that code is correct, and does making it think first make the signal clearer?
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.plain_answer: Yes, but not for the reason you would expect. From one snapshot of internal activity, whether the model's own code is correct is readable well above a coin-flip, about 64 to 76 percent of the time. Thinking first sharpens that signal. But scrambling the thinking words into gibberish sharpens it just as much, so the boost comes from the act of thinking, not its logic.
qwen35_4b_thinking_separability_probe.why_it_matters: If you plan to filter a small model's outputs by reading its internal confidence, the signal is real but weak: it appears only after the model generates thinking tokens, and it is never strong enough to trust as a standalone judge.

charts.chart_takeaway: Every thinking bar rises above no-thinking, and the two scrambled-thinking bars are the tallest of all, so content-free thinking reads correctness best.

charts.chart_takeaway: At nearly every layer the four thinking lines sit above no-thinking, and the scrambled lines match or top the real ones, so the ordering holds throughout the network.

charts.chart_read: Five conditions along the bottom; bar height is how well the probe ranks truly-correct code above code that passes a quick visible test but fails hidden ones. Higher is better; 50 percent means no better than guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: The no-thinking bar sits near 52 percent and is useless; thinking bars reach 60 to 68 percent, a weak but real filter that appears only after the model has thought.

qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.concept_primer: This small model writes little programs by chaining list operations. It normally improves by restudying solutions it found itself — but it almost never discovers three-operation chains, so none enter its study pile. Here a plain brute-force search supplies those missing three-chain answers for it to copy.
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.plain_question: If a small model never discovers a three-step program on its own, can a mechanical search find those solutions and retraining teach it to produce them?
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.plain_answer: Partly. A brute-force search found working three-step programs the model never produces itself, and retraining on them lifted three-step success from a hard zero to 5 of 40 fresh, never-seen tasks when it can reason across sixteen tries — a real, significant crossing. But in one shot, the production setting, it still solved none, versus 15% on two-step tasks. The knowledge crossed the wall, yet barely stuck in the weights.
qwen35_4b_tool_seeded_banking.why_it_matters: To push a small model one composition-step deeper, use an external search to supply examples it cannot generate itself — but expect the new skill only with test-time reasoning, not one-shot production calls. Seed each depth separately; deeper installs weaker.

key_numbers.label: Three-step success, reasoning across sixteen tries
key_numbers.sub: novel unseen tasks unlocked, up from a hard zero

key_numbers.label: Three-step success, single best shot
key_numbers.sub: the skill never installed for one-shot deployment

key_numbers.label: Two-step success, reasoning across sixteen tries
key_numbers.sub: shallower chains install far more strongly

key_numbers.label: Hard programs the search found
key_numbers.sub: three-step tasks brute-forced on CPU, no model involved

charts.chart_plain_title: Where the tool-seeded training helps and where it doesn't
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by program depth — two, three, and four chained operations. Within each group: the untrained model's success across sixteen tries, the retrained model's success across sixteen tries, and the retrained model's single-shot production success. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At three operations the retrained multi-try bar jumps off zero while the untrained bar stays flat — but its single-shot bar is still zero, and four operations never moves. Knowledge crossed, not deployable.

charts.chart_plain_title: Three-step success climbs with more tries but stays low
charts.chart_read: Share of three-step tasks solved (vertical) as allowed tries rise from one to sixteen (horizontal). Flat bottom line: the untrained model, pinned at zero. Middle line: the retrained model. Top line: that same retrained model on easier two-step tasks. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The retrained three-step line climbs steadily off zero to 5 of 40 while the untrained line stays flat at zero — real learning, but far below the two-step line's 42%.

qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.verdict_tag: Two cheap checks beat a fine-tuned model
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.concept_primer: For each table puzzle the model gives two answers: a quick guess, and a small program it writes and test-runs on one example whose answer is known. A referee commits just one, trusting the program only when it passes that test and disagrees with the guess.
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.plain_question: When a small model can either answer a table task directly or run a program it wrote, what is the best way to decide which answer to submit?
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.plain_answer: Surprisingly, yes, and with almost no learning. Trusting the program only when it passes a worked example and disagrees with the quick answer lifted accuracy from 56% to 66%, exactly matching the best any picker could reach, and never overwriting a correct answer. Fine-tuning learned most of the signal but landed lower, at 64%. Letting the model commit its own program every time collapsed to 24%, because programs that pass the example are often still secretly wrong.
qwen35_4b_tool_state_policy_lora.why_it_matters: Before fine-tuning a selector, try cheap tool signals: does the generated program pass its worked example, and does it disagree with the direct answer? Those two checks captured every recoverable win here. Never let a small model commit its own code unchecked.

charts.chart_takeaway: The two-check rule reaches the perfect-hindsight ceiling, while committing the program blindly sits lowest, below even always answering directly. Knowing when to override beats raw effort.

charts.chart_takeaway: The two-check rule and fine-tuned policy rescue about 5 tasks with zero overwrites, while committing the program blindly destroys 10 to 21 correct answers.

charts.chart_read: Two training-loss curves over 80 steps: fitting the real pick-the-answer labels versus randomly shuffled labels. Lower loss means the model found a pattern; the shuffled line is the cannot-learn-noise baseline.
charts.chart_takeaway: Real-label loss plunges toward zero while shuffled-label loss stays stuck high, confirming the tool state carries a genuine choose signal, not memorized noise.

qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.verdict_tag: Trained code fixer loses to simply retrying
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.concept_primer: Imagine a student who gets a problem wrong. You could coach them to patch up their own broken answer, or you could just let them attempt the problem fresh a few more times. Which approach recovers more problems?
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.plain_question: When a model's first tries at a small programming puzzle all fail, does training it to fix its own broken code recover more puzzles than just trying again?
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.plain_answer: No. Across 150 programming puzzles, 57 had no working solution after four fresh tries. A fixer trained to repair broken code recovered just 2 of those, and misfired more often. An untrained fixer recovered 3, but spending the same effort on more fresh tries recovered the most: 5.
qwen35_4b_trained_vs_frozen_repair_mdp.why_it_matters: When first attempts fail, spend extra compute on more diverse fresh attempts rather than on training a specialized code fixer. A trained repair step can produce code that passes the visible checks but quietly fails the hidden ones.

charts.chart_takeaway: The more-fresh-attempts bar is tallest and the trained fixer is shortest, so retrying beat fixing and training the fixer made it worse.

qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.verdict_tag: Light tuning picks every right recipe
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.concept_primer: Think of a small library of data-cleanup recipes. For each task the model is handed a menu of candidate recipes and must pick the one whose output is correct; a machine then runs the chosen recipe exactly.
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.plain_question: Can a little tuning teach the model to pick the correct data-transformation recipe from a menu of candidates?
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.plain_answer: Yes. After a light round of tuning the model produced the correct output on 100% of the 48 tasks, up from 92% untuned and matching a perfect answer key. Simple example-matching rules already reached about 90%, so the menu was fairly easy.
qwen35_4b_transform_abi_compiler_pilot.why_it_matters: When answers can be run and checked, a small amount of tuning can push a fixed model to reliably choose the exact right procedure. But easy candidate menus flatter the score, so retest on less-templated tasks.

qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.verdict_tag: Fill-in-the-blank templates beat writing whole fixes
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.concept_primer: Instead of writing a whole program fix in one go, the model writes a fill-in-the-blank template, and a separate checker tries every valid way to fill the blanks, keeping fills that pass the visible tests.
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.plain_question: Does letting the model sketch a fill-in-the-blank template, then have a checker complete it, fix broken programs better than writing the whole fix directly?
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.plain_answer: Yes. On the hardest problems, having the model write a fill-in-the-blank template and letting a checker try valid fills raised the pass rate from 33% to 88%, far beyond the model writing the whole fix itself.
qwen35_4b_typed_sketch_synthesis.why_it_matters: When a small model struggles to write deep, correct code in one shot, have it sketch the overall structure and let an automated checker fill and test the fiddly details.

charts.chart_plain_title: Passing unseen tests: writing the whole fix vs filling a template
charts.chart_read: Bars group by difficulty tier (easy, medium, hardest); height is the share of unseen tests passed, taller is better. Each tier shows writing the whole fix directly, filling a template, the two combined, and the best-possible pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the hardest tier the template bars tower over the short direct bar, and the combined approach reaches 88% versus 33% for writing the fix directly.

charts.chart_plain_title: Hardest problem types solved: writing the fix directly vs filling a template
charts.chart_read: One row of bars per problem type, twelve problems each; height is how many were solved, taller is better. Two bars per type compare writing the whole fix directly against filling in a template.
charts.chart_takeaway: The template bar clears whole problem types the direct bar misses entirely, though direct still edges ahead on a couple of text-matching types.

qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.verdict_tag: Even practice beat targeting weak spots
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.concept_primer: Picture a fixed study budget of practice problems. You can spread them evenly across every topic, or cram only the topics you currently flunk. This experiment tests which spending plan trains the model better.
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.plain_question: When you can only add a fixed batch of extra practice problems, is it better to target the model's weak spots than to spread practice evenly?
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.plain_answer: No. Spreading the extra practice evenly across all task types worked best, solving 98% of unseen tasks, while concentrating practice on the model's current mistakes reached only about 85% and left some task types unsolved.
qwen35_4b_unsaturated_frontier_active_br.why_it_matters: When your training budget is fixed and the task space is broad, spread practice evenly rather than chasing the model's current mistakes. Targeting fixes a few weak spots but quietly starves others.

key_numbers.label: Evenly-spread practice
key_numbers.sub: unseen tasks solved, versus no extra practice

key_numbers.label: Cramming the mistakes
key_numbers.sub: best either targeting method reached

key_numbers.label: Worst blind spot from targeting
key_numbers.sub: one task type left completely unsolved

charts.chart_plain_title: Which practice-example strategy fixes the most broken programs
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by four training recipes (left to right: no extra practice, evenly-spread practice, two ways of cramming the model's own mistakes). Height is the share of tasks it gets fully right on unseen inputs; taller is better. Two bars per group: one single try versus the best of three tries.
charts.chart_takeaway: The evenly-spread bar towers near the top while both cram-the-mistakes bars sit well below it, so spreading practice broadly beat targeting weak spots.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much the shown worked-out steps actually help
charts.chart_read: Three bars for the same trained model, differing only in what worked-out solution steps it is shown alongside the broken program: correct steps, no steps, or deliberately jumbled steps. Height is the share of tasks solved correctly; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy falls sharply when the steps are removed and collapses when they are jumbled, showing the model genuinely reads and relies on them rather than ignoring them.

charts.chart_plain_title: On the hardest task types, targeted practice fixes some but abandons others
charts.chart_read: Five hardest task types along the bottom, with four colored bars each for the training recipes (no extra practice, evenly-spread, and two cram-the-mistakes versions). Height is share solved on unseen inputs; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The evenly-spread bars stay tall across every type, while the cram-the-mistakes bars swing to zero on at least one type, exposing the blind spots targeting creates.

qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.verdict_tag: Matched past solutions recover missed problems
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.concept_primer: The model usually cracks a coding task by trying many times. For the ones it keeps missing, we hand it code from a similar problem it already solved, like adapting a proven recipe for a new dish.
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.plain_question: When the model keeps failing certain coding problems, does handing it proven solutions to similar problems help it finally solve them?
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.plain_answer: Yes. Giving the model a closely-matched past solution to adapt cracked 3 of the 8 problems that repeated tries kept missing, lifting overall success from 67% to 79%; unrelated solutions helped none.
qwen35_4b_verified_algorithm_retrieval_a.why_it_matters: Relevant past solutions are a cheap boost for stubborn problems, but the model often looks right on the shown examples while failing hidden ones, so add a checker before trusting an answer.

key_numbers.label: Problems solved overall
key_numbers.sub: with a matched past solution to adapt

key_numbers.label: Stuck problems newly cracked
key_numbers.sub: matched solutions vs 0 for unrelated ones

key_numbers.label: Looked right but were wrong
key_numbers.sub: passed shown examples yet failed hidden checks

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of problems solved: trying more alone vs adding matched solutions
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different way of supplying candidate solutions; height is the share of 24 problems solved, so taller is better. The leftmost bar is trying more alone; the others add various retrieved solutions.
charts.chart_takeaway: The matched-solution bar rises highest, from 67% to 79%, while the unrelated-solution bar stays flat at 67%, so the content of the retrieved solution matters.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stuck problems newly cracked by each type of retrieved solution
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one type of retrieved solution; height is how many of the 8 problems that trying-more kept missing were newly solved, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Matched solutions crack 3 stuck problems versus 0 for unrelated ones, showing the match, not just more attempts, does the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Answers that looked right on shown examples but failed hidden checks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one type of retrieved solution; height is the share of answers that passed the shown examples yet still failed the hidden checks, so lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar is tall, from 57% up to 100%, meaning passing the shown examples alone is not safe enough to trust an answer.

qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.verdict_tag: Trying tiny fixes beats retraining the model
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.concept_primer: A small model's wrong program is usually one slip from correct: a swapped operation, a missing "not." Picture a spell-checker that only knows single-edit fixes. It generates every nearby variant, runs each against the example cases, and keeps whichever one now passes.
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.plain_question: When a small model writes a program that is almost right but fails, can automatically trying tiny edited versions and testing them fix more than just asking again?
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.plain_answer: Yes, on the hardest unseen tasks. Testing small edits of the model's own near-miss program and keeping whichever passes the example cases raised fully-correct answers from 39% to 52%, while re-sampling the model and re-ranking its guesses gave nothing over its first try. But it stalled near 58%: some wrong programs need a rewrite, not a tweak.
qwen35_4b_verified_edit_closure.why_it_matters: For small-model code repair, wrap generation in a cheap edit-and-run loop before spending on more training. Accept a fix only when it passes every example the model missed, which halves the edits that break working code.

key_numbers.label: Hard unseen tasks fully repaired
key_numbers.sub: re-sampling the model vs testing small edits of its answer, across 120 tasks

key_numbers.label: Best any edit could reach
key_numbers.sub: ceiling if the perfect edit were always picked

key_numbers.label: Re-sampling the model's own guesses
key_numbers.sub: 40% first answer vs 39% re-ranked, within noise

key_numbers.label: Programs the edits made worse
key_numbers.sub: halved by accepting only fixes that pass every example

charts.chart_plain_title: How each repair method scores on the hardest unseen tasks
charts.chart_read: Five bars, one per repair method; height is the share of 120 hard unseen tasks fully solved on the hidden tests. Left to right: single guess, re-ranking guesses, edit testing, strict edit testing, best-possible edit. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both edit-testing bars jump from about 39% to roughly 52%, nearly reaching the 58% best-possible bar: verified edits, not more sampling, carry the gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task families the edits rescue, and which stay stuck
charts.chart_read: Six task families across the bottom, three bars each, counting fully-solved tasks out of 12: re-ranking guesses, edit testing, and best-possible edit. Higher is better; families are ordered by how much editing helped.
charts.chart_takeaway: Editing lifts one family from zero to nine solved, but two families stay flat at zero even for the best-possible edit: no such fix exists in the menu.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stricter acceptance trades some wins for far less breakage
charts.chart_read: Three acceptance rules along the bottom; paired bars count tasks the edits improved versus damaged, out of 120. More improved is good, fewer damaged is good. The strictest rule, accepting only fixes that pass every example, sits on the right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Moving to strict acceptance drops improved tasks from 49 to 33 but halves damaged tasks from 8 to 4: the safer setting to deploy.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.verdict_tag: Fixing the memory crunch just revealed endless looping
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.concept_primer: A server holds each active prompt's growing memory in a fixed pool of fast storage — a parking garage with set spaces. Raising the context limit lets each car park taller but adds no spaces; admit more full prompts than fit, and cars get towed and re-parked, wasting work.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.plain_question: If you give a small model a huge context and a huge thinking budget, is that enough to make it actually reason over long inputs?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.plain_answer: No. Two hidden limits bit first. Configuring the server for 64 simultaneous long prompts demanded about 2.4 million tokens of fast memory from a pool holding roughly 1 million, forcing constant re-reading. Cutting to 19 prompts fixed that overflow — but the model then looped instead of finishing: 37 of 48 attempts fell into exact repeating cycles, and none stopped on their own.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_capacity_fit_re.why_it_matters: Before serving long-context batches, don't just raise the length limit. Measure your live fast-memory pool and cap concurrent prompts so worst-case demand fits with room to spare. And separately, guard against models that loop forever instead of stopping.

key_numbers.label: Simultaneous long prompts allowed
key_numbers.sub: cut so worst-case memory fit the fixed cache

key_numbers.label: Worst-case memory demand
key_numbers.sub: against a cache holding about 1 million tokens

key_numbers.label: Runs that ended by looping
key_numbers.sub: exact repeated-token loops; the gate allows at most 1 in 4

key_numbers.label: Runs that stopped on their own
key_numbers.sub: every sample instead ran into the length limit

charts.chart_plain_title: Fitting fewer prompts brings memory demand under the cache limit
charts.chart_read: Two setups on the horizontal axis: the over-committed 64-prompt config and the fitted 19-prompt config. For each, one bar is worst-case memory demand and the other is memory actually available, in cache tokens; demand below available is safe, and lower demand is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At 64 prompts, demand towers to about 2.4 million against a roughly 1-million cache — guaranteed overflow and re-reading. At 19, demand drops just under the cache.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even with memory fixed, every stop-quality check failed
charts.chart_read: Three failure types along the horizontal axis: prompts that never resolved, prompts stuck repeating themselves, and prompts that maxed out the answer length. Each shows its observed share of the 48 samples beside its allowed ceiling; staying below the ceiling passes.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every observed bar overshoots its ceiling — repeating loops hit about 77% against a 25% cap — so the model kept rambling instead of finishing, and nothing could be scored.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.verdict_tag: More thinking room only deepened the loops
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.concept_primer: Picture the model working through a problem on scratch paper before it answers. On these tasks it kept rewriting the same few lines instead of concluding. Hand it a bigger pad and it just fills more pages with the identical loop.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.plain_question: If a small model runs out of room to think before answering, does giving it more room help it finish, or does it just keep looping?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.plain_answer: No, it just loops harder. At both budgets, all 48 reasoning attempts ran out of thinking room and had to be force-stopped, none finishing on its own. Widening the budget from 49,152 to 61,440 tokens pushed the share stuck endlessly repeating the same lines from 79% up to 83%. More space feeds the loop rather than breaking it.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_exact_cudagraph.why_it_matters: Before enlarging a small reasoning model's thinking budget, measure how often its runs end by repeating themselves. If they loop, more budget just burns compute; the real fix is anti-repetition decoding or training, not more room.

key_numbers.label: Attempts that finished thinking on their own
key_numbers.sub: every sample hit the reasoning limit and was force-stopped, at each budget

key_numbers.label: Stuck endlessly repeating the same lines
key_numbers.sub: share of samples in exact loops as the budget rose from 49,152 to 61,440 tokens

key_numbers.label: Loop rate still allowed to pass
key_numbers.sub: registered ceiling; the actual rate ran past three times higher

key_numbers.label: Speed gain from the engineering fix
key_numbers.sub: pre-recording the exact GPU batch shapes vs letting the engine guess them

charts.chart_plain_title: Spelling out batch sizes covered every width the model used
charts.chart_read: Four inference setups along the bottom: two thinking budgets, each run once with the engine guessing fast-path batch sizes and once with them spelled out. Per setup, one bar is the width actually used, the other the largest width pre-built; matching them is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Guessing pre-built only up to widths 16 and 8, below the 19 and 15 actually used; spelling them out matched exactly, closing the slow uncovered gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: More thinking room raised the share of looping runs
charts.chart_read: Two thinking budgets side by side, each over 48 attempts. Three colored shares per budget: attempts stuck repeating a block, attempts hitting the limit for other reasons, and attempts running out of final-answer space. Lower is better on all three.
charts.chart_takeaway: Moving to the bigger budget nudged the looping share up from 79% to 83% — extra room deepened the loop instead of resolving it.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.verdict_tag: Perfect shortcut syntax masked wrong rewrites
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.concept_primer: A "shortcut" here is a named stand-in for a fixed recipe of basic steps — like writing "make a roux" instead of its five exact stirring instructions. It counts as right only if spelling it back out reproduces those exact steps, unchanged.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.plain_question: If you hand a small model ready-made shortcuts and the exact step-by-step plan, can it simply re-write that plan using the right shortcuts?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.plain_answer: No. Even handed the finished plan and asked only to re-express it — no problem to actually solve — the model got it exactly right just one time in four, short of the three-in-four bar. Every output looked flawless: valid syntax, a shortcut always called, nothing cut off. It named the right first shortcut, then invented extra ones that quietly expanded into different steps.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_invention.why_it_matters: When a small model drives a tool or shortcut interface, never treat clean formatting and high shortcut-usage rates as passing. Verify that every call, expanded literally, reproduces the intended steps — score meaning, not surface.

key_numbers.label: Rewrites that matched the plan exactly
key_numbers.sub: the gate needed at least 3 of 4 to continue

key_numbers.label: Outputs that were cleanly formatted and used a shortcut
key_numbers.sub: with deliberate thinking turned off, every sample parsed and called a shortcut

key_numbers.label: Failed rewrites that kept the right shortcut but added wrong ones
key_numbers.sub: correct leading shortcut, then extra shortcuts that meant something else

key_numbers.label: How far the wrong rewrites over-expanded
key_numbers.sub: the plan had exactly five steps; every wrong answer inflated past that

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct rewrites never reached the bar needed to continue
charts.chart_read: Two bar groups, one per attempt: thinking budgeted to 768 tokens, then thinking turned off. The tall bar counts how many of four test cases were rewritten exactly right; the flat reference marks the three needed to pass. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both attempts fell short — two, then one, of four — so the run halted at the interface check before the real shortcut question was ever tested.

charts.chart_plain_title: Turning off thinking fixed formatting but not correctness
charts.chart_read: Two groups over sixteen samples each: thinking at 768 tokens, then thinking off. One bar counts cleanly parsed samples, the other counts samples cut off mid-answer. Thinking off pushed clean parses to all sixteen and cut-offs to zero. More parsed, fewer cut off is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Turning thinking off made every sample parse cleanly and eliminated all cut-offs, yet exact expansion still failed on thirteen of sixteen — clean output was not correct output.

qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.verdict_tag: More thinking room only bought longer loops
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.concept_primer: Same model, two jobs on identical coding puzzles. Copy out a blueprint it is handed: quick, done. Or reverse-engineer that blueprint from only a photo of the finished part: it cannot, so it circles the same guess forever. A bigger budget is a longer track, not a finish line.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.plain_question: If a small model keeps rambling and never finishes a hard coding puzzle, can you fix it just by handing it far more room to think?
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.plain_answer: No. The model was not short on thinking space, it was stuck repeating itself, and more space only bought longer loops. Handed the blueprint, all 16 tasks finished cleanly. Asked to invent the program from examples alone, every attempt ran off the end of its allowance at every budget, and pure repetition climbed from 9 percent of traces to 83 percent as the room grew.
qwen35_4b_verified_macro_long_context_re.why_it_matters: Calibrate a thinking budget on the exact task you will ship, not a look-alike: copying a plan and inventing one behave nothing alike. When a model loops, add loop detection and hard stops rather than buying more tokens.

key_numbers.label: Invent-from-examples tries that never stopped
key_numbers.sub: 131 of 144 samples ran off the end at the ~16,000-token budget

key_numbers.label: Attempts stuck repeating the same tokens
key_numbers.sub: as thinking room grew from about 16,000 to 61,000 tokens

key_numbers.label: Thinking room scaled up
key_numbers.sub: roughly quadrupled, yet every attempt still hit the ceiling

charts.chart_plain_title: How invent-the-program attempts failed as thinking room grew
charts.chart_read: The bottom groups three thinking budgets (about 16k, 32k, 49k tokens) on the invent-from-examples task. Within each group: share that never resolved, share stuck in exact repeating loops, share whose answer hit its length cap. Lower is better everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: As the budget grows, the never-resolved share shrinks while the repeating-loop share balloons: the failure just changes shape, and no budget ever lets attempts finish.

charts.chart_plain_title: Handed the plan, the model cleared every task
charts.chart_read: One group, the supplied-plan check. The taller bar counts records that produced at least one exactly correct rewrite using the library (16 of 16); the shorter reference bar marks the 12 needed to pass. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All 16 records cleared, above the 12 needed: the model and its interface work fine when handed the plan, isolating failure to inventing the program itself.

qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.verdict_tag: A tiny slice of text broke unrelated behavior
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.concept_primer: A coding agent loops: read the code, edit, run the tests, commit. Trained only on flawless runs, it never meets a failed test — like a driver who practiced on empty roads and freezes at the first skid. So it replays its own fixes from real broken states.
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.plain_question: If you teach a small coding agent to recover by replaying moments it once fixed, does it learn to recover — and what does that cost?
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.plain_answer: Yes — replaying real failures taught recovery, lifting success from about half of cases to more than nine in ten. But the best version also added a little "explain your fix" text, and that supposedly tiny slice actually carried most of the training force, disturbing unrelated knowledge past a safety limit. The run stopped before it could even test whether recovery transfers to new code.
qwen35_4b_verifier_conditioned_recovery_.why_it_matters: Don't gauge a training slice's impact by its share of the words. A handful of tokens the model finds unnatural can dominate the update and quietly corrupt unrelated skills. Measure the actual size of the update, not the word count.

key_numbers.label: Recovery success, untrained vs best recipe
key_numbers.sub: share of 60 controlled failure cases the agent fixed

key_numbers.label: The "tiny" explanation dose
key_numbers.sub: nominally 5% plan text, yet its update magnitude ran 42.1 versus 1.8 for the no-explanation arm — far more than 5% of the real training pressure

key_numbers.label: Damage to unrelated knowledge
key_numbers.sub: unrelated-output drift of 31% versus the 15% ceiling — this stopped the run

key_numbers.label: New code families tested for transfer
key_numbers.sub: run halted at the safety gate before any unseen family was touched

charts.chart_plain_title: Replaying real failures lifts recovery from half to nine in ten
charts.chart_read: Four bars, left to right: the untrained agent, one trained only on clean successful runs, one trained on real failure moments, and one that also explains its fix. Bar height is the share of 60 controlled recovery cases solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Each step up adds recovery skill, and the explain-your-fix bar is highest at more than nine in ten — but this is trained-family practice, not transfer to new code.

charts.chart_plain_title: The winning recipe breaks the safety limit for unrelated knowledge
charts.chart_read: Three bars show how much each trained version disturbs the model's unrelated outputs; a flat reference line marks the allowed ceiling. Both action-only recipes sit well under it; the version that adds explanation text spikes far above. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Adding a nominally small amount of explanation text roughly triples the disturbance and clears the safety line, so the run halted before any transfer test ran.

qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.verdict_tag: Confidence can rank answers but can't clean training
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.concept_primer: This small model teaches itself: it writes many candidate programs, keeps the ones it is surest are right, and retrains on those. Like a cook who tastes to serve tonight's best plate (fine), but writes the cookbook by taste alone — subtly-wrong recipes slip in and the apprentice learns the flaws.
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.plain_question: If a small model grades its own generated code by how confident it feels instead of actually running it, can it still build a training set and teach itself to improve?
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.plain_answer: No. When training on answers checked by actually running the code lifted single-shot accuracy from 8% to 24%, confidence-filtered data — fifteen times purer than a random grab of the model's own outputs — landed right on the no-filter floor at 12%, adding nothing. Self-training tolerates almost no wrong examples, and the confident mistakes a filter lets through are plausible near-misses that teach the worst habits.
qwen35_4b_verifier_free_banking.why_it_matters: In a self-training loop, keep a real code-runner cleaning the training data — confidence leaks plausible-but-wrong examples that poison learning at small data sizes. Use confidence only to rank candidates, filtering by rank within difficulty tiers, never by a fixed score cutoff.

key_numbers.label: Accuracy from execution-checked training data
key_numbers.sub: depth-2 single-shot, thinking mode — the only filter that moved the needle

key_numbers.label: Accuracy from confidence-filtered data
key_numbers.sub: identical to an unfiltered random sample — a statistically insignificant nudge, not a real gain

key_numbers.label: Purity edge that bought nothing
key_numbers.sub: about 15x cleaner than random, yet trained the model no better

key_numbers.label: Self-grading right from wrong on one task
key_numbers.sub: blind until the model was allowed to think before scoring, which fixed it

charts.chart_plain_title: Only execution-checked training data improved the model; confidence-picked matched no filter
charts.chart_read: One bar per training filter, showing depth-2 single-shot accuracy in thinking mode after fine-tuning on 70 self-generated solutions drawn from one shared pool: no training, execution-verified (all correct), confidence-picked (43% correct), and unfiltered random (3% correct). Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the fully-verified bar rises, 8% to 24%; the confidence-picked bar sits at 12%, dead level with random despite being fifteen times cleaner — near-purity bought nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: The one-token self-score is blind unless the model thinks first
charts.chart_read: Two judging methods side by side: an instant one-token self-score versus one given room to reason first. Dark bars measure how reliably it separates right from wrong answers on the SAME task (higher is better); light bars are a flattering score inflated by mixing easy and hard tasks together.
charts.chart_takeaway: Instant scoring sits at a coin flip within a task; thinking first lifts it to reliable. The flattering pooled bar hides this failure — always check the within-task bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does self-training break the judge? Ranking holds, scores creep up
charts.chart_read: Three models each grade the identical set of 777 candidates: untrained, trained on verified data, and trained on its OWN approvals. Dark bars: how well it separates right from wrong answers (higher is better, near-flat across all three). Light bars: confidence wrongly given to incorrect answers (lower is better, creeping up after training).
charts.chart_takeaway: The judge's ranking survives even after training on its own approvals, but its confidence on wrong answers drifts upward — so filter by rank each round, never by a fixed score cutoff.

charts.chart_plain_title: Side effect: instant-answer variety collapses onto the trained answer-family
charts.chart_read: Bars show depth-1 answer variety in instant no-think mode after each fine-tune — the share of tasks where at least one of 16 tries is correct (higher is better). Left to right: base, execution-verified, confidence-stratified, confidence-global, and unfiltered random.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most trained arms roughly halve base variety (72% down to 32-40%) as correct answers get crammed onto one banked program family; the light-dose stratified arm (76%) is spared, and reasoning mode stays untouched.

qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.verdict_tag: Self-teaching did not improve new-problem coding
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.concept_primer: Think of a student who tries to get smarter by re-studying only the practice problems they already solved correctly, with a checker confirming each solution actually runs.
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.plain_question: Can this coding model get better at unseen problems by practicing on its own verified-correct solutions?
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.plain_answer: No. Re-studying its own correct solutions did not help the model on fresh problems; it landed at 65%, essentially unchanged, while simply having it try more times reached 69%.
qwen35_4b_verifier_guided_self_improveme.why_it_matters: Do not spend effort retraining this model on its own successes to widen its coding reach. Simply letting it make more attempts and picking a working one is the more reliable lever.

key_numbers.label: New problems solved, after self-teaching
key_numbers.sub: No real change versus the original model

key_numbers.label: New problems solved, just trying more times
key_numbers.sub: The approach that actually helped

key_numbers.label: Practiced problems solved

charts.chart_plain_title: New-problem success: self-teaching arms versus simply trying more times
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of unseen problems the model eventually solves, one bar per approach; taller is better. The rightmost bar is the plain 'try more times' approach, the others are self-teaching variants.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 'try more times' bar is the tallest, standing above every self-teaching bar, so extra attempts beat teaching the model on its own work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-teaching helps on practiced problems but not on new ones
charts.chart_read: Three pairs of bars compare the original model to the self-taught one across practiced problems, new problems, and a different problem set; taller is better. Left pair is practice, the other two are unseen.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-taught bar edges higher only on practiced problems and sits slightly lower on both unseen sets, so the gain does not carry over.

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-teaching leaves the model offering fewer distinct attempts
charts.chart_read: Bars show the average number of distinct answers the model produces per new problem, one per approach; more attempts give more chances to land a working one. Rightmost is the 'try more times' approach.
charts.chart_takeaway: Self-taught bars are shorter than the original model's, meaning self-teaching narrows the variety of attempts it makes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Whichever answer-picking rule is used, the original model still wins
charts.chart_read: Four pairs of bars compare final answer accuracy on new problems for the original versus self-taught model, one pair per answer-picking rule; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: In every pair the original-model bar is at least as tall, so switching the picking rule cannot rescue the self-taught model.

charts.chart_plain_title: A tiny 20-problem preview looked promising but reversed at full scale
charts.chart_read: Two pairs of bars compare the original and self-taught model on a small 20-problem preview versus the full 150-problem test; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-taught bar leads on the small preview but falls slightly behind on the full test, showing the early promise was noise.

qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.verdict_tag: A cheap test makes slow self-checking pointless
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.concept_primer: Picking the best of eight code drafts is like hiring: run each past one cheap screening test, and the handful that survive are so close in quality that a quick gut-feel judgment ranks them as well as slow, careful deliberation.
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.plain_question: When a small model writes eight candidate programs and you must pick one to ship, is it worth having it slowly reason through which one is correct?
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.plain_answer: No. When you can run even a single example test on each candidate, that filter alone lifts the share of shipped programs that fully work from 77% to 85%. Adding a free, instant self-confidence rating reaches 87% — matching a careful step-by-step self-check that costs five times the compute. Slow deliberation only earns its keep when no test can be run.
qwen35_4b_verifier_selector_showdown.why_it_matters: When your task ships an example test, spend compute running it first, then break ties among survivors with the model's free instant confidence score. Reserve slow step-by-step self-verification for settings where you cannot execute the code at all.

key_numbers.label: Random pick vs best selector
key_numbers.sub: share of chosen programs that pass every hidden test

key_numbers.label: Cost of slow self-checking
key_numbers.sub: for zero extra accuracy once a test exists

key_numbers.label: Programs that pass the example but still fail
key_numbers.sub: 6.6% of test-passers fail the hidden tests

key_numbers.label: Gap to a perfect picker closed
key_numbers.sub: of the distance from a random pick to a flawless one

charts.chart_plain_title: Which selector picks a working solution most often?
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method for picking one of eight programs; height is the share of picks that pass every hidden test (taller is better), running from random pick at 77% up to a non-deployable perfect picker at 89%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The test-plus-free-confidence bar reaches 87%, level with the five-times-costlier deliberate check and just below the 89% perfect ceiling; cheap methods capture almost all the gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Compute cost per task for each picking method
charts.chart_read: Each bar is estimated compute per task for one method; shorter is cheaper. The two deliberate self-check bars tower near 4,960 while the test and quick-confidence options sit down at 960.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deliberate check costs about five times the cheap combo yet, from the accuracy chart, buys no extra accuracy — the visual signature of wasted spend.

charts.chart_plain_title: Ranking the survivors: quick versus deliberate self-check
charts.chart_read: Two bars show how reliably each self-check sorts a truly-correct survivor above a secretly-broken one; higher is better, and a coin flip would score 50%. Deliberate scores 76%, quick 70%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The free quick check trails the costly one only slightly, because after the test filters candidates few broken survivors remain to sort apart.

qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.verdict_tag: Drilling two-step problems never unlocks three-step ones
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.concept_primer: Self-training here means fine-tuning a model only on problems it already solved by itself. Picture a climber drilling every hold she can reach: practice makes those moves rock-solid, but she never touches the higher hold, so there is nothing to learn from and her reach never extends.
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.plain_question: If you fine-tune a small model on the multi-step problems it can already solve, does it gain the ability to solve problems needing one more step?
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.plain_answer: No. Fine-tuning the model on the two-step solutions it could already produce tripled its two-step success on fresh tasks, from 12% to 36%. But its three-step success stayed at exactly zero, unchanged from before: both models solved none of the three-step tasks within sixteen tries. Becoming fluent at one difficulty tier taught it nothing about the tier just above.
qwen35_4b_wall_climbing.why_it_matters: Self-training on a model's own correct outputs sharpens skills it already demonstrates; it cannot invent a harder skill it never produces. To reach a new difficulty tier, first generate correct examples at that tier with search or tools, then fine-tune on those.

key_numbers.label: Two-step tasks solved, before vs after training
key_numbers.sub: fresh tasks solved within sixteen tries (tripled)

key_numbers.label: Three-step tasks solved, before vs after training
key_numbers.sub: the unlock that never happened

key_numbers.label: Four-step tasks solved, before vs after
key_numbers.sub: unchanged — no accidental leap either

key_numbers.label: Training examples used
key_numbers.sub: all one- or two-step problems the model already solved

charts.chart_plain_title: Two-step success triples after training; three-step stays at zero
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare the untrained model against the self-trained model at three difficulties — tasks needing two, three, or four chained steps. Bar height is the share of fresh tasks solved within sixteen tries; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the two-step bar grows, from 12% to 36%. The three- and four-step bars are identical between models, three-step pinned flat at zero — training never reached the next rung.

charts.chart_plain_title: More attempts lift two-step solving but never three-step
charts.chart_read: Lines track the cumulative share of tasks solved as attempts rise from one to sixteen. Two flat lines sit at zero: three-step tasks for both the untrained and self-trained models. One rising line is two-step for the self-trained model. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two-step line climbs steadily to 36% while both three-step lines stay glued to zero across all sixteen tries — extra sampling never surfaces a single three-step solution.

qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.verdict_tag: Learning from its own drafts barely helped
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.concept_primer: The model writes tiny programs to solve puzzles, and it can actually run each draft to see what it computes. The idea: let it learn from running its own drafts to pick and reuse the good ones.
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.plain_question: Can the model get better at writing correct little programs by running its own drafts and learning which ones actually work?
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.plain_answer: Partly. Learning to judge its own drafts barely beat the model's gut instinct for picking one, but reusing the best drafts still nudged its direct writing up from 18% to 23% of puzzles solved — far below the 82% it reaches when simply shown correct answers.
qwen_action_conditioned_vm_echo_policy_i.why_it_matters: Running your own drafts to reuse the good ones gives a small, real lift, but the payoff is capped by how well the model can tell a right draft from a wrong one. Improve that judgment first.

key_numbers.label: Fresh puzzles solved by writing directly
key_numbers.sub: before vs after reusing its own best drafts

key_numbers.label: Picking the right draft
key_numbers.sub: learned judgment ~ gut instinct, vs 37% if it always picked the best draft

key_numbers.label: When shown correct answers instead
key_numbers.sub: the ceiling this approach stayed well under

key_numbers.label: Hard puzzles solved when allowed to search drafts
key_numbers.sub: biggest gain from reusing its own drafts

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving puzzles by writing directly, across training methods
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a group of puzzles; taller bars mean more solved by writing a program directly, with no second tries. Bars compare the frozen starting model, two ways of reusing its own drafts, and a model simply shown correct answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reuse bars sit just above the starting model, while the shown-answers bar towers over all of them, so self-teaching helps only slightly.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving puzzles when allowed to try several drafts
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a group of puzzles; taller bars mean more solved when the model is allowed to generate and test several drafts. Bars compare the starting model against two ways of reusing its own drafts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reusing the best drafts gives the clearest lift here, with hard puzzles rising from 35% to 52% solved.

charts.chart_plain_title: How well the model picks its best draft
charts.chart_read: Taller bars mean the chosen draft is more often correct, for practice puzzles and fresh ones. Bars compare gut instinct, learned judgment, and the score if it always grabbed its best available draft.
charts.chart_takeaway: Learned judgment barely rises above gut instinct while the best-possible bar sits far higher, showing weak judgment is the bottleneck.

charts.chart_plain_title: Being pickier about which drafts to reuse
charts.chart_read: Two puzzle groups; taller bars mean more solved by direct writing afterward. Bars compare reusing every draft versus keeping only higher-scoring drafts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Keeping only the pickier drafts raises fresh-puzzle accuracy, showing quality of reused drafts matters more than quantity.

charts.chart_plain_title: Direct puzzle-solving per group, self-teaching vs shown answers
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a puzzle group; taller bars mean more solved by writing directly. Bars compare the starting model, two self-teaching methods, and a model shown correct answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: Self-teaching bars edge only a few points above the start, dwarfed by the shown-answers bars reaching 52-82%.

qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.verdict_tag: Model labels did not build reliable rules
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.concept_primer: Imagine watching someone reformat a few names by hand, then trying to write one recipe that reformats every future name automatically, instead of redoing each one by hand.
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.plain_question: Can the model's yes/no judgments turn a handful of worked examples into one reusable recipe that handles every new case?
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.plain_answer: No. Using the model's judgments to pick a recipe worked on 25% of tasks, barely ahead of the 22.5% you get from deliberately scrambled judgments, and it never rose above the best recipe the tool could build.
qwen_active_crystallizer_public_gate.why_it_matters: The model can reformat individual cases well, but that skill does not compile into one dependable rule. If you need a single recipe that always works, expect to hand-build better recipe options first.

key_numbers.label: Recipe picked using the model's judgments
key_numbers.sub: share of tasks where one recipe handled every new case

key_numbers.label: Recipe picked using scrambled judgments
key_numbers.sub: the same setup with judgments deliberately jumbled — nearly a tie

key_numbers.label: Model answering cases one at a time
key_numbers.sub: same model, but solving each case by hand instead of via a recipe

key_numbers.label: Recipe that worked on every case in a strict check
key_numbers.sub: on a matched set of 40 tasks, versus 37.5% for hand-solving

charts.chart_plain_title: Recipe-picking methods versus solving cases one at a time
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; height is the share of tasks solved on unseen cases, taller is better. Bars cover the model's judgments, scrambled judgments, plain shortest-recipe picking, the best recipe available, and hand-solving one case each.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model-judgment bar sits level with the scrambled-judgment bar and the best-available ceiling, all far below the tall hand-solving bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting single cases right versus getting a whole task right
charts.chart_read: Three bars on the same 40 tasks; height is exact-match rate, taller is better. Left: model solving individual cases; middle: model getting every case in a task right; right: one recipe getting every case right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tall single-case bar collapses when every case must be right, and the recipe bar collapses furthest, showing consistency is the weak point.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where recipe-building breaks down across all tasks
charts.chart_read: Three bars, height is share of tasks, taller is better. Left: tasks where some recipe fits the shown examples; middle: tasks where a fitting recipe also handles unseen cases; right: picking the shortest fitting recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every task has a recipe fitting the examples, yet only about a third of those recipes generalize, so fitting examples is easy but generalizing is not.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best-possible recipe coverage by kind of task
charts.chart_read: Task kinds along the bottom; paired bars show the best recipe available versus the plain shortest-recipe pick; taller is better. The two largest kinds, date-and-time and numbers, appear first.
charts.chart_takeaway: The date-and-time and number bars are lowest despite being the biggest groups, dragging overall recipe coverage down well below simpler task kinds.

charts.chart_plain_title: Recipe-picking results hold steady across three run sizes
charts.chart_read: Three run sizes along the bottom; grouped bars show each method's share of tasks solved on unseen cases, taller is better. Bars include the model's judgments, scrambled judgments, and hand-solving cases one at a time.
charts.chart_takeaway: Across all three sizes the model-judgment bars stay pinned near the scrambled-judgment and best-available bars, while hand-solving stays far higher.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best-possible recipe coverage shifts with the task sample
charts.chart_read: Three sample sizes along the bottom; paired bars show the best recipe available versus the plain shortest-recipe pick; taller is better. Only the largest sample gives a stable reading.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-available bar swings widely between small samples, so only the full-size run gives a trustworthy estimate of how far recipes can reach.

qwen_active_example_acquisition.verdict_tag: Smartly chosen extra examples help a little
qwen_active_example_acquisition.concept_primer: Before answering hidden test questions, the model is allowed to request a few extra worked examples. This tests whether deliberately choosing which examples to add beats grabbing random ones.
qwen_active_example_acquisition.plain_question: If the model can request a few extra worked examples before a test, does choosing them deliberately beat picking them at random?
qwen_active_example_acquisition.plain_answer: It depends. Deliberately choosing gave a small lift, solving 70% of tasks versus 67% with no extras, while random or answer-scrambled examples actually did worse, at 63% and 60%.
qwen_active_example_acquisition.why_it_matters: When you add examples to steer a small model, choose them deliberately rather than at random. Targeting the confusing cases helps modestly, but random extras can backfire, especially on formatting-heavy tasks.

key_numbers.label: Tasks fully solved with no extra examples
key_numbers.sub: starting point with the four given examples

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved after a deliberately chosen extra
key_numbers.sub: the small lift from choosing well

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved with random extra examples
key_numbers.sub: picking badly does worse than adding nothing

key_numbers.label: Best result from any pick that was tried
key_numbers.sub: the ceiling if every choice were ideal

charts.chart_plain_title: How each way of picking extra examples solves tasks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of tasks fully solved on unseen test rows, one bar per way of choosing extra examples; taller is better. Leftmost adds nothing, rightmost is the best pick that was tried.
charts.chart_takeaway: The deliberately chosen bars sit above the no-extras baseline while random and label-scrambled bars fall below it, so how you pick matters more than how many you add.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as more extra examples are added, by method
charts.chart_read: Each line is a strategy for choosing extra examples; the horizontal axis is how many were added (none, one, or three) and the vertical axis is the share of tasks fully solved; up is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the deliberate-choice line stays above its starting point at both amounts, while the random and diversity lines dip below, showing extra examples help only when chosen well.

charts.chart_plain_title: Date and time tasks: the hardest, largest group
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of the 13 date-and-time tasks fully solved, one bar per method, from no extras through deliberately chosen, random, and scrambled-label extras; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One deliberately chosen example lifts this hard group the most, while random or scrambled extras push it to the lowest bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting every row right versus getting single rows right
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method: the lighter bar is the share of individual test rows answered correctly, the darker bar is the share of tasks where every row is right; the darker one is stricter and higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The whole-task bars always trail the single-row bars, meaning one wrong row sinks a task; deliberate choosing narrows that gap the most.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types still have room to improve
charts.chart_read: Two bars per task type: starting accuracy with the four given examples versus the best pick of extras that was tried; each bar is the share of tasks fully solved, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Six of nine task types are already maxed out, so extra examples can only move the date-time and number groups, and one type stays stuck at zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Number tasks: smart picks help, scrambled labels hurt
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of the six number-formatting tasks fully solved, one bar per method, from no extras through deliberate, diverse, scrambled-label, and best-pick extras; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Deliberate and diversity picks reach the best-achieved level, while examples with scrambled answers drag accuracy below the no-extras start.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do different self-check styles agree on which example to add?
charts.chart_read: Three bars per hard task, one for each style of self-check the model uses to decide which example to request; each bar is the share of that task's rows solved, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three self-check styles mostly agree, but on the hardest tasks one style swings the result by a noticeable chunk in either direction.

qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.verdict_tag: Answering all at once did not help
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.concept_primer: Think of a clerk given a few worked examples, then a stack of similar entries to reformat. We tested whether letting it see the whole stack at once, versus one entry at a time, makes it more consistent.
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.plain_question: Does letting the model reformat all of a task's entries together, instead of one at a time, make it get the whole task right more often?
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.plain_answer: No. Solving one entry at a time got every entry in a task right 50% of the time; doing them all together dropped to 45%. Grouping never rescued a task it had failed, and it broke two that one-at-a-time had solved.
qwen_batched_transduction_consistency.why_it_matters: When you need every row of a task correct, keep reformatting entries one at a time. Bundling them into one shared answer adds a new failure mode where a single wrong item spoils the whole task.

key_numbers.label: Whole task fully correct, one at a time
key_numbers.sub: baseline of tasks with every entry right

key_numbers.label: Whole task fully correct, all together
key_numbers.sub: best grouped approach still trails

key_numbers.label: Tasks the grouping rescued
key_numbers.sub: never fixed a task it had failed

key_numbers.label: Tasks the grouping broke
key_numbers.sub: lost tasks the one-at-a-time solved

charts.chart_plain_title: Doing entries together versus one at a time, six ways
charts.chart_read: Bars group six methods along the bottom, from one-at-a-time to ever-larger bundles; taller is better. Darker bars show how often the whole task was fully correct, lighter bars how often an individual entry was correct.
charts.chart_takeaway: The leftmost one-at-a-time bar is the tallest on whole-task correctness, so no bundling method beats simply handling entries separately.

charts.chart_plain_title: Stricter grouping instructions still fall short
charts.chart_read: Four methods along the bottom, taller is better: one-at-a-time versus three ways of grouping entries with tougher instructions. Darker bars are whole-task correctness, lighter bars single-entry correctness.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every grouped bar sits at or below the one-at-a-time bar, so tighter instructions never close the gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: Task-by-task: did grouping help, tie, or hurt?
charts.chart_read: Three bars count how many of the 40 tasks grouping improved, matched, or worsened versus one-at-a-time. Taller wins bar would be good; taller loses bar is bad.
charts.chart_takeaway: The wins bar is empty and the loses bar shows two tasks, so grouping only ever hurt or made no difference.

qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.verdict_tag: Right answers generated but rarely chosen
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.concept_primer: Picture the model as a student who scribbles many candidate answers on scratch paper: the correct one is usually there, but they must circle just one, with no answer key to check against.
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.plain_question: Can a learned scoring model pick the right little program from the model's own guesses without ever peeking at the answer?
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.plain_answer: Mostly no. A learned scorer lifted accuracy only a couple of points over plain search, from 67% to 70% on new tasks, while a version allowed to peek at the answer key reached 82%. The right program is usually generated but rarely chosen.
qwen_budgeted_action_value_compiler.why_it_matters: The bottleneck is picking, not generating. Effort should go into training a stronger judge on complete candidate programs, checked by actually running them, rather than scoring half-finished ones.

key_numbers.label: Learned picker vs plain search
key_numbers.sub: correct answers on new everyday tasks; only a tiny lift

key_numbers.label: If it could peek at the answer key

key_numbers.label: Correct answer already on the scratch paper
