charts.chart_plain_title: Learned picker vs peeking at the answer, on two task sets
charts.chart_read: Bars show task accuracy (taller is better) for four ways of choosing a final program, grouped into everyday paired tasks and harder multi-step tasks; the rightmost bar is allowed to check candidates against the answer key.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned-picker bar sits just above plain search, but the answer-checking bar towers far above both, so the learned scorer captures little of the available gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: The correct program is usually generated but not picked
charts.chart_read: For four task sets, the short bar is how often plain search's single top pick is right and the tall bar is how often any generated candidate was right; taller is better, and the gap between them is missed opportunity.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every tall bar sits far above its short partner, meaning a correct program was generated 15-20 points more often than it was actually chosen.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much to trust the learned score before it backfires
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy (higher is better) as the learned score is given more weight when choosing the final program; the flat left region is light weight, the right edge is heavy weight.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines dip at the far right, showing that leaning too hard on the learned score makes choices worse, not better.

charts.chart_plain_title: How cleanly each kind of move-label can be told apart
charts.chart_read: Bars show how reliably a learned score separates good partial moves from bad ones (taller is better) for four training signals, from a simple exact-match label to richer graded scores.
charts.chart_takeaway: The simple exact-match bar is tall and clean while the richer graded-score bars are much shorter, so nuance is exactly what the lightweight scorer struggles to learn.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training examples let the compiler actually learn
charts.chart_read: Lines show how accurately the model turns prompts into runnable programs (higher is better) as training progresses, comparing a small-data pilot against the main run that used four times the examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: The pilot line stays flat near the floor while the main-run line climbs steadily to about 72%, showing data volume, not tuning, unlocked the compiler.

charts.chart_plain_title: Exactly rebuilding the intended program stays low everywhere
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each method reproduces the exact intended program (taller is better) across four task sets, comparing plain search, the best learned picker, and the answer-checking version.
charts.chart_takeaway: All bars stay well below half and collapse to about a third on the hardest set, so exact program recovery is hard even with answer checking.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most moves aren't the textbook move, yet still reach the answer
charts.chart_read: For three data-collection runs, the short bar is the share of partial moves matching the single textbook program and the tall bar is the share that can still reach a correct answer; taller means more workable moves.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recoverable bar towers over the textbook bar in every run, about 72% versus 4% in the main run, so textbook-only supervision misses most good moves.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training longer doesn't sharpen the learned scores
charts.chart_read: Lines track how reliably each of four learned scores separates good moves from bad (higher is better) across eight training rounds; the four lines sit at clearly different heights.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line is essentially flat from the first round onward, so the scorer's quality is capped by its design, not by how long it trains.

qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.verdict_tag: Reading candidate fixes did not help pick them
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.concept_primer: Picture a broken program and a pile of suggested fixes, each with a receipt of what happened when you ran it. The test: can the model read those receipts and reliably pick the fix that actually works?
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.plain_question: Can the model pick the fix that works by reading each candidate and its run receipt, instead of just leaving the program alone?
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.plain_answer: No. A picker that read the fixes and their receipts never beat simply leaving the program alone (both about 44%), even though a working fix was usually in the pile and picking it perfectly would have reached about 90%.
qwen_candidate_conditioned_trace_verifie.why_it_matters: Letting the model freely swap in these fixes is unsafe: it rewrites almost everything and breaks working code. Until a picker can tell good fixes from bad, keep a fallback that leaves already-correct programs untouched.

key_numbers.label: Fix-picker accuracy
key_numbers.sub: reading the fixes gained nothing over doing nothing

key_numbers.label: If it always picked the right fix
key_numbers.sub: a working fix was usually available

key_numbers.label: Correct programs it broke
key_numbers.sub: when the model edited freely

charts.chart_plain_title: No trained picker beat leaving the program alone
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy (taller is better) for each way of choosing a fix. The two left bars, doing nothing and the best trained picker, tie; the two right bars are cheat pickers that already know the right answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best trained picker's bar sits exactly level with doing-nothing, while the freely-editing pickers collapse to near the floor and only the cheat pickers rise far above.

charts.chart_plain_title: A safety fallback stops the damage but adds no gains
charts.chart_read: For each model variant, one bar is accuracy (taller better) and one is how often it broke a working program (shorter better). Left of each pair edits freely; right of each pair uses a fallback that keeps the original when unsure.
charts.chart_takeaway: The free-editing variants show sky-high breakage bars, while the fallback variants zero out the damage but their accuracy only climbs back to the do-nothing level, never past it.

charts.chart_plain_title: A working fix exists in the pile on every test set
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per test set: doing nothing versus two cheat pickers that always grab a correct fix. Taller cheat-picker bars mean more room to improve is available. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On every test set the cheat-picker bars tower over doing-nothing, proving correct fixes are present and the problem is choosing them, not lacking them.

charts.chart_plain_title: Free-editing pickers rewrite almost every program
charts.chart_read: Per approach, one bar is how often it changed the original program and one is how often that change broke a working one. Both lower is safer. Cheat pickers and the fallback variants sit low; free-editing variants sit high.
charts.chart_takeaway: The free-editing bars nearly max out on both measures, meaning they touch almost everything and wreck most working programs, unlike the cheat pickers that edit only where a fix helps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Genuine fix-readers end up no better than scrambled controls
charts.chart_read: Bars show each training approach's final usefulness score, two bars per approach for two repeat runs; higher is better. Simple baselines sit high; the real fix-reading models sit low, near controls fed scrambled or corrupted inputs.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real fix-reading bars land down near the deliberately-broken control bars on both runs, showing they learned nothing the receipts should have provided.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the base program is already right swings by data batch
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show how often the untouched program is already correct, split by data batch and test set. Each colored series is one batch of source problems; higher just means fewer things left to fix.
charts.chart_takeaway: One batch sits near the top on every test set while another drops to zero, so the room for repair is concentrated in particular batches of problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: On unfamiliar problems, the fallback is all that prevents collapse
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy on a batch of problems the model never trained on; taller is better. They span doing nothing, cheat picker, and free-editing versus fallback versions of the model.
charts.chart_takeaway: With doing-nothing already near the top, the free-editing bars crash to the floor while the fallback bars climb back near do-nothing but never pass it.

qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.verdict_tag: Finding the fix is easy; picking it isn't
qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.concept_primer: A word problem becomes a 24-step arithmetic recipe, and the machine often fumbles a step. A search spins off about 1,300 near-miss versions, each showing its own worked-out running totals. A checker reads only those totals — never the answer — and picks the version that hangs together best.
qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.plain_question: When a small model garbles a short arithmetic program, can another model pick the right fix from many candidates without ever seeing the answer?
qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.plain_answer: Yes. A small checker that reads each candidate's worked-out steps — never the true answer — lifted correctly-running programs from 30% to 54%, and to 56% when it cross-checks two wordings of one task. The twist: a picker handed the answer key hits 88%, so the right fix is usually in the pile; the checker just fails to spot it. Selection, not search, is the wall.
qwen_candidate_trace_verifier.why_it_matters: When repairing shaky small-model outputs, spend on the selector, not just on generating more candidates — the correct fix usually already exists. First measure your answer-key ceiling: a big gap means selection, not coverage, is where to invest.

key_numbers.label: Correct-running programs, before and after the checker
key_numbers.sub: fresh paired programs, base compiler vs the trace-reading checker

key_numbers.label: If it could see the answer key
key_numbers.sub: ceiling — how often a correct fix is present in the candidate pile

key_numbers.label: With a two-wording cross-check
key_numbers.sub: reranking two phrasings of the same task for agreement

key_numbers.label: Candidates weighed per program
key_numbers.sub: local repair options enumerated for each 24-step prompt

charts.chart_plain_title: Programs that run right: no repair, checker's pick, answer-key ceiling
charts.chart_read: Three fresh test sets along the bottom (standard wording, reworded, paired wordings). Each has three bars: no-repair base (lowest), the trace-reading checker's pick (middle), and an answer-key picker (highest). Taller means more programs run exactly right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The checker's bar roughly doubles the base on every set yet sits far below the answer-key bar — real gains, but most of the reachable headroom stays unclaimed.

charts.chart_plain_title: Paired wordings: where per-guess judging helps and where it hurts
charts.chart_read: Four measures along the bottom (runs exactly right, matching step prefix, both wordings correct, agreement between the two wordings), each with four bars: base, single-guess checker, pair-aware reranker, and answer-key ceiling. Higher is better everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: The single-guess checker lifts most bars but drops wording-agreement below base (71% to 58%); reranking the pair jointly restores agreement to 87% and lifts both-correct to 52%.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training programs, higher repair accuracy
charts.chart_read: Two rising lines — checker accuracy on the fresh standard set and the fresh paired set — plotted against the number of training programs (128, then 192, then 512 across the bottom). Up and to the right is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines climb steadily, from about 41% at 128 programs to 50-54% at 512, so accuracy was still rising with more training data — not yet plateaued.

qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.verdict_tag: A light dose of step coaching helps
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.concept_primer: Picture teaching someone a long string of mental math by having them say each running total out loud; a little of this "show your work" coaching during practice can make the final answers more reliable.
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.plain_question: Does coaching the model to show its running totals — and keeping the best version instead of the last — help it solve long arithmetic chains?
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. A light amount of running-total coaching, kept on the whole way, beat giving no coaching at all — but heavy coaching that was switched off for the hardest, longest problems did worse than none.
qwen_checkpoint_scheduled_state_compiler.why_it_matters: Add a small, steady amount of running-total coaching rather than a heavy dose you drop later, and always keep the best-scoring version from training, not whatever the run happens to end on.

key_numbers.label: Long chains solved correctly
key_numbers.sub: no coaching vs light coaching kept on throughout

key_numbers.label: Same steps whether worded plainly or reworded
key_numbers.sub: no coaching vs light coaching kept on late

key_numbers.label: Coaching switched fully off for long problems
key_numbers.sub: worst recipe, falls below giving no coaching

charts.chart_plain_title: Light coaching kept on beats no coaching on long chains
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one coaching recipe; one bar shows how often it solves long chains correctly, the other how often plainly worded and reworded versions produce the same running totals. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two 'light coaching kept on' recipes stand tallest, while the 'coaching switched off late' recipe sinks below giving no coaching at all.

charts.chart_plain_title: The best version shows up mid-training, not at the end
charts.chart_read: Each recipe gets a pair of bars: its best-scoring saved version versus its final end-of-training version, measured on solving long chains. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every final-version bar is roughly half its best-saved counterpart, so simply ending training hands you a noticeably worse model.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coaching mainly protects the reworded problems
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by wording — plainly stated, reworded, and both-must-match — with one colored bar per coaching recipe. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Recipes barely differ on plainly worded problems but split widely on reworded ones, where light coaching stays high and 'switched off late' collapses.

qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.concept_primer: Think of re-running the same training three times, each from a different random starting point ("seed"). A recipe is only trustworthy if it works no matter which starting point you happen to draw.
qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.plain_question: When this small model is trained to run 24-step number programs, does it reliably learn — and does one training recipe consistently come out on top?
qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.plain_answer: No. The same recipe swings from total failure to strong success depending only on the random starting point, so no recipe can be crowned a reliable winner. The best average reaches about 43% on plain wording, but that hides runs scoring anywhere from 0% to 81%.
qwen_compiler_multiseed_reattribution.why_it_matters: Never trust a single lucky training run here — judge a recipe only across several random starting points, since one good run can look impressive while a repeat of it collapses to near-zero.

key_numbers.label: Best recipe, plain wording (3-run average)
key_numbers.sub: share of 24-step programs run correctly

key_numbers.label: Same recipe, three random starts
key_numbers.sub: swings from total failure to strong

key_numbers.label: Train-everything-at-once recipe
key_numbers.sub: almost never works on plain wording

key_numbers.label: Early steps right vs whole program right
key_numbers.sub: most steps land; the full program often doesn't

charts.chart_plain_title: Which training recipe runs 24-step programs best, on average
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars: four ways of asking (plain wording, unseen wording, matched pairs, unseen pairs) along the bottom, three training recipes as colored bars, height is share of programs run correctly. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The copy-and-grow recipe is tallest on most groupings, yet even its best plain-wording bar sits below half, while training everything at once barely clears the floor.

charts.chart_plain_title: The same recipe's plain-wording score swings wildly by random start
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by random starting point (three of them), colored by recipe, height is share of plain-wording 24-step programs run correctly. Taller is better; consistent heights within a color would mean a dependable recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best recipe's bar goes from zero at one starting point to 81% at another, so a single run tells you almost nothing about the recipe.

charts.chart_plain_title: The early steps are right; the whole program is where it breaks
charts.chart_read: For each recipe, two bars: how often the program's opening run of steps is correct versus how often the entire program is correct. Height is accuracy; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every recipe gets a long opening run of steps right far more often than the full program, so the failure is finishing consistently, not knowing the individual steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Programs work at length 16, then fall off a cliff at length 24
charts.chart_read: Each run (recipe and starting point) has two bars: accuracy on 16-step programs versus 24-step programs. Height is share run correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Almost every run nails the 16-step programs but crashes on the 24-step ones, showing the trouble is tied to that extra length, not a general inability.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training looks like a clean success even when the final test collapses
charts.chart_read: Lines track, step by step during training, how often the model's intermediate work is right for the staged recipe; one line per random start. Rising toward the top means training is going well.
charts.chart_takeaway: All the lines climb to near-perfect and look interchangeable, yet their final test scores differ hugely, so a smooth training curve gives no warning of a bad outcome.

charts.chart_plain_title: Familiar reworded questions are far easier than the plain ones
charts.chart_read: Each run has two bars: accuracy on the plain question wording versus on reworded versions of the same programs. Height is share run correctly; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most runs handle the reworded questions far better than the plain ones — some near 100% reworded while plain wording is near zero — revealing sensitivity to phrasing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting one wording right rarely means getting both
charts.chart_read: For each run, two bars: how often a single question is answered correctly versus how often both rephrasings of the same program are answered correctly. Height is the rate; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The both-versions bar sits well below the single-question bar in nearly every run, so the model's success on a program usually doesn't hold up when the wording changes.

qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.verdict_tag: Trained picker cannot find the right solution
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.concept_primer: Picture a multiple-choice test where a correct option is almost always present. The system builds many candidate step-by-step solutions and trains a helper to pick the best one by watching each run, never seeing the answer key.
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.plain_question: Can a small trained helper pick better step-by-step solutions by watching them run, instead of writing every step out as text?
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.plain_answer: No. A correct candidate is available for almost every prompt, but the helper adds only a point or two on easy problems and actually does worse than doing nothing on longer or reworded ones. The barrier is picking, not availability.
qwen_complete_program_trace_reranker.why_it_matters: When a correct answer is usually reachable but hard to identify, invest in sharper training signals that reward genuinely correct reasoning, not just any candidate that lands on the right final answer.

key_numbers.label: A correct solution is available
key_numbers.sub: share of prompts where a right candidate already exists

key_numbers.label: Easy problems, before to after
key_numbers.sub: picking helper barely improves accuracy

key_numbers.label: Longer problems, before to after
key_numbers.sub: the helper does worse than doing nothing

key_numbers.label: Most of the reachable gain captured
key_numbers.sub: of the room between the system and the best possible pick

charts.chart_plain_title: Picking helper barely beats the plain system, far below the best possible pick
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars, one cluster per test set. In each cluster: the plain system, the picking helper, and the best-possible pick. Taller is better; the best-possible bar towers over the other two everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: The helper's bar sits almost on top of the plain system's in every cluster while the best-possible bar stays near the top, so almost all of the reachable improvement is left untouched.

charts.chart_plain_title: On longer problems the helper drops below the plain system
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy as problems get longer (more steps), left to right. One line each for the plain system, the picking helper, and the best-possible pick. Higher is better; the best-possible line stays flat near the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Past the easy length the helper line dips under the plain-system line, even though the best-possible line stays near-perfect, showing the helper actively hurts on harder problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of available improvement the helper captured, by test set
charts.chart_read: One bar per test set showing how much of the gap between the plain system and the best-possible pick the helper closed. Above zero is good; below zero means it did worse than the plain system.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars reach at most a small positive sliver and turn negative on the paired and longer problems, so the helper captured almost none of the available headroom.

charts.chart_plain_title: Helper helps a little on most task types, hurts yes-or-no ones
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per task type: plain system, picking helper, best-possible pick. Higher is better. The best-possible bar is near the top for every type.
charts.chart_takeaway: The helper nudges four task types up slightly, leaves one flat, and drops the yes-or-no type by about 9 points, where answers collapse to few values.

charts.chart_plain_title: A calculation-scoring shortcut loses to simply trusting the system
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per test set: the plain system, a shortcut that scores candidates by simulating them, and the trained helper. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The shortcut's bar sits below the plain system on every test set except a tie on the hardest reworded one, so scoring by simulation alone underperforms doing nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded questions widen the accuracy gap as problems lengthen
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy as problems get longer, split into plainly worded and reworded versions, for both the plain system and the helper. Higher is better; reworded lines sit lower.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reworded lines fall well below plainly worded ones at every length, and the helper widens rather than narrows that reworded gap on the longer problems.

qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.verdict_tag: Practice on short recipes fixes longer ones
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.concept_primer: Picture the model as a translator that turns a word problem into a step-by-step recipe a machine then runs. We only care whether the machine gets the right answer from that recipe.
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.plain_question: Does teaching the translator a few short two- and three-step recipes help it get longer, unseen recipes right?
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.plain_answer: Yes. Adding a handful of short multi-step examples raised correct answers on much longer unseen problems from 72% to 83% at six steps and 78% to 89% at eight steps, while feeding only single-step examples did not budge.
qwen_compositional_curriculum_abi.why_it_matters: When a translator keeps producing valid-looking but wrong instructions on long tasks, mix a little genuine multi-step practice into its training rather than just piling on more single-step examples.

key_numbers.label: Correct on six-step problems
key_numbers.sub: single-step-only training vs. adding short multi-step practice

key_numbers.label: Correct on eight-step problems
key_numbers.sub: same before-and-after on longer unseen problems

key_numbers.label: Wrong-order mistakes on long problems
key_numbers.sub: share of programs with steps in the wrong order

charts.chart_plain_title: Longer unseen problems solved, by what the translator practiced
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of six-step and eight-step problems solved correctly, grouped by training diet; the three bars per group are single-step-only, short two-step mix, and short two-and-three-step mix. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two-and-three-step-practice bar stands clearly tallest in both groups, meaning only the richer practice diet handled longer problems better.

charts.chart_plain_title: How accuracy holds up as recipes get longer
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks one training diet's share of correct answers as problems grow from two to eight steps; higher lines are better. Watch the two-and-three-step-practice line against the single-step-only line.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the richer-practice line stays high and even climbs at eight steps, while the other two sag to about 72% by six steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: What goes wrong on the longest problems
charts.chart_read: Bars split every generated program on the longest problems into exactly correct, wrong step order, and a correct alternate route, grouped by training diet. A taller first group and shorter middle group are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The richer-practice diet has the biggest exactly-correct bar and nearly halves the wrong-order bar, showing it fixed real reasoning, not just neatness.

charts.chart_plain_title: Guided generation versus letting the translator run free
charts.chart_read: For eight-step problems, bars compare three ways of generating the recipe (guided, retry-until-valid, and free) for two training diets; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guided bars are tallest for both diets, and the single best result pairs the richer-practice diet with guided generation at 89%.

qwen_constrained_abi_parser.concept_primer: Picture asking a small AI to turn a plain-English request into a short recipe of steps a computer then runs. A "grammar guardrail" blocks any half-written step that would break the recipe's rules.
qwen_constrained_abi_parser.plain_question: Does forcing the AI to obey the recipe's rules as it writes make its steps actually run correctly more often?
qwen_constrained_abi_parser.plain_answer: Yes. On the hardest requests the guardrail got 75% of recipes running to the right answer versus 60% without it, and it beat both simply re-rolling until valid (63%) and answering with no steps at all (0%).
qwen_constrained_abi_parser.why_it_matters: When a small model writes machine-runnable steps, keeping its output well-formed as it writes buys real accuracy and holds up when requests are reworded. Simply retrying until valid barely helps.

key_numbers.label: Correct on hardest requests
key_numbers.sub: plain writing vs grammar guardrail

key_numbers.label: Just re-rolling until valid
key_numbers.sub: cheap alternative, barely helps

key_numbers.label: When the request is reworded
key_numbers.sub: plain writing vs guardrail

key_numbers.label: Answering with no steps
key_numbers.sub: gets none of them right

charts.chart_plain_title: Hardest requests: grammar guardrail gets the most recipes running right
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a different way to turn the request into steps; height is the share of runs whose steps produced the right final answer. Taller is better. The rightmost bar is the best-possible ceiling.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail bar (75%) stands clearly above plain writing (60%) and cheap re-rolling (63%); only the perfect-input ceiling is higher.

charts.chart_plain_title: The guardrail leads at every recipe length
charts.chart_read: Left-to-right is how many steps the recipe needs (longer is harder); height is the share of steps that ran to the right answer. Higher lines are better. Each line is one method of writing the steps.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail line sits on top at every length, staying above plain writing and cheap re-rolling as recipes get longer.

charts.chart_plain_title: When the request is reworded, only the guardrail holds up
charts.chart_read: Left-to-right is recipe length; height is the share running to the right answer, higher is better. Lines show methods when the request is phrased in unfamiliar wording.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail line stays high and flat near 75%, while plain writing drops to about 48% and the parse-first approach collapses below that.

charts.chart_plain_title: The guardrail's trade-off: everything well-formed, but not all correct
charts.chart_read: Three groups: how often the recipe is well-formed, how often it runs to the right answer, and how often well-formed ones are actually right. Two bars each: plain writing versus guardrail. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail forces 100% well-formed and lifts right-answers to 75%, but the last group dips to 75% too, meaning some forced-valid recipes are still wrong.

qwen_context_trace_verifier.verdict_tag: Choosing, not availability, is the bottleneck
qwen_context_trace_verifier.concept_primer: The model writes a short step-by-step recipe to compute each answer. The system generates many alternative recipes and trains a small "picker" to choose the best one, without ever seeing the answer key.
qwen_context_trace_verifier.plain_question: Can a small add-on pick a better recipe by inspecting how each one runs, instead of trusting the model's first draft?
qwen_context_trace_verifier.plain_answer: No. A correct recipe was almost always among the candidates, about 98% of the time, but the picker added only 1 to 2 points on easy prompts and lost ground on harder ones, so the wall is choosing the right recipe, not generating one.
qwen_context_trace_verifier.why_it_matters: The recipes you need usually already exist among the candidates. Effort should go into teaching the picker to tell truly-correct recipes apart from ones that only luck into the right final answer.

key_numbers.label: Easy prompts answered right
key_numbers.sub: picker barely moved the model's own choice

key_numbers.label: Hardest prompts answered right
key_numbers.sub: picker actually made harder prompts worse

key_numbers.label: A correct recipe was available
key_numbers.sub: of prompts had a right candidate to pick

charts.chart_plain_title: Picker barely beats the model, though a correct answer was almost always available
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a prompt type, from easier on the left to harder on the right. Height is the share of prompts answered correctly, so taller is better. Three bars per group: the model's own first choice, the trained picker, and a best-possible bar showing what perfect selection would reach.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-possible bar sits near 98% everywhere while the picker bar hugs the model's own bar, so correct answers exist but the picker rarely selects them.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much of the available room to improve the picker actually captured
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one prompt type. Height shows how much of the available room for improvement the picker captured; the zero line means no change, above zero is better, below zero means it made things worse.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars are only slightly positive on easy prompts and drop below zero on paraphrase-paired and longer prompts, so the picker helps a little then backfires.

charts.chart_plain_title: By task type: small gains everywhere except one, which got worse
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a task type. Height is the share answered correctly, taller is better, with bars for the model's own choice, the trained picker, and best-possible selection.
charts.chart_takeaway: The picker bar edges above the model bar for most tasks but drops sharply on true-or-false tasks, where few possible answers make competing solutions hard to tell apart.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which training signal actually changed the picker's choices
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is a different way of teaching the picker what counts as a good solution. Height is the share of mixed prompts answered correctly, taller is better, with the model's own choice next to the trained picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: Four of five training signals leave the picker identical to the model; only rewarding a correct final answer nudged it above, and only slightly.

charts.chart_plain_title: A rule-based scorer trails the model at every solution length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many steps the solution takes, longer to the right. Height is the share answered correctly, taller is better. Lines track a rule-based scorer, the model's own choice, and the trained picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rule-based scorer line sits well below the other two at every length, ruling it out as a standalone way to pick the best solution.

qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.verdict_tag: Clever test-picking tied random guessing; direct answers won
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.concept_primer: A small model sees a few input-output examples, drafts several little rule-programs, then invents tricky inputs where the programs disagree and grades itself on them to crown a winner — like a cook-off decided by ever-harder taste tests, pointless when every dish is already spoiled.
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.plain_question: If a small model writes little rule-programs from a few examples, can inventing tricky test cases help it pick the one that truly works?
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.plain_answer: No. Answering each row directly solved three-quarters of tasks completely, while the best rule-program the method could pick solved only about four in ten — and a perfect picker that peeks at the answers did no better. The clever edge-case grading tied random cases and even meaningless shuffled labels. The real problem: usually no correct program existed to pick.
qwen_counterexample_guided_ephemeral_pro.why_it_matters: Before building elaborate selection or verification machinery, measure whether a correct candidate even exists among what your model generates. If a perfect picker still loses to the plain baseline, invest in better generation, not smarter ranking.

key_numbers.label: Answering rows directly vs. writing a rule
key_numbers.sub: whole tasks fully correct: direct answers vs. the best program the method could pick

key_numbers.label: A perfect picker that peeks at the answers
key_numbers.sub: even flawless hindsight selection loses to direct answers

key_numbers.label: Tasks the program approach improved
key_numbers.sub: no task got better; ungated programs broke up to 9

key_numbers.label: Clever probes vs. random vs. shuffled nonsense
key_numbers.sub: three selection strategies scored identically

charts.chart_plain_title: Whole-task success: direct answering vs. program methods
charts.chart_read: Bars give whole-task success (every unseen row correct) for seven methods; taller is better. Direct row-by-row and the two fallback-gated program methods reach 75%; batch is 58%; the counterexample program and a perfect-hindsight picker sit at 42%; visible-only is lowest at 38%.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every program-only bar, including a picker that peeks at answers, sits far below direct answering; gated methods only tie by falling back.

charts.chart_plain_title: Clever test-picking scores no better than random or nonsense
charts.chart_read: Bars show whole-task success for five ways of choosing a program; taller is better. Counterexample-guided probes, random probes, shuffled nonsense labels, and a perfect-hindsight picker all land at 42%; using visible examples alone is 38%.
charts.chart_takeaway: Four bars sit at identical height — the clever selector matches random and even meaningless labels — so the picking step is not what limits results.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy climbs only as programs get used less
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method: whole-task success and the share of tasks that actually run a program; higher accuracy is better. As program usage falls from 63% to 21%, success climbs from 38% to 75%.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy rises only as the method leans on its direct-answer fallback and abandons the programs — the programs add no wins of their own.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks helped versus hurt against direct answering
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method count tasks made worse or better than direct answering, out of 24; fewer hurt and more helped is better. The helped bar is zero everywhere; hurt reaches 8 for the counterexample program and 9 for visible-only.
charts.chart_takeaway: No method helps a single task, while ungated programs break up to 9 of 24 — programs can only match or damage direct answering here.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task families the programs break
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per task family give whole-task success for direct answering versus the counterexample program; taller is better. Direct is near-perfect everywhere; the program drops City, EmergencyCall, Log, and Product to zero.
charts.chart_takeaway: Programs match direct answering only on Abbreviation, Name, and Phone and wipe out whole families direct solves cleanly — the failure is broad, not one odd task.

charts.chart_plain_title: Strict all-rows-correct scoring widens the gap
charts.chart_read: Two bars per method: lenient per-row success versus strict whole-task success (every row right); taller is better. Batch drops most, from 74% of rows to 58% of tasks; the program methods trail on both.
charts.chart_takeaway: Requiring every row correct penalizes batch and program methods most, pushing them well below direct row-by-row answering.

charts.chart_plain_title: On broken tasks, almost no program even fits the examples
charts.chart_read: For the eight tasks the counterexample program hurt, one bar counts candidate programs generated (seven or eight each) and one counts how many fit the four visible examples; more passing is better. Seven tasks have zero passers.
charts.chart_takeaway: On 7 of 8 damaged tasks not one candidate fit even the visible rows, so no selection step could have rescued them — the failure is generation.

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-grading agreement does not predict whether a program works
charts.chart_read: Bars show the chosen program's self-grading agreement score for four solved and four failed unseen tasks; a useful signal would be high on solved and low on failed. Instead all four failed tasks score 100% and a solved one scores 0%.
charts.chart_takeaway: High agreement shows up on failing programs and low agreement on a working one — the selection signal is uncalibrated and misleading.

qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.verdict_tag: Fixed-recipe distillation lost to direct answers
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.concept_primer: A model can answer a table one row at a time, or you can try to boil its guesses down into a single fixed recipe applied to the whole column at once, like turning scattered hand-copied answers into one reusable rule.
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.plain_question: Can a model's shaky row-by-row guesses be distilled into one fixed rule that transforms a whole column correctly?
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.plain_answer: No. Forcing one fixed recipe solved just 20% of tasks completely versus 50% for answering row by row, and its supposedly informed guesses did no better than randomly shuffled ones.
qwen_counterexample_guided_projection.why_it_matters: When a task has no clean rule the model can express, forcing one throws away accuracy. Answer row by row by default, and reserve a single fixed recipe only for tasks where a valid one actually exists.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved completely
key_numbers.sub: answering row by row versus forcing one fixed recipe

key_numbers.label: Informed clues versus shuffled clues
key_numbers.sub: no difference, so the clues added nothing

key_numbers.label: Tasks with no usable recipe
key_numbers.sub: left the recipe method nothing to select

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks fully solved: direct answers beat fixed recipes
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; height is the share of tasks solved completely, with every row correct, so taller is better. The left three answer directly; the right three build one fixed recipe.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three direct bars cluster near 50% while the three recipe bars sit at 20%, and real clues match shuffled ones, so the recipe approach lost 30 points.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting most rows right does not mean solving the whole task
charts.chart_read: For each method, one bar shows the share of individual rows answered correctly and a second shows the share of tasks fully solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Direct methods get about 72% of rows right but only half of tasks fully right, while the recipe method collapses on both to roughly 21% and 20%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most tasks had no usable recipe to choose from
charts.chart_read: Bars count the 40 tasks by how many valid fixed recipes were found: none, at least one, or more than one. A tall left bar means the recipe method had little to work with.
charts.chart_takeaway: 29 of 40 tasks had zero valid recipes, so on most tasks the clues had nothing at all to pick among.

charts.chart_plain_title: Recipe method collapses on the biggest task groups
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per task family show three methods side by side; height is the share of tasks fully solved, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the two largest groups, dates and numbers, the recipe bars fall to near zero while direct answers hold 56% and 30%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Row accuracy across the main task groups
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per task family show three methods side by side; height is the share of individual rows answered correctly, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The recipe method drops to zero on several groups while direct methods stay high; combining guesses nudges dates up but drags numbers down.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where recipes existed, there were often many
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one task, and its height is how many valid fixed recipes were found; only the handful of tasks that had any recipe at all are shown.
charts.chart_takeaway: Counts ranged from about two dozen up to 400, one hitting the cap, but this covered just 11 of 40 tasks while the rest had none.

charts.chart_plain_title: The two times the safeguard fired, it never helped
charts.chart_read: For the only two tasks where the safeguard triggered, paired bars compare direct answers against the gated recipe; height is the share of rows correct, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On both tasks the gate locked in a fixed constant answer; one task got worse and the other stayed at zero.

qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.verdict_tag: Contradictory examples teach a model to read its
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.concept_primer: Picture flashcards where the very same word demands a different answer in each deck, so memorizing never pays off — the only way to score is to first read that deck's example pairs. Training on such deliberately contradictory decks forces the model to read the prompt's examples.
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.plain_question: If you train a small model on made-up tasks where the same input can demand opposite answers, does it learn to actually read the examples instead of guessing?
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.plain_answer: Yes. Untrained, a 4-billion-parameter model solved 23% of real text-transformation tasks perfectly; after this training, 57% — but only when it could see the prompt's examples. Scramble those examples and it fell to 17%, below where it started; remove them and it collapsed to about 3%. The entire gain lives in reading the examples, not memorizing a familiar transformation.
qwen_counterfactual_episodic_icl.why_it_matters: To make a small model obey in-context examples instead of falling back on a familiar guess, train it on example sets whose answers can only come from the examples — and always retest with the examples shuffled or removed; real conditioning makes accuracy crater.

key_numbers.label: Real tasks solved perfectly
key_numbers.sub: public text-transformation tasks, untrained vs trained (examples shown)

key_numbers.label: Made-up tasks solved perfectly
key_numbers.sub: unseen contradictory tasks, untrained vs trained

key_numbers.label: Trained, but examples scrambled
key_numbers.sub: real tasks — below the 23% untrained level

key_numbers.label: Trained, but examples removed
key_numbers.sub: real tasks collapse without any demonstrations

charts.chart_plain_title: Whole-task accuracy across four example conditions
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks solved completely correctly; taller is better. Four conditions along the bottom: untrained with examples, trained with examples, trained with scrambled examples, trained with no examples. One color is made-up unseen tasks, the other real public tasks.
charts.chart_takeaway: Trained-with-examples towers over every other bar; scrambling or removing the examples crashes accuracy to near or below the untrained level — winning requires reading the examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-line accuracy under the same four conditions
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of individual output lines matched exactly, not whole tasks; taller is better. Same four conditions and same two task groups — made-up unseen versus real public — as the whole-task chart.
charts.chart_takeaway: The looser per-line scoring shows the identical shape: big gains only when correct examples are present, confirming genuine example-reading rather than an artifact of strict all-or-nothing scoring.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training error falls fast and stays low
charts.chart_read: A single line traces the model's training error (vertical) against training step from 1 to 120 (horizontal); lower is better. Only the model's answer tokens are scored during training, not the prompt examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: Error plunges from about 7 to near zero within roughly 40 steps, then stays flat — the model masters the contradictory tasks quickly and cheaply.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training tasks lift transfer to real tasks
charts.chart_read: Whole-task accuracy bars; taller is better. Groups pair each task set — made-up unseen and real public — across a smaller 240-task run and the larger 800-task run. Three colors: untrained, trained with examples, trained with scrambled examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: Going from 240 to 800 training tasks raises real-task accuracy from 38% to 57% and deepens the drop under scrambled examples — stronger, more genuine example-reading.

qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.verdict_tag: Cheap example drills triple accuracy; twist adds nothing
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.concept_primer: The training puzzles follow invented rules that contradict anything memorized — like a cipher whose key changes every round. The only way to solve one is to decode the worked examples shown in the prompt, forcing the model to read them instead of recalling a familiar format.
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.plain_question: Can cheap made-up puzzles that force a small model to copy its examples teach it to follow examples on real tasks — and is the special recipe why it works?
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.plain_answer: Yes, but not for the reason expected. Tuning tripled whole-task accuracy on real text tasks, from 20% to about 61%, stable across three training runs — and the model genuinely reads its examples: remove them and it collapses to under 2%. But plain tuning on ordinary made-up puzzles reached 64%, so the clever counterintuitive twist added nothing. The lift is generic synthetic tuning.
qwen_counterfactual_icl_public_multiseed.why_it_matters: Before crediting a clever data recipe for a small model's gains, run two cheap controls: tune on plain synthetic puzzles, and strip the examples at test time. Here both showed the win was generic synthetic tuning, not the advertised mechanism.

key_numbers.label: Public accuracy, before vs after tuning
key_numbers.sub: whole-task exact match on unseen real text-transformation tasks

key_numbers.label: Clever recipe vs plain synthetic tuning
key_numbers.sub: counterfactual tuning tied ordinary tuning, so the special ingredient is not isolated

key_numbers.label: Accuracy with the examples removed
key_numbers.sub: the tuned model collapses without in-context support examples

key_numbers.label: Public tasks improved vs worsened
key_numbers.sub: out of 45 tasks, in every training seed

charts.chart_plain_title: Public accuracy: untuned model versus three tuned adapters
charts.chart_read: Bars give whole-task exact-match accuracy on public text tasks with examples intact; left to right: untuned base, the counterfactual adapter (mean of three seeds), the plain-synthetic adapter, and the shuffled-label adapter. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three tuned bars cluster near 60 to 64%, roughly tripling the untuned 20% and indistinguishable from each other, so the clever counterfactual ingredient adds nothing visible.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy depends on the examples in the prompt
charts.chart_read: For each adapter, bars show public whole-task accuracy under intact, shuffled, and removed support examples; colors mark the counterfactual, ordinary, and shuffled-label-trained adapters; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every adapter collapses to near zero once examples are removed, proving they read them, but the shuffled-label adapter still scores 51% on shuffled examples, hinting at learned formatting.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do the training gains reach unseen public tasks?
charts.chart_read: Two evaluation splits — the synthetic training-style tasks and the unseen public tasks; two bars each, untuned base versus the counterfactual adapter, both with intact examples; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The adapter lifts both splits, from 38% to 93% on synthetic and 20% to 61% on public, so the skill transfers to genuinely unseen tasks, not just the generator.

charts.chart_plain_title: Improvement holds across three training runs
charts.chart_read: For each of three training seeds, one bar counts public tasks that improved and one counts those that worsened versus the untuned base, out of 45; more helped and fewer hurt is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every seed improves about 20 of 45 tasks and worsens only one, so the gain is a stable effect rather than one lucky run.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which kinds of public task the tuning fixes
charts.chart_read: Ten public task families across the axis; paired bars give untuned base versus counterfactual-adapter whole-task accuracy; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Structured formats like codes, file paths and currency jump to 100%, dates stay hardest, and the single address task actually regresses to zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: The shuffled-label adapter ignores whether examples are right
charts.chart_read: Four arms on the synthetic split — base and the counterfactual, ordinary, and shuffled-label adapters; two bars each for normal versus shuffled support examples; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most arms roughly halve under shuffled examples, but the shuffled-label adapter holds 92%, evidence it learned output formatting rather than truly reading the examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: Strict whole-task scoring lowers every arm's score
charts.chart_read: Four arms on the public split — base and the three adapters; two bars each, lenient per-row scoring versus strict whole-task scoring where every row must match; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Requiring every row to match costs the adapters about 10 points but nearly halves the base model, so strict scoring widens the tuned-versus-untuned gap.

qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.verdict_tag: Judging attempts without answers stays too noisy
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.concept_primer: The system turns each question into a tiny program it can actually run. This test asks whether it can learn to pick its best attempt just by watching how the attempts run, without ever being shown the correct answer.
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.plain_question: Can the system learn to choose its best attempt by running the options, without being told which final answer is correct?
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.plain_answer: Partly. It found a faint real signal and nudged accuracy up slightly on the hardest questions, but its picks stayed too noisy to beat simply trusting the model's first attempt.
qwen_counterfactual_trace_preference_dis.why_it_matters: Teaching a model to grade its own attempts without an answer key is appealing but fragile. Filter its picks by confidence, or give it richer clues, before trusting them as training material.

key_numbers.label: Picks that were actually correct
key_numbers.sub: when choosing with no answer key; 100% when given one

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on the hardest questions
key_numbers.sub: small nudge from the new self-picked targets

key_numbers.label: Best pick available in the batch
key_numbers.sub: headroom the picker left untouched

key_numbers.label: Accuracy when given the answer key
key_numbers.sub: shows the system can master the task when told answers

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-picked targets give a small bump but stay far below full training
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by the two hardest question sets. Within each group, they compare the starting system, two ways of choosing training examples, and the answer-key ceiling; taller means more accurate.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-picked bars edge just above the starting system, yet all three huddle near the floor while the answer-key bar towers above them.

charts.chart_plain_title: Letting the picker choose does worse than trusting the first attempt
charts.chart_read: For each question set, three bars: the model's first attempt, the picker's choice, and the best attempt available in the batch. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The picker's bar sits at or below the first-attempt bar everywhere, while the best-available bar stands far higher, so the picker leaves the headroom untouched.

charts.chart_plain_title: Examples the picker chose for training are mostly wrong
charts.chart_read: One bar per way of choosing training examples, showing the share that were actually correct; taller means cleaner training material.
charts.chart_takeaway: The picker-chosen bar reaches only about a tenth as high as the two answer-key methods, which are fully correct by construction.

charts.chart_plain_title: Self-picked targets drop accuracy below the untrained starting point
charts.chart_read: One bar per training stage on the paired question set, showing accuracy when the system is allowed several tries; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The self-picked bar dips below even the starting bar, while the answer-key ceiling stands far above both.

charts.chart_plain_title: With an answer key the system nearly masters every question set
charts.chart_read: For each question set, four bars: full training with one try or with several tries, and the starting system with one try or several. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-training bars cluster near the top across all sets while the starting bars stay near the floor, showing the task itself is very learnable.

charts.chart_plain_title: Repair training rarely reproduces the exact intended program
charts.chart_read: For each question set, bars for the starting system, the two repair methods, and full training, showing how often the exact target program is recovered; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every repair bar hugs zero while the full-training bar reaches half or more, so repair barely changes the program the system writes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra clues helped the small pilot but the gain vanished at scale
charts.chart_read: For a validation set and the two hardest sets, bars compare a bare pilot, a pilot given extra clues, and the full-size run; taller means the picker chose better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The extra-clue pilot bar roughly doubles the bare pilot, but the full-size run bar slips back toward the bare level on the hardest sets.

qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.verdict_tag: Showing work barely helps, costs far more
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.concept_primer: Imagine teaching someone a recipe two ways: hand them only the finished dish, or make them write out every step. This tests whether writing the steps teaches the model better.
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.plain_question: Does making a small model write out its step-by-step work teach it procedures better than just giving it the final answer?
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.plain_answer: It depends, but mostly no. On familiar problems, writing out steps beat answer-only by only about 2 percentage points while using 3 to 6 times more words, and on genuinely new step combinations every teaching style collapsed together.
qwen_crystallized_trace_abi_tournament.why_it_matters: For simple, familiar tasks, teaching a small model to just give the answer is cheaper and nearly as accurate. Verbose step-writing does not buy real generalization to new problem types.

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on familiar problems
key_numbers.sub: answer-only versus best step-writing style

key_numbers.label: Words used to answer
key_numbers.sub: answer-only versus the step-writing styles

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on new step combinations
key_numbers.sub: the best style; all styles fell here

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy of each teaching style across three kinds of test problems
charts.chart_read: Bars show percent correct (taller is better) for five teaching styles, grouped by test type: familiar problems, reworded problems, and brand-new step combinations. The untrained model is the flat zero baseline.
charts.chart_takeaway: On familiar and reworded problems the trained styles cluster high near 85-94%, but all of them crash to roughly a third correct on brand-new step combinations.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many words each teaching style needs to answer
charts.chart_read: Bars show the average number of words the model writes to produce an answer for each output style; shorter is cheaper. Answer-only is far left, the verbose structured style far right.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-writing styles use 3 to 6 times more words than answer-only, a large cost for at most a 2-point accuracy gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each style produces a usable answer
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of responses that yield a readable final answer (taller is better), for each style across the three test types. Simple line-based styles versus the verbose structured style.
charts.chart_takeaway: The simple styles always produce a usable answer, while the verbose structured style fails to give one about a quarter of the time.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types break on brand-new step combinations
charts.chart_read: Bars show percent correct (taller is better) for the step-writing style on new step combinations, broken out by task type: text cleanup, unit conversion, table lookup, and date math.
charts.chart_takeaway: Unit conversion stays fully correct while text cleanup and date math drop to zero, showing failures cluster where a new combination of steps is required.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded-problem accuracy by task type and teaching style
charts.chart_read: Bars show percent correct (taller is better) on reworded problems, grouped by task type, with one bar per teaching style. The verbose structured style is one of the four.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most styles stay above 83% on most task types, but the verbose structured style drops to zero on table lookup and date math, failing whole categories.

qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.verdict_tag: Step-by-step editing helps but falls short
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.concept_primer: Instead of writing a whole answer at once, the model works like someone fixing a program one small edit at a time in a scratch machine, running it, seeing the result, then editing again.
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.plain_question: Can teaching the model to solve tasks with small, checkable edits beat having it answer in one shot?
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.plain_answer: It depends. The trained editing agent beat the model's one-shot answers on four of five task types, including the hardest ones, but stayed far below a flawless reference solver, so it is useful, not a replacement.
qwen_dense_state_dagger_vm_agent.why_it_matters: Letting a small model work in small, checkable steps can unlock harder compositional tasks than answering directly, but the payoff caps out unless you also teach it to plan whole edit sequences, not just single moves.

key_numbers.label: Mixed tasks solved
key_numbers.sub: answering directly vs the step-by-step agent

key_numbers.label: Flawless reference solver
key_numbers.sub: ceiling the agent stays far below

key_numbers.label: Task types improved
key_numbers.sub: editing agent beat direct answers

charts.chart_plain_title: Trained editing agent vs direct answers and a flawless solver
charts.chart_read: Bars group each task type by four solvers: an empty-machine baseline, the model answering directly, the trained step-by-step agent, and a flawless reference solver. Taller means more tasks solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained agent's bars top the direct-answer bars on four of five task types, yet sit far under the flawless reference solver.

charts.chart_plain_title: How accuracy changes with the number of editing steps allowed
charts.chart_read: Each line is one task type; the horizontal axis is how many editing steps the agent may take, the vertical axis is how many tasks it solves. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line dips near zero at a few steps then jumps up around eight steps, meaning half-finished edits hurt while enough steps pay off.

charts.chart_plain_title: Three editing skills improve across training stages
charts.chart_read: Bars track three skills across training stages: choosing the right edit, choosing the right value to fill in, and knowing when to stop. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three skills climb across stages, but the fill-in-the-value bars stay shortest throughout, flagging it as the weakest skill.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra practice rounds mostly help the hardest tasks
charts.chart_read: Bars group each task type by training stage: after basic imitation, then two rounds of extra practice on the agent's own attempts. Taller means more tasks solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The extra practice rounds steadily lift the hardest composition tasks from about 19% to 31%, while other task types just shuffle up and down.

charts.chart_plain_title: Quitting too early gets worse with more steps allowed
charts.chart_read: Each line is one task type; the horizontal axis is how many steps the agent may take, the vertical axis is how often it quits before finishing. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All lines rise steeply as steps increase, so the more room it is given, the more often the agent stops early, capping the benefit of longer runs.

charts.chart_plain_title: Two stopping rules trade wins across task types
charts.chart_read: Bars compare two stopping rules for each task type: letting the agent decide when to stop versus a confidence check before it stops. Taller means more tasks solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two rules trade wins across task types, adding a little on some and losing a little on the hardest, so neither is clearly better.

charts.chart_plain_title: Full-scale agent far outperforms early prototypes
charts.chart_read: Bars group each task type by version: two small early prototypes and the full-scale trained agent. Taller means more tasks solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-scale agent's bars tower over both prototypes, and the earliest text-based prototype sits near zero, showing the design changes and extra training were both essential.

qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.verdict_tag: Tie-breaker quiz added no selection power
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.concept_primer: When the model writes several small programs to solve a whole task, you must pick the best one. This test tries a "tie-breaker quiz": invent tricky inputs where the programs disagree and ask the model which answer is right.
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.plain_question: Does a tie-breaker quiz help the model pick a better program than just checking the visible examples?
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.plain_answer: No. The tie-breaker quiz picked programs no better than a random version of itself; both solved 64% of tasks, exactly matching a simpler safety-net rule that skips the quiz entirely. The quiz added no selection power.
qwen_disagreement_probe_program_inductio.why_it_matters: The real win came from caution: use a generated program only when it looks trustworthy, otherwise answer by hand. Skip the elaborate disagreement quiz, which costs effort but changed no decisions.

key_numbers.label: Solved by hand, item by item
key_numbers.sub: plain answering, the baseline to beat

key_numbers.label: Solved with a cautious safety-net gate
key_numbers.sub: use a program only when it looks trustworthy

key_numbers.label: Extra lift from the tie-breaker quiz
key_numbers.sub: quiz gate also 64%, same as a random quiz

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks fully solved by each approach
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of tasks solved perfectly by one approach, from plain by-hand answering, to a raw program, to a safety-net gate and three tie-breaker-quiz versions. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three gate bars sit at exactly the same height, so the tie-breaker quiz matches both the no-quiz and random-quiz gates and adds nothing beyond the simple safety net.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks made better vs made worse than plain answering
charts.chart_read: For each approach, one bar counts tasks it improved over plain answering and another counts tasks it broke, out of 25. More improvements and fewer breakages is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Raw generated programs break six or seven tasks, while the safety-net gates keep the two wins and break none, showing the gate's whole value is caution.

charts.chart_plain_title: Success by task type: plain answering vs the quiz gate
charts.chart_read: Bars pair plain by-hand answering against the tie-breaker-quiz gate for each type of task, such as dates, names, and numbers. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every task type shows two equal bars except numbers, so all of the gate's gain comes from number-formatting tasks and nowhere else.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of individual items answered correctly by approach
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of individual items answered correctly by one approach, counting single items rather than whole tasks. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All gated versions tie near the top while raw programs roughly halve accuracy and scrambled-answer programs collapse to near nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often a program that fits the examples is trusted
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows the share of tasks where a generated program fit the visible examples and was actually used by that approach. Here the bars just describe behavior, not quality.
charts.chart_takeaway: Programs fit the examples on about half of tasks, but the cautious gates only trust them on 40 to 44 percent, and scrambled-answer quizzes trust none.

charts.chart_plain_title: By task type: plain answering vs raw programs
charts.chart_read: Bars pair plain by-hand answering against raw generated programs for each task type, counting individual items. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Raw programs match plain answering on several types but collapse on dates and file paths, which is why they are unsafe to use without the gate.

qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.verdict_tag: Learned hint no better than direct answering
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.concept_primer: Imagine clipping a small custom note to every request a fixed model sees. The note is learned from a handful of the task's own solved examples. The model never changes; only the note tries to steer better answers.
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.plain_question: If we give a fixed model a small custom note learned from a task's own solved examples, does it copy the task's format more reliably than plain instructions do?
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.plain_answer: No. The learned note matched plain direct answering but never beat it: about 53% of tasks were solved perfectly either way. The note did outperform one trained on scrambled examples, so the training itself worked; it simply added nothing over asking the model directly.
qwen_episodic_soft_prompt_task_vectors.why_it_matters: Before investing in per-task learned notes to make a model's output format more consistent, confirm they actually beat plain instructions on your own tasks. Here the extra training paid off no better than simply asking the model directly.

key_numbers.sub: no improvement from adding the learned note

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved perfectly with a scrambled-example note
key_numbers.sub: worse; the learned note did beat this control

key_numbers.label: Single answers matched exactly: plain vs learned note
key_numbers.sub: too small a change to be real

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each method got answers right on new examples
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each method produced correct answers on new examples it never trained on; taller is better. Each method has two bars: the whole task fully correct, and single answers correct. Six methods are compared, including plain direct answering.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain-answering bar and the learned-note bar reach the same height for whole tasks, so the note added nothing over asking directly.

charts.chart_plain_title: Change in fully-solved tasks compared with plain answering
charts.chart_read: Bars show each method's change in fully-solved tasks against plain direct answering, which sits at the zero line. Bars rising above zero would mean improvement; every bar here sits on or dips below the line.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned note's bar sits exactly on the zero line and all the others dip below it, so nothing beat plain direct answering.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training progress: real examples versus scrambled examples
charts.chart_read: Two lines track how far off the note's fit is as training proceeds step by step; lower is better. One line trains on the task's real examples, the other on deliberately scrambled ones.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-example line drops steadily and far lower, showing the note genuinely learned, so the flat overall result is not a training failure.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks made better, worse, or unchanged versus plain answering
charts.chart_read: For each method, three bars count how many of the forty tasks it improved, worsened, or left unchanged compared with plain direct answering. More improved and fewer worsened is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Nearly every task lands in the unchanged pile; the learned note helped one task and hurt one, a wash overall.

charts.chart_plain_title: Change in single-answer accuracy versus plain answering
charts.chart_read: Bars show each method's change in how often individual answers matched exactly, compared with plain direct answering at the zero line; above zero is better. Both the learned note and an untrained note rise slightly.
charts.chart_takeaway: An untrained note rises just as much as the learned one, so the tiny gain comes from having any note, not from training it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Single-answer accuracy at two different test-set sizes
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often single answers matched exactly for each method, measured on a smaller and a larger set of test tasks; taller is better. The two sizes let you check the ranking holds up.
charts.chart_takeaway: Method ordering barely shifts between the two test sizes, and the learned note's edge over plain answering stays tiny either way.

qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.verdict_tag: Short-recipe training carries all the way
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.concept_primer: Think of teaching someone to follow recipes: the model turns an instruction into a short step-by-step program a calculator then runs. We trained it only on short recipes and tested it on much longer ones.
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.plain_question: If you only teach the model to build short 3-step recipes, can it still build correct 12- and 16-step ones?
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.plain_answer: Yes. Training on recipes of at most three steps already produced nearly perfect 12- and 16-step programs, jumping from 61% correct with single-step-only training to 100%; adding longer training recipes bought nothing, and one deeper mix even slipped back.
qwen_extrapolation_bound_abi.why_it_matters: You can keep the teaching material short and cheap rather than hand-building long example recipes. A little compositional practice generalizes far; piling on ever-longer examples is wasted effort and can even backfire.
