key_numbers.sub: model taught only one-step recipes

key_numbers.label: 16-step tasks solved, short-recipe training
key_numbers.sub: taught recipes up to three steps

key_numbers.label: 12-step tasks: before to after
key_numbers.sub: single-step vs short-recipe training

key_numbers.label: Deeper training at 16 steps
key_numbers.sub: longest training mix did worse, not better

charts.chart_plain_title: Long-task success by how deep the training recipes went
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of long programs the model got right; the four groups along the bottom are training diets from single-step-only up to eight-step recipes, with separate bars for 12-step and 16-step tests. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The jump happens between single-step and up-to-three-step training, where bars leap to near the top; the two deeper diets are no taller, so extra depth added nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as the test recipes get longer
charts.chart_read: Each line is one training diet; the horizontal axis is how many steps the test recipe has and the vertical axis is share solved correctly. Higher and flatter is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the single-step-trained line droops as tasks lengthen; every line that saw even short recipes stays flat and high out to sixteen steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Letting the model write freely versus adding formatting guardrails
charts.chart_read: On 16-step tasks, paired bars per training diet compare writing freely against writing with output-format guardrails. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guardrail bars tower over the free-writing bars everywhere, showing the correct steps were already known and messy formatting, not reasoning, was the bottleneck.

charts.chart_plain_title: What the long-task answers looked like, right and wrong
charts.chart_read: For long tasks, bars split every answer into exactly right, a valid alternative that still works, wrong step order or choice, and wrong number used. Taller 'correct' bars and shorter error bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Single-step training produced wrong-step-order mistakes about a third of the time, while any short-recipe training shrank that error to a sliver and pushed most answers to exactly right.

qwen_fastweight_hook.verdict_tag: Extra silent thinking steps did not help
qwen_fastweight_hook.concept_primer: Imagine giving the model a private mental scratchpad and letting it loop through extra silent thinking steps before answering, instead of thinking out loud in words. More loops should mean more careful reasoning.
qwen_fastweight_hook.plain_question: Does giving a fixed small model extra silent thinking steps on a private scratchpad make it better at multi-step arithmetic?
qwen_fastweight_hook.plain_answer: No. Extra silent thinking steps did not reliably improve accuracy on multi-step arithmetic quizzes. A few early gains looked promising on small tests but disappeared on larger rechecks, matching what a do-nothing control also produced by chance.
qwen_fastweight_hook.why_it_matters: Small apparent gains from a new add-on can be pure sampling noise. Always recheck promising results on larger tests and against a do-nothing control before believing an internal mechanism actually learned to compute.

key_numbers.label: Random-guess score
key_numbers.sub: five answer choices, so blind guessing lands here

key_numbers.label: No steps vs. extra steps
key_numbers.sub: bigger recheck: extra thinking did not help

key_numbers.label: Small-test promise, then rechecked
key_numbers.sub: the early gain evaporated on more questions

charts.chart_plain_title: More private thinking steps did not raise accuracy
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy on two quiz sets (everyday problems and harder longer-chain problems) as the model runs 0 up to 8 extra invisible thinking steps before answering. Taller is better; the leftmost zero-step bar is the reference.
charts.chart_takeaway: The zero-step bar is the tallest on both quiz sets, so adding private thinking steps left accuracy flat or slightly lower rather than climbing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Early improvements vanished on a bigger test
charts.chart_read: Bars compare no extra thinking steps against the best extra-step setting, on two quiz sets each scored first on 100 questions then rechecked on 250. Taller is better; the pairs show whether the early lead survives.
charts.chart_takeaway: The extra-step bars lead on the 100-question checks but fall level or behind on the 250-question rechecks, showing the early edge was just luck of a small sample.

charts.chart_plain_title: The step advantage flickered and a do-nothing control bumped too
charts.chart_read: Lines track how much the best extra-step setting beat zero steps across training, in points, for the real system and for a control that never actually trained the thinking loop. The flat zero line means no advantage; above it is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines wobble above and below zero and the untrained control bumps up just as much, so the occasional gains reflect noise, not real learned computation.

qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.verdict_tag: Smart picker didn't beat plain decoding
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.concept_primer: Imagine asking the model to fill in a table several times, then having a trained "picker" read all the drafts and choose the one complete table it thinks is fully correct.
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.plain_question: Can a trained picker choose the fully-correct completed table more often than just taking the model's first answer?
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.plain_answer: No. The trained picker solved 50% of tasks completely, exactly the same as taking the model's first answer, even though a perfect table was available among the drafts on 62% of tasks.
qwen_full_table_consistency_reranker.why_it_matters: Generating extra drafts only helps if you can reliably pick the winner. Here the winning table exists but the picker can't find it, so the bottleneck is selection, not generation.

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved completely, first answer
key_numbers.sub: every row in the table correct

key_numbers.label: Tasks solved completely, trained picker
key_numbers.sub: no improvement over first answer

key_numbers.label: Tasks where a perfect table was available
key_numbers.sub: the ceiling the picker could have reached

key_numbers.label: Winnable tasks the picker captured

charts.chart_plain_title: Fully-correct tables: each picking method versus the best-possible
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks solved completely (every row right) for each method; taller is better. The leftmost bar is the best-possible table available among drafts; the rest are real selection methods including the trained picker.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-possible bar stands well above the rest, while the trained picker sits level with plain first-answer decoding, so the extra machinery added nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting single rows right versus getting the whole table right
charts.chart_read: For each method, one bar shows the share of individual rows correct and another the share of tasks where every row is correct; taller is better. The gap between them shows how strict all-rows-correct scoring is.
charts.chart_takeaway: Rows are about 72% correct but the whole-table bars drop to 50% for every method except the best-possible, showing one wrong row sinks the task.

charts.chart_plain_title: Can the picker tell good tables from bad: real versus scrambled labels
charts.chart_read: For each of four data splits, one bar is the picker's ability to rank correct tables above wrong ones and the other is the same picker trained on scrambled labels; higher is better, and around 50% means random guessing.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real-label bars tower over the scrambled ones near 90%, proving the picker ranks tables well yet still fails to solve any extra tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which data types leave room to improve on complete tables
charts.chart_read: For five data types, one bar is first-answer complete-table accuracy and the other is the best-possible complete-table accuracy; taller is better and a gap between them means untapped room.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the Number type shows a real gap (30% versus 70%), meaning almost all the missed opportunity lives in numeric tables.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which data types get more rows right from extra drafts
charts.chart_read: For five data types, one bar is first-answer row accuracy and the other is best-possible row accuracy; taller is better and a gap means extra drafts recovered rows.
charts.chart_takeaway: Number rows jump from about 62% to 89% with better drafts while Address and City rows never move, showing draft variety helps only some types.

charts.chart_plain_title: Five winnable tasks the picker still got wrong
charts.chart_read: For five tasks where a perfect table existed, three bars per task show first-answer, trained-picker, and best-possible row accuracy; the best-possible reaches full accuracy while higher is better for the others.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first-answer and picker bars are identical and short on every task, meaning the picker chose the same imperfect table and captured none of the five wins.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much the mix of correct tables shifts between data splits
charts.chart_read: For four data splits, one bar is the share of correct tables in the training portion and another in the testing portion; the point is how unequal the two are, not which is taller.
charts.chart_takeaway: Test-side correct-table share swings from 7% to 48% across splits, an instability that helps explain why a good ranker still can't reliably pick winners.

qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.verdict_tag: Reward coaching made the controller worse
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.concept_primer: Picture the model as an apprentice building a tiny program one edit at a time: it first learns by copying finished examples, then gets a round of trial-and-error coaching that rewards correct final answers.
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.plain_question: Does a round of reward-based coaching sharpen a small model that builds programs step by step, beyond just copying worked examples?
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.plain_answer: No. Both reward-coaching methods left the model less accurate than plain imitation did. The coaching produced encouraging training signals, but it scrambled the model's sense of which edit to make and when to stop.
qwen_fuyu_vm_grpo_echo.why_it_matters: Before letting reward feedback rewrite a step-by-step controller, use it only to score or rank candidate moves. A single coaching round can quietly break the model's timing for editing and stopping.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reward tuning lowered accuracy for both approaches
charts.chart_read: Each pair of bars is one coaching approach; the left bar is accuracy after learning by imitation, the right bar after one reward-tuning round. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both right-hand bars sit below their left partner, so the tuning round cost accuracy rather than adding any.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best-of-eight accuracy fell after tuning
charts.chart_read: Two coaching approaches, each with a before-tuning and after-tuning bar; height is the share of tasks solved when the model gets eight attempts. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One approach's after-tuning bar drops all the way to zero, wiping out its earlier best-of-eight wins.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tuning disturbed when the model decides it is finished
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the model wrongly declares the task done, before versus after tuning, for each approach. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The preference approach's after-bar roughly doubles, a sign the tuning scrambled the model's stopping decisions.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model's sense of when to stop dropped sharply
charts.chart_read: Left group is how often it picks the right edit, right group is how often it correctly decides to stop; three bars each for after imitation and after each tuning round. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The stop-decision bars fall from high to under a third after either tuning round, while edit-picking barely moves.

charts.chart_plain_title: Imitation policy barely beats an empty program
charts.chart_read: For each task type, three bars: leaving the program blank, the imitation-trained model, and an exhaustive search that tries many attempts. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model's bars hug the blank-program bars while exhaustive search towers far above, showing the tasks are solvable.

charts.chart_plain_title: More attempts let exhaustive search solve far more tasks
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many attempts are allowed, vertical is share of tasks solved by exhaustive search, one line per task type. Up and to the right is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line climbs steeply toward most tasks solved, so the ceiling is the model's decisions, not the task itself.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trial-and-error tuning broke edit legality; preference tuning kept edits legal
charts.chart_read: For each task type, three bars: after imitation, after the trial-and-error round, after the preference round; height is the share of edits that are legal moves. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trial-and-error bars roughly halve while the preference bars stay at full height, yet accuracy still fell in both.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-part training scores stayed near baseline while real accuracy collapsed
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by training stage; each colored bar is how well one part of the model's move choice fits its training targets. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tuning-stage bars stay near the imitation levels, so the deployment collapse was not a training-fit failure.

qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.concept_primer: Instead of blurting an answer, the model writes a hidden worksheet of steps that a calculator then runs. We tried two coaching styles to make those worksheets hold up on longer problems.
qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.plain_question: Does coaching the model with corrected worksheets make its hidden step-by-step working more reliable, especially on longer and harder problems?
qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.plain_answer: No. Coaching with corrected worksheets made things worse: it roughly halved accuracy on matched problems versus plain step-by-step training, and collapsed on longer ones. The plain training itself, however, held up well.
qwen_hidden_vm_curriculum_repair.why_it_matters: Handing a model patched-up examples can quietly unlearn good habits. A correct worksheet almost always sits nearby, so build a checker that picks it at answer time rather than retraining on patches.

key_numbers.label: Matched-problem accuracy
key_numbers.sub: plain step-by-step training vs corrected-worksheet coaching

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on the longest problems
key_numbers.sub: plain training held; coached model collapsed

key_numbers.label: A correct worksheet sits nearby

charts.chart_plain_title: Corrected-worksheet coaching lags plain training
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy on matched fresh problems for four methods, taller is better: the model's blurted answer, the coached hidden worksheet, the plain-trained worksheet, and the best possible if worksheets were hand-checked.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain-trained bar (72%) towers over the coached bar (35%), so coaching roughly halved accuracy even though a correct worksheet nearly always exists.

charts.chart_plain_title: Plain training degrades gracefully, coaching collapses on long problems
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is problem length (both models were trained only up to length 6); vertical axis is worksheet accuracy, higher is better. Two lines compare plain training against corrected-worksheet coaching.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain line stays high and slopes gently to 30% at length 10, while the coached line drops to near-baseline 9%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Hidden worksheet beats the blurted answer in almost every domain
charts.chart_read: For each task domain, three bars compare the blurted answer, the hidden worksheet, and the best possible with hand-checking; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hidden worksheet beats the blurted answer in every domain except boolean, and hand-checking reaches near 100% everywhere.

charts.chart_plain_title: The coaching phase itself causes the drop
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training step, vertical axis is accuracy on matched problems, higher is better. Both runs are identical until step 700, then only one gets the extra coaching phase.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both runs hit 72% at step 700, then the 220-step coaching phase alone drags accuracy down to 35%.

charts.chart_plain_title: A correct nearby worksheet exists even where the model fails
charts.chart_read: Evaluation splits run from easy to long-and-hard; each has two bars, the model's own guess versus the best correct worksheet found nearby by checking. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even on the longest problems where the model's guess falls under 10%, a correct nearby worksheet exists 88-96% of the time.

charts.chart_plain_title: Coaching erodes exactly-correct worksheets
charts.chart_read: Four training runs, each with two bars: the fraction of exactly-correct worksheets and the fraction with correct early steps. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Plain training keeps 56% exactly-correct worksheets, while coaching cuts that to 18%.

qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.concept_primer: Instead of blurting out an answer, the model secretly writes a tiny step-by-step recipe that a built-in calculator then runs exactly, and the calculator's result becomes the answer.
qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.plain_question: Can a small model be taught to solve many kinds of word problems by writing a hidden recipe a calculator runs, instead of guessing the answer?
qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.plain_answer: Yes. When the model wrote a hidden recipe for a calculator to run, it was right about 78% of the time on fresh problems, versus only 15% when it answered directly.
qwen_hidden_vm_mixed_domains.why_it_matters: Teaching the model to write out an executable recipe, and showing it the full worked steps rather than just final answers, both raise accuracy sharply. More training examples help too; longer-than-trained problems remain the weak spot.

key_numbers.label: Using a hidden recipe

charts.chart_plain_title: Hidden recipe beats plain guessing on fresh mixed problems
charts.chart_read: Each bar is accuracy on fresh problems for one method: guessing directly, a hidden recipe learned from final answers only, and a hidden recipe learned from full worked steps. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-worked-steps bar is tallest at 78%, far above the 15% for direct guessing, showing the hidden recipe is what does the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy by task type: guessing versus hidden recipe
charts.chart_read: Six kinds of problems sit along the bottom. For each, one bar is plain guessing and a second is the hidden recipe; bar height is accuracy, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hidden-recipe bar is taller in every task type, with four types jumping from near zero to between 67% and 88%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Showing worked steps produces cleaner recipes
charts.chart_read: Three quality measures across the bottom, each with two bars: training on full worked steps versus training on final answers only. Taller bars mean better, cleaner recipes.
charts.chart_takeaway: Worked-step bars are taller on all three measures, nearly doubling the rate of recovering the exact recipe, 64% versus 34%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Problems longer than the training examples lose accuracy
charts.chart_read: Two prompt styles, plainly worded and reworded, each with a bar for problems of the trained length and a bar for longer problems. Height is hidden-recipe accuracy, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The longer-problem bars are shorter, dropping to 36% on reworded prompts, so the recipe stretches only partway beyond its training length.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training examples sharply improve the hidden recipe
charts.chart_read: Three runs ordered by how many training examples they saw (160, 256, 512), each with bars for three quality measures. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar rises as examples increase; accuracy climbs from 16% at 160 examples to 78% at 512, so the method is still hungry for data.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training lifts the hidden recipe, not plain guessing
charts.chart_read: Two phases, before and after training, each with bars for plain guessing, the hidden recipe result, and recovering the exact recipe. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: After training the hidden recipe jumps from 29% to 78% and exact recipes from 0% to 64%, while plain guessing barely moves.

qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.concept_primer: Instead of blurting an answer, the model quietly writes out a step-by-step recipe that a fixed calculator then runs and checks — like showing your work so a machine can execute it.
qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.plain_question: Does teaching the model from auto-fixed versions of its own recipes make it better than plain training?
qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.plain_answer: No. Writing hidden recipes lifted accuracy from 9% to 61% versus answering directly, but training on the auto-fixed recipes matched simpler training almost exactly (a 2-point gap that is not a real change) and made long tasks no better.
qwen_hidden_vm_onpolicy_canonical_repair.why_it_matters: Use the checker to pick or rerank good recipes at answer time, not to retrain the writer — folding fixes back in just reproduces plain training and can hurt on longer tasks.

charts.chart_takeaway: The hidden-recipe bar beats direct answering in every task type, and the checker's best-case bar recovers most of what the recipe still missed.

charts.chart_takeaway: The gap between plain and reworded bars widens sharply at longer lengths, and patching cannot close it, so rewording bites hardest where tasks are hard.

charts.chart_takeaway: Recipes trained heavily on fixes raise the patched-best-case bar but drop the raw-recipe bar, trading first-try quality for how much the checker can salvage.

charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars fall off a cliff on the hardest reworded group, so the checker's best-case help vanishes exactly where the model needs it most.

charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained model never writes a perfect recipe, while training lifts the checker-patched best case from 28% to 95% on new tasks.

qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.verdict_tag: Watching its own programs helps only modestly
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.concept_primer: Picture a student who solves puzzles by writing tiny programs, running each one, and watching what happens. This experiment asks whether watching those outcomes teaches better puzzle-solving than just being told which answers were right.
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.plain_question: Can the model get better at solving puzzles by watching what its own attempted programs actually do, beyond just learning which answers turned out correct?
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.plain_answer: It depends. Watching its own programs taught the model to predict how they behave and slightly raised its best-of-many success, but its first-attempt accuracy did not move and stayed far below what complete answer keys reach.
qwen_inpolicy_vm_echo_distillation.why_it_matters: Watching outcomes teaches a model the structure of what its programs do, but not the harder judgment of which attempt is actually right. Sharper training would compare near-identical attempts that differ only in correctness.

qwen_iterative_repair_policy.verdict_tag: Step-by-step fixer nearly doubled correct recipes
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.concept_primer: Think of the model writing a 24-step recipe that mostly works but has a few wrong steps. A small helper checks the result and nudges one step at a time until the whole recipe runs correctly.
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.plain_question: Can a small helper fix a mostly-right recipe from the model by nudging one step at a time, instead of just guessing more?
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.plain_answer: Yes. Nudging one step at a time lifted fully-correct recipes from about 30% to 53% on fresh problems, nearly doubling the model's own rate and slightly beating a helper that rewrote everything at once.
qwen_iterative_repair_policy.why_it_matters: When a model's output is close but not exact, a small learned fixer that edits one piece at a time can rescue near-misses cheaply, without making the model write out its reasoning or generate more full attempts.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixing steps one at a time doubles how often the whole recipe runs correctly
charts.chart_read: Four bars on fresh problems; height is how often the full step-by-step recipe runs exactly right (taller is better). Left is the model alone, then a one-shot fixer, then the step-by-step fixer, then the best a perfect fixer could reach.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-by-step fixer bar rises far above the model-alone bar, roughly doubling correct recipes, and edges past the one-shot fixer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most of the improvement comes from the very first repair step
charts.chart_read: Lines show how often the recipe runs exactly right as more repair rounds are allowed, from zero rounds up to three (higher is better); each line is a different fresh problem set. Rising left-to-right means each round helps.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line jumps sharply after the first round then flattens, showing one fix does most of the work and extra rounds add nothing.

qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.verdict_tag: Small-branch practice transfers to wider branches
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.concept_primer: Think of the model as writing a short recipe of steps that a fixed machine then runs; it only counts as a success if that machine reaches the correct final answer.
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.plain_question: Does a much bigger toolbox of steps, or recipes that split into several branches, break the model's ability to write recipes that actually run correctly?
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.plain_answer: Yes. Showing the model just a few small branching recipes taught it to handle much wider branching, jumping from near-random to about 87% correct, while models shown only straight-line recipes stayed near the floor. A four-times larger toolbox did not hurt straight-line recipes.
qwen_large_abi_nested_compiler.why_it_matters: You don't need to train on every branch size; a little practice on small branching cases carries over to wider ones. But reworded prompts still trip up branching recipes, so test wording robustness before trusting it.

key_numbers.label: Hardest branching recipes solved right
key_numbers.sub: never-shown-branches vs shown-a-few-branches

key_numbers.label: Long straight-line recipes with a 4x bigger toolbox
key_numbers.sub: no drop from the bigger toolbox

key_numbers.label: Reworded branching recipes, best model
key_numbers.sub: different wording still trips it up

charts.chart_plain_title: Practice on small branches carries over to wider branches
charts.chart_read: Left to right the recipes split into more sub-tasks; higher means more final answers came out right. Two lines are models trained on a few small branching examples; two were only ever shown straight-line recipes.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two branch-trained lines stay pinned near the top while the straight-line-only lines sink to the bottom, showing a little branch practice generalizes far wider.

charts.chart_plain_title: A four-times bigger toolbox does not hurt straight-line recipes
charts.chart_read: Left to right the straight-line recipes get longer; higher means more correct answers. Lines compare a small toolbox of steps against one four times larger.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line hugs the top of the chart across all lengths, so quadrupling the toolbox leaves long straight-line recipes fully intact.

charts.chart_plain_title: A guardrail roughly doubles accuracy on the hardest branching recipes
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by how each model was trained; taller is better. One bar in each pair lets a guardrail keep the model to valid steps, the other lets it write freely.
charts.chart_takeaway: For branch-trained models the guardrail bar is about twice the free-writing bar, but no guardrail rescues models never shown branches.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded prompts hurt branching recipes more than straight-line ones
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by training; taller means more correct answers on prompts phrased differently than training. One set is reworded straight-line recipes, the other reworded branching recipes.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best model keeps most of its score on reworded straight-line recipes but drops sharply on reworded branching ones, so wording robustness is unsolved for branches.

qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.verdict_tag: Small model writes exact hidden programs
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.concept_primer: Instead of doing arithmetic out loud in words, the model punches a compact program — a start value and a list of operations — into fixed hidden slots, like holes in a player-piano roll that a separate built-in calculator plays back step by step.
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.plain_question: Can a small model solve multi-step math not by writing the steps out in words, but by quietly filling hidden slots with a program a built-in calculator then runs?
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.plain_answer: Yes, up to a point. It wrote exact programs that a fixed calculator ran perfectly at eight and twelve steps, and its answers matched those programs — so it truly computed rather than guessing, where chance is about one in a hundred. But at twenty-four steps it ran roughly 85% of the chain, then erred late. Producing four candidate programs and keeping the best stayed at chance.
qwen_latent_beam_program_compiler.why_it_matters: Offloading multi-step reasoning to a fixed runtime works for short and medium chains and gives exact, checkable traces. Don't reach for candidate sampling to fix depth — picking the best of four is useless when none are correct. Grow depth gradually instead.

key_numbers.label: Medium programs written exactly right
key_numbers.sub: exact twelve-step program recovery

key_numbers.label: Deepest programs, best vs worst wording
key_numbers.sub: exact twenty-four-step recovery, reworded vs plain prompts

key_numbers.label: Picking the best of four candidate programs
key_numbers.sub: best-of-four never above 4.7%, near one in a hundred

key_numbers.label: Long chain run before failing
key_numbers.sub: of twenty-four steps correct, yet final answer wrong

charts.chart_plain_title: How exact program accuracy falls as programs get longer
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is program length in steps (8, 12, 24); vertical axis is the share of programs written exactly right, higher is better. One line is plain-worded prompts, the other reworded prompts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines sit near 100% through twelve steps, then split hard at twenty-four — reworded holds near 69% while plain collapses to zero, exposing a depth wall.

charts.chart_plain_title: Candidate search failed where one trained compiler succeeded
charts.chart_read: Bars group four training setups on twelve-step plain prompts. Two bars each: the picked answer and the best of all candidates. Vertical axis is answer accuracy, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both candidate-search setups sit at the floor even for their best candidate, while the two single compilers reach 88 to 100 percent — search never held a correct program.

charts.chart_plain_title: Long programs run mostly right but end wrong
charts.chart_read: Three twenty-four-step splits (plain, reworded, matched-pair prompts). Each has two bars: share of fully exact programs and share of the step-by-step trace matched before the first error. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Trace bars all stand near 85% while exact-program bars sit at zero — the compiler executes most steps, then makes a late fatal error.

charts.chart_plain_title: Twenty-four-step wins arrive late in training and do not hold
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training step; vertical axis is answer accuracy on twenty-four-step splits, higher is better. Four lines cross two training runs with plain and reworded prompts.
charts.chart_takeaway: Most lines hug zero; the mixed run's reworded line jumps to 69% only at the final step, and the paired plain line peaks near 23% mid-run then crashes to zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even the best of four candidate programs stays near chance
charts.chart_read: Six evaluation splits (twelve- and twenty-four-step across three prompt styles). Two bars each: the selected candidate and the best of four. Vertical axis is answer accuracy, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every bar hugs the floor, with best-of-four never topping about 5% — the four sampled programs held no correct one, so selection could not help.

charts.chart_plain_title: Balancing worded prompt pairs traded one win for another
charts.chart_read: Four twenty-four-step measures: plain and reworded answer accuracy, plain and reworded trace-prefix. Two bars each: mixed-template training versus balanced paired-template training. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Paired training lifted plain trace prefixes from 49% to 85% but dropped reworded answers from 69% to 3% — a swap with no net gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even the first working compiler favored reworded prompts
charts.chart_read: Two program lengths (4 and 8 steps). Two bars each: plain template versus reworded template. Vertical axis is answer accuracy, which equals exact program recovery here, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At eight steps the plain bar drops to 78% while the reworded bar holds at 94.5% — prompt wording alone shifts reliability by thirteen points.

qwen_learned_active_interrogation.verdict_tag: Handpicked examples tie just grabbing them in order
qwen_learned_active_interrogation.concept_primer: A worker retypes messy data into a clean format, like dates and phone numbers. Before the graded test they may open the answer key for a few practice rows, picked from a pile of six. Does choosing the most useful rows beat opening whichever sit on top?
qwen_learned_active_interrogation.plain_question: When a small model can unlock the true answers to a few extra practice examples before a task, do its own picks beat just taking the first few?
qwen_learned_active_interrogation.plain_answer: No. Unlocking four extra answer keys did raise fully-solved tasks from 63% to 70%, and scrambling those answers sank it to 47%, so real labels genuinely matter. But the model's deliberate picks scored exactly the same 70% as blindly taking the first four in order. A hidden best-possible choice reached 80%, so better picks existed; the model simply never found them.
qwen_learned_active_interrogation.why_it_matters: Before building a smart example-selector, confirm it beats trivial fixed-order and random baselines on your strict all-rows-correct score. Here the model picked by slot position, not content, so spend the budget on more true labels rather than clever selection.

key_numbers.label: Extra true answers help
key_numbers.sub: tasks fully solved: two starter examples vs four unlocked

key_numbers.label: Choosing vs taking in order
key_numbers.sub: model's four picks match blindly taking the first four

key_numbers.label: Scrambling the unlocked answers
key_numbers.sub: same rows revealed, corrupted instead of true labels

key_numbers.label: Best pick that existed
key_numbers.sub: hidden ceiling the model failed to reach

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks solved perfectly under each way of picking four extra examples
charts.chart_read: Bars give the share of tasks solved perfectly, every fresh row correct and higher is better, for eight strategies: no extra examples, model-chosen, fixed-order, random, diversity, entropy, model-chosen with scrambled answers, and the best-possible hidden pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: Model-chosen and fixed-order land level at 70%; scrambled answers sink to 47% and the best-possible pick reaches 80%, marking real headroom the selector missed.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tasks solved perfectly as more examples are revealed
charts.chart_read: Lines track the share of tasks solved perfectly (higher is better) as revealed examples climb from zero to four, one line each for model-chosen, fixed-order, random, entropy, and diversity picking.
charts.chart_takeaway: No line leads throughout; only at four examples does model-chosen rise above its two-example start, and fixed-order arrives at the same 70% point.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where extra examples still fail: date tasks versus number tasks
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare no extra examples, model-chosen, fixed-order, and the best-possible pick (higher is better) on the two largest task types: date and time formatting and number formatting.
charts.chart_takeaway: Extra examples lift date and time tasks sharply, but model-chosen and fixed-order both slip on number tasks, where the best-possible bar shows those gains were available.

charts.chart_plain_title: More examples help date tasks but hurt number tasks
charts.chart_read: Lines plot the share of tasks solved perfectly (higher is better) against revealed examples, split into date-and-time tasks versus number tasks under two picking methods.
charts.chart_takeaway: Date and time lines climb steadily with more examples while number lines fall, so a blended average at four examples hides two opposite trends.

charts.chart_plain_title: Row-by-row scores look close; strict whole-task scores separate them
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per strategy show per-row accuracy (individual rows correct) beside whole-task accuracy (every row correct); both higher is better, shown across eight strategies.
charts.chart_takeaway: Strategies sit within about three points on per-row accuracy but spread ten points on whole-task accuracy, so the strict metric is what actually distinguishes them.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which task types collapse when the unlocked answers are wrong
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per task type compare four model-chosen examples with true answers versus scrambled answers (higher is better); the gap shows how much each type leans on real labels.
charts.chart_takeaway: Scrambled answers wipe out file-path tasks and halve city and number tasks, while phone, language, and user-agent tasks survive on the model's built-in knowledge alone.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model keeps picking the same pool position
charts.chart_read: Bars count how often each of the six pool slots, labeled index 0 through 5, was chosen across 210 selection steps; this is a behavior tally, not an accuracy score, and no slot is inherently more useful.
charts.chart_takeaway: One slot, index 3, is chosen 76 times while the last slot is taken just 5, revealing the selector follows position rather than the examples' content.

qwen_learned_repair_verifier.verdict_tag: The fix exists; picking it blind is hard
qwen_learned_repair_verifier.concept_primer: A wrong program is surrounded by 1,299 nearby rewrites, and one is correct nearly nine times in ten. It is like a multiple-choice test where the right answer sits on the page most of the time, but you must circle it with no answer key.
qwen_learned_repair_verifier.plain_question: If a small model writes a wrong program, can a cheap judge that never sees the correct answer reliably pick a fixed version from many candidates?
qwen_learned_repair_verifier.plain_answer: Partly. Without ever seeing the true answer, the judge lifted correct execution from 30% to 47%. But a checker allowed to peek at the answer key found a correct fix already sitting in the candidate pile 88% of the time, so blind picking claimed only about a third of the fixable errors. Comparing two wordings of the same task and rewarding agreement did better, reaching 51%.
qwen_learned_repair_verifier.why_it_matters: When repairing small-model output, spend on the selector, not the candidate generator: the fix is almost always already in the pool. And if you can pose one task two ways, rewarding answers that agree beats a trained scorer alone.

key_numbers.label: Programs that run correctly, before vs after blind repair
key_numbers.sub: raw model vs a judge that never sees the answer

key_numbers.label: Fixes already sitting in the candidate pool
key_numbers.sub: share where a correct rewrite exists, found by a checker with the answer key

key_numbers.label: Accuracy from cross-wording agreement
key_numbers.sub: same task posed two ways, answers rewarded for agreeing

key_numbers.label: Rewrite candidates generated per program
key_numbers.sub: nearby edits of the original, plus the original itself

charts.chart_plain_title: Blind repair-picker lifts correct programs across three fresh splits
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for three fresh test splits: standard, reworded, and paired-wordings. Each split shows three bars: the raw model near 29-30%, the answer-blind picker at 44-48%, and a checker peeking at the answer key near 87-91%. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The blind picker clears the raw model on every split but sits far below the peeking checker: a real gain, with most of the fixable headroom still unclaimed.

charts.chart_plain_title: On paired tasks, rewarding agreement beats the scorer alone
charts.chart_read: Four selection methods along the bottom: raw model, answer-blind picker, agreement reranker, answer-peeking checker. Two bars each: single-task accuracy and the stricter both-wordings-correct rate. Higher is better; the checker tops out near 88% and 86%.
charts.chart_takeaway: The agreement reranker beats the blind picker on both bars, and its jump is largest on both-wordings-correct: consistency repairs the disagreements the picker itself created.

qwen_lora_parser_compiler.verdict_tag: Partial readable recipe, breaks on long chains
qwen_lora_parser_compiler.concept_primer: Imagine teaching a calculator-in-your-head to "show its work" as a recipe: a starting number, then a list of steps, then the number each step uses. We check whether that recipe can be read straight out of the model.
qwen_lora_parser_compiler.plain_question: Can special coaching make a small model expose its arithmetic as a readable step-by-step recipe, instead of just spitting out a final answer?
qwen_lora_parser_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. The coaching did create a readable recipe that ordinary answer-training never produced: short 4-step problems came out exactly right 33-41% of the time, versus a random-guess level for the untrained controls. But it falls apart on longer problems.
qwen_lora_parser_compiler.why_it_matters: Coaching a model to show its work can genuinely open a window into its reasoning, but only as far as the weakest part reads cleanly. Fix the noisiest piece before chasing longer or fancier problems.

key_numbers.label: Short 4-step problems solved, best case
key_numbers.sub: coached model versus plain answer-training

key_numbers.label: Reading the number inside each step
key_numbers.sub: too noisy to chain many steps correctly

key_numbers.label: Twelve-step problems solved
key_numbers.sub: small errors pile up as the recipe grows

charts.chart_plain_title: Short problems solved: coached model versus plain training
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of 4-step problems answered exactly for each training approach; the two colors are ordinary versus reworded question wording, and taller is better. The two coached approaches on the left stand tall; the two plain-training controls on the right sit near the floor.
charts.chart_takeaway: The coached bars reach 33-41% while the control bars stay near zero, showing the readable recipe comes from the coaching, not ordinary answer-fitting.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy collapses as problems get longer
charts.chart_read: Each line tracks how often the model gets the exact answer as problems grow from 4 to 24 steps; higher is better. The coached lines start well above the flat, near-floor control line but slide down toward it as length increases.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every coached line drops to near zero by 12 steps, showing small per-step errors pile up and wreck long problems even when short ones work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which parts of the recipe the model reads cleanly
charts.chart_read: Each line shows how accurately one piece of the recipe is read as problems lengthen; higher is better. The starting-number line stays pinned at the top, the operation line sits just below, and the argument-number line trails lowest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The argument-number line sits near 80% and keeps falling while the others stay near perfect, pinpointing number-reading as the weak link.

qwen_lora_typed_bytecode_trace_compiler.verdict_tag: Fully worked recipes beat showing only answers
qwen_lora_typed_bytecode_trace_compiler.concept_primer: Instead of guessing an answer, the model writes a short list of steps that a simple calculator machine runs to get the answer. The test is whether showing it fully worked step-lists teaches this skill.
qwen_lora_typed_bytecode_trace_compiler.plain_question: Does teaching the model with fully worked step-by-step recipes, instead of just final answers, make it write instructions a simple machine can run to the right result?
qwen_lora_typed_bytecode_trace_compiler.plain_answer: Yes. Trained on fully worked recipes, the model wrote runnable instructions that reached the right answer about 68% of the time, versus only 15% when it saw final answers alone.
qwen_lora_typed_bytecode_trace_compiler.why_it_matters: When you want a small model to produce runnable, checkable steps, invest in showing it fully worked examples; final answers alone teach almost nothing, and letting it check a few nearby tries adds a further boost.

key_numbers.label: Answer-only teaching vs worked-recipe teaching
key_numbers.sub: share of new prompts solved with a runnable recipe

key_numbers.label: Adding a quick check of a few nearby tries
key_numbers.sub: keeping whichever version matches the answer

key_numbers.label: Plain model vs tuned model, both recipe-taught
key_numbers.sub: the teaching method mattered far more than tuning the model

charts.chart_plain_title: Runnable-answer rate by how the model was taught
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one teaching method; height is the share of new prompts where the model's recipe ran to the correct answer. Taller is better. The rightmost answer-only bar is by far the lowest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The three recipe-trained bars tower over the answer-only bar, showing worked recipes, not final answers, drive the win.

charts.chart_takeaway: The checked color sits 13-20 points above the first-try color everywhere, meaning a correct recipe is usually close by.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is each self-teaching round; the rising line is the self-taught model, the flat line is the full-recipe reference. Higher is better; the flat reference stays on top.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rising line more than doubles from 22% to 48% but never reaches the flat 68% reference, so self-made examples underteach.

charts.chart_takeaway: Found-rate rises while rewrite-rate falls, a sign the model is settling on its own answers.

charts.chart_plain_title: Runnable-answer rate across prompt types, three methods
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by prompt type from easy to hard; each color is a teaching method. Higher is better; every method dips on the hardest group at the far right.
charts.chart_takeaway: All methods sag on the hardest prompts, but full-recipe teaching leads on every prompt type.

charts.chart_plain_title: Runnable-answer rate as training progresses
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training step, the vertical axis the share of runnable correct answers; each line is a method. Higher and earlier is better. The plain-model line rises first.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tuned-recipe line jumps late from 31% to 66%, while the self-teaching line stays low throughout.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many worked examples it takes to work at all
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by prompt type; one color was trained on 128 worked recipes, the other on 512. Higher is better; the 512 color is far taller everywhere.
charts.chart_takeaway: Going from 128 to 512 recipes lifts the model off the floor to 55-71%, showing the method is data-hungry.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the model writes the exact intended recipe
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by prompt type; each color is a method. Height is how often the recipe exactly matched the intended one. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Exact-match rates sit well below correct-answer rates, so the model often writes a different recipe that still works.

qwen_mixed_domain_trace_verifier.verdict_tag: Learned picker lifts a frozen model
qwen_mixed_domain_trace_verifier.concept_primer: The frozen model writes a step-by-step recipe for each task, and several slightly-edited versions sit nearby. A small helper picks the best one without ever seeing the answer, like a proofreader choosing among drafts.
qwen_mixed_domain_trace_verifier.plain_question: Can a small helper pick a better step-by-step recipe from a frozen model, without ever being shown the correct answer?
qwen_mixed_domain_trace_verifier.plain_answer: Yes. The learned helper raised the frozen model's accuracy from 46% to 57%, and cross-checking reworded versions of a prompt pushed it further to 61%, clearly beating the model's own recipe pick.
qwen_mixed_domain_trace_verifier.why_it_matters: You can squeeze more correct answers from a fixed model at answer-time by generating a few nearby recipe variants and training a lightweight picker. But most of the available room stays untapped, so a stronger picker is the next win.

key_numbers.label: Frozen model alone
key_numbers.sub: its own recipe pick

key_numbers.label: With the learned helper
key_numbers.sub: helper picks best nearby recipe

key_numbers.label: Helper plus cross-check
key_numbers.sub: agreement across reworded prompts

key_numbers.label: A perfect picker's ceiling
key_numbers.sub: how much room still remains

charts.chart_plain_title: A learned helper and cross-check beat the model's own recipe pick
charts.chart_read: Each bar is how often a way of choosing a recipe gets the task right: the frozen model's own pick, the learned helper, the helper plus cross-check, and a perfect picker. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The helper and cross-check bars rise well above the frozen model's own pick, but the perfect-picker bar towers over all, showing much room remains.

charts.chart_takeaway: The cross-check bar rises clearly above the frozen model, closing part of the gap to the ceiling for getting both wordings right together.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training on mixed-length recipes recovers more of the available room
charts.chart_read: Two bars per task type showing how much of the gap to a perfect picker each helper closes: one trained on short recipes, one trained on mixed-length recipes. Higher means more room recovered.
charts.chart_takeaway: The mixed-length helper's bars are taller on most task types, especially the harder ones, but even the best bars recover only a minority of the room.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the learned helper beats the frozen model; simpler pickers do not
charts.chart_read: Bars for five ways to pick a recipe — no edit, a prior-based pick, a support-based pick, the learned helper, and a perfect picker — shown for two task types. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The prior-based pick just ties the frozen model and the support-based pick drops below it; only the learned helper rises above, though well short of the ceiling.

charts.chart_plain_title: The helper's practice accuracy is bumpy, so pick the best checkpoint
charts.chart_read: Wiggly lines track each helper's accuracy on a practice set across training rounds, with a flat line for the untrained frozen baseline. Higher is better; the peak is the round worth keeping.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned lines bounce up and down and can dip near the flat baseline, so the single best round must be chosen rather than just training longer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training on mixed-length recipes wins on most task types
charts.chart_read: Two bars per task type: a helper trained only on short recipes versus one trained on mixed lengths, across easy-to-hard task types. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The mixed-length bar is taller on five of seven task types, notably the hard reworded one, at a small cost on two others.

qwen_noisy_row_program_crystallizer.verdict_tag: Folding noisy answers into one rule backfires
qwen_noisy_row_program_crystallizer.concept_primer: The model gives a rough answer for each entry in a list. This experiment tries to distill those noisy answers into one fixed rule, a recipe that must transform every entry the same way.
qwen_noisy_row_program_crystallizer.plain_question: Is it better to distill the model's per-entry answers into one fixed rule, or just use the answers directly?
qwen_noisy_row_program_crystallizer.plain_answer: No. Using the model's per-entry answers directly fully solved 50% of tasks, while distilling them into a single fixed rule solved only 23%, worse even than a scrambled comparison at 25%.
qwen_noisy_row_program_crystallizer.why_it_matters: When a model already answers each item fairly well, don't force those answers through a rigid rule library. A rule is all-or-nothing and a narrow library caps you below the raw answers; keep the direct answers and vote among them.

key_numbers.label: Whole tasks solved, distilled into one rule
key_numbers.sub: 9 of 40 tasks, a clear drop

key_numbers.sub: reshuffling did just as well, so no real signal

key_numbers.sub: a correct answer often existed but went unpicked

charts.chart_plain_title: Folding answers into one rule solves fewer tasks than using them directly
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one method; height is the share of 40 tasks fully solved, taller is better. Left bars use the model's per-entry answers directly; right bars fold them into a single fixed rule.
charts.chart_takeaway: The direct-answer bars stand well above every fold-into-a-rule bar, which even dip below a scrambled comparison, so folding hurts.

charts.chart_read: Two bars per method: one for the share of individual entries correct, one for the share of whole tasks correct, taller is better. Direct-answer methods sit left, rule-based methods and the best-case ceiling to the right.

charts.chart_takeaway: A fixed rule even exists for only 11 of 40 tasks, below the 20 the direct answers already solve, so the rule library is the bottleneck.

charts.chart_plain_title: Whole-task accuracy by task type: direct answers versus one fixed rule

charts.chart_takeaway: On the two biggest families the rule bar collapses far below direct, with date tasks dropping from 56% to 13%; they only tie on tiny, trivially rule-like families.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do the rule-picking methods beat their own scrambled version?
charts.chart_read: Two rule-picking methods on the axis; for each, one bar for the real signal and one for a scrambled control, taller is better. A genuine method should top its scrambled twin.
charts.chart_takeaway: The pseudo-label method beats its scramble (18% versus 0%), but agreement-voting ties or trails its own scramble (23% versus 25%), so its signal is not real.

charts.chart_plain_title: Best-possible answer versus the model's direct pick, by task type
charts.chart_read: Task families on the axis; three bars each for the best answer available in the pool, a table-level best, and the model's direct pick, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On number tasks a correct answer sits in the pool 70% of the time but direct picking lands only 30%, real recoverable headroom the rule approach fails to capture.

charts.chart_plain_title: Agreement among answers does not mean the chosen rule is right
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one task where agreement-voting swapped in a different rule; height is how much of the answer pool backed the winner. All ten shown were wrong on the hidden entries.
charts.chart_takeaway: Vote share ranged from 0 to 100% yet every pick was wrong; on one task a 71% majority even overrode a correct rule.

charts.chart_plain_title: The same loss appears at both 6 tasks and 40 tasks
charts.chart_read: Two run sizes on the axis; three bars each for direct answers, the best-possible rule ceiling, and folding into one rule, taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: At both sizes the rule ceiling trails direct answers, confirming the rule space itself caps performance rather than being a fluke of the small run.

qwen_numeric_copy_compiler.verdict_tag: Marking and copying beats direct answering
qwen_numeric_copy_compiler.concept_primer: Instead of doing arithmetic in its head, the model just marks which words in the problem are the numbers and operations, copies them exactly, and hands them to a hidden calculator that runs the steps.
qwen_numeric_copy_compiler.plain_question: Can a small model run long chains of arithmetic more reliably by marking where the numbers are instead of computing the final answer itself?
qwen_numeric_copy_compiler.plain_answer: Yes. Marking each number and operation and copying them into a hidden calculator solved about 90% of short chains, versus a random-guess 3% when the model simply wrote the final answer. It even held 20% on chains twice as long as anything it practiced on.
qwen_numeric_copy_compiler.why_it_matters: For long step-by-step symbolic tasks, have the model point to the exact symbols and let a real calculator run them, rather than asking it to produce the answer directly. The bottleneck becomes finding the right slots, not doing the math.

key_numbers.label: Short chains solved exactly
key_numbers.sub: writing the answer vs marking and copying

key_numbers.label: Long chains, twice the practice length
key_numbers.sub: still solved exactly at 24 steps

key_numbers.label: Just writing the final answer
key_numbers.sub: never rose off the floor at any length

charts.chart_plain_title: Exact program solving by chain length and method
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by how many steps the arithmetic chain has (4 to 24), height is the share of chains solved exactly right; taller is better. Each color is a different method, from mark-and-copy down to just writing the answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The mark-and-copy bars tower over the direct-answering bars, which stay pinned near the floor at every chain length.

charts.chart_plain_title: Staying accurate as chains grow past the practiced length
charts.chart_read: Lines track the share of chains solved exactly as chains get longer (up to 24 steps, though practice only went to 12); higher is better. Lines compare the two training styles on both normal and reworded prompts.
charts.chart_takeaway: The mark-and-copy line holds around 20% at 24 steps on normal prompts, while the plain frozen version collapses to near zero.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which part of the program the errors come from
charts.chart_read: Lines show the share correct for each piece of the program (starting value, operations, the number arguments, and the whole program together) as chains get longer; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Starting values and operations stay pinned near the top while the whole-program line sags, showing small slips in the number slots are what compound.

qwen_onpolicy_repair_compiler.verdict_tag: Practicing on its own near-misses builds reliability
qwen_onpolicy_repair_compiler.concept_primer: Think of a translator that turns a plain instruction into a tiny step-by-step program a machine can run. We test whether letting it practice fixes its future work.
qwen_onpolicy_repair_compiler.plain_question: Can letting the translator practice on its own near-misses, each nudged into a correct version, make it reliable on brand-new instructions?
qwen_onpolicy_repair_compiler.plain_answer: Yes. Practice lifted the translator from getting about 29% of new instructions right to about 99%, nearly the best any small tweak could ever reach.
qwen_onpolicy_repair_compiler.why_it_matters: A little targeted practice can turn a shaky translator into a dependable one. But practicing on plain correct examples works nearly as well, so the clever auto-fixes are not the only path.

key_numbers.label: New instructions answered correctly
key_numbers.sub: before vs after practice

key_numbers.label: Both wordings of a task correct
key_numbers.sub: same task phrased two ways

key_numbers.label: Auto-fixes alone, no plain examples
key_numbers.sub: still strong but a weaker signal

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct on new instructions before vs after practice
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by test set; height is the share of instructions the translator gets fully right. Three bars per group: before practice, after practice, and the best any small tweak could reach. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: After-practice bars leap from near the floor to almost the top, matching the best-reachable line, so practice nearly closes the gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: Which kind of practice drives the gain
charts.chart_read: Three practice recipes on new paired prompts; bar height is the share answered correctly. Compares mixing plain examples with auto-fixes, plain examples alone, and auto-fixes alone. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Plain-examples-alone ties the main recipe near the top; auto-fixes alone trail behind, so the fixes help but are not uniquely responsible.

charts.chart_plain_title: How reachable a correct answer was, before vs after practice
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by test set; height is the share of cases where a small tweak could reach a fully correct program. Two bars per group, before and after practice. Higher means more fixable.
charts.chart_takeaway: After practice every remaining slip is tweakable to correct, versus only about two-thirds before, because the translator now starts much closer to right.

charts.chart_plain_title: Final accuracy across test sets by practice recipe
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by test set; height is the share answered correctly after training. Four bars per group, one per practice recipe. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Recipes using plain examples stay near the top everywhere, while auto-fixes-only and the small-practice run dip on reworded prompts.

charts.chart_plain_title: Consistency when the same task is worded differently
charts.chart_read: Each group is a way of checking pairs of prompts that mean the same thing; height is the share of pairs handled consistently. Two bars per group, before and after practice. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: After practice, pairs answered correctly on both phrasings jump from about a quarter to nearly all, closing the wording-robustness gap.

charts.chart_plain_title: Leftover fixable mistakes after practice
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by test set; height is the share of programs a small tweak still had to change, meaning still wrong. Three bars per group, one per recipe. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Leftover mistakes are few overall but cluster on reworded prompts, worst for the auto-fixes-only recipe at about one in six.

qwen_oracle_distilled_acquisition_policy.verdict_tag: A trained example-picker loses to grabbing variety
qwen_oracle_distilled_acquisition_policy.concept_primer: You are teaching a small model a text-formatting rule from two worked examples. Before it tackles fresh items, you may reveal the true answer for a few of six more practice cards. A trained advisor tries to rank which cards teach the rule fastest.
qwen_oracle_distilled_acquisition_policy.plain_question: When a small model can reveal the true answers to a few more examples before solving a task, does a trained picker choose better ones than simple rules of thumb?
qwen_oracle_distilled_acquisition_policy.plain_answer: No. The trained picker does read real signal: it beats revealing nothing (50 to 57 percent of tasks fully solved) and crushes a version fed scrambled answers (27 percent). But it lost to simply grabbing a varied spread of examples (67 percent), and even to picking at random (63 percent). Clever ranking underperformed cheap diversity at the same budget.
qwen_oracle_distilled_acquisition_policy.why_it_matters: When you can feed a small model a few extra labeled examples, spend the budget on coverage: choose a varied spread rather than training a scorer to rank which example is best. On small task pools, diversity is the stronger, cheaper bet.

key_numbers.label: Revealing nothing vs the trained picker
key_numbers.sub: tasks where all three fresh test rows are answered exactly

key_numbers.label: Varied spread vs the trained picker
key_numbers.sub: tasks fully solved at the same four-example budget

key_numbers.label: Random picks vs the trained picker
key_numbers.sub: random still beats the trained picker at the same budget

key_numbers.label: Picker fed scrambled answers
key_numbers.sub: control proving the revealed answers carry real signal

charts.chart_plain_title: How many tasks each way of picking examples fully solves
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one policy: revealing nothing, a scrambled-answer control, the trained picker, fixed order, the model's own choice, random, a varied spread, and a best-possible single pick chosen with hindsight. Height is the share of tasks fully solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained picker sits low, above only revealing nothing and the scrambled control; the varied spread and random both top it, so smarter selection did not pay off.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does revealing more examples help each policy?
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per policy compare revealing one answer against four; height is the share of tasks fully solved, taller is better. Policies are the trained picker, fixed order, random, the varied spread, and the model's own choice.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every policy improves with four answers, but the varied spread leads at both budgets and the trained picker never takes the lead at either one.

charts.chart_plain_title: How reliably the picker ranks useful examples, group by group
charts.chart_read: One bar per data group shows how well the scorer ordered candidate examples by their true usefulness; 50 percent is a coin flip and higher is better. Four scorable groups are shown.
charts.chart_takeaway: Three groups rank well above chance, from 74 to 96 percent, but one falls to 44 percent, below a coin flip, so the skill is real yet unstable.

charts.chart_plain_title: Where the picker falls behind, by task type
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars per task type (dates, names, numbers, phones) compare revealing nothing, the trained picker, the varied spread, random, and the best single pick. Height is the share of tasks fully solved; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On number tasks the trained picker drops to zero while every other approach holds a third or more, so its losses cluster in a couple of task types.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scrambling the revealed answers breaks even easy tasks
charts.chart_read: Paired bars per task type compare the picker fed true revealed answers versus scrambled ones; height is the share of tasks fully solved, taller is better. Types run from easy (abbreviations, currency) to hard (dates).
charts.chart_takeaway: Five task types the true-answer picker solves completely collapse to zero when the answers are scrambled, proving the revealed answers carry real information the model uses.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting single rows right versus solving the whole task
charts.chart_read: Two bars per policy: the share of individual test rows answered exactly, and the stricter share of tasks where all three rows are exact. Higher is better; eight policies span revealing nothing to the best single pick.
charts.chart_takeaway: A 10-to-18-point gap separates the lenient per-row score from the strict all-rows score, but the ranking of policies stays about the same on both.

qwen_pairwise_table_judge.verdict_tag: Comparing two tables did not beat first try
qwen_pairwise_table_judge.concept_primer: The idea: instead of trusting the model's first finished table, show it two candidate tables and ask which better fits the examples, then keep the winner, like a judge comparing two essays.
qwen_pairwise_table_judge.plain_question: If the model compares two finished tables and keeps the one it judges better, does it produce more fully correct tables than just taking its first try?
qwen_pairwise_table_judge.plain_answer: No. The comparison step matched the first try and was even slightly worse, 47.5% correct versus 50%. On the hard tables it added nothing, simply picking whichever table happened to be shown first rather than the better one.
qwen_pairwise_table_judge.why_it_matters: Adding a pick-the-better-of-two step does not reliably improve a small model's structured outputs; it tends to follow which option is shown first, so always test for that ordering bias before trusting such a selector.

key_numbers.label: Fully correct tables: first try vs judged pick
key_numbers.sub: the comparison step did not help; it was slightly worse

key_numbers.label: Best table available in the pool
key_numbers.sub: the ceiling if it always kept the best candidate

key_numbers.label: Correct-pick rate when answer shown second vs first
key_numbers.sub: it just chose whichever table came first

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each method produced a fully correct table
charts.chart_read: Bars show accuracy for four methods: the model's first table, its judged pick between two, the best table available in the pool, and a version hand-fed correct rows. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The judged-pick bar sits just below the first-try bar, so adding the comparison step slightly lowered whole-table accuracy instead of raising it.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model just picks whichever table is shown first
charts.chart_read: Two bars for the hard tables where the first try was wrong: left, the correct table was placed second; right, placed first. Height is how often it picked the correct table; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rate jumps from none to all when the correct table moves to first position, meaning the model followed order, not content.

charts.chart_plain_title: Overall pick rate looks fine but is misleading
charts.chart_read: Four bars, one per prompt style, showing how often the model picked the correct table across all comparisons; higher looks better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All bars sit near 80-90%, but that height comes from easy tables where both choices were identical, hiding the failure on hard ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-row accuracy versus whole-table accuracy by method
charts.chart_read: For each method, one bar for how many individual rows were right and one for how many whole tables were fully right; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every method's whole-table bar drops well below its per-row bar, showing one wrong row is enough to sink an otherwise-good table.

charts.chart_plain_title: On fixable tasks a better table existed but went unpicked
charts.chart_read: Ten tasks along the bottom; for each, a bar for the first-try row accuracy and a bar for the best available table's row accuracy; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-available bar towers over the first-try bar on all ten, several reaching a perfect score, yet the comparison step never chose them.

charts.chart_plain_title: A small pilot hid the result the full run revealed
charts.chart_read: Four methods, each with a bar from the small six-task pilot and a bar from the full forty-task run, measuring how often a fully correct table appeared; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: In the small pilot the comparison step tied the first try, but the full run shows it falling below the first try.

charts.chart_plain_title: The single table the comparison step changed got worse
charts.chart_read: One task; a bar for the first-try table's row accuracy and a bar for the swapped-in table's row accuracy; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The swapped table's bar is lower than the original, turning a perfect table into an imperfect one; the only swap it made hurt.

qwen_prefix_state_process_verifier.verdict_tag: Partial-program coach barely moves the needle
qwen_prefix_state_process_verifier.concept_primer: To answer each puzzle the model writes a short step-by-step program. It usually produces a correct program somewhere among its handful of tries, but does not reliably choose that one as its final answer.
qwen_prefix_state_process_verifier.plain_question: Can a helper that scores half-finished programs get the model to choose a correct program more often, without ever peeking at the right answer?
qwen_prefix_state_process_verifier.plain_answer: Barely. The scoring helper nudged accuracy up only a few points over the plain first guess, far short of the answer-checked method, even though a correct program was usually already sitting among the model's candidates.
qwen_prefix_state_process_verifier.why_it_matters: When a model already generates a correct solution but ranks it low, teach the picker to judge whether a half-finished attempt can still reach the right answer, not just whether it matches one textbook path.

key_numbers.label: Everyday problems: chosen program correct
key_numbers.sub: plain first guess vs coach-guided pick, barely moves

key_numbers.label: Harder multi-step problems: chosen program correct
key_numbers.sub: coach adds only a few points

key_numbers.sub: the room the coach failed to capture

charts.chart_plain_title: Coach-guided pick barely beats the plain first guess
charts.chart_read: Each group is one way of choosing the final program, from the plain first guess to an answer-checked pick; bar height is how often the chosen program is correct (higher is better), split into everyday and harder problems.
charts.chart_takeaway: The coach-guided bar sits just above the plain first-guess bar, while the answer-checked and shortlist bars tower far higher, so most of the room went uncaptured.

charts.chart_plain_title: A little trust in the coach helps; heavy trust hurts
charts.chart_read: Left to right the coach's opinion counts more toward the choice (zero means ignore it); height is how often the final program is correct, one line per problem set, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line rises slightly at low weight then falls, so leaning lightly on the coach helps but leaning hard makes picks worse.

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct programs sit in the shortlist but go unpicked
charts.chart_read: For each problem set, the lighter bar is how often the coach's chosen program is correct and the taller bar is how often the shortlist held a correct program at all; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The taller bar stands well above the chosen-program bar in every set, showing the right program is usually present but not selected.

charts.chart_plain_title: More training data helps far more than any picking trick
charts.chart_read: Bars compare a small and a large training set, for both the plain first guess and the answer-checked pick, across problem sets; height is accuracy, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars for the larger training set dwarf the smaller one, so adding training data lifts accuracy far more than any picking trick.

charts.chart_plain_title: The coach learned to judge partial programs, then plateaued
charts.chart_read: Left to right is more training passes; height is how well the coach separates promising partial programs from dead ends (higher is better), with one line for the large training set and one for a smaller pilot.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines climb then flatten near the top, so the coach became a strong judge, yet that skill never turned into better final picks.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model learns single instructions faster than whole programs
charts.chart_read: Left to right is training progress; height is accuracy, with one line for getting individual instructions right and one for getting the entire program exactly right, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The instruction line climbs well above the whole-program line, so the model nails pieces more often than complete programs, the gap the coach was meant to close.

qwen_program_only_executable_abi.concept_primer: Instead of teaching the model to simply state an answer, you can teach it to write a short recipe of steps that a calculator then runs — like showing your work as instructions a machine can follow, rather than just guessing the result.
qwen_program_only_executable_abi.plain_question: Does teaching the model to write a runnable recipe of steps, instead of just stating an answer, help it handle brand-new multi-step tasks?
qwen_program_only_executable_abi.plain_answer: Yes, teaching the 4B model to write a runnable recipe solved 73% of brand-new multi-step tasks versus 44% when it just stated an answer. But the gain swung a lot between training runs, so it is a promising lever, not a finished recipe.
qwen_program_only_executable_abi.why_it_matters: When tasks can be checked by running code, have the model emit runnable steps rather than a bare answer — it composes new procedures better. Watch for overfitting to prompt wording and run-to-run swings before relying on it.
