charts.chart_plain_title: Writing a runnable program beats blurting the answer on new tasks
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars, one cluster per teaching style; bar height is share of tasks solved (taller is better). The three colors are familiar tasks, brand-new multi-step tasks, and reworded tasks.
charts.chart_takeaway: On brand-new multi-step tasks the tallest bar is the program-writing style, clearly above the answer-blurting style.

charts.chart_plain_title: The written recipe is right far more often than the answer it types
charts.chart_read: Two bars per task group; height is share solved (taller is better). One color scores the answer the model typed, the other scores what happens when you actually run the recipe it wrote.
charts.chart_takeaway: On new multi-step tasks the run-the-recipe bar towers over the typed-answer bar, so the model builds correct steps but fumbles the final number.

charts.chart_plain_title: Different teaching styles are good at different kinds of tasks
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by task type (text, unit conversion, table lookup, dates); height is share of new combinations solved (taller is better), one color per teaching style.
charts.chart_takeaway: The styles have opposite strengths: program-writing aces text and date tasks where answer styles score near zero, and the reverse on unit tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded prompts trip up the program-writers most
charts.chart_read: Two bars per teaching style; height is share solved (taller is better). One color is familiar wording, the other is the same tasks reworded.
charts.chart_takeaway: Program styles are perfect on familiar wording but fall hardest when prompts are reworded, while answer styles barely dip.

charts.chart_plain_title: Runnable programs cost about three times as many words
charts.chart_read: One bar per teaching style; height is the average number of words the model writes per task (shorter is cheaper).
charts.chart_takeaway: The program-writing bars are roughly three times taller than the bare-answer bar, the price paid for the accuracy gains.

qwen_progressive_repair_compiler.verdict_tag: The right repair exists but goes unpicked
qwen_progressive_repair_compiler.concept_primer: A frozen model turns each prompt into a small arithmetic program that is often slightly wrong. The system lists a handful of tiny edits that might fix it, then a separate small judge circles the one it thinks will run correctly, never seeing the true answer.
qwen_progressive_repair_compiler.plain_question: If a small model's first attempt at a program is usually wrong, can a separate judge pick the correct fix from a short list of candidate edits?
qwen_progressive_repair_compiler.plain_answer: Partly. The correct fix sits in the candidate list almost nine times in ten, yet the judge finds it only about half the time, lifting exactly-correct programs from 30% to 49% against the 88% a flawless chooser would reach. Generating candidate fixes is easy; reliably recognizing the right one is the real wall.
qwen_progressive_repair_compiler.why_it_matters: When a repair pipeline stalls, measure two things separately: whether a correct fix exists among your candidates, and whether your selector picks it. Here candidates were fine, so invest in the chooser, not more edits, and skip easy warm-ups.

key_numbers.sub: base compiler vs learned selector on fresh paired prompts

key_numbers.label: If the judge were a flawless chooser
key_numbers.sub: ceiling: the correct fix was on the list this often

key_numbers.label: Share of the fixable gap actually closed
key_numbers.sub: 31.8% of the base-to-ceiling headroom recovered

key_numbers.label: Adding a cross-phrasing check
key_numbers.sub: pair reranking beat the single-prompt judge's 49%

charts.chart_plain_title: Learned selector beats the base program but stays far below the ceiling
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for three fresh test splits (standard, paraphrase, paired). Each split shows base compiler (low), learned selector (middle), and a flawless-chooser ceiling (high). Bar height is the share of programs that run to the exactly correct answer; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned selector's middle bar lands roughly halfway up in every split, clearly above the base near 30% but far below the roughly 90% ceiling, so most fixable errors stay unselected.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy climbs only once training reaches the full set of fixes
charts.chart_read: Line of validation accuracy per training epoch for the learned selector, with flat reference lines for the base compiler (bottom, near 32%) and the flawless chooser (top, near 85%). Epochs 1-3 use one-edit sets, 4-7 medium, 8-18 the full two-edit space; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The line hugs the base rate flat through the small and medium stages, then jumps only after full-space training begins around epoch eight: the warm-up bought nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: On paired prompts, a cross-phrasing check beats the single-prompt judge
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars on the paired split for three measures: exact-run accuracy, both-phrasings-correct, and cross-phrasing consistency. Four bars each compare base compiler, learned selector, pair reranking, and the flawless-chooser ceiling; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Pair reranking tops the single-prompt selector on every measure and, unlike it, lifts consistency above the base rate rather than dropping it below (71% to 57%).

qwen_public_prose_abi_gate.verdict_tag: Fixed recipe toolbox covers too few tasks
qwen_public_prose_abi_gate.concept_primer: Picture a fixed toolbox of text-editing recipes (trim, split, capitalize). We froze the toolbox, then tested whether its recipes could reproduce an outside library of 309 real spreadsheet-cleanup tasks.
qwen_public_prose_abi_gate.plain_question: Can a fixed, pre-built set of text-transformation recipes handle an independent public collection of everyday spreadsheet-cleanup tasks?
qwen_public_prose_abi_gate.plain_answer: No. Even the richest fixed toolbox reliably reproduced only about 19% of the 309 outside tasks; for roughly 77% no recipe fit at all. The toolbox is simply too narrow.
qwen_public_prose_abi_gate.why_it_matters: A frozen recipe library will not generalize to messy real-world text tasks. The model itself can often infer the transformation from a few examples, so the fix is exposing richer operations, not adding recipes blindly.

key_numbers.label: Tasks with no matching recipe
key_numbers.sub: no recipe even fit the worked examples

charts.chart_plain_title: Even the richest toolbox solves under a fifth of tasks
charts.chart_read: Each group is one toolbox size (small to largest). Bars split every task into solved, a recipe that only matched the examples by coincidence, and no recipe fit at all; taller solved bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The solved slice never rises above roughly a fifth while the tall 'no recipe fit' bar dominates every toolbox, meaning tasks are simply out of reach.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model answers most tasks the toolbox cannot
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the model, given a few examples, produced the exact right answer, for all sampled tasks, only tasks the toolbox missed, and only tasks it covered. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The model still nails about two-thirds of the tasks the toolbox misses, showing the shortfall is the fixed recipes, not the transformations being impossible.

charts.chart_plain_title: Simple text edits work; date rounding and ranges do not
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a type of text operation; height is the share of tasks of that type the best toolbox solved. Left types are simple surface edits, right types are date and range math; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Coverage falls from roughly two-in-five for plain substring edits to zero for date-range and rounding tasks, marking exactly where the toolbox runs out.

charts.chart_plain_title: Structured families work; dates and numbers barely do
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a task family (like dates, phone numbers, emails); height is the share of that family the best toolbox solved. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Neat families like shipping codes and emails reach half or more, while the two biggest families, dates and numbers, sit near the bottom, dragging the overall total down.

charts.chart_plain_title: Failures are missing recipes, not lucky-but-wrong ones
charts.chart_read: For each toolbox size, bars split all tasks into solved, a recipe matching only by coincidence, and no recipe fit. The coincidence sliver being tiny is the point.
charts.chart_takeaway: The coincidence slice stays near 3% while 'no recipe fit' fills roughly four-fifths, so failures are genuinely missing tools, not overfit guesses.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model stumbles most on date tasks
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a task family in the sampled set; height is how often the model gave the exact right answer from examples. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Names and phone numbers hit 100% while dates fall to half, the same family where the fixed toolbox is weakest.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trying vastly more recipes barely helps
charts.chart_read: Each bar is a toolbox size; height is how many candidate recipes it sifts through per task. Note that a taller bar means far more work, not better results.
charts.chart_takeaway: The largest toolbox searches over 500 times more recipes than the smallest yet lifts tasks solved only from 13% to 19%, so search size is not the bottleneck.

qwen_python_shaped_silent_executor.verdict_tag: Private thinking slots did no real thinking
qwen_python_shaped_silent_executor.concept_primer: The model runs a short arithmetic program using a fixed set of blank, silent hidden steps, then reports only the final answer with no work shown. The check: shuffle those steps. If scrambling their order changes nothing, no real step-by-step computing ever happened inside them.
qwen_python_shaped_silent_executor.plain_question: Can a small model learn to run little multi-step programs entirely in its head, using private silent thinking steps, instead of writing out its work as text?
qwen_python_shaped_silent_executor.plain_answer: No. The silent hidden steps never cleared single digits: about 8% at best on the simplest programs, 4.5% on unseen longer ones, barely above a zero untrained model and near the 3% you would get by guessing. The tell: scrambling the steps' order worked just as well, so no real step-by-step computing happened. Even visible written reasoning, which hit 62.5% on the shortest programs, crashed to 0% as they grew longer.
qwen_python_shaped_silent_executor.why_it_matters: Do not expect extra hidden thinking tokens to buy a small model real multi-step execution. Always run a scrambled-order control: if shuffling the hidden steps does not hurt accuracy, you added raw capacity, not reasoning. Test on unseen program lengths, not trained ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy by program length: written steps versus silent hidden steps
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program length (4, 8, 16, 24 steps). Height is final-answer accuracy; taller is better. Three bars per group: explicit written-out reasoning, silent ordered hidden steps, and silent shuffled-order hidden steps.
charts.chart_takeaway: Written reasoning towers at 62.5% on the shortest programs then falls to zero by length 16, while both silent arms stay stuck in single digits everywhere, nearly identical.

charts.chart_plain_title: More hidden steps on unseen long programs: ordered versus scrambled
charts.chart_read: On unseen 32-step programs. The horizontal axis is the number of private hidden compute steps granted (4 to 32); the vertical axis is final-answer accuracy, higher is better. One line ordered, one scrambled-order.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines wander between 3% and 5% with no upward trend as steps increase; the scrambled control even peaks highest at 4.9%, so ordered steps buy nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: More hidden steps on unfamiliar operation mixes does not help
charts.chart_read: On programs combining operations never mixed during training. The horizontal axis is the number of private hidden compute steps (4 to 32); the vertical axis is final-answer accuracy, higher is better. Ordered versus scrambled-order steps.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines flatline near 3% at every step count; piling on hidden compute never recovers execution when the operation mix is new.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much accuracy swings from the training random seed alone
charts.chart_read: Bars group by unseen split (length 32, composition 16, composition 24). Height is final-answer accuracy, higher is better. Three bars per group, one for each of three training seeds.
charts.chart_takeaway: On the same split accuracy swings from about 1% to over 8% just by changing the random seed, a lottery as large as any effect the method produces.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trained silent models barely clear a zero untrained baseline
charts.chart_read: Bars group by five unseen splits. Height is final-answer accuracy, higher is better. Three bars per group: the untrained frozen model, the trained ordered arm, and the trained scrambled arm.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained baseline sits at exactly zero; both trained arms add only 3 to 5 points and match each other everywhere, so training bought almost nothing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tokens spent per answer: written steps versus silent hidden steps
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program length (4, 8, 16, 24). Height is mean output tokens per problem; fewer is cheaper. Two bars: explicit written-out reasoning versus silent hidden-step execution.
charts.chart_takeaway: Written reasoning emits roughly 750 tokens per answer against 1 for silent, yet still hits 0% on the longer programs, so the cost bought no durable accuracy.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training loss never settles over 500 steps
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training step (1 to 500); the vertical axis is total training loss, lower is better. Three lines, one per training seed. A learning model would trend steadily downward.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three curves stay high and jagged between 6 and 8 with no downward trend, showing the silent-execution objective was never actually learned.

qwen_readable_candidate_verifier.verdict_tag: Reading fixes helps, but only weakly
qwen_readable_candidate_verifier.concept_primer: Picture a proofreader handed several rewrites of a broken recipe, each spelled out plainly, and asked to pick the one that actually works just by reading them.
qwen_readable_candidate_verifier.plain_question: If we show a small model each candidate fix written out as readable code, can it reliably pick the one that actually works?
qwen_readable_candidate_verifier.plain_answer: Somewhat. Letting the model read each candidate fix beats leaving the program untouched, but only by about 7 points (44% to 51%), closing just 15% of the distance to a perfect chooser. Piling on more detail actually made it worse.
qwen_readable_candidate_verifier.why_it_matters: Rendering candidate fixes as plain code lets a small model pick a little better than doing nothing, but the gain is thin and adding execution detail backfires, so keep the reader's input lean.

key_numbers.label: Correct picks, no repair
key_numbers.sub: leaving the program as-is

key_numbers.label: Correct picks, best reader
key_numbers.sub: trained reader with a safety fallback

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each method picks a working fix
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each method lands on a working fix on the standard set; taller is better. Left to right runs from doing nothing, through simple voting and reader-based pickers, to two ceilings that always grab the best available fix.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best reader-based bar sits just above the no-repair bar and far below both ceiling bars, so reading helps only a little.

charts.chart_plain_title: Giving the reader more context, and its accuracy
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the reader's accuracy as it is handed more information, from just the two programs on the left to programs plus execution text, or the answer alone, on the right; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars fall steeply from left to right, collapsing from about half correct to near zero, so extra execution text distracts rather than informs.

charts.chart_read: Grouped bars for each test set show no-repair, the trained reader-picker, and the best-possible chooser; taller is better. Compare the middle bar against the left one within each group.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-picker bar meets or tops the no-repair bar in every group yet never reaches the ceiling bar, a small but consistent gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Training error: real labels vs shuffled labels
charts.chart_read: Lines track training error over repeated passes through the data; lower is better. Separate lines compare the reader given real labels against one given shuffled, meaningless labels.
charts.chart_takeaway: Real-label lines slope downward while the shuffled-label line stays flat and high, showing the picker learns genuine signal rather than noise.

qwen_real_task_abi_coverage_gate.verdict_tag: Fixed kit covers most, not all
qwen_real_task_abi_coverage_gate.concept_primer: Think of a fixed kit of prebuilt parts, like a small box of standard LEGO bricks. The question is whether everyday tasks can be assembled from bricks already in the box, without inventing new ones.
qwen_real_task_abi_coverage_gate.plain_question: Can a fixed set of prebuilt data-cleanup building blocks handle everyday office tasks it was not designed for?
qwen_real_task_abi_coverage_gate.plain_answer: It depends: the full kit built 84% of realistic office tasks from prebuilt parts alone, far above the 21% a bare kit managed, but every task deliberately designed to fall outside the kit's parts failed completely.
qwen_real_task_abi_coverage_gate.why_it_matters: If your work stays within common transforms like cleaning names, dates, and prices, a fixed kit is a solid base. For unusual logic, plan to add new parts or a way to fetch them rather than trusting the kit alone.

charts.chart_plain_title: Bigger toolkit and more combining steps solve more tasks
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of 38 everyday tasks assembled from prebuilt parts, grouped left-to-right by how rich the toolkit is, with two shades for how many combining steps were allowed. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars climb steadily rightward, from a short bar for the bare toolkit to the tallest for the full one, so a richer parts library solves far more tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: Common tasks fully solved, out-of-scope tasks all fail
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one group of tasks; height is the share solved from prebuilt parts, so taller is better. The last bar holds tasks deliberately designed to need parts the toolkit lacks.
charts.chart_takeaway: The first four bars sit at or near full height while the final out-of-scope bar drops to zero, showing the toolkit handles familiar work but nothing beyond its parts.

charts.chart_read: Three bars show what happened to the 38 tasks with the full toolkit: solved correctly, solved only on the shown examples, or never assembled at all. The tall solved bar is best.
charts.chart_takeaway: One tall bar dominates at 84%, with two small failure bars, meaning a few tasks matched only the shown examples and a few could not be built at all.

qwen_recurrent_vm_repair_policy.concept_primer: Instead of writing an answer once, the model works like a student who drafts, checks the result, makes one small fix, checks again, and repeats until it decides to stop.
qwen_recurrent_vm_repair_policy.plain_question: Does letting the model make repeated small fixes to its own answer beat writing the answer in one shot?
qwen_recurrent_vm_repair_policy.plain_answer: Yes, partly. The fix-and-retry loop lifted correct answers to about two-to-three times the one-shot rate on every test set, but it still fell far short of what an ideal fixer or a fully trained version reached.
qwen_recurrent_vm_repair_policy.why_it_matters: Spending extra compute on guided self-repair is a real lever for a small model, but the payoff is capped by how well the fixing skill is trained, not by the task being out of reach.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fix-and-retry versus one-shot and the best-possible ceilings
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars across five test sets; height is the share of correct answers, so taller is better. Each set compares one-shot answers, the learned fix-and-retry loop, a perfect fixer, and a fully trained version.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fix-and-retry bar sits two-to-three times above the one-shot bar on every set, yet stays well below the perfect-fixer and fully-trained bars, showing most headroom is unused.

charts.chart_plain_title: Practice rounds sharpen the model's own fixing
charts.chart_read: Lines rising left to right over successive practice rounds; height is a success rate, so up is better. Separate lines track how often its own fixes worked, how often it got the numbers right, and overall answer accuracy.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines climb steadily across rounds, with number-accuracy rising most, showing that repeatedly correcting the model on its own mistakes is what drives the gains.

qwen_recursive_ephemeral_program_inducti.verdict_tag: Recipe-writing helps only with a safety gate
qwen_recursive_ephemeral_program_inducti.concept_primer: Instead of answering each row by hand, the model can write a small reusable recipe from a few worked examples, then run that recipe on every remaining row.
qwen_recursive_ephemeral_program_inducti.plain_question: Does having the model write a reusable recipe from a few examples beat just answering each row directly?
qwen_recursive_ephemeral_program_inducti.plain_answer: It depends. Left alone, the recipes backfire, solving fewer whole tasks than plain answering (40% versus 56%). But adding a safety gate that keeps a recipe only when it looks trustworthy, and otherwise answers directly, lifts success to 64%.
qwen_recursive_ephemeral_program_inducti.why_it_matters: Don't trust a machine-written recipe just because it fits the examples you showed it. Keep a fallback to plain answering and only use the recipe when it clearly isn't just memorizing.

charts.chart_takeaway: The gated recipe is the tallest bar for whole-task success, edging out plain answering, while raw recipes and the scrambled control fall far below both.

charts.chart_takeaway: Raw recipes break six or seven tasks to win two, but the gated version keeps both wins with a zero-height broken bar.

charts.chart_read: Each group is a task type (dates, numbers, names, and so on); bars compare plain answering, raw recipes, and the gated recipe. Taller is better. Raw recipes climb on Number tasks but collapse to zero on dates, file paths, and billing codes.

charts.chart_read: Each method has two bars: the share of individual rows correct and the share of whole tasks correct with no mistakes anywhere. Taller is better. Plain answering scores high on rows but drops sharply once every row in a task must be right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Plain answering loses a big chunk moving from rows to perfect whole tasks, while recipe methods stay low on both because they tend to miss an entire task at once.

charts.chart_plain_title: Fitting the shown examples does not mean solving new rows
charts.chart_read: For each recipe method, one bar shows how often a recipe matched the few shown examples and the other how often it solved the whole task on new rows. A recipe that truly works would have both bars high. The scrambled control fits examples yet solves almost nothing.
charts.chart_takeaway: Raw recipes match the shown examples about half the time but solve fewer new tasks, and the scrambled control fits examples while solving almost nothing, exposing false matches.

qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.verdict_tag: Locking in one recipe hurts accuracy
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.concept_primer: Instead of solving each item fresh, you can study a few worked examples, write one reusable recipe, and apply that same recipe to every remaining item. This tests whether the recipe route wins.
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.plain_question: Does writing one reusable recipe first and applying it to every item beat solving each item on its own?
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.plain_answer: No. Committing to one reusable recipe did worse than solving items one at a time: individual-item accuracy fell from 69% to 59%, and a recipe borrowed from a different task fell further still.
qwen_recursive_task_decomposition.why_it_matters: Let the model reason case by case rather than forcing it to commit to a single rule up front. Locking in one recipe throws away useful item-by-item judgment, and checking the recipe against the practice examples cannot catch this.

charts.chart_plain_title: Writing one recipe first does not beat solving items one at a time
charts.chart_read: Three approaches along the bottom: solve each item fresh, commit to one recipe first, or borrow a recipe from another task. Taller bars are better; one bar counts getting every item in a task right, the other counts individual items right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars are tallest for solving items fresh; committing to one recipe drops individual-item accuracy to 59%, and a borrowed recipe drops lower still.

charts.chart_plain_title: Breaking tasks into steps raises the ceiling, not what you can actually pick
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks a fixed formula library solves perfectly, taller is better: a simple one-shot formula and a step-by-step version, each shown as its best-possible pick and the pick chosen from the examples, plus a scrambled-labels control.
charts.chart_takeaway: Step-by-step lifts the best-possible score from 29% to 34%, but the version you can actually choose from examples ties the simple one at 23%, and the scrambled control collapses to near zero.

charts.chart_takeaway: Even after keeping the best-fitting recipe, the recipe approach stays below solving items one at a time, so more recipe tries did not help.

charts.chart_read: For each type of task, two bars show the best-possible perfect-task rate: a one-shot formula versus a step-by-step formula. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Step-by-step jumps to 100% on cleanly delimited types like logs and URLs, while dates and numbers barely move.

charts.chart_plain_title: Picking a recipe from a few examples loses most of the step-by-step gain
charts.chart_read: For the largest task types, two bars: the best step-by-step recipe available versus the one actually chosen from the few examples. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Example-picking loses the entire gain on addresses and cities and a large share on names and phones, keeping full coverage only on emails.

charts.chart_plain_title: Locking a recipe rescues two tasks but wrecks several the model already solved
charts.chart_read: For each task where locking a recipe changed things, two bars compare individual-item accuracy: solving fresh versus committing to one recipe. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Locking rescues two date tasks but collapses number tasks the model had solved outright, dropping some from 100% to 17% or even 0%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Matching the practice examples cannot tell which recipes will fail
charts.chart_read: For each task, two bars: how perfectly the recipe matched the practice examples versus how well it then worked on new items. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every recipe scored a perfect match on the practice examples yet many scored zero on new items, so fitting the examples cannot flag a recipe that will fail.

qwen_register_structured_runtime.verdict_tag: Nearly-right steps compound into wrong long answers
qwen_register_structured_runtime.concept_primer: The model never says the answer. It writes a step-by-step arithmetic program into a fixed row of hidden memory slots — a starting number, then one operation each — and a fixed calculator runs the slots in order, blindly, unable to notice or fix a wrong one.
qwen_register_structured_runtime.plain_question: Can a small model secretly write a step-by-step math program into hidden memory slots and have a fixed calculator run it correctly, even for long chains?
qwen_register_structured_runtime.plain_answer: Only up to a point. For chains of four to twelve steps the hidden program runs flawlessly, at 100 percent. But at 24 steps exact execution collapses to 25 percent — versus about 1 percent from pure guessing. The catch: each individual step is 80 to 94 percent right, yet a single wrong step anywhere breaks the whole answer, so mostly-right steps still add up to a mostly-wrong long program.
qwen_register_structured_runtime.why_it_matters: If you route reasoning through hidden slots run by a fixed engine, expect a hard length cliff: 90-percent-per-step still fails most long chains. Measure whole-chain exactness, not per-step accuracy, and add a repair pass over long programs before trusting them.

charts.chart_takeaway: Every trained bar beats its control — 25 versus 4 percent on standard wording — yet all stay low, so long-chain execution is helped but far from solved.

charts.chart_read: Each line follows one prompt type — standard, reworded, paired — as program length grows from 4 to 24 steps along the horizontal axis. Vertical axis is the share of chains executed exactly right; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines hug 100 percent through 12 steps, then plunge to between 5 and 25 percent at 24 — a sharp cliff, not a gentle slope.

charts.chart_read: Bars for each piece of a 24-step standard program: starting number, operation choices, operation inputs, running-state prefix, full program, and final answer. Height is accuracy; higher is better; the left pieces are easiest.
charts.chart_takeaway: The starting number is perfect and each piece scores 80 to 94 percent, but full-program and final accuracy crash to 25 percent as small errors compound.

qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.verdict_tag: Almost every step right, whole program still wrong
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.concept_primer: The model writes a step-by-step calculation into a fixed row of blank slots — a starting number, then one operation and one number per step. A hidden calculator reads only those slots, never the original question, and runs them exactly.
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.plain_question: Can a small model quietly encode a runnable step-by-step calculation into a fixed set of hidden slots, so a separate calculator can then execute it correctly?
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.plain_answer: Only for short chains. Up to twelve steps it builds the correct hidden program about nine times in ten, while stripped-down versions trained on the final answer alone never find the interface and stay at chance. But at twenty-four steps whole-program accuracy crashes to 22 percent — each slot is still about 90 to 97 percent right, yet many near-misses multiply into failure.
qwen_register_token_latent_compiler.why_it_matters: When you route reasoning through predicted or latent steps, per-step accuracy flatters you — measure whole-chain correctness and agreement across rephrasings. For reliable long chains, train directly against full-trajectory errors, not just the final answer.

charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines sit near 90 to 95 percent through twelve steps, then plunge at twenty-four — standard to 22 percent, reworded to 3 percent: short chains work, long ones break.

charts.chart_takeaway: The trained run tops every split (22, 3, 13 percent) while both controls sit near chance (0 to 3 percent): step-by-step training, not final-answer training, built the interface.

charts.chart_takeaway: The first three bars stand near 90 to 97 percent, but whole-program and executed-answer bars crater to about 22 and 3 percent: all twenty-four parts must land at once.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training step (1 to 600); the vertical axis is executed-answer accuracy, higher is better. Four lines track chain lengths 4, 8, 12, and 24 as the curriculum lengthens the chains.
charts.chart_takeaway: All lines hug zero until step 300, then short and medium lengths leap together toward 90 percent while length twenty-four crawls, reaching only 22 percent by the end.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the final recipe moved long-chain accuracy off zero

charts.chart_takeaway: Five pilots all stalled at 0 to 3 percent; only the final 600-step curriculum run with adapters and step-by-step supervision reached 22 percent.

charts.chart_takeaway: Both rates hold near 98 to 99 percent through twelve steps, then crash to about 3 percent at twenty-four: identical tasks now compile to different hidden programs.

qwen_register_trace_refiner.verdict_tag: Even a perfect picker can't rescue a too
qwen_register_trace_refiner.concept_primer: A small model rewrites each math word problem as a tiny step-by-step program a calculator runs exactly. When the program is wrong, a repair loop swaps one or two steps for runner-up guesses and keeps whichever runs cleanest, never seeing the answer. A badly wrong program needs more swaps than that.
qwen_register_trace_refiner.plain_question: If a small model turns a math word problem into a little program and gets it wrong, can trying a batch of nearby edits and keeping the best one fix it?
qwen_register_trace_refiner.plain_answer: Rarely. Even a flawless picker that always grabbed the correct edit reached only 37% on plainly worded problems and 7% on reworded ones, because the correct program usually isn't among the roughly 1,300 nearby edits at all. The trained picker did worse, nudging 23% to 27% and leaving reworded problems stuck near 5%.
qwen_register_trace_refiner.why_it_matters: Before training a scorer to rescue a small model's outputs, first check whether a perfect chooser could even win: the correct answer must exist in your candidate set. If it rarely does, widen or steer the search instead of tuning the scorer.

charts.chart_takeaway: The repair loop barely clears the raw model and adds nothing on reworded problems, while the perfect-picker bar towers over both, so the ceiling, not the scorer, is the wall.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the correct program even exists among the tried edits
charts.chart_read: One bar per split: training, validation, fresh plainly worded, fresh reworded, fresh matched pairs. Height is the share of problems whose correct program appears somewhere among the roughly 1,300 nearby edits; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even on plainly worded problems the correct fix is present only about a third of the time, and on reworded ones almost never (7%), which caps any picker.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of the reachable gain the repair loop actually captures
charts.chart_read: One bar per fresh split: plainly worded, reworded, matched pairs. Height is the fraction of the gap between raw accuracy and perfect-picker accuracy that the trained repair loop closed; higher means more of the reachable gain captured.
charts.chart_takeaway: Even where good candidates exist, the loop captures under a quarter of the reachable gain on plainly worded problems, less on pairs, and none on reworded ones.

qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.concept_primer: Picture the model as a machine operator that solves a task by making one small edit to a program, checking the result, then editing again. The test: does a smarter training routine make it edit better?
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.plain_question: Does letting the model practice and then retraining it on automatically-found fixes make its step-by-step editing solver more accurate?
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.plain_answer: No. The fix-retraining did not improve the step-by-step solver: it matched or trailed the simpler training on four of five test sets, and both stayed far below what a perfect edit sequence can reach.
qwen_search_augmented_rollout_distillati.why_it_matters: Collecting verified fixes is the easy part; turning them into better whole-solution choices is the real bottleneck. Future work should reward complete solution attempts rather than scoring single edits in isolation.

key_numbers.label: Fix-retraining on reworded tasks
key_numbers.sub: the extra retraining slightly lowered the simpler controller's best score

key_numbers.label: Trained controller vs the plain model
key_numbers.sub: the simply-trained editor did beat the plain model on reworded tasks

key_numbers.label: What a perfect edit sequence reaches
key_numbers.sub: huge room to improve that neither training routine captured

charts.chart_plain_title: Solve rates by test set: fix-retraining versus simpler training
charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one test set. Within it, bars compare the plain model, the simply-trained editor, the fix-retrained editor, and an ideal solver. Taller bars mean more tasks solved, so higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fix-retrained bars sit at or below the simply-trained bars on most test sets, while the ideal-solver bars tower far above every trained version.

charts.chart_plain_title: Does allowing more edit steps help the retrained solver?
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many edits the model is allowed; the vertical axis is the share of tasks solved. Each line is one test set, and higher lines mean more solved.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only one line climbs as edits increase; the rest stay flat or drop, so more editing does not reliably improve results.

charts.chart_plain_title: Per-step correctness across the training stages
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by training stage, from basic training to fix-retraining. Colors show three kinds of per-step correctness. Taller bars mean more steps done right, so higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Choosing the right edit fell from the basic stage's peak to the fix-retraining stage, and stronger per-step scores never produced better full solutions.

qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.verdict_tag: Reachability scoring fails to beat plain search
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.concept_primer: Imagine guiding a builder assembling a calculation step by step, but you are never shown the right answer. You must score each half-finished attempt using only a hunch about whether it is on track.
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.plain_question: Can scoring half-finished programs by whether they could still reach a correct answer guide the search better than just following the model's own confidence?
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.plain_answer: No. Scoring half-finished attempts by whether they could still reach a correct answer did no better than plain confidence-based search. Only a stricter scorer, which checks each step against the one known correct program, added a small edge.
qwen_semantic_prefix_value_model.why_it_matters: The bottleneck is picking the right candidate from those already generated, not generating it. A coarse could-this-still-work signal is too blunt; a scorer that rates how promising each path is looks like the better bet.

key_numbers.label: Everyday tasks solved, best scorer
key_numbers.sub: strict exact-match scoring adds a little

key_numbers.label: Everyday tasks, reachability scorer
key_numbers.sub: 68%, same as plain confidence search

key_numbers.label: Best answer in the shortlist
key_numbers.sub: correct answer usually available but not picked first

key_numbers.label: Hard tasks, with answer peeking
key_numbers.sub: upper limit when the answer is allowed

charts.chart_plain_title: Which scorer picks the correct answer most often
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often the final answer is correct (taller is better) for five ways of choosing a program, split into everyday tasks and harder multi-step tasks. The last bar is allowed to peek at the answer as an upper limit.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the strict exact-match scorer nudges the everyday-task bar upward; the reachability scorer sits level with plain search, far below the answer-peeking bar.

charts.chart_plain_title: The correct program is usually available but not picked
charts.chart_read: For everyday and harder tasks, bars compare the answer chosen first, the best answer sitting in the candidate shortlist, and an answer-peeking pick. Taller is better; the gap between first pick and shortlist is wasted potential.
charts.chart_takeaway: The best-in-shortlist bar towers over the first-pick bar, showing the right program is usually generated but ranked below wrong ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each labeling rule marks a step as good
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of program steps each rule labels as good; higher means a broader, looser target. Exact matching is strict, raw reachability is loose, and filtered reachability sits between them.
charts.chart_takeaway: The raw-reachability bar is roughly seven times the exact bar, marking far more steps as good and giving the scorer a much looser target to learn.

charts.chart_plain_title: Both scorers learn their target well
charts.chart_read: Bars rate each scorer's ability to sort good steps from bad on fresh data, from about 0.5 (coin-flip) up to 1.0 (perfect); taller is better. Left is the exact-match scorer, right the reachability scorer.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both bars sit far above coin-flip, and the exact-match scorer is cleaner, yet even it turns into only a small real-task gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tightening the reachability labels across trial runs
charts.chart_read: Across four successive runs, two bars per run show the share of steps marked good by exact matching versus reachability. Lower reachability bars mean a tighter, less permissive target.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reachability bars shrink run over run while the exact bars stay low and flat, showing the target was progressively tightened.

qwen_shared_parser_compiler.verdict_tag: Teaching each step works but stays brittle
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.concept_primer: A large AI reads a math word problem and works out the answer silently, inside itself. Imagine attaching a small reader that peeks at its internal scratch notes and tries to reconstruct the exact steps it took, one by one.
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.plain_question: When a large AI quietly works through a multi-step math problem in its head, can a small reader peek at its internal notes and rebuild the exact steps it took?
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. Taught each step directly, the reader rebuilds short programs well — about 78% of four-step problems come out exactly right — but accuracy fades to 39% by twelve steps and collapses to random-guess level on longer or reworded problems. Reading only the final answer never beats chance.
qwen_shared_parser_compiler.why_it_matters: To make an AI's hidden reasoning auditable and re-runnable, teach the reader each step directly rather than hoping it emerges from final answers alone. But expect it to break on longer or reworded problems, so keep the teaching broad.

key_numbers.label: Four-step problems, steps taught
key_numbers.sub: exact final answer, trained wording

key_numbers.label: Twelve-step problems, same method
key_numbers.sub: accuracy fades as programs lengthen

key_numbers.label: Reading only the final answer
key_numbers.sub: about 1 in 97 — no better than chance

key_numbers.label: Same programs, reworded
key_numbers.sub: reader keyed on phrasing, not meaning

charts.chart_plain_title: Rebuilding the steps beats reading only the answer, until programs grow long
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many steps the problem has; the vertical axis is how often the final answer is exactly right, and higher is better. One line teaches each step directly; two lines just read the answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-teaching line starts high and slides down as programs lengthen, while both answer-reading lines hug the bottom near random-guess level the whole way.

charts.chart_plain_title: Same programs, different wording drops accuracy to chance
charts.chart_read: Both lines are the step-teaching reader on the same math programs; one uses the exact training wording, the other reworded prompts. The vertical axis is how often the final answer is exactly right, higher better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reworded line lies flat near random-guess level at every length while the trained-wording line rides far above it — the reader keyed on phrasing, not meaning.

charts.chart_plain_title: Extra final-answer-only training erases the taught steps
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars at each program length compare the working step-teacher before extra training, after training on final answers only, and after the same at a gentler pace. Taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: After extra answer-only training the bars for longer programs fall to the floor; even the gentle version keeps only the four-step case standing.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tiny per-step reading errors pile up across a long program
charts.chart_read: Three lines track the step-teacher by program length: how often each operation is read right, each number is read right, and the whole program is exactly right. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Per-step reading stays high, but the whole-program line falls far faster — one wrong step anywhere spoils the answer, so small errors compound.

charts.chart_plain_title: Deeper notes and careful number-finding let longer programs run correctly
charts.chart_read: Each line is a different design of the reader, scored by how often the executed answer is right across program lengths. Higher is better. Designs differ in how deep they peek and how they locate each step's number.
charts.chart_takeaway: Reading deeper internal notes and pinning each number beside its operation lift the lines highest, while the shallowest design dies past four steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Confidence in the right answer falls to chance beyond four steps
charts.chart_read: Two lines by program length show how much probability the step-teaching system versus a plain answer-reader places on the true answer. Higher is better; the flat lower line marks random-guess level.
charts.chart_takeaway: The step-teacher leads only at four steps, then drops to meet the flat answer-reader line at random-guess level — past four steps the edge disappears.

qwen_slot_repair_distillation.verdict_tag: Fixable programs exist; the editor can't find them
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.concept_primer: A model writes short number-crunching programs that run correctly only about a quarter of the time. A fix almost always sits one or two small edits away. But a helper must spot which pieces are wrong from the program alone, with no answer key to check against.
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.plain_question: If a computer usually writes a slightly-wrong program, can a small model learn to spot and fix the few wrong pieces on its own, without ever seeing the answer?
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.plain_answer: No. A correct program almost always sits one or two edits away — a search that peeks at the answer lifts solve rates from about a quarter to roughly 86%. But the blind helper couldn't pick which edits to make: on reworded problems it dropped accuracy from 25% to 19%, and on fresh problems it barely moved. It only helped on the exact problems it was tuned on.
qwen_slot_repair_distillation.why_it_matters: Before bolting a learned fixer onto a model's output, test it on fresh and reworded inputs, and keep a do-nothing baseline. A high "a fix exists nearby" ceiling proves fixes are reachable — not that a blind one-shot patcher can find them.

key_numbers.label: Fixes are almost always reachable
key_numbers.sub: a search that checks the answer lifts solve rate this far above the base program

key_numbers.label: Reworded problems after editing
key_numbers.sub: the helper made paraphrased problems worse, not better

key_numbers.label: Tuning-set accuracy after editing
key_numbers.sub: the only split where editing helped, and only slightly

key_numbers.label: Same answer to both phrasings
key_numbers.sub: editing nearly halved agreement across two wordings of a problem

charts.chart_plain_title: Editor helps only on its tuning set, not on new problems
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars of how often programs run correctly across four problem sets — the tuning set plus three sets of fresh problems. Each set has three bars: the untouched base program, the trained editor's fix, and the answer-key repair ceiling. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The editor bar tops the base only on the tuning set; on every fresh set it ties or dips below, all far under the roughly 85% ceiling.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much of the fixable gap the editor actually closed
charts.chart_read: One bar per problem set showing the share of the base-to-ceiling gap the editor captured. Positive means it fixed programs; negative means it broke them; zero is doing nothing. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars are positive on the training and tuning sets but turn negative on all three fresh sets, worst at minus ten percent on rewordings — the skill doesn't transfer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Editor only edges past the baseline on the final round
charts.chart_read: A line of the editor's tuning-set accuracy across 18 training rounds against a flat line for the untouched base program. Above the flat line is better; higher means more programs run correctly.
charts.chart_takeaway: The editor line bounces between 13% and 35% and only clears the flat base line on the last round — the win is fragile, not a settled skill.

charts.chart_plain_title: Editing makes answers more sensitive to wording
charts.chart_read: For pairs of prompts asking the same question two ways, bars for the base program, the answer-key repairs, and the editor. Two series: giving the same answer to both wordings, and getting both right. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The editor's same-answer bar falls to about 38% from the base's 70%, while answer-key repairs lift it to 88% — the editor's patches make rewordings disagree.

charts.chart_plain_title: No editor recipe wins on both the tuning set and fresh problems
charts.chart_read: For six editor setups, paired bars compare tuning-set accuracy against fresh-problem accuracy. Higher is better; a good recipe would raise both bars together.
charts.chart_takeaway: No setup wins both — the main run tops tuning accuracy at 35% yet a smaller value-stabilized pilot beats it on fresh problems, 36% versus 28%.

charts.chart_plain_title: The model never reliably learns which parts to change
charts.chart_read: Two lines across 18 training rounds: how precisely the editor targets the parts to change (of edits it makes, how many were right) and how completely (of needed edits, how many it made). Higher and steadier is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Precision swings between 45% and 71% round to round and never settles — the choice of which few parts to edit stays unreliable throughout training.

qwen_slot_stability_compiler.verdict_tag: Reword-consistency rule helps some lengths, not others
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.concept_primer: Picture a multi-step math word problem. Instead of doing the arithmetic in its head, the model acts like a clerk who points at each number and operation and types them into a hidden calculator that runs the steps exactly.
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.plain_question: When the same problem is reworded, does forcing the clerk to point to the same numbers and operations both ways make its final answers more reliable?
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. The clerk-and-calculator method itself works well, solving most short and medium problems, while a model that just guesses the final answer directly never rises above random. But the extra rule forcing matching choices across rewordings only helped at some problem lengths and hurt at others.
qwen_slot_stability_compiler.why_it_matters: To make a small model do exact multi-step arithmetic, have it point at the numbers and hand them to a real calculator instead of guessing the answer. A reword-consistency penalty on top of that is not worth adding.

key_numbers.label: Short problems solved exactly
key_numbers.sub: guessing the answer directly versus the clerk-and-calculator method

key_numbers.label: Hardest reworded problems solved
key_numbers.sub: longest chains, once the reword-consistency rule is added

key_numbers.label: Both wordings agree, medium length
key_numbers.sub: where adding the reword-consistency rule backfired

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each method solves the exact answer, by problem length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many operation steps a problem has; the vertical axis is how often the exact final answer is right. The two upper lines are the clerk-and-calculator method; the bottom flat line guesses the answer directly. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both clerk-and-calculator lines sit far above the flat bottom line, which never lifts off the floor: pointing-and-copying works, guessing the answer directly does not.

charts.chart_plain_title: Solving reworded problems: accuracy by length for each method
charts.chart_read: Same setup, but every problem is rephrased into different wording. Horizontal axis is problem length, vertical axis is how often the exact answer is right; the two upper lines are the two clerk-and-calculator versions, the bottom line guesses directly. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two clerk lines nearly overlap except at the longest length, where the version with the reword-consistency rule pulls slightly ahead, 23% versus 16%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do the two wordings give the same answer, with and without the rule?
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by problem length. Height is how often one problem, worded two ways, produces the same final answer. One bar per length adds the reword-consistency rule, the other leaves it out. Taller means more consistent.
charts.chart_takeaway: The rule wins at the shortest length but loses clearly at medium length, 75% versus 88%: it is a mixed bag, not a steady gain in consistency.

charts.chart_plain_title: An early lead for the rule fades by the end of training
charts.chart_read: Bars compare the two versions on medium-length reworded pairs, halfway through training and at the end. The left group is exact-answer accuracy, the right group is how often both wordings agree. One bar per group uses the rule, the other does not. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Halfway through, the rule's version agrees far more often, 36% versus 5%, but by the end both versions land in the same place: the early edge evaporates.

charts.chart_plain_title: Why long problems fail: tiny per-step slips add up
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is problem length. Each upper line tracks how often one piece is identified correctly, the starting number, the operations, or the step numbers. The bottom line is getting the whole sequence exactly right. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Individual pieces stay near-perfect, but the whole-sequence line collapses to zero on the longest problems: one small slip anywhere ruins the entire chain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Most reworded-problem accuracy is gained in the second half of training
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by problem length, for reworded prompts. Each version is measured halfway through training and at the end. Height is how often the exact answer is right. Taller is better; compare halfway against end within each version.
charts.chart_takeaway: End-of-training bars tower over halfway bars at medium and long lengths: the second half of training is where the reworded problems actually get solved.

qwen_span_free_compiler.verdict_tag: The add-on helps only where it drilled
qwen_span_free_compiler.concept_primer: Think of the frozen model as a clerk who silently reads a math word-problem, and a small add-on translator that turns the clerk's private notes into the exact steps a calculator can run.
qwen_span_free_compiler.plain_question: Can a small add-on read a frozen model's private notes and write out the exact math steps to solve the problem, without being handed the numbers?
qwen_span_free_compiler.plain_answer: It depends. On the exact problem style it practiced, the add-on nailed the full sequence of steps 100% of the time, versus random-guess level for every shortcut method. But it fell apart on longer chains or reworded problems.
qwen_span_free_compiler.why_it_matters: A tiny translator can decode a frozen model's internal notes into runnable steps, but only if you teach it where to look. Expect it to memorize one narrow format, not to generalize.

charts.chart_takeaway: Even with each step around 90% right at longer chains, the whole-sequence line drops below 6%, because small per-step errors pile up.

The trained line shoots up early and flattens near the top; the reworded line stays pinned at the bottom throughout.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trained-wording line reaches near-perfect within the first stretch and holds, while the reworded line never lifts off random-guess level.

qwen_state_ladder_compiler.verdict_tag: Gradual practice helped, step-checking did not
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.concept_primer: Picture the model working through a recipe of many arithmetic steps in a row, keeping a running total; one slip anywhere ruins the final answer, so staying exact over a long chain is the whole challenge.
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.plain_question: Does forcing the model to show and check its running total after every single step help it follow long arithmetic chains correctly?
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.plain_answer: No. Checking the running total after every step never beat a matched model trained without it, and full-strength checking made things worse. The real gain came from a separate trick, gradually working up to longer chains, which lifted the hardest problems from random-guess level to about 21%.
qwen_state_ladder_compiler.why_it_matters: To get a small model to nail long chains of exact steps, invest in a gradual difficulty ramp rather than forcing it to justify every intermediate step; heavy step-checking can even make it fragile on reworded problems.

qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.verdict_tag: Copying steps was not the cause
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.concept_primer: Picture the model writing a short step-by-step program to reach an answer, then running it. The test is whether that program still works when it grows to 24 steps instead of just a few.
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.plain_question: When a model writes longer step-by-step programs, does copying its earlier steps into the new ones explain why it handles the longest 24-step programs?
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.plain_answer: No. Copying earlier steps was not the reason. What actually worked was training on full-length problems from the start while gradually raising difficulty, which reached 97% on the longest programs versus about 2% for the copying approach.
qwen_structural_compiler_attribution_abl.why_it_matters: To get a model handling longer step-by-step problems, train on the full length from the start while gradually raising difficulty, rather than stitching longer programs together from copied earlier pieces.

key_numbers.label: Winning approach on longest programs
key_numbers.sub: full-length training with gradually rising difficulty

key_numbers.label: Copying-steps approach on longest programs
key_numbers.sub: the suspected cause, refuted here

key_numbers.label: Every approach on shorter programs

charts.chart_plain_title: Longest-program accuracy by training approach
charts.chart_read: Each group is a training approach; the three bars show accuracy on the hardest 24-step programs for standard wording, reworded wording, and matched twin-prompt pairs. Taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-length-plus-rising-difficulty approach towers over the rest on standard wording and twin-prompts; copying steps only helps on reworded prompts.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy holds until programs reach full length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is program length from 8 to 24 steps; each line is one training approach; higher is better. Lines stay high on short programs and split apart at the longest length.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line sits near the top through 16 steps, then all but the winning approach plunge toward zero at 24 steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Winning approach's longest-program skill appears late in training
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training progress; the lines track the winning approach's accuracy on the longest programs for three test types; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines stay low, then jump sharply in the final training stretch, so the skill emerges late rather than building steadily.

charts.chart_plain_title: Reworded prompts flip which approaches look good
charts.chart_read: Each group is a training approach; two bars compare accuracy on standard wording versus reworded-but-familiar prompts at full length; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The copying-steps approach scores 75% on reworded prompts but 2% on standard ones, so wording, not just the approach, drives the failures.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the winning approach answers twin prompts consistently
charts.chart_read: Each group is a training approach; the bars show how often it correctly answers both halves of a matched prompt pair at full length; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every approach except the winner drops to essentially zero, meaning none of them can reliably get both twin prompts right.

charts.chart_plain_title: Failing approaches get most steps right but botch the final answer
charts.chart_read: Each group is a training approach; one bar is final-answer accuracy, the other is the share of intermediate steps traced correctly, on full-length standard prompts; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Losing approaches keep 62-86% of intermediate steps correct while final answers sink near zero, so errors cluster in the last steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Short-program skill dips mid-training then partly recovers
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training progress; the lines show accuracy on short reworded programs for three approaches as longer programs are introduced; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The lines sag in the middle as longer programs arrive; the winning approach rebounds to 91% while one control ends lower at 58%.

qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.verdict_tag: Stretched to longer problems without breaking
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.concept_primer: Picture the model with a built-in calculator that follows a hidden, step-by-step recipe in its head instead of writing the steps out. Here we try to stretch that recipe to more steps.
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.plain_question: Can the model's hidden step-by-step recipe be stretched to handle much longer problems without losing accuracy on the shorter ones it already solved?
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.plain_answer: Yes. Growing the hidden recipe from short to long problems kept the shorter ones perfectly solved while the longest reached 83 to 100 percent, all without writing steps out or trying many guesses.
qwen_structural_latent_compiler_expansio.why_it_matters: You can extend a model's internal step-by-step solving to harder, longer tasks by growing it in stages, rather than retraining from scratch or forcing it to spell every step out.

key_numbers.label: Short problems still solved
key_numbers.sub: 8- and 16-step recipes after growing to the longest

key_numbers.label: Longest problems solved
key_numbers.sub: 24-step recipes across three prompt styles

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the grown solver got the right answer, by problem length
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show how often the grown solver reached the right answer for short (8-step), medium (16-step), and long (24-step) problems, split by three prompt styles. Taller bars are better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars stay at full height for short and medium problems and dip only slightly for the longest, so growing the recipe cost almost no accuracy on what it already handled.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy as longer problems were added in stages
charts.chart_read: Lines track answer accuracy over training, with one line per problem length; markers appear as each new length is introduced. Higher lines mean more correct answers, so up is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Each length's line stays flat near the top as new, longer lengths are added, and the medium line even climbs to perfect, so adding longer problems never dragged shorter ones down.

charts.chart_plain_title: Do reworded prompts produce the same hidden steps?
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show three ways of checking whether two reworded versions of the same problem produce the same hidden steps, across short, medium, and long problems. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Consistency holds at full height for short and medium problems and eases only to about seven-eighths for the longest, so reworded prompts mostly compile to the same internal steps.

qwen_structured_bridge.verdict_tag: Translate to steps, then a calculator runs them
qwen_structured_bridge.concept_primer: Instead of asking the frozen model to blurt out a final answer, a small add-on translates its reading of the words into a list of button-presses that a separate calculator then executes exactly.
qwen_structured_bridge.plain_question: Can a frozen model's reading of a word problem be turned into steps a hidden calculator runs, beating just asking for the answer?
qwen_structured_bridge.plain_answer: Yes. Translating the words into calculator steps hit 96-100% on chains up to 12 operations, while asking the same model to name the final answer stayed at random-guess level. The win requires showing the translator the correct steps during training.
qwen_structured_bridge.why_it_matters: When a task is a chain of exact operations, wire the model to emit the steps and let a reliable calculator do the math, rather than trusting it to compute the answer in its head.

key_numbers.label: Asking for the answer directly
key_numbers.sub: same model, no explicit steps

key_numbers.label: Translating into steps, then computing
key_numbers.sub: on chains up to 12 operations

key_numbers.label: Chains 6x longer than training
key_numbers.sub: 24 steps, trained only on 1-4

charts.chart_plain_title: Steps-then-calculator wins; asking for the answer stays at guessing
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars compare three approaches at 4, 8, and 12-operation chains (left to right); height is share of answers correct, taller is better. Only the step-translation bars rise near the top; the other two sit at the floor.
charts.chart_takeaway: The trace-taught translator towers near 100% across all chain lengths while both the direct-answer and no-guidance versions flatline at random-guess level.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy fades gently on chains far longer than training
charts.chart_read: Lines track accuracy as chains grow from 4 to 24 operations, well past the 1-4 seen in training; higher and flatter is better. The lines drift down only slightly toward the right.
charts.chart_takeaway: Accuracy slopes down gradually to about 88% at 24 operations, six times the training depth, showing graceful decline rather than a collapse.

charts.chart_plain_title: Long-chain slips come from misreading an operation, not the numbers
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each piece of the translated program is right at 24 operations: starting number, the operation, its number, and the whole chain; taller is better. The first three are near-perfect, the last is lower.
charts.chart_takeaway: Numbers are read essentially perfectly, so the 12% of failed chains trace to the occasional wrong operation, whose errors pile up over many steps.

qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.verdict_tag: Models can ace tasks while ignoring your examples
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.concept_primer: Think of the worked examples in a prompt as a recipe card handed to a cook. One who memorized the dish cooks it even from a scrambled card: high score, card ignored. Contrastive training rewards only the cook who actually reads the card.
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.plain_question: When you fine-tune a small model to follow worked examples in its prompt, does it actually read them, or just recognize the task and answer from memory?
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.plain_answer: Not by default. One tuned model scored 49% whether its worked examples were intact or scrambled, meaning it had memorized the task and ignored the examples entirely. A contrastive objective forced genuine reading: 50% with intact examples, collapsing to 7% when scrambled. The catch is this honesty cost a few points versus plain tuning's peak of 56%.
qwen_support_contrastive_meta_icl.why_it_matters: Before trusting a fine-tuned model to follow in-prompt examples, rerun it with those examples scrambled. If accuracy barely drops, it is ignoring your demonstrations and just pattern-matching the task family, and a high raw score hides exactly that.

key_numbers.label: Task accuracy, untrained vs contrastive-tuned
key_numbers.sub: share of public tasks fully correct, with real examples

key_numbers.label: Contrastive accuracy when examples are scrambled
key_numbers.sub: it collapses because it truly reads the examples

key_numbers.label: A plainly tuned rival, examples real vs scrambled
key_numbers.sub: identical score proves it ignores the examples

key_numbers.label: Best raw-accuracy recipe
key_numbers.sub: beats contrastive but does not depend on the examples

charts.chart_plain_title: Public task accuracy by training recipe and example condition
charts.chart_read: Five training recipes across the bottom; three bars each for full-task accuracy with real examples, scrambled examples, and no examples in the prompt. Taller is better; a big drop from real to scrambled means the recipe truly reads its examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: The contrastive recipe's real-example bar near half towers over its scrambled bar at 7%, while the shuffled-labels recipe's two bars are equal, so it ignores examples.

charts.chart_plain_title: How much each recipe's accuracy depends on real examples
charts.chart_read: One pair of bars per recipe, public benchmark and synthetic tasks, showing accuracy with real examples minus accuracy with scrambled ones. Taller is better here: a bigger drop means the answer truly depends on the examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: Contrastive towers over every recipe on both sets (43 and 77 points); the shuffled-labels recipe sits at zero, confirming it disregards the demonstrations.

charts.chart_plain_title: Synthetic-task accuracy by recipe and example condition
charts.chart_read: Five recipes across the bottom on made-up transformation tasks; three bars each for real, scrambled, and no examples, as share of tasks fully correct. A high real bar next to a low scrambled bar signals the recipe reads its examples.
charts.chart_takeaway: Contrastive collapses from 84% to 7% once examples are scrambled, while other recipes still score 37 to 83% on scrambled examples, the sharpest example-dependence.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy by task family: base model versus contrastive-tuned
charts.chart_read: Ten task families (dates, numbers, phone, email, shipping codes and more) across the bottom; two bars each for the untrained base and the contrastive-tuned model, as share of tasks fully correct. A higher contrastive bar means the tuning helped.
charts.chart_takeaway: Contrastive lifts most families, several from 0% to 100%; only the address family regresses to 0% and one billing-code family stays stuck at 0%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Contrastive accuracy across three training runs
charts.chart_read: Three training runs with different random starts across the bottom; two bars each for public benchmark and synthetic tasks, both measured with real examples. Steady bar heights across runs mean the result is reliable, not luck.
charts.chart_takeaway: Public accuracy holds near half across all three runs; synthetic accuracy wobbles more, dipping to 77% on the third run.

charts.chart_plain_title: Public tasks helped, hurt, or unchanged by contrastive training
charts.chart_read: Three training runs across the bottom; three bars each counting, out of 45 public tasks, how many contrastive tuning helped, hurt, or left tied versus the untrained base. More helped and fewer hurt is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every run helps 17 to 18 tasks and hurts exactly 3, so the gains are broad and repeatable, not a few lucky tasks.

qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.concept_primer: Think of a proofreader who is handed a mostly-correct recipe with a buggy ending and several candidate corrections, and must pick the right fix without ever seeing the answer key.
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.plain_question: Can a learned proofreader fix the buggy endings of mostly-correct programs by picking the right candidate, without peeking at the answer?
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.plain_answer: No. Both learned proofreaders left the share of correct programs exactly where it started, near 45%, even though a picker allowed to peek could reach 91% from the very same candidate fixes.
qwen_tail_repair_stability_critic.why_it_matters: Choosing the fix, not having one, is the wall. A picker that only reads summary statistics fails; it must actually compare each candidate program against the original prompt to be trusted.

key_numbers.label: Programs that run correctly
key_numbers.sub: unchanged after the learned proofreader

key_numbers.label: If allowed to peek at the answer
key_numbers.sub: a perfect picker over the same fixes

key_numbers.label: Weakest program source, with peeking
key_numbers.sub: where nearly all missed gains sit

key_numbers.label: Programs the proofreader edited
key_numbers.sub: it chose to change nothing

charts.chart_plain_title: Learned repair picker never beats leaving the program alone
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one way of choosing a fix; height is the share of 24-step programs that run correctly start to finish, so taller is better. Bars cover no repair, two learned proofreaders, a scrambled-label control, and two peek-allowed pickers.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two learned-proofreader bars sit exactly on the no-repair bar while the peek-allowed answer bar towers far above, so a good fix exists but is never chosen.

charts.chart_plain_title: The repair gap stays wide across every batch of programs
charts.chart_read: Four batches of programs, three bars each: leave the program as-is, the learned proofreader, and a peek-allowed picker. Height is the share that run correctly, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned-proofreader bar is identical to leave-as-is on every batch, always sitting far below the peek-allowed bar that could reach it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Correct fixes are plentiful, so having a fix is not the problem
charts.chart_read: For each batch, one bar is the share of programs where a correct ending-fix already exists among the tried edits, the other is the share already correct with no fix. Taller means more fixable.
charts.chart_takeaway: The fix-exists bar sits near ninety percent everywhere, roughly double the already-correct bar, so the raw material for repair is abundant.

charts.chart_plain_title: Peeking would rescue mostly the weakest program source
charts.chart_read: Three source generators, two bars each: leaving programs as-is versus a peek-allowed picker. Height is the share that run correctly, so taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The peek-allowed picker lifts the weakest source from near-zero to about 78%, so nearly all the unclaimed gains sit on that weak source.

charts.chart_plain_title: Scrambled control edits and harms programs; the real picker touches nothing
charts.chart_read: Four batches of programs. Bars show how often a scrambled-label control edits programs and how often it harms them, next to how often the real proofreader edits. Lower editing by the real picker means it stayed hands-off.
charts.chart_takeaway: The scrambled control changes and damages roughly a third of programs while the real proofreader edits zero, showing its stillness is a deliberate retreat, not a broken pipeline.

qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.verdict_tag: Extra copying hints did not help
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.concept_primer: Picture a student transcribing a long chain of arithmetic into a calculator one step at a time; a single mis-copied number ruins the final answer. The extra training aid pointed out exactly where each number sat in the wording.
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.plain_question: Does pointing the model to exactly where each number sits in the wording help it copy and run long multi-step math problems more accurately?
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.plain_answer: No. Adding location hints did not improve exact answers over the plain version (about 28% versus 27%, essentially tied), and the hints actually made the model less consistent when the same problem was reworded.
qwen_teacher_distilled_slot_compiler.why_it_matters: The model already finds the right numbers; the real problem is that tiny copy slips pile up across many steps. Future work should check and repair the running calculation, not point out where numbers sit.

key_numbers.label: Exact answers, plain vs hinted

charts.chart_read: Each cluster is one training setup: the plain version and two hint-added versions. In each cluster one bar shows how often the final answer is exactly right, the other how often two rewordings of the same problem agree. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain version's agreement bar towers over both hint versions while the exact-answer bars are all roughly equal, so the hints added nothing and cost consistency.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy falls as problems get longer, for every setup

charts.chart_takeaway: All three lines drop steeply from short to long problems and sit almost on top of each other, showing length, not the missing hints, drives the failures.
