charts.chart_takeaway: The soft-position hint bar collapses from about a quarter correct to roughly one in nine when reworded, while the other two setups barely move.

qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.concept_primer: Think of teaching a student a method with worked examples that show every step, then switching to only grading their final answers. The question is whether the method survives once you stop showing the steps.
qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.plain_question: Once a helper has learned a step-by-step method from worked examples, can it keep that method while you only grade its final answers?
qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.plain_answer: Yes, it holds. A helper first taught with worked steps keeps solving about 97% of the hardest problems even after you switch to grading only final answers, while a helper given only final answers from the start stays near 1%, no better than guessing.
qwen_trace_bootstrap_retention.why_it_matters: Give a small model the method through worked examples first; afterward, cheap final-answer feedback is enough to preserve and even sharpen it. Final-answer feedback alone never teaches the method from scratch.

key_numbers.label: Hardest problems solved, worked-steps helper

charts.chart_read: Each bar is one teaching recipe; bar height is the share of the longest 24-step problems the helper solves exactly, so taller is better. The two right-hand bars that used worked examples tower over the near-flat bars that never did.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two recipes that first showed worked steps reach near the top of the chart, while every recipe trained only on final answers sits almost flat on the floor.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is problem length in steps and the vertical axis is the share solved exactly, so lines that stay high are better. The worked-example recipes hold near the top even far past the lengths they trained on, while the final-answer-only lines lie flat along the bottom.

charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training progress; worked-example coaching is switched off after the halfway point. The vertical axis is accuracy on the longest problems, so higher is better. One line dips sharply right after the switch, another barely moves, and both climb back up by the end.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-speed line dips right after coaching stops but then recovers to finish highest, showing the helper keeps and even sharpens the method on its own.

qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.verdict_tag: Writing the recipe beats doing the math
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.concept_primer: Think of a recipe versus cooking: the model can write out a correct step-by-step recipe that a machine then follows exactly, even when the model would botch the cooking (the arithmetic) if it tried to do it in its head.
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.plain_question: If the model only ever practiced single steps, can it still write correct recipes for much longer multi-step tasks?
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.plain_answer: Yes. Trained only on one-step examples, its written six-step recipes ran correctly about 63% of the time, while just asking it for the answer directly got 0% right at that length.
qwen_trace_procedure_depth_stress.why_it_matters: Have the model emit an explicit step list for a machine to run, rather than trusting its head math. The recipe holds up on long tasks; the weak spot now is understanding reworded instructions, not running the steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Rewording the same task lowers success at every length
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is how many steps the task needs; vertical axis is how often the machine-run recipe solved it, higher is better. The upper line is the familiar wording, the lower line is the same tasks reworded.
charts.chart_takeaway: The reworded line sits below the familiar-wording line across all lengths, showing the wording of the request is the remaining hurdle.

charts.chart_plain_title: When a recipe fails, it usually fails to run at all
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one type of outcome for the written recipes across all task lengths; taller means a bigger share of recipes fall in that group. The first bar is fully correct; the rest are kinds of mistakes.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tallest failure bar is recipes that won't run at all, while picking the wrong steps is a tiny sliver, so parsing not reasoning is the main breakdown.

qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.verdict_tag: Self-teaching helps, but worked examples win
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.concept_primer: The model turns word problems into a short recipe of steps a calculator runs exactly. You can teach it with full worked-out recipes, or let it guess-and-check toward the right answer and learn from its wins.
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.plain_question: Can letting the model guess-and-check its way to correct answers make it as good as showing it full worked-out recipes?
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.plain_answer: It depends. Learning from its own guess-and-check wins raised first-try accuracy from 62% to 73%, beating the untrained starting point, but full worked-out recipes reached nearly 100%, so self-teaching helped without closing the gap.
qwen_typed_bytecode_expert_iteration.why_it_matters: When you can score final answers but lack worked examples, self-teaching gives a real lift for free, yet a modest stock of full worked examples stays far more powerful and worth collecting.

charts.chart_takeaway: The self-taught bar climbs from 62% to 73% unaided, but the full-recipe bars tower near 100%, dwarfing the self-teaching gain.

charts.chart_read: Two rising lines track unaided accuracy across rounds of self-teaching, starting at round zero; higher is better. One line is ordinary fresh problems, the other is harder multi-step problems.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines jump at round one then flatten, and the harder-problems line stays well below, showing self-teaching stalls early and struggles with longer recipes.

charts.chart_read: Bars for three training methods on fresh problems; taller is better. The light bar is answering unaided, the dark bar is with guess-and-check. Left to right: starting point, self-taught, full worked recipes.
charts.chart_takeaway: Self-teaching nearly triples unaided accuracy from 18% to 50%, but the full-recipe bar near 95% again far outreaches it.

charts.chart_read: Two lines across rounds; higher means more. One is the share of problems where guess-and-check found a correct recipe, the other is the share of those that were genuinely new corrections rather than already-known answers.
charts.chart_takeaway: The found line climbs to 93% while the new-correction line collapses toward 5%, so later rounds mostly re-confirm what the model already knew.

charts.chart_plain_title: Guess-and-check stays ahead of unaided answering every round
charts.chart_read: Two lines across self-teaching rounds on fresh problems; higher is better. The lower line is the model answering unaided, the upper line is it allowed to guess-and-check toward the right answer.
charts.chart_takeaway: The guess-and-check line sits a steady 14-16 points above the unaided line throughout, meaning fixable mistakes remain in the model's first answers.

charts.chart_plain_title: Even full worked recipes cannot fully crack harder problems
charts.chart_read: Four groups of problem types, each with two bars for the two model front ends after training on full worked recipes; taller is better. The first three groups are ordinary problems, the last is harder multi-step ones.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both front ends near-max the first three groups but drop 15-20 points on the harder multi-step group, marking longer recipes as the stubborn weak spot.

charts.chart_plain_title: The 4B-model version finds fewer, messier self-taught lessons
charts.chart_read: Lines across rounds; higher means more. Two lines are the 4B-model version's share of problems solved and share needing heavy rewriting, one line is the simpler main version's solved share for comparison.
charts.chart_takeaway: The 4B version starts far lower and rewrites more heavily, but its solved line climbs toward the main version's, showing a slower version of the same trend.

qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.verdict_tag: Retrieving a similar example did not help
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.concept_primer: Imagine a worker reformatting messy data one field at a time — dates, phone numbers, addresses. Before each new batch, you hand them a worked example of a similar job solved earlier, hoping it guides them to fewer mistakes.
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.plain_question: When a small language model reformats messy data like dates and phone numbers, does handing it a worked example of a similar past job help it get more batches perfectly right?
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.plain_answer: No. Handing the model a matched worked example did not help. It got half of its batches perfectly right when working alone and slightly under half when given the example. A randomly chosen or scrambled example did no better, so the extra material was dead weight.
qwen_verified_skill_memory_rag.why_it_matters: If you are pushing a small model through repetitive data-cleanup work, do not count on a library of similar solved examples to lift accuracy. The reliable gains here came from clean item-by-item prompting, not from looking up past work.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each approach got a whole batch perfectly right
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one working style; taller means more batches where every field was exactly right. The leftmost bar is the model working through items one at a time on its own; the rest add a looked-up example or process the whole batch at once.
charts.chart_takeaway: The plain one-at-a-time bar is tallest; every version that adds a looked-up example or batches the job sits lower, so the extra example never paid off.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often each approach got a single field right
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one working style; taller means more individual fields exactly correct, a looser test than needing the whole batch perfect. The leftmost bar is the model working through items one at a time alone.
charts.chart_takeaway: Working alone stays tallest even under this gentler scoring; every added-example or batched version lands lower, confirming the pattern is not a fluke of strict grading.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many tasks each shortcut helped versus hurt
charts.chart_read: For each working style, two bars count the tasks it improved versus the tasks it worsened, compared against the model working one field at a time alone. A taller help bar and shorter hurt bar is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The help bars sit at or near zero everywhere while the hurt bars climb; the matched example improved none of the forty tasks and worsened one.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the lookup found a genuinely similar example
charts.chart_read: Two bars: the real lookup versus a random pick. Each shows how often the fetched example came from the same kind of task. Taller means the fetched example was more relevant to the job at hand.
charts.chart_takeaway: The real lookup fetched a genuinely similar example most of the time, far above the random pick, so the flat result is not a broken lookup.

charts.chart_plain_title: Tiny six-task trials swung wildly from run to run
charts.chart_read: Each group is one working style; the three bars within it are three separate tiny trials of only six tasks each. Height is the share of fields correct. The point is how much the matched-example bars jump between trials.
charts.chart_takeaway: The matched-example bars swing from worst to best across the three tiny trials, showing six tasks are too few to trust and motivating the full forty-task test.

charts.chart_plain_title: On the hardest task type, examples did not rescue accuracy
charts.chart_read: Ten of the hardest number-formatting tasks run along the bottom. For each, one bar is the model working alone and the other is the model given a matched example. Height is the share of fields correct; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: On these number tasks the matched-example bars are mostly equal or shorter, losing on four and winning on two, so the failures are not about finding a relevant example.

charts.chart_plain_title: Only processing the whole batch at once broke the format
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one working style; height is the share of tasks whose output could be read back cleanly. Taller is better. The bar worth watching is the one that processes the whole batch in a single pass.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every style produced clean, readable output except processing the whole batch at once, which garbled the format on a small slice of tasks.

charts.chart_plain_title: The single best-matched example is the most relevant
charts.chart_read: Two lines run across the first, second, and third best-matched examples. One line tracks how similar the match is; the other tracks how often it is the same kind of task. Both fall as you move to lower-ranked picks.
charts.chart_takeaway: Both lines slope downward from the top match, so lower-ranked examples are less relevant — the reason only the single best example was ever handed to the model.

qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.verdict_tag: Small fixes recover most failed runs
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.concept_primer: The model builds a 24-step arithmetic recipe from memory, and one wrong step ruins the final answer. A checker that knows the correct running total at each step can hunt down and swap the few bad steps.
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.plain_question: If the model gets a long 24-step calculation almost right, can a checker that knows the correct running total fix the few bad steps and recover the answer?
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.plain_answer: Yes, mostly. Swapping just one or two wrong steps lifted the share of exactly-correct 24-step runs from about 27% to 91%, versus staying at 27% with no fixing at all.
qwen_verifier_guided_slot_repair.why_it_matters: When a model nearly solves a long problem, its mistakes are usually small and local, so a reliable checker can repair them in place rather than needing a bigger model or many more attempts. The catch: this test used a perfect answer key, so the next job is building a checker that works without one.

key_numbers.label: Exactly-correct 24-step runs
key_numbers.sub: before vs after fixing one or two bad steps

key_numbers.label: Fixed by a single swap
key_numbers.sub: changing just one step already recovers most failures

key_numbers.label: True recipe recovered
key_numbers.sub: the real program is rebuilt, not just a lucky final answer

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixing one or two steps recovers most failed 24-step runs
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by how many steps the checker is allowed to swap: none, one, or two. In each group, one bar counts runs with the right final answer and one counts runs with the fully correct recipe. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars climb from about 27% with no fixing to 91% with two swaps, and the two heights stay matched, meaning it recovers the real recipe, not just a lucky answer.

charts.chart_plain_title: Longer calculations fail far more, but fixing recovers most of them
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many steps the calculation has; the vertical axis is the share of runs that come out exactly right. One line is before fixing, one is after. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The before-fixing line drops from about 86% down to 25% as runs reach 24 steps, while the after-fixing line stays near 82%, so repair holds up even on the longest chains.

charts.chart_plain_title: The repair gain holds across different prompt wordings
charts.chart_read: Bars are grouped by prompt style: plain, reworded, and matched pairs. In each group one bar is before fixing and one is after. Higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every style jumps from roughly a quarter of runs correct before fixing to around 86 to 91% after, so the gain is not a quirk of one phrasing.

qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.verdict_tag: Edit-and-run loop helped, the add-on tweak didn't
qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.concept_primer: Instead of guessing an answer in one shot, the model works like a coder: it makes one small edit to a tiny program, runs it, sees the result, and edits again over several rounds.
qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.plain_question: Does letting the model edit-run-repeat beat a single guess, and does teaching it to also predict the machine's output help even more?
qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.plain_answer: Mixed. The edit-and-run loop lifted accuracy from 10% to 43%, beating a plain one-shot answer, but the extra tweak of teaching the model to predict the machine's output actually hurt, dropping it to 36%.
qwen_vm_agent_echo_qlora.why_it_matters: Letting a small model act in a loop and check its own work pays off, but teaching it to narrate what the machine will show is a distraction; reward finishing the right program instead.

key_numbers.label: One-shot guess vs edit-and-run loop
key_numbers.sub: average tasks solved when the model works in a loop

key_numbers.label: Extra output-predicting tweak
key_numbers.sub: lower than the plain loop's 43%

key_numbers.label: Best possible with a perfect guide
key_numbers.sub: how far a flawless step-by-step teacher got, showing the room left

charts.chart_plain_title: The edit-and-run loop beats one-shot; the extra tweak does not help
charts.chart_read: Grouped bars show the share of tasks solved across five test sets plus their average; taller is better. Each group compares a plain one-shot answer, the edit-and-run loop, the same loop with the extra output-predicting tweak, and a perfect step-by-step teacher.
charts.chart_takeaway: The loop's bars sit above the one-shot bars on average, while the tweak's bars are no taller and often shorter, and the teacher's bars tower over all, showing plenty of unclaimed room.

qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.concept_primer: Think of a helper that turns a word problem into a tiny program a computer runs. Here we also asked it to predict what happens when that program runs, like guessing a recipe's taste before cooking.
qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.plain_question: If we also teach the program-writing helper to predict what its programs do when run, does it write better programs?
qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.plain_answer: It depends. The helper learned to predict run outcomes very well, jumping from near-zero to about 43%, and its answer-checking retries improved slightly. But its first-try program writing did not broadly improve, and on one everyday test it dropped a few points.
qwen_vm_echo_trace_distillation.why_it_matters: Teaching a model to foresee consequences is a real, attachable skill, but predicting outcomes of a given answer barely helps it pick better answers. Next, have it predict outcomes of its own attempts, not the ideal one.

key_numbers.label: Predicting what a program does
key_numbers.sub: new outcome-guessing skill the helper gained

key_numbers.label: Success after retry attempts
key_numbers.sub: small gain on the paired everyday test

key_numbers.label: First-try success, standard test
key_numbers.sub: a slight drop, not an improvement

charts.chart_plain_title: What the outcome-prediction training changed
charts.chart_read: Three paired bars compare the original helper against the outcome-predicting one on first-try success, success-after-retry, and guessing a program's result; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The outcome-prediction bar leaps from almost nothing to about 43%, retry success edges up, but first-try success is identical, so the new skill barely spills over.

charts.chart_plain_title: First-try success across four kinds of test problems
charts.chart_read: Four paired bars, one per problem style, comparing the original helper with the outcome-predicting one on first-try success; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The two helpers sit nearly level everywhere, and on the standard style the outcome-predicting bar is actually shorter, confirming no broad first-try gain.

charts.chart_plain_title: Useful new examples gathered during self-practice
charts.chart_read: Paired bars for two practice rounds show the share of problems where each helper found a working program to learn from; taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The bars stay within a couple of points across both rounds, showing the extra training did not shrink the pool of examples the helper could learn from.

real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.verdict_tag: Fixed toolbox fits clean data, not messy
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.concept_primer: Think of a fixed toolbox of ready-made data-cleanup tools, tested by asking whether one tool can correctly do each job, including deliberate trick jobs designed to fool a lazy match.
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.plain_question: Can one fixed toolbox of ready-made data transformations handle both tidy spreadsheet cleanup and messy date, ID, and text jobs?
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.plain_answer: It depends on the job. The fixed toolbox handled every tidy spreadsheet task, but slipped on messy date and ID work, covering 85% there versus 100% on clean pipelines once trick tests weeded out lucky-looking wrong answers.
real_transform_abi_gate_with_counterexam.why_it_matters: Build a compiler onto this toolbox for tidy pipeline work now, but expand the toolbox and add tougher trick tests before trusting it on messy extraction and formatting jobs.

key_numbers.label: Tidy spreadsheet jobs solved
key_numbers.sub: all 20 clean pipeline tasks, before and after trick tests

key_numbers.label: Messy date and ID jobs solved
key_numbers.sub: trick tests removed 2 lucky-looking wrong answers

key_numbers.label: Trick jobs a lazy match faked
key_numbers.sub: both fooled the easy tests, both removed by trick tests

key_numbers.label: Easy-test winners actually wrong
key_numbers.sub: share that passed the shown examples but failed hidden ones

charts.chart_plain_title: Jobs solved per group, before and after trick tests
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks solved for tidy pipelines, messy date/ID work, and overall; each group pairs coverage from easy tests (lighter) against coverage after tougher trick tests (darker). Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The tidy-pipeline bars stay at full height after trick tests while the messy group drops, showing the fixed toolbox holds up only on clean work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Trick jobs caught by tougher tests
charts.chart_read: Two bars for the same set of deliberately deceptive tasks: coverage under easy tests versus coverage after trick tests. A drop to zero is the intended, better outcome here.
charts.chart_takeaway: Coverage falls from full to nothing, confirming trick tests removed every lucky-looking wrong answer that the easy tests had let through.

charts.chart_plain_title: How many solved jobs used one tool versus two
charts.chart_read: Bars count solved tasks that needed a single tool versus two tools chained together; lighter bars are before trick tests, darker after. Height just shows how common each pattern is.
charts.chart_takeaway: The single-tool bar towers over the two-tool bar, meaning the toolbox mostly proves it can pick the right one tool, not chain several.

charts.chart_plain_title: Share of easy-test winners that were actually wrong
charts.chart_read: One bar per group showing how often a candidate that passed the shown examples still failed hidden checks, for tidy, messy, and overall work. Lower is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: All three bars sit near a third, showing thin visible examples routinely let wrong answers slip through and why the tougher tests are needed.

rule_family_diversity_scaling.verdict_tag: Wider training variety unlocks some new-type transfer
rule_family_diversity_scaling.concept_primer: Think of the bug-fixer as a mechanic. Instead of more cars of the same make, you give it the same number spread across more makes, hoping it can then fix a make it has never touched.
rule_family_diversity_scaling.plain_question: If we train a bug-fixer on a wider variety of problem types without adding any examples, can it fix problem types it never saw during training?
rule_family_diversity_scaling.plain_answer: Yes, but modestly. Spreading the same examples across 12 problem types instead of 3 lifted fixes on never-seen types from none to about 29%, while fixes on familiar types stayed high. Nearly all the gain came from one lucky new type.
rule_family_diversity_scaling.why_it_matters: When you cannot add more training data, broadening the variety of problem types can buy some transfer to unfamiliar cases. But check whether it generalizes broadly or just happens to crack one lucky case.

key_numbers.label: Never-seen problem types fixed
key_numbers.sub: as training variety grew from 3 to 12 types

key_numbers.label: Total training examples
key_numbers.sub: held fixed at every variety level

key_numbers.label: Fixes without the step-by-step hint
key_numbers.sub: the transfer gain vanishes if the hint is removed or scrambled

charts.chart_plain_title: Fixes on new problem types rise only at the widest training variety
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is how many problem-type varieties were in training (3, 6, 12); the vertical axis is the share of bugs fixed, higher is better. One line tracks familiar problems, one the same problems in new formats, and one brand-new problem types.
charts.chart_takeaway: The brand-new-type line stays flat near zero until 12 varieties, where it jumps to about 29%, while familiar-problem fixes stay high throughout.

charts.chart_plain_title: The new-type fixes need a genuine step-by-step hint at both training and test
charts.chart_read: Bars compare how many never-seen-type bugs each setup fixes; taller is better. The first bar is the full trained helper with a real hint, then variants that drop or scramble the hint at test or training time, then the untrained base.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the full setup with a real hint reaches about 29%; every stripped or scrambled variant collapses to near zero, so the hint is doing the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Almost all transfer comes from a single new problem type
charts.chart_read: Each bar is one never-seen problem type (12 tasks each); height is the share fixed, higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: One type is nearly solved at 92% and another partly at 25%, while two types get zero fixes, so the gain is narrow, not broad.

charts.chart_plain_title: On familiar problems, only one bug family takes a hit as variety grows
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of familiar bugs fixed for three bug families; grouped by 3, 6, and 12 training varieties; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Two families stay perfectly fixed at every variety level, and all the wobble lands on the affine bug family.

charts.chart_plain_title: Robustness to new formats depends heavily on which bug family
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of same-problems-in-new-formats bugs fixed for three bug families, grouped by 3, 6, and 12 training varieties; higher is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The threshold family stays near-perfect across formats, the affine family is stuck near the bottom, and the slug family slips at the widest variety.

charts.chart_plain_title: Getting the right label early climbs before full fixes catch up
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is training variety (3, 6, 12); vertical axis is a share on new-type problems, higher is better. One line is how often the correct rule label appears, the other how often the bug is fully fixed.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-label line rises steadily while full fixes stay flat until 12 varieties, suggesting partial progress builds before complete repairs arrive.

charts.chart_plain_title: The transfer gain is not bought with extra thinking time
charts.chart_read: Bars show median seconds to produce one attempt on new-type problems for each setup; there is no better direction, this checks whether some setups simply ran longer.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every trained setup takes about eight seconds regardless of variety, so the widest-variety gain is not explained by spending more time per attempt.

sampled_query_filter_executor.verdict_tag: One sampled answer teaches the entire hidden world
sampled_query_filter_executor.concept_primer: Two unknown numbers sit locked a fixed distance apart. A short program nudges them and drops clues that rule out combinations. To answer any question the model must track every still-possible pair of numbers, though it is graded on just one guessed final answer per problem.
sampled_query_filter_executor.plain_question: If you only ever grade a model on one possible answer per practice problem, can it still learn the full hidden set of possibilities behind every answer?
sampled_query_filter_executor.plain_answer: Yes. Graded on just one sampled final answer per problem, the model rebuilt the entire set of still-possible number pairs it was never shown, capturing 94 to 98 percent of it, because holding that full set is the cheapest way to answer varied questions. Simpler models that tracked each number separately, or skipped step-by-step execution, stayed under 6 percent.
sampled_query_filter_executor.why_it_matters: When every answer in a task shares one hidden structure, you can supervise only final answers and let the model build that structure itself, but only if its design can hold the correlated whole rather than separate pieces, and you allow enough internal steps.

key_numbers.label: Hidden set of pairs recovered, never taught
key_numbers.sub: share of the never-shown set of possible number pairs the internal state captured

key_numbers.label: Same score for the best simpler model
key_numbers.sub: controls that track numbers separately or skip step-by-step execution

key_numbers.label: Stretch beyond training length
key_numbers.sub: trained on programs up to 8 steps, still about 95% correct at 24 steps

key_numbers.label: Longest programs, before vs after enough steps
key_numbers.sub: answer accuracy once internal steps reach the program length

charts.chart_plain_title: Answer accuracy on longer programs: full tracker versus two simpler models
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program length (4 to 24; models trained only up to 8). Height is probability placed on the correct answer, higher is better. Three bars per length: the full joint tracker, a separate-numbers tracker, and a one-shot no-steps model.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full tracker holds above 95 percent even at three times the training length, while both simpler models collapse toward 5 to 12 percent.

charts.chart_plain_title: Accuracy jumps exactly when internal steps reach program length
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is the number of internal thinking steps allowed; the vertical axis is probability on the correct answer, higher is better. Each line is one program length (4, 8, 12, 16, 24).
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line stays flat and low until the steps reach its own program length, then snaps up to 95 to 99 percent, the signature of genuine step-by-step execution.

charts.chart_plain_title: The never-shown hidden set of pairs is recovered anyway
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program length; height is probability placed on the true set of still-possible number pairs, which was withheld from training entirely. Higher is better. Three bars each: full joint tracker, separate-numbers tracker, one-shot model.
charts.chart_takeaway: The joint tracker recovers 94 to 98 percent of a hidden set it was never taught, while both simpler models stay under 6 percent.

charts.chart_plain_title: All four question types clear the bar, including the combined ones
charts.chart_read: Bars group by unseen program length; height is answer accuracy once internal steps reach program length, higher is better. Four bars per length, one per question type: first number, second number, their sum, and their difference.
charts.chart_takeaway: No question type lags: even the harder sum and difference questions stay above 94 percent, so the model holds a genuinely joint state, not two separate ones.

charts.chart_plain_title: Hidden-state accuracy climbs during training while chasing the graded answer
charts.chart_read: The horizontal axis is training step; the vertical axis is probability on the correct target, higher is better. One line is the graded answer accuracy, the other the never-graded hidden set-of-pairs accuracy.
charts.chart_takeaway: The hidden line starts near 3 percent while the graded answer sits at 39 percent, then both climb and converge near 98 percent, hidden structure trailing the reward by roughly 200 steps.

charts.chart_plain_title: A separate smaller setup reproduces the same win
charts.chart_read: Bars group by program length in an independent smaller-number version of the task; height is answer accuracy at a fixed internal-step budget, higher is better. Three bars each: full joint tracker and two simpler controls.
charts.chart_takeaway: The joint tracker holds near 99 percent at every length while both controls decay below 31 percent, so the result is not a fluke of one problem size.

sparse_support_memory_executor.verdict_tag: One memory slot per possibility restores perfect accuracy
sparse_support_memory_executor.concept_primer: Imagine tracking a puzzle where several answers are still possible; you keep one sticky note per candidate. If you have enough notes, you never lose the true one.
sparse_support_memory_executor.plain_question: Does a small working memory need a separate slot for every possible answer to solve step-by-step reasoning puzzles exactly?
sparse_support_memory_executor.plain_answer: Yes, when the memory has one slot for every candidate answer, it solves every puzzle perfectly at all lengths; with fewer slots, accuracy falls steadily the longer the puzzle runs.
sparse_support_memory_executor.why_it_matters: Reasoning that keeps multiple options alive breaks not from too little raw memory size but from too few addressable slots; give one slot per live possibility.

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on longest puzzles, too few slots
key_numbers.sub: half the needed slots, hardest length

key_numbers.label: Accuracy on longest puzzles, full slots
key_numbers.sub: one slot per possibility

key_numbers.label: Puzzles solved exactly at full capacity
key_numbers.sub: every length, no errors

charts.chart_plain_title: Fewer memory slots lose accuracy as puzzles get longer
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is puzzle length; vertical axis is how often the exact answer is kept, higher is better. Each line is a memory size; the top line has one slot per possibility.
charts.chart_takeaway: The full-capacity line stays flat at the top across all lengths, while every smaller-memory line slopes downward, meaning missing slots cost accuracy on longer puzzles.

charts.chart_plain_title: The same pattern repeats with far more possibilities
charts.chart_read: Horizontal axis is puzzle length; vertical axis is how often the exact answer is kept, higher is better. Each line is a memory size, with the top line holding one slot per possibility.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the full-capacity line stays perfectly flat at the top, confirming that even a large-but-incomplete memory leaks accuracy as puzzles lengthen.

structured_slot_initializer_ladder.verdict_tag: Constrained slot setup beats a bigger network
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.concept_primer: The job splits in two: first place each unknown value into its own labeled slot, then a flawless calculator runs the steps. Any error can only come from a sloppy setup.
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.plain_question: When a flawless calculator handles the math, what kind of setup best places each value into its own slot?
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.plain_answer: Yes, a specific structure won: a setup that forces each value into its own distinct slot landed 98.1% of answers on the exact right target, versus 55.5% for a bigger general-purpose network.
structured_slot_initializer_ladder.why_it_matters: When a task has a fixed, exact backend, invest in a setup that guarantees every value claims its own slot rather than scaling up a generic network. The constraint, not size, closes the gap.

key_numbers.label: Answer landing on the exact right target
key_numbers.sub: generic network vs constrained setup

key_numbers.label: Values that got their own slot
key_numbers.sub: generic network vs constrained setup

key_numbers.label: Gap to a perfect setup
key_numbers.sub: constrained setup trails the ideal ceiling

charts.chart_plain_title: How well each setup style places the answer
charts.chart_read: Each group along the bottom is a setup style, from a perfect reference on the left to a plain general-purpose network on the right; taller bars mean more of the answer landed on the exact right target, which is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Bars climb steadily as more structure is added, with the constrained setup nearly reaching the perfect reference while the plain network sits far below it.

charts.chart_plain_title: Answer quality stays flat as the task runs longer
charts.chart_read: The bottom axis is how many steps the calculator runs; the height is how much of the answer stays on the exact target, higher being better; each line is one setup style, with the perfect reference on top.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every line runs nearly flat across all lengths, showing the calculator neither repairs nor worsens the setup, so final quality is locked in at the start.

charts.chart_plain_title: Learning the rule is not enough without slot coverage
charts.chart_read: Each group is a setup style; one bar shows whether it learned the value relationship, the other whether every value got its own slot; taller is better on both.
charts.chart_takeaway: Some setups score high on learning the rule yet leave many slots doubled up, and only the constrained setup reaches full marks on giving each value its own slot.

charts.chart_plain_title: Constrained setup tracks the ideal across problem sizes
charts.chart_read: The bottom axis is increasing problem size, from small to largest; the height is how much of the answer lands on target, higher being better; one bar is the learned constrained setup, the other the ideal reference.
charts.chart_takeaway: The learned setup's bars stay within about two points of the ideal reference at every size, holding near-perfect even at the largest problem.

targeted_bridge_allocation.verdict_tag: Targeting hard cases wins only narrowly
targeted_bridge_allocation.concept_primer: Think of a fixed study budget: the model gets a set number of worked examples showing how to combine two skills it never saw combined, and you decide which combinations to spend them on.
targeted_bridge_allocation.plain_question: When you can only give a fixed set of worked examples, does concentrating them on the hardest skill-combinations beat spreading them evenly?
targeted_bridge_allocation.plain_answer: It depends. Concentrating the worked examples on the hardest skill-combinations edged out spreading them evenly (33% vs 28% of new combinations repaired), but the gap was small; where examples went, and whether each came with a correct step-by-step solution, mattered far more than how many there were.
targeted_bridge_allocation.why_it_matters: When you cannot add more training data, spend your limited worked examples on the combinations the model currently fails, and always pair each one with a correct step-by-step solution, since that pairing is what actually makes them work.

key_numbers.label: Even spread vs concentrated on hard cases
key_numbers.sub: share of never-seen skill-combinations repaired

key_numbers.label: Untrained starting point
key_numbers.sub: the frozen model repairs none of them

key_numbers.label: With correct solution vs without
key_numbers.sub: removing the step-by-step solution collapses repair

charts.chart_plain_title: Where you spend a few worked examples changes repair
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of never-seen skill-combinations a trained model fixes; the leftmost frozen bar is the untrained starting point; bars move from spreading examples evenly to concentrating them on the hardest cases. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The concentrated hard-case bar is tallest at 33%, only 5 points above even spreading, yet every trained bar towers over the frozen model's 0%.

charts.chart_plain_title: A correct worked-out solution in training is what makes it work
charts.chart_read: Three groups of bars, one per test type; colors are training with a correct step-by-step solution, with none, or with a scrambled one. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: The correct-solution bars dwarf the others on every test, dropping from 78% to under 20% even on familiar problems once the solution is removed or scrambled.

charts.chart_plain_title: Concentrating examples opens skill-combinations even spreading leaves stuck
charts.chart_read: Bars grouped by unseen skill-combination family; colors are even spread, concentrated on hard cases, and a control that concentrates on easy cases. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Concentrating on hard cases lifts two families off 0% that even spreading never cracks, while the already-easy families stay maxed out.

charts.chart_plain_title: Flooding one skill with examples is not the lever
charts.chart_read: Each bar shows repair on never-seen combinations for the even-spread baseline versus pouring sixteen examples into a single skill family. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Overloading one family helps modestly at best (34% for modulo) and actually lowers overall repair for length, so sheer volume is not what helps.

charts.chart_plain_title: Concentrating examples costs nothing on familiar problems
charts.chart_read: Bars per training condition, two colors for familiar problems and reworded versions of them; the frozen bar is the untrained baseline. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Every trained condition clusters in a 69-83% band, so concentrating the worked examples on hard cases sacrifices nothing on familiar or reworded problems.

charts.chart_plain_title: At test time the model still needs the worked-out solution shown
charts.chart_read: Three test types along the bottom; colors are giving the model a correct step-by-step solution, none, or a scrambled one in the prompt. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Stripping or scrambling the solution from the prompt crashes even familiar-problem repair from 78% to under 20%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Repair depends heavily on the kind of task
charts.chart_read: One bar per task mechanism, sorted from most to least repaired, for the concentrated hard-case model on never-seen combinations. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Repair splits sharply: step-through and text-cleanup tasks reach 100%, while remainder-arithmetic tasks stay flat at 0%.

charts.chart_plain_title: Total number of examples does not predict repair
charts.chart_read: Each dot is a training condition; left-to-right is the total worked examples used, up is repair on never-seen combinations. A rising trend would mean more examples help.
charts.chart_takeaway: Conditions with the exact same example count scatter from 20% to 33%, so how examples are allocated matters more than how many.

trace_keyed_symbol_repair.verdict_tag: Reading the error output enables repair
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.concept_primer: When a test fails, its error message prints the correct value the code should have used. This task asks whether the model can learn to pull that value out of the error and paste it into a fix.
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.plain_question: Can the model learn to fix a broken value by copying the right one out of the failing test's error message?
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.plain_answer: Yes. After training to use the error message, the model fixed all 60 test cases on both familiar and unseen value styles, while every version that lacked the error message or was trained to ignore it fixed none.
trace_keyed_symbol_repair.why_it_matters: A failing test's output is a usable source of answers, not just a red light. Training a model to actually read it, rather than assuming it will on its own, is what unlocks the fix.

key_numbers.label: Cases fixed after training to use the error message
key_numbers.sub: none fixed before, all 60 fixed after

key_numbers.label: Same trained model when the error message is taken away
key_numbers.sub: the message content, not the format, carries the answer

key_numbers.label: Untrained model copying the right value from the message
key_numbers.sub: rarely does it on its own

charts.chart_plain_title: Only the model trained to read the error message fixes the code
charts.chart_read: Each bar is the share of tasks fixed for one setup; two bars per setup show familiar value styles and brand-new value styles. Taller is better. Only the rightmost setup, trained to use the error message and given it, rises.
charts.chart_takeaway: Four setups sit flat at zero and the trained-with-message one hits the top on both styles, so reading the error message is what does the work.

charts.chart_plain_title: Take the error message away and the same model stops fixing anything
charts.chart_read: Bars show the share of tasks fixed by the one trained model under three inference conditions: correct message, no message, and a mismatched message. Two bars per condition for familiar and new value styles. Taller is better.
charts.chart_takeaway: Only the correct-message bars reach the top; no-message and mismatched-message drop to zero, showing the answer lives in the message content itself.

charts.chart_plain_title: How often the right value gets copied out of the error message
charts.chart_read: Bars show how often each setup pastes the exact correct value into its fix, split into familiar and new value styles. Taller is better. The untrained model shows a short bar; the trained model reaches the top.
charts.chart_takeaway: The untrained model copies the right value only about 18% of the time and near-never on new styles, while training makes copying it reliable every time.

charts.chart_plain_title: Deleting the wrong value is easy; writing the right one is not
charts.chart_read: For each setup, one bar is how often it removes the wrong value and another is how often the code is actually fixed, on familiar-style tasks. Taller is better. Most setups remove the wrong value but leave the fix bar flat.
charts.chart_takeaway: Nearly every trained variant deletes the wrong value, but only the one that reads the correct message writes the right replacement and passes.

charts.chart_plain_title: Well-formed edits are cheap; correct edits need the error message
charts.chart_read: For each setup, bars show how often the edit is well-formed, the code still runs, and the tests actually pass, on familiar-style tasks. Taller is better. The first two measures fill up for every trained setup; only the last separates them.
charts.chart_takeaway: All trained setups produce clean, runnable edits, but only the model that reads the error message produces edits that pass the tests.

charts.chart_plain_title: Give the model enough room to write the whole fix
charts.chart_read: Two setups differ only in how much output length the model is allowed. Bars show whether the edit is usable and whether it fixes the code, on a small pilot. Taller is better. The tight-limit setup is flat; the roomier one fills up.
charts.chart_takeaway: With too little output room the model cannot finish a usable edit at all, but doubling the room flips it to fully fixing the pilot cases.
